topics.title: Principal Component Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT745 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principal Component Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** is a classical, unsupervised linear dimensionality-reduction technique that projects a high-dimensional dataset onto a new set of mutually orthogonal axes — called *principal components* — such that the **maximum variance** of the data is preserved along the first axis, the **second-highest residual variance** along the second axis, and so on, in strictly decreasing order.

Mathematically, PCA computes an orthogonal linear transformation

$$
\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{W}^{\top} \mathbf{X}
$$

where $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ is the centered data matrix (with $n$ samples and $p$ features), $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times k}$ is the projection matrix whose columns are the top-$k$ eigenvectors of the sample covariance matrix, and $\mathbf{Y} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ is the low-dimensional representation. By convention, the *variance ratio* retained by the first $k$ components is given by

$$
\eta_k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i}
$$

where $\lambda_i$ denotes the $i$-th eigenvalue of the covariance matrix. The most widely used threshold is **$\eta_k \geq 0.95$** (i.e., retain enough components to capture **95 %** of total variance).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are photographing a 3-D sculpture of a human figure. Instead of capturing 100 photographs from random angles, you realize that almost all the "interesting information" lies along two main directions: the **length** (head to toe) and the **width** (shoulder to shoulder). The third direction (front to back) is fairly thin and repetitive. So you decide to project the sculpture onto a 2-D plane that contains these two informative directions.

PCA does **exactly the same thing** to data:

- The **3-D sculpture** = a dataset with $p$ features (dimensions).
- The **two informative directions** = the principal components (eigenvectors).
- The **2-D photograph** = the reduced dataset $\mathbf{Y}$ with $k \ll p$ features.
- The **"importance" of each direction"** = the eigenvalue (variance) associated with that direction.

## 1.3 Why PCA is a Foundational Topic in Deep Learning

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU PEITT745 syllabus, PCA is the gateway concept that links **classical statistical learning** to **modern representation learning** used in deep neural networks. It introduces the student to:
> - Eigen-decomposition of the covariance matrix.
> - Variance-maximization and reconstruction-error-minimization as dual objectives.
> - Whitening, which is a precursor to Batch Normalization in deep nets.
> - The Curse-of-Dimensionality mitigation principle that motivates autoencoders.

## 1.4 Geometric and Statistical Interpretation

PCA can be derived from **two equivalent viewpoints**, both of which frequently appear in KTU questions:

1. **Variance-Maximization (MaxVar) View** — Find orthogonal directions along which the projected data has maximum variance.
2. **Reconstruction-Error Minimization (MinErr) View** — Find an orthogonal $k$-dimensional subspace that minimizes the mean-squared distance from each data point to its projection on that subspace.

These two views lead to the **same mathematical answer** — the top-$k$ eigenvectors of the covariance matrix — but each opens up a different proof technique, and examiners regularly test both.

## 1.5 Assumptions and Limitations

> [!WARNING]
> KTU students must memorize the following limitations of PCA — they appear every year as 2- or 3-mark questions:
> - PCA assumes a **linear** relationship between features.
> - PCA is **sensitive to the scale** of features (always standardize first).
> - PCA is **not robust to outliers** (use Robust PCA in that case).
> - Principal components are **not interpretable** in terms of original features.
> - PCA assumes the directions of greatest variance are the most informative, which is **not always true** (e.g., in supervised settings, low-variance directions can be highly discriminative).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow of PCA — Step-by-Step Logic

The standard PCA algorithm is performed in **five logical stages**. Each stage maps to a specific exam question and to a specific KTU Course Outcome (CO1: *Apply mathematical foundations to deep learning models*).

### Step 1 — Data Centering
Subtract the mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\mathbf{x}_j$ from every sample.

- **Why**: PCA's variance objective is defined w.r.t. the origin. Without centering, the first component will simply point towards the centroid's location rather than the direction of maximum spread.

### Step 2 — Optional Standardization
Divide each feature by its standard deviation $\sigma_i$ to obtain a unit-variance matrix.

- **Why**: When features live on different scales (e.g., height in cm, weight in kg, age in years), the high-variance feature will dominate the principal components.

### Step 3 — Compute the Sample Covariance Matrix
The $p \times p$ symmetric positive-semi-definite matrix

$$
\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}
$$

captures pairwise feature co-variation.

### Step 4 — Eigen-Decomposition
Solve the characteristic equation

$$
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\,\mathbf{w}_i = \lambda_i\,\mathbf{w}_i, \qquad i = 1, 2, \ldots, p
$$

The eigenvectors $\mathbf{w}_i$ are the **principal directions** and the eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ are the **amount of variance** carried by each direction.

### Step 5 — Rank, Select, Project
Sort the eigenvectors in decreasing order of $\lambda_i$ and select the top $k$ that satisfy the chosen variance threshold (e.g., **$\eta_k \geq 0.95$**). Form $\mathbf{W}$ and compute $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}$.

## 2.2 The Dual Objective — Proof Sketch of Equivalence

The MaxVar view chooses $\mathbf{w}_1$ to maximize

$$
\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_1 \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\mathbf{w}_1 = 1
$$

The MinErr view chooses a $k$-D subspace $\mathcal{S}$ that minimizes

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n}\Vert\,\mathbf{x}_j - \mathbf{P}_{\mathcal{S}}\mathbf{x}_j\,\Vert_2^{2}
$$

where $\mathbf{P}_{\mathcal{S}} = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{\top}$ is the projection operator. Both objectives, when optimized via Lagrange multipliers or the Eckart–Young theorem, yield the **same** top-$k$ eigenpair solution.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete list of equations** you must commit to memory for the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination. Every 14-mark PCA question will require at least 3–4 of these.

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|-------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Centered data matrix | $\mathbf{X} = [\mathbf{x}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_n - \boldsymbol{\mu}]^{\top}$ | $n \times p$ |
| 2 | Mean vector | $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\mathbf{x}_j$ | $p \times 1$ |
| 3 | Sample covariance | $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1}\,\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$ | $p \times p$, symmetric, PSD |
| 4 | Eigen-decomposition | $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \mathbf{W}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{W}^{\top}$ | $\boldsymbol{\Lambda} = \mathrm{diag}(\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_p)$ |
| 5 | Orthonormality | $\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{I}_p$ | Eigenvectors of symmetric $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ |
| 6 | Projection | $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}_k$ | $n \times k$ reduced data |
| 7 | Reconstruction | $\hat{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{Y}\mathbf{W}_k^{\top}$ | $n \times p$ back to original space |
| 8 | Reconstruction error (MSE) | $J_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\Vert\,\mathbf{x}_j - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_j\,\Vert_2^{2} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{p}\lambda_i$ | Equals sum of *discarded* eigenvalues |
| 9 | Variance ratio retained | $\eta_k = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{p}\lambda_i}$ | Typically $\geq 0.95$ |
| 10 | Total variance | $\mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) = \sum_{i=1}^{p}\lambda_i = \sum_{j=1}^{p}\mathrm{Var}(x^{(j)})$ | Trace invariance |
| 11 | Singular value link | $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{V}\mathbf{S}^{2}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$ | From SVD of $\mathbf{X}$ |
| 12 | Explained variance per PC | $\mathrm{EV}_i = \dfrac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{p}\lambda_j}$ | Expressed as percentage |
| 13 | Whitening transform | $\mathbf{X}_{\text{white}} = \boldsymbol{\Lambda}^{-1/2}\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$ | Decorrelates + unit-variance |
| 14 | Lagrange objective (MaxVar) | $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w},\alpha) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} - \alpha(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} - 1)$ | KKT first-order conditions |
| 15 | KKT condition | $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} = \alpha\mathbf{w}$ | Eigenvalue $\alpha$ = max variance |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of PCA

PCA is not just textbook material — it is the workhorse of modern data preprocessing:

- **Computer Vision (CV)**: Used in classical face recognition (the famous *Eigenfaces* of Turk & Pentland). Each eigenface is a principal component of a face-image dataset.
- **Medical Imaging**: Dimensionality reduction of fMRI, MRI, and CT scans before feeding them to a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). This reduces GPU memory by 5–10×.
- **Genomics & Bioinformatics**: Reducing gene-expression datasets (with $p \approx 20{,}000$ genes) to 50–100 principal components for downstream clustering.
- **Anomaly Detection**: In semiconductor manufacturing, the *last few* PCs often capture noise; reconstruction error on those PCs is used as a fault indicator.
- **Pre-training of Deep Networks**: Whitened inputs (PCA + ZCA) accelerate the convergence of early deep-learning training — a trick now largely replaced by Batch Normalization but historically critical.
- **Recommender Systems**: Matrix factorization used in collaborative filtering is, mathematically, a generalization of PCA to rectangular matrices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section contains the **canonical derivations** that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce in full on the answer sheet. Partial credit is awarded for every correct intermediate step.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PCA via Variance Maximization (MaxVar Derivation)

We want to find the unit vector $\mathbf{w}_1$ along which the projected data has the **largest variance**. The variance of the scalar projection $\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\mathbf{x}$ is

$$
\mathrm{Var}(\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_1
$$

We therefore solve the constrained optimization problem

$$
\max_{\mathbf{w}_1}\;\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_1 \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\mathbf{w}_1 = 1
$$

**Step 1 — Construct the Lagrangian.** Introduce a Lagrange multiplier $\alpha_1$ for the unit-norm constraint:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}_1, \alpha_1) = \mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_1 - \alpha_1\!\left(\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\mathbf{w}_1 - 1\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Take the gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}_1$ and set it to zero.** Using the standard matrix identity $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{w}) = 2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{w}$ (when $\mathbf{A}$ is symmetric):

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}_1} = 2\,\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_1 - 2\alpha_1\,\mathbf{w}_1 = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to obtain the eigen-equation.**

$$
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\,\mathbf{w}_1 = \alpha_1\,\mathbf{w}_1
$$

This is the **eigen-equation** of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$. Hence the optimal $\mathbf{w}_1$ must be an eigenvector of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$.

**Step 4 — Determine which eigenvector maximizes the variance.** Left-multiply by $\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}$:

$$
\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_1 = \alpha_1\,\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\mathbf{w}_1 = \alpha_1
$$

Therefore, the *projected variance equals the eigenvalue*. To maximize the projected variance, we choose the eigenvector corresponding to the **largest** eigenvalue $\lambda_1$:

$$
\mathbf{w}_1^{\star} = \text{eigenvector of } \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \text{ associated with } \lambda_{\max}
$$

**Step 5 — Sequential derivation for $\mathbf{w}_2$.** We repeat the same procedure with an additional constraint that $\mathbf{w}_2$ must be orthogonal to $\mathbf{w}_1$:

$$
\max_{\mathbf{w}_2}\;\mathbf{w}_2^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \mathbf{w}_2^{\top}\mathbf{w}_2 = 1,\;\; \mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\mathbf{w}_2 = 0
$$

The Lagrangian becomes

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}_2,\alpha_2,\beta) = \mathbf{w}_2^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_2 - \alpha_2(\mathbf{w}_2^{\top}\mathbf{w}_2 - 1) - \beta\,\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\mathbf{w}_2
$$

Taking the gradient and setting it to zero:

$$
2\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_2 - 2\alpha_2\mathbf{w}_2 - \beta\mathbf{w}_1 = \mathbf{0}
$$

Left-multiply by $\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}$ and use $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_1 = \lambda_1\mathbf{w}_1$:

$$
2\lambda_1\,\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\mathbf{w}_2 - 2\alpha_2\,\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\mathbf{w}_2 - \beta = 0
$$

Since $\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\mathbf{w}_2 = 0$, we obtain $\beta = 0$. The equation reduces back to the eigen-problem, and $\mathbf{w}_2$ is the eigenvector of the **second-largest** eigenvalue $\lambda_2$.

**Conclusion.** Continuing this way, the $k$-th principal direction $\mathbf{w}_k$ is the eigenvector of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ associated with the $k$-th largest eigenvalue $\lambda_k$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PCA via Reconstruction-Error Minimization (MinErr Derivation)

We want a $k$-dimensional subspace $\mathcal{S}$ that minimizes the mean-squared reconstruction error:

$$
J_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\Vert\,\mathbf{x}_j - \mathbf{P}_{\mathcal{S}}\mathbf{x}_j\,\Vert_2^{2}
$$

Let $\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{w}_1,\ldots,\mathbf{w}_k]$ be an orthonormal basis of $\mathcal{S}$ (i.e., $\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{I}_k$). The orthogonal projection of $\mathbf{x}_j$ onto $\mathcal{S}$ is $\mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j$, so the projected coordinates are $\mathbf{z}_j = \mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j$.

**Step 1 — Expand the squared error.**

$$
J_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\big(\mathbf{x}_j - \mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j\big)^{\top}\big(\mathbf{x}_j - \mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j\big)
$$

**Step 2 — Use the identity $\Vert\mathbf{u}\Vert^{2} = \mathbf{u}^{\top}\mathbf{u}$ and expand.**

$$
J_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\big[\mathbf{x}_j^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j - 2\mathbf{x}_j^{\top}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j + \mathbf{x}_j^{\top}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j\big]
$$

Because $\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{I}_k$ and $\mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{\top} = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{\top}$ (idempotent projector):

$$
J_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\big[\mathbf{x}_j^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j - \mathbf{x}_j^{\top}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j\big]
$$

**Step 3 — Note that $\frac{1}{n}\sum_j \mathbf{x}_j^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j = \mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ is constant.** Therefore minimizing $J_k$ is equivalent to **maximizing** the projected variance:

$$
\max_{\mathbf{W}} \;\mathrm{tr}\!\left(\mathbf{W}^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{W}\right) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{I}_k
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Eckart–Young–Mirsky Theorem** (or Lagrange multiplier derivation). The optimal $\mathbf{W}$ consists of the top-$k$ eigenvectors of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$. Substituting this optimal $\mathbf{W}$ back:

$$
J_k^{\star} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{p}\lambda_i
$$

This is the **sum of the discarded eigenvalues** — a result that is tested virtually every KTU examination cycle.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Frequently Asked Style)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 scheme places high weight on **hand-computed PCA** for small $2 \times 2$ or $3 \times 3$ matrices. Practice this example until the steps are automatic.

**Given** the centered, scaled data matrix

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \\ 4 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Verify that $\mathbf{X}$ is already centered** (each column sums to 10, so the column mean is 2.5 and is not zero). Re-center by subtracting column means $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (2.5, 2.5)^{\top}$:

$$
\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & -1.5 \\ \phantom{-}0.5 & \phantom{-}1.5 \\ \phantom{-}1.5 & \phantom{-}0.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c$ with $n = 4$:**

$$
\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 4 \\ 4 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 4 \\ 4 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5/3 & 4/3 \\ 4/3 & 5/3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the characteristic equation $\det(\boldsymbol{\Sigma} - \lambda\mathbf{I}) = 0$:**

$$
\det\!\begin{pmatrix} 5/3 - \lambda & 4/3 \\ 4/3 & 5/3 - \lambda \end{pmatrix} = \left(\tfrac{5}{3} - \lambda\right)^{2} - \left(\tfrac{4}{3}\right)^{2} = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^{2} - \tfrac{10}{3}\lambda + \tfrac{25-16}{9} = \lambda^{2} - \tfrac{10}{3}\lambda + 1 = 0
$$

Solving the quadratic:

$$
\lambda = \frac{\frac{10}{3} \pm \sqrt{\frac{100}{9} - 4}}{2} = \frac{5 \pm 3}{3}
$$

$$
\lambda_1 = \frac{8}{3}, \qquad \lambda_2 = \frac{2}{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the eigenvectors.** For $\lambda_1 = 8/3$:

$$
\!\begin{pmatrix} 5/3 - 8/3 & 4/3 \\ 4/3 & 5/3 - 8/3 \end{pmatrix}\!\mathbf{w}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 4/3 \\ 4/3 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\!\mathbf{w}_1 = \mathbf{0}
$$

So $-w_{1,1} + \tfrac{4}{3}w_{1,2} = 0 \Rightarrow w_{1,1} = \tfrac{4}{3}w_{1,2}$. Choosing $w_{1,2} = 3$ gives $\mathbf{w}_1 = (4, 3)^{\top}$. Normalize:

$$
\mathbf{w}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{16+9}}\begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}
$$

By orthogonality, $\mathbf{w}_2 = \frac{1}{5}(-3, 4)^{\top}$.

**Step 5 — Variance ratio retained by first PC.**

$$
\eta_1 = \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2} = \frac{8/3}{8/3 + 2/3} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8
$$

So $k=1$ PC retains **80 %** of total variance. To reach the **95 %** threshold, we would need to retain both components.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for Selecting $k$

| Scenario | Recommended $k$ | Justification |
|----------|----------------|---------------|
| Visualization | $k = 2$ or $k = 3$ | Human eyes can interpret only 2-D / 3-D plots |
| Pre-processing for a downstream classifier | $k$ such that $\eta_k \geq 0.95$ | Standard ML practice (95 % variance rule) |
| Hard memory budget (e.g., embedded device) | Smallest $k$ with $\eta_k \geq 0.90$ | Engineering trade-off |
| Specific downstream task (e.g., classification) | Cross-validated | Task-driven, not purely variance-driven |
| Noisy dataset (noise dominates small eigenvalues) | Drop components with $\lambda_i \approx 0$ | Kaiser / scree-plot rule |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of PCA

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **end-to-end data flow** of PCA — from raw input to reconstructed output. It is the kind of block diagram a KTU 14-mark question on PCA would expect in part (a).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA["RAW DATA MATRIX X (n x p)"]
    blockB["STAGE 1 : MEAN CENTERING subtract column means mu"]
    blockC["STAGE 2 : OPTIONAL STANDARDIZATION divide by std deviation sigma"]
    blockD["STAGE 3 : COVARIANCE COMPUTATION Sigma equals X transpose X divided by n minus 1"]
    blockE["STAGE 4 : EIGEN DECOMPOSITION solve Sigma w equals lambda w"]
    blockF["STAGE 5 : SORT EIGENPAIRS descending order of lambda"]
    blockG["STAGE 6 : SELECT TOP K COMPONENTS variance ratio greater than 0.95"]
    blockH["STAGE 7 : FORM PROJECTION MATRIX W k from top k eigenvectors"]
    blockI["STAGE 8 : COMPUTE REDUCED DATA Y equals X W k"]
    blockJ["STAGE 9 : OPTIONAL RECONSTRUCTION X hat equals Y W k transpose"]
    blockK["STAGE 10 : COMPUTE RECONSTRUCTION ERROR sum of discarded eigenvalues"]
    outL["LOW DIMENSIONAL OUTPUT Y (n x k)"]
    outM["RECONSTRUCTED DATA X hat (n x p)"]

    inputA --> blockB
    blockB --> blockC
    blockC --> blockD
    blockD --> blockE
    blockE --> blockF
    blockF --> blockG
    blockG --> blockH
    blockH --> blockI
    blockI --> outL
    blockI --> blockJ
    blockJ --> outM
    blockJ --> blockK
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Schematic — Geometric Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Original Data Cloud in p-D space"] -- "Project onto" --> nodeB["Principal Component 1 direction of maximum variance"]
    nodeA -- "Project onto" --> nodeC["Principal Component 2 orthogonal to PC1 second max variance"]
    nodeB -- "Combined 2-D coordinates" --> nodeD["Reduced Representation Y in R k"]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD -- "Reconstruct via W W transpose" --> nodeE["Approximation of Original Data X hat"]
```

## 4.3 Eigen-Decomposition as a Functional Module

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeSigma["Covariance Matrix Sigma (p x p) symmetric positive semi definite"]
    nodeSolver["EIGEN SOLVER characteristic polynomial"]
    nodeLambdas["Eigenvalues lambda 1 greater than lambda 2 greater than ... greater than lambda p"]
    nodeW["Eigenvectors W orthogonal columns"]
    nodeRecon["Verify Sigma equals W Lambda W transpose"]
    nodeSigma --> nodeSolver
    nodeSolver --> nodeLambdas
    nodeSolver --> nodeW
    nodeLambdas --> nodeRecon
    nodeW --> nodeRecon
```

## 4.4 Variance-Retention Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{"Total variance target?"}
    q1 -- "Visualization 2D" --> a1["Set k = 2"]
    q1 -- "Engineering 95 percent" --> a2["Find smallest k with eta k greater than or equal 0.95"]
    q1 -- "Scree plot elbow" --> a3["Locate elbow in eigenvalue curve"]
    q1 -- "Kaiser rule" --> a4["Retain eigenvalues lambda greater than 1"]
    q1 -- "Cross validation" --> a5["Choose k maximizing downstream task accuracy"]
```

## 4.5 KTU Frequently Drawn Diagram — Eigenface Pipeline

The *Eigenface* pipeline is the most commonly asked PCA diagram in KTU 2019–2024 papers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fa["Face Image Dataset n images each p pixels"] --> fb["Vectorize and Center each image becomes a column"]
    fb --> fc["Compute Covariance Matrix Sigma"]
    fc --> fd["Eigen Decomposition yields eigenfaces"]
    fd --> fe["Project new test face onto eigenface space"]
    fe --> ff["Reconstruct or Classify in low dimensional space"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Pro tip for the exam:** Even when a question does not explicitly ask for a diagram, drawing the block-level data flow (similar to the diagram in §4.1) earns at least **1–2 easy marks** as it demonstrates structural understanding of the algorithm.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — 3 Marks
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Explain the term "Principal Component" in the context of PCA. Why is it necessary to center the data before applying PCA?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **principal component** is a linear combination of the original features of a dataset. Each principal component corresponds to a direction in feature space along which the data exhibits maximum variance, with the components ordered so that the first carries the largest variance, the second the next largest, and so on. Mathematically, the $i$-th principal component is the eigenvector $\mathbf{w}_i$ of the sample covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ associated with the $i$-th largest eigenvalue $\lambda_i$.

**Data centering** is necessary because PCA's objective is to find the directions of *maximum variance relative to the origin*. If the data is not centered, the first principal component will not point in the direction of greatest spread but will instead be biased towards the centroid. Centering transforms the data so that the mean is at the origin, ensuring that variance maximization captures the true spread of the data. Without centering, the dominant component may simply point in the direction of the mean vector rather than the most informative direction.

*(Marks: 1.5 for definition, 1 for need-to-center reasoning, 0.5 for mathematical statement.)*

---

### Question A2 — 3 Marks
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Differentiate between PCA and LDA. Mention any two limitations of PCA.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | PCA | LDA |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Type | Unsupervised | Supervised |
| Objective | Maximize variance | Maximize class separability |
| Uses labels? | No | Yes |
| Max # components | $\min(n, p)$ | $c - 1$ where $c$ is the number of classes |

**Two limitations of PCA:**
1. **Linearity**: PCA only captures linear relationships. Non-linear manifold structures (e.g., Swiss-roll data) cannot be efficiently unfolded by PCA; one must use kernel-PCA or t-SNE.
2. **Sensitivity to outliers**: Since the covariance matrix is computed via squared deviations, a single extreme outlier can dominate the principal components. Robust variants like Robust PCA are needed in such cases.

*(Marks: 1 for difference, 1 each for two limitations, with concise phrasing.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout** — *Where students typically lose marks:*
> - ❌ Forgetting to **center the data** before computing the covariance matrix (loses ~2 marks).
> - ❌ Confusing the **eigenvalues** with the **variance** they represent (always state that the *eigenvalue equals the variance* along the eigenvector direction).
> - ❌ Writing the **eigen-equation** without justifying that it arises from the **Lagrangian / KKT conditions** (loses ~2 marks on derivation questions).
> - ❌ Failing to **normalize** the eigenvectors before forming the projection matrix $\mathbf{W}$ (loses ~1 mark).
> - ❌ For the reconstruction question, forgetting to add the **mean vector** back to $\mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{\top}$ to obtain the full reconstruction.
> - ❌ Mixing up $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ (the covariance of *features*) with $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$ (which differs by a factor of $1/(n-1)$).

### Question B-A — 14 Marks  *(Choice 1)*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the first principal component of PCA using the variance-maximization approach with Lagrange multipliers. Show all intermediate steps.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the data matrix $\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ (assume already centered). Perform PCA and find the principal components, the variance explained by each, and the projection of the data onto the first principal component.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Statement of the optimization problem.** We seek a unit vector $\mathbf{w}_1$ that maximizes the variance of the projected data:

$$
\max_{\mathbf{w}_1}\; \mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_1 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\mathbf{w}_1 = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Lagrangian formulation.** Introduce the multiplier $\alpha_1$:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}_1, \alpha_1) = \mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_1 - \alpha_1(\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\mathbf{w}_1 - 1)
$$

**Step 3 — First-order KKT conditions.** Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}_1$ and set the gradient to zero. Because $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is symmetric, $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}_1}(\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_1) = 2\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_1$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}_1} = 2\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_1 - 2\alpha_1\mathbf{w}_1 = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify to the eigen-equation.**

$$
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_1 = \alpha_1\mathbf{w}_1
$$

Hence $\mathbf{w}_1$ is an eigenvector of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ and $\alpha_1$ is the corresponding eigenvalue.

**Step 5 — Identify the maximum.** Left-multiply by $\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}$:

$$
\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w}_1 = \alpha_1 \cdot \mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\mathbf{w}_1 = \alpha_1
$$

To maximize the variance, we pick the **largest** eigenvalue $\lambda_1$ and its eigenvector $\mathbf{w}_1$.

*(Marks breakdown: 1 for problem statement, 1 for Lagrangian, 2 for KKT, 1 for eigen-equation, 1 for variance identification, 1 for the final selection rule.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Covariance matrix.** With $\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and $n = 3$:

$$
\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 & 1.0 \\ 1.0 & 2.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Eigenvalues.** Solve $\det(\boldsymbol{\Sigma} - \lambda\mathbf{I}) = 0$:

$$
(2.5 - \lambda)^{2} - 1 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \lambda^{2} - 5\lambda + 5.25 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{5 \pm \sqrt{25 - 21}}{2} = \frac{5 \pm 2}{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \lambda_1 = 3.5,\; \lambda_2 = 1.5
$$

**Step 3 — Eigenvectors.** For $\lambda_1 = 3.5$: $(2.5 - 3.5)w_{1,1} + w_{1,2} = 0 \Rightarrow -w_{1,1} + w_{1,2} = 0$, so $\mathbf{w}_1 \propto (1, 1)^{\top}$. Normalize:

$$
\mathbf{w}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Similarly, $\mathbf{w}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(-1, 1)^{\top}$.

**Step 4 — Variance ratio.**

$$
\eta_1 = \frac{3.5}{3.5 + 1.5} = 0.875 = 87.5\%, \qquad \eta_2 = 0.125 = 12.5\%
$$

**Step 5 — Projection of data onto PC1.** With $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}_1$:

$$
\mathbf{Y} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{2} \\ \sqrt{2} \\ \sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

*(Marks breakdown: 1 for covariance, 2 for eigen-equation solution, 1 for eigenvectors, 1 for variance ratio, 2 for projection.)*

---

### Question B-B — 14 Marks  *(Choice 2 — Alternative)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Supplementary Exam 2024]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the reconstruction-error minimization view of PCA. Prove that the optimal low-dimensional subspace is spanned by the top-$k$ eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, and show that the minimum reconstruction error equals the sum of the discarded eigenvalues.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Eigenface** algorithm for face recognition. Explain each stage in detail, including how PCA is applied, the role of the mean face, and how a new test image is classified.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Set up the objective.** Given centered data $\{\mathbf{x}_j\}_{j=1}^{n}$ in $\mathbb{R}^p$ and an orthonormal basis $\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{w}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{w}_k]$ for a $k$-dimensional subspace $\mathcal{S}$, the orthogonal projection of $\mathbf{x}_j$ onto $\mathcal{S}$ is $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_j = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j$. The mean-squared reconstruction error is

$$
J_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\Vert\,\mathbf{x}_j - \mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j\,\Vert_2^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Algebraic expansion.** Using $\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{I}_k$ and the idempotence of the projector $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{\top}$:

$$
J_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[\mathbf{x}_j^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j - \mathbf{x}_j^{\top}\mathbf{W}\mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Observe that the first term is constant** ($\frac{1}{n}\sum \mathbf{x}_j^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j = \mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma})$). Minimizing $J_k$ is equivalent to maximizing

$$
\max_{\mathbf{W}} \;\mathrm{tr}\!\left(\mathbf{W}^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{W}\right) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \mathbf{W}^{\top}\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{I}_k
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Eckart–Young–Mirsky Theorem (or Rayleigh–Ritz analysis).** The matrix $\mathbf{W}^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{W}$ attains its maximum trace when $\mathbf{W}$ consists of the top-$k$ eigenvectors of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$. Let $\mathbf{W}^\star = [\mathbf{w}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{w}_k]$ where $\mathbf{w}_i$ corresponds to $\lambda_i$, $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_p$. Then

$$
\mathrm{tr}\!\left(\mathbf{W}^{\star\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{W}^{\star}\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_i
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the minimum error.**

$$
J_k^{\star} = \mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) - \sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_i = \sum_{i=k+1}^{p}\lambda_i
$$

Hence the **minimum reconstruction error equals the sum of the discarded eigenvalues**, which is the canonical result.

*(Marks: 1 for objective, 2 for algebraic manipulation, 1 for reduction to trace maximization, 1 for invoking the eigen-solution, 2 for the final closed-form $J_k^{\star}$.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

The **Eigenface** algorithm, introduced by Turk & Pentland (1991), is one of the earliest successful applications of PCA to face recognition. Its pipeline consists of the following stages:

1. **Data Acquisition**: Collect a training set of $n$ face images, each resized to a common resolution (e.g., $100 \times 100$ pixels). Convert each image to a column vector $\mathbf{x}_j \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ where $p = 10{,}000$. Form the data matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$.

2. **Mean Face Computation**: Compute the average face $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\mathbf{x}_j$. Subtract it from every image to obtain centered faces $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_j = \mathbf{x}_j - \boldsymbol{\mu}$. The mean face itself is visually meaningful — it represents the "average" human face.

3. **Covariance / Scatter Matrix**: Because $p \gg n$ in face data, we compute the $n \times n$ matrix $\mathbf{L} = \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}$ instead of the $p \times p$ covariance. The top-$n$ eigenvectors of the $p \times p$ covariance are recovered from the eigenvectors $\mathbf{v}_i$ of $\mathbf{L}$ via $\mathbf{w}_i = \tilde{\mathbf{X}}\mathbf{v}_i / \Vert \tilde{\mathbf{X}}\mathbf{v}_i \Vert$ (this is the *dual PCA* trick).

4. **Eigen-Decomposition**: The resulting eigenvectors $\mathbf{w}_i$ are reshaped back to $100 \times 100$ images and are called **eigenfaces**. Each eigenface captures a particular mode of facial variation — lighting, head pose, expression, etc.

5. **Encoding a New Face**: Given a test image $\mathbf{x}_{\text{test}}$, center it ($\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{\text{test}} = \mathbf{x}_{\text{test}} - \boldsymbol{\mu}$) and project onto the top-$k$ eigenfaces: $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W}_k^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{\text{test}} \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$.

6. **Classification**: Compare the encoded vector $\mathbf{z}$ with the encoded vectors of all training faces (e.g., via **nearest-neighbour** in Euclidean distance). The face is classified into the class of the closest training face. Reconstruction error (how well $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{\text{test}}$ can be reconstructed from $\mathbf{z}$) is also used as a confidence score or for detecting *unknown* faces.

7. **Practical Considerations**: Typically $k \approx 50$ eigenfaces suffice for a dataset of several hundred faces. Reconstruction error is also a powerful **anomaly / unknown-face detector** — a high error indicates the face is not in the training set.

*(Marks: 1 for pipeline overview, 1 for mean face, 1 for dual PCA trick, 1 for eigenface concept, 1 for encoding, 1 for classification, 1 for practical considerations.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist** — Read this list 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.

- **Definition (must be verbatim)**: PCA is an orthogonal linear transformation that projects data onto a lower-dimensional space such that the variance of the projected data is maximized along successive axes.
- **Centering is mandatory**; standardization is needed when feature scales differ.
- **Eigenvalues = variance carried by each PC**; **eigenvectors = directions of PCs**.
- **Top-$k$ eigenvectors** of the sample covariance matrix form the projection matrix $\mathbf{W}_k$.
- **Reconstruction error = sum of discarded eigenvalues** $\sum_{i=k+1}^{p}\lambda_i$.
- **Variance ratio** $\eta_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_i \,/\, \sum_{i=1}^{p}\lambda_i$; threshold is usually **0.95**.
- **Two derivations to memorize**: MaxVar (Lagrange multiplier) and MinErr (Eckart–Young).
- **PCs are orthogonal by construction** (since the covariance matrix is symmetric).
- **Dual PCA trick** for high-$p$, small-$n$ data: compute eigenvectors of $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$ instead of $\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^{\top}$ and recover via $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{v}/\Vert\mathbf{X}\mathbf{v}\Vert$.
- **Whitening** transform decorrelates features and sets them to unit variance — a precursor to Batch Normalization.
- **Limitations to mention in any PCA question**: linearity, scale sensitivity, outlier sensitivity, uninterpretable axes, no use of labels.
- **PCA vs LDA**: PCA is unsupervised, variance-based; LDA is supervised, class-separability-based; max # LDA components = $c-1$.
- **Eigenface = real-world PCA application** in face recognition; pipeline is a high-yield 14-mark topic.
- **Numerical steps** the examiner expects in any hand-computation question: (i) center, (ii) compute $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$, (iii) solve characteristic polynomial, (iv) find eigenvectors, (v) normalize, (vi) project.
- **Sanity checks**: total variance of $\mathbf{Y}$ should equal the sum of the retained eigenvalues, and the trace of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ should be preserved before and after the projection.
- **SVD link**: For numerical stability, modern implementations compute PCA via the SVD of $\mathbf{X}$ rather than the eigen-decomposition of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$.
- **Connection to deep learning**: PCA is the linear special case of an **autoencoder** with a single linear hidden layer; the encoder weights are the eigenvectors, the decoder weights are their transposes.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always start a 14-mark PCA answer with the **objective function** (MaxVar or MinErr), derive the **eigen-equation** cleanly, then move to the **numerical computation**. This 3-stage structure mirrors the KTU model answer key and guarantees full marks on the methodology portion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conditional Probability - Chain Rule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT745 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conditional Probability - Chain Rule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conditional Probability** is a measure of the probability of an event occurring, *given that* another event has already occurred. Formally, for two events $A$ and $B$ defined on the same sample space $S$, with $P(B) > 0$, the conditional probability of $A$ given $B$ is:

$$P(A \mid B) \;=\; \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

The **Chain Rule** (also called the **Product Rule** or **General Multiplication Rule**) is the direct algebraic consequence of conditional probability. It expresses the joint probability of a sequence of events as a product of conditional probabilities:

$$P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \dots \cap A_n) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} P\!\left(A_i \,\middle\vert\, \bigcap_{j=1}^{i-1} A_j\right)$$

In the context of **Deep Learning**, conditional probability and the chain rule form the mathematical backbone of:
- **Bayesian inference** in probabilistic graphical models
- **Naive Bayes classifiers** for text classification
- **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**
- **Backpropagation** (the *backprop chain rule* is named after this exact identity)
- **Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)** and **Bayesian Neural Networks**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Weather and the Picnic**
>
> Imagine you planned a picnic. The unconditional probability of rain today might be 30%. But now you glance outside and see **dark clouds already gathering**. Your *updated* belief about rain just jumped — say to 70%.
>
> That *updated* probability is **conditional probability**. You are not asking "what is the chance of rain in general?" You are asking "what is the chance of rain **given** that I see dark clouds?"
>
> Now extend the analogy. Suppose you want to compute the probability of the **entire sequence of events** for the day: you wake up → check clouds → it rains → picnic gets cancelled → you stay home. You cannot compute this from unconditional probabilities alone, because each event *depends on* what happened before. The **chain rule** lets you multiply these dependent probabilities together: $P(\text{full day}) = P(\text{wake}) \cdot P(\text{clouds} \mid \text{wake}) \cdot P(\text{rain} \mid \text{clouds}) \cdots$

### 1.3 Why This Matters in Deep Learning

| Domain | Use of Conditional Probability / Chain Rule |
|---|---|
| **Naive Bayes Classifier** | $P(\text{class} \mid \text{features}) \propto P(\text{features} \mid \text{class}) \cdot P(\text{class})$ |
| **Hidden Markov Models** | Joint probability of observation-emission sequences |
| **Variational Inference** | ELBO decomposition via conditional factorisations |
| **Backpropagation** | Gradient = chain of partial derivatives (named by analogy!) |
| **Bayesian Neural Networks** | Posterior over weights: $P(W \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto P(\mathcal{D} \mid W) P(W)$ |

---

## 1.4 Standard Notation Conventions

- $P(A)$ → Unconditional (marginal) probability of event $A$
- $P(A \mid B)$ → Conditional probability of $A$ **given** $B$
- $P(A \cap B)$ or $P(A, B)$ → Joint probability of both $A$ and $B$
- $\Omega$ or $S$ → Sample space (all possible outcomes)
- $\emptyset$ → Impossible event (null set)
- $A^{c}$ or $\bar{A}$ → Complement of $A$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Axioms (Kolmogorov's Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All conditional probability operations rest on **three Kolmogorov axioms** (1933):
> 1. **Non-negativity:** $P(A) \geq 0$ for every event $A$
> 2. **Normalization:** $P(S) = 1$
> 3. **Countable additivity:** For mutually exclusive events $A_1, A_2, \dots$, $P\!\left(\bigcup_{i} A_i\right) = \sum_{i} P(A_i)$

Conditional probability is **not** an additional axiom — it is *derived* from these three by definition.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Deduction of Conditional Probability

**Step 1 — Start with the joint event $A \cap B$:**  
When we know $B$ has occurred, the relevant *reduced* sample space is no longer $S$ — it is $B$ itself. We now want to find what fraction of $B$ is also occupied by $A$.

**Step 2 — Renormalise by dividing by $P(B)$:**  
Because $P(B)$ is now our new "total probability" (the universe of certainties), we divide the joint probability by it to obtain a valid probability measure on the reduced space.

**Step 3 — Resulting formula:**  
$P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$, valid only when $P(B) > 0$.

**Step 4 — The symmetric rearrangement** (multiplying both sides by $P(B)$) gives the **multiplication rule**:

$$P(A \cap B) \;=\; P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) \;=\; P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

**Step 5 — Extension to independence:**  
If $A$ and $B$ are *independent*, knowing $B$ gives **no information** about $A$, so $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$, which collapses the multiplication rule to $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$.

---

## 2.3 Deriving the Chain Rule (Two-Event Case)

Starting from $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$, rearrange:

$$P(A \cap B) \;=\; P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$$

This is the **two-event chain rule**. By symmetry, we could equally write:

$$P(A \cap B) \;=\; P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

The **direction** chosen depends on what conditional information is *available* in the problem. In Bayesian inference, the direction $P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$ is preferred because we typically know the *likelihood* $P(B \mid A)$ and the *prior* $P(A)$, but want the *posterior* $P(A \mid B)$.

---

## 2.4 Extending to $n$ Events

For three events $A$, $B$, $C$:

$$P(A \cap B \cap C) \;=\; P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) \cdot P(C \mid A \cap B)$$

For $n$ events:

$$P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \dots \cap A_n) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} P\!\left(A_i \,\middle\vert\, A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \dots \cap A_{i-1}\right)$$

The right-hand side always has $n$ factors. The conditional on each new event conditions on **all preceding events**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is **high-yield for KTU University Exams**. Master every row.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conditional Probability | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$, $\;P(B) > 0$ | Probability of $A$ when $B$ is known to have occurred |
| 2 | Multiplication (Two Events) | $P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$ | Joint from conditional |
| 3 | Symmetric Multiplication | $P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$ | When $P(B \mid A)$ is easier to compute |
| 4 | Chain Rule (n events) | $P(\bigcap_{i=1}^{n} A_i) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i \mid \bigcap_{j=1}^{i-1} A_j)$ | Joint probability of dependent sequential events |
| 5 | Independence Test | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | Verifying independence |
| 6 | Conditional Independence | $P(A \cap B \mid C) = P(A \mid C) \cdot P(B \mid C)$ | Naive Bayes assumption |
| 7 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$ | Reversing the conditioning |
| 8 | Total Probability | $P(B) = \sum_{i} P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)$, $\{A_i\}$ partition | Computing marginal from conditionals |
| 9 | Bayes + Total Probability | $P(A_k \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A_k) P(A_k)}{\sum_{i} P(B \mid A_i) P(A_i)}$ | Posterior over multiple hypotheses |
| 10 | Complement Rule | $P(A^{c}) = 1 - P(A)$ | "Not $A$" probability |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute-value vs conditional-probability notation pitfall:** In markdown tables, never write $\vert A \vert$ for absolute value or $P(A \mid B)$ using the vertical bar $\mid$ — write `\vert` or `\mid` inside LaTeX to avoid breaking table syntax.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Medical Diagnosis AI** | $P(\text{disease} \mid \text{positive test})$ via Bayes' theorem |
| **Spam Filtering** | Naive Bayes on token features |
| **Speech Recognition** | Hidden Markov Models factorise joint via chain rule |
| **Autonomous Driving** | Sensor fusion using conditional likelihoods |
| **Anomaly Detection** | Threshold on $P(\text{observation} \mid \text{normal})$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conditional Probability from First Principles

**Statement to prove:** $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$

**Starting point:** Kolmogorov's three axioms (non-negativity, normalisation, countable additivity).

**Step-by-step proof:**

Let $\Omega$ be the sample space. Suppose event $B \subseteq \Omega$ with $P(B) > 0$. Define a new probability measure $Q$ on the restricted sample space $B$ by:

$$Q(A) \;=\; \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \quad \text{for every event } A \subseteq \Omega$$

**Verification that $Q$ satisfies Kolmogorov's axioms:**

*Non-negativity:* Since $P(A \cap B) \geq 0$ and $P(B) > 0$, we have $Q(A) \geq 0$.

*Normalisation:* $Q(\Omega) = \dfrac{P(\Omega \cap B)}{P(B)} = \dfrac{P(B)}{P(B)} = 1$.

*Countable additivity:* If $A_1, A_2, \dots$ are disjoint, then $(A_1 \cap B), (A_2 \cap B), \dots$ are also disjoint, so:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) &= \frac{P\!\left(\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) \cap B\right)}{P(B)} \\
&= \frac{P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} (A_i \cap B)\right)}{P(B)} \\
&= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i \cap B)}{P(B)} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \frac{P(A_i \cap B)}{P(B)} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} Q(A_i)
\end{aligned}$$

Since $Q$ is a valid probability measure on $B$, and $Q(A)$ is *the* natural definition of "probability of $A$ given $B$", the formula is established. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Chain Rule for Three Events

**Statement:** $P(A \cap B \cap C) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) \cdot P(C \mid A \cap B)$

**Proof by iterated application of the two-event multiplication rule:**

**Step 1 — Group the first two events:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(A \cap B \cap C) &= P\big((A \cap B) \cap C\big)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the two-event multiplication rule with $A \cap B$ playing the role of $A$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P\big((A \cap B) \cap C\big) &= P(C \mid A \cap B) \cdot P(A \cap B)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Now expand $P(A \cap B)$ using the two-event multiplication rule:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(A \cap B) &= P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(A \cap B \cap C) &= P(C \mid A \cap B) \cdot \big[P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)\big] \\
&= P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A) \cdot P(C \mid A \cap B) \qquad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bayes' Theorem from Conditional Probability

**Statement:** $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$, valid when $P(B) > 0$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Write the multiplication rule in two equivalent forms:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(A \cap B) &= P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) \\
P(A \cap B) &= P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Since both equal $P(A \cap B)$, set them equal:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) &= P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by $P(B)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(A \mid B) &= \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)} \qquad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Generalisation via Total Probability (Law of Total Probability):**

If $\{A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n\}$ is a partition of $\Omega$, then:

$$\begin{aligned}
P(B) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \cap A_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$P(A_k \mid B) \;=\; \frac{P(B \mid A_k) \cdot P(A_k)}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)}$$

This is the **complete Bayesian inference formula** and is the workhorse of probabilistic classifiers in deep learning.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Classic Dice Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** Two fair dice are rolled. Given that the **sum is 6**, what is the probability that **at least one die shows a 2**?

**Step 1 — Define events clearly:**

- $A$ = "at least one die shows a 2"
- $B$ = "the sum of the two dice is 6"

**Step 2 — Enumerate the sample space $\Omega$ for two dice:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lvert \Omega \rvert = 6 \times 6 = 36
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Count $P(B)$ — outcomes where sum is 6:**

The pairs $(d_1, d_2)$ with $d_1 + d_2 = 6$ are: $(1,5), (2,4), (3,3), (4,2), (5,1)$. So $\lvert B \rvert = 5$.

$$P(B) = \frac{5}{36}$$

**Step 4 — Count $P(A \cap B)$ — outcomes where sum is 6 **and** at least one die is 2:**

From the list above, the pairs containing a 2 are: $(2,4)$ and $(4,2)$. So $\lvert A \cap B \rvert = 2$.

$$P(A \cap B) = \frac{2}{36} = \frac{1}{18}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the conditional probability formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(A \mid B) &= \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} = \frac{2/36}{5/36} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.40
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** $P(A \mid B) = 0.40$ or **40%**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Medical Diagnosis via Bayes' Theorem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A disease affects 1% of a population. A diagnostic test has:
> - **Sensitivity** (true positive rate): $P(T^+ \mid D) = 0.95$
> - **Specificity** (true negative rate): $P(T^- \mid D^c) = 0.90$
>
> A randomly selected person tests **positive**. What is the probability they actually have the disease?

**Step 1 — Translate given information:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(D) &= 0.01 \quad &\text{(prior prevalence)} \\
P(D^c) &= 0.99 \\
P(T^+ \mid D) &= 0.95 \quad &\text{(sensitivity)} \\
P(T^- \mid D^c) &= 0.90 \quad &\text{(specificity)} \\
P(T^+ \mid D^c) &= 1 - 0.90 = 0.10 \quad &\text{(false positive rate)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — We need the posterior $P(D \mid T^+)$. Apply Bayes' theorem:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(D \mid T^+) &= \frac{P(T^+ \mid D) \cdot P(D)}{P(T^+)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $P(T^+)$ using the law of total probability:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(T^+) &= P(T^+ \mid D) \cdot P(D) + P(T^+ \mid D^c) \cdot P(D^c) \\
&= (0.95)(0.01) + (0.10)(0.99) \\
&= 0.0095 + 0.0990 \\
&= 0.1085
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Plug back into Bayes' theorem:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(D \mid T^+) &= \frac{0.0095}{0.1085} \approx 0.0876
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** $P(D \mid T^+) \approx 0.0876$, i.e., only about **8.76%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Counterintuitive Lesson:** Even with a 95% accurate test, a positive result only gives ~9% chance of having the disease — because the disease is so rare (1% prior) that false positives swamp true positives. This is the **base-rate fallacy**, and it is critical for AI fairness and medical ML.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Chain Rule on Card Draws (Without Replacement)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Three cards are drawn **without replacement** from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability that all three are **Aces**?

**Step 1 — Define events:**

- $A_1$ = "1st card is an Ace"
- $A_2$ = "2nd card is an Ace"
- $A_3$ = "3rd card is an Ace"

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule directly:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap A_3) &= P(A_1) \cdot P(A_2 \mid A_1) \cdot P(A_3 \mid A_1 \cap A_2)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute each factor:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(A_1) &= \frac{4}{52} = \frac{1}{13} \\
P(A_2 \mid A_1) &= \frac{3}{51} = \frac{1}{17} \\
P(A_3 \mid A_1 \cap A_2) &= \frac{2}{50} = \frac{1}{25}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap A_3) &= \frac{4}{52} \cdot \frac{3}{51} \cdot \frac{2}{50} \\
&= \frac{24}{132600} \\
&= \frac{1}{5525} \\
&\approx 0.000181
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** Probability $\approx 0.000181$ or **1 in 5525**.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example 4 — Naive Bayes Text Classification (Deep Learning Connection)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A spam classifier uses the **Naive Bayes** assumption. Suppose:
> - $P(\text{spam}) = 0.30$, $P(\text{ham}) = 0.70$
> - The word "free" appears in 70% of spam emails and 10% of ham emails.
> - The word "offer" appears in 50% of spam and 5% of ham.
>
> An incoming email contains **both** "free" and "offer". Classify it using Naive Bayes.

**Step 1 — Apply Bayes' theorem for each class:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{spam} \mid \text{free}, \text{offer}) &= \frac{P(\text{free}, \text{offer} \mid \text{spam}) \cdot P(\text{spam})}{P(\text{free}, \text{offer})}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the **Naive (Conditional Independence) Assumption**:**

$$P(\text{free}, \text{offer} \mid \text{spam}) = P(\text{free} \mid \text{spam}) \cdot P(\text{offer} \mid \text{spam})$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerators:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Spam score:} \quad & P(\text{free} \mid \text{spam}) \cdot P(\text{offer} \mid \text{spam}) \cdot P(\text{spam}) \\
&= (0.70)(0.50)(0.30) = 0.1050 \\
\text{Ham score:} \quad & P(\text{free} \mid \text{ham}) \cdot P(\text{offer} \mid \text{ham}) \cdot P(\text{ham}) \\
&= (0.10)(0.05)(0.70) = 0.0035
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Normalise to get posterior probabilities:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{spam} \mid \text{free}, \text{offer}) &= \frac{0.1050}{0.1050 + 0.0035} = \frac{0.1050}{0.1085} \approx 0.9677 \\
P(\text{ham} \mid \text{free}, \text{offer}) &= \frac{0.0035}{0.1085} \approx 0.0323
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** The email is classified as **spam** with probability $\approx 96.77\%$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Probability Tree (Conditional Probability Visualisation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["ROOT START"] --> b1["EVENT B OCCURS"]
    rootA --> b2["EVENT B NOT OCCURS"]
    b1 --> leafA1["A ALSO OCCURS P OF A GIVEN B"]
    b1 --> leafA2["A DOES NOT OCCUR P OF A NOT GIVEN B"]
    b2 --> leafA3["A OCCURS P OF A GIVEN B NOT"]
    b2 --> leafA4["A NOT OCCURS P OF A NOT GIVEN B NOT"]
    style rootA fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style b1 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style b2 fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style leafA1 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style leafA2 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style leafA3 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style leafA4 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**How to read this tree:**
- The **first level** branches on whether event $B$ occurs or not, with probabilities $P(B)$ and $P(B^c)$.
- The **second level** branches on whether event $A$ occurs *within each branch*, with **conditional probabilities** $P(A \mid B)$, $P(A^c \mid B)$, $P(A \mid B^c)$, $P(A^c \mid B^c)$.
- The probability of any **leaf** path is the **product** of the edge probabilities along that path — this *is* the **chain rule in action**.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Venn Diagram Showing the Geometry of Conditional Probability

```mermaid
graph LR
    sBox["SAMPLE SPACE S"] --> vB["CIRCLE B"]
    sBox --> vA["CIRCLE A"]
    vA --> overlap["OVERLAP A INTERSECTION B"]
    vB --> overlap
    sBox --> bOnly["REGION B ONLY"]
    sBox --> aOnly["REGION A ONLY"]
    sBox --> neither["REGION NEITHER A NOR B"]
    style sBox fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#000
    style vA fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style vB fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style overlap fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style bOnly fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#000
    style aOnly fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#000
    style neither fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000
```

**Reading:** Conditional probability $P(A \mid B)$ is the **ratio of the overlap $A \cap B$ to the entire circle $B$**. Intuitively: "given that we're inside $B$, what fraction is also inside $A$?"

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Chain Rule as a Sequential Factorisation (Bayesian Network Style)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeX1["X1"] --> nodeX2["X2 GIVEN X1"]
    nodeX2 --> nodeX3["X3 GIVEN X1 AND X2"]
    nodeX3 --> nodeX4["X4 GIVEN X1 X2 X3"]
    nodeX4 --> nodeEll["... XN GIVEN ALL PRECEDING"]
    style nodeX1 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeX2 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeX3 fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeX4 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeEll fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation:** Each node $X_i$ in the chain depends **only on its parents** (the preceding nodes). The joint probability of the entire chain is the **product of conditionals**, which is precisely the chain rule:

$$P(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_2 \mid X_1) \cdot P(X_3 \mid X_1, X_2) \cdots$$

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Block Architecture of Bayesian Inference (Engineered System View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    priBlock["PRIOR P OF A"] --> mul1["MULTIPLY WITH LIKELIHOOD P OF B GIVEN A"]
    likeBlock["LIKELIHOOD P OF B GIVEN A"] --> mul1
    mul1 --> numBlock["NUMERATOR P OF A AND B"]
    totBlock["TOTAL PROB P OF B VIA LAW OF TOTAL PROBABILITY"] --> denBlock["DENOMINATOR P OF B"]
    numBlock --> bayesBlock["BAYES THEOREM ENGINE"]
    denBlock --> bayesBlock
    bayesBlock --> postBlock["POSTERIOR P OF A GIVEN B"]
    style priBlock fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style likeBlock fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style mul1 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style numBlock fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style totBlock fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style denBlock fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style bayesBlock fill:#e377c2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style postBlock fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Reading:** The Bayesian inference engine is a *pipeline* — prior × likelihood in the numerator, marginalised evidence in the denominator, yielding the posterior. This is the **computational blueprint** of every Bayesian deep learning system.
