| Aspect | L1 Regularization | L2 Regularization |
|---|---|---|
| Penalty Term | $\lambda \sum \vert w_j \vert$ | $\lambda \sum w_j^2$ |
| Effect on Weights | Drives some weights to **exactly zero** | Shrinks weights toward zero but rarely zero |
| Resulting Model | **Sparse** (feature selection) | **Dense** (all features retained) |

L1 uses the $L_1$ norm (absolute values) and produces sparse solutions useful for feature selection. L2 uses the $L_2$ norm squared (squared values) and produces dense solutions useful for general overfitting prevention.

*(Allocation: 1 mark penalty terms + 1 mark effect on weights + 1 mark distinguishing characteristic)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of L1 and L2 regularization with suitable mathematical formulations. Derive the gradient descent update rules for both. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A neural network has a single weight $w = 2.0$, learning rate $\alpha = 0.05$, original loss gradient $\partial J/\partial w = 0.3$, and regularization coefficient $\lambda = 0.4$. Compute the updated weight after one iteration using both L1 and L2 regularization. Compare and comment on the results. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Regularization modifies the loss function to penalize large weights, preventing overfitting in deep networks. The regularized loss is:

$$J_{\text{reg}}(\mathbf{w}) = J(\mathbf{w}) + \lambda R(\mathbf{w})$$

**L1 Regularization (3 marks):**

The L1 penalty is the $L_1$ norm of the weight vector:

$$R_{L1}(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert w_j \vert$$

The full loss becomes:

$$J_{L1}(\mathbf{w}) = J(\mathbf{w}) + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert w_j \vert$$

The sub-gradient is:

$$\frac{\partial J_{L1}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(w_j)$$

Gradient descent update rule:

$$w_j := w_j - \alpha \left( \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(w_j) \right)$$

**L2 Regularization (3 marks):**

The L2 penalty is the squared $L_2$ norm:

$$R_{L2}(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j^2$$

The full loss becomes:

$$J_{L2}(\mathbf{w}) = J(\mathbf{w}) + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j^2$$

The gradient is:

$$\frac{\partial J_{L2}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} + 2\lambda w_j$$

Gradient descent update rule:

$$w_j := w_j(1 - 2\alpha\lambda) - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $w = 2.0$, $\alpha = 0.05$, $\partial J/\partial w = 0.3$, $\lambda = 0.4$

**L2 Regularization Update (3 marks):**

Using $w_{\text{new}} = w_{\text{old}}(1 - 2\alpha\lambda) - \alpha \cdot \partial J/\partial w$:

Step 1 — Compute decay factor:

$$1 - 2 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 0.4 = 1 - 0.04 = 0.96$$

Step 2 — Compute gradient term:

$$0.05 \cdot 0.3 = 0.015$$

Step 3 — Final computation:

$$w_{\text{new}} = 2.0 \cdot 0.96 - 0.015 = 1.92 - 0.015 = 1.905$$

**L1 Regularization Update (3 marks):**

Using $w_{\text{new}} = w_{\text{old}} - \alpha \cdot \partial J/\partial w - \alpha\lambda \cdot \text{sign}(w_{\text{old}})$:

Since $w = 2.0 > 0$, $\text{sign}(2.0) = +1$.

Step 1 — Compute gradient term:

$$0.05 \cdot 0.3 = 0.015$$

Step 2 — Compute regularization push:

$$0.05 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 1 = 0.02$$

Step 3 — Final computation:

$$w_{\text{new}} = 2.0 - 0.015 - 0.02 = 1.965$$

**Comparison and Comments (1 mark):**

Both methods reduce the weight, but:
- L2 reduced $w$ from $2.0$ to $1.905$ (change of $-0.095$)
- L1 reduced $w$ from $2.0$ to $1.965$ (change of $-0.035$)

For large weights, L2 applies a stronger penalty proportional to magnitude, while L1 applies a constant penalty regardless of magnitude.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024] — Alternative Choice

**Q: (a)** Discuss the role of regularization in deep learning. Explain with diagrams how L1 and L2 regularization produce different weight patterns (sparse vs dense). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the geometric interpretation of L1 and L2 regularization using the concept of constraint regions. Show how the shape of these regions leads to sparsity in L1 and shrinkage in L2. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Role of Regularization in Deep Learning (3 marks):**

Deep neural networks have millions of trainable parameters, making them prone to **overfitting** — a phenomenon where the model memorizes training data instead of learning generalizable features. Regularization addresses this by:

1. **Adding a penalty term** to the loss function that discourages large weights
2. **Constraining the hypothesis space** to simpler models
3. **Improving generalization** on unseen test data
4. **Stabilizing training** by preventing weight explosions

**L1 — Sparse Weight Patterns (2 marks):**

L1 regularization adds $\lambda \sum \vert w_j \vert$ to the loss. The gradient $\lambda \cdot \text{sign}(w_j)$ applies a **constant** push toward zero regardless of weight magnitude. Consequently, weights with small magnitudes get pushed to **exactly zero**, resulting in a **sparse weight vector** where only the most important features have non-zero weights. This is equivalent to **automatic feature selection**.

**L2 — Dense Weight Patterns (2 marks):**

L2 regularization adds $\lambda \sum w_j^2$ to the loss. The gradient $2\lambda w_j$ is **proportional** to the weight magnitude — small weights receive a small penalty, large weights receive a large penalty. Consequently, weights are **shrunk but rarely zeroed**, resulting in a **dense weight vector** where all features contribute small amounts.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Geometric Interpretation Concept (3 marks):**

Consider the optimization problem in 2D weight space $(w_1, w_2)$. The unregularized solution is at the minimum of the loss contour (an ellipse). Regularization constrains the weights to lie within a feasible region defined by the regularization term.

**L1 Constraint Region (2 marks):**

The L1 constraint $\vert w_1 \vert + \vert w_2 \vert \leq t$ forms a **diamond** (rhombus) in weight space. The diamond has **sharp corners** on the $w_1$ and $w_2$ axes. When the elliptical loss contours expand outward until they first touch the diamond, the contact point often lands on a corner where one coordinate is exactly zero. This produces **sparsity**.

**L2 Constraint Region (2 marks):**

The L2 constraint $w_1^2 + w_2^2 \leq t$ forms a **circle** in weight space. The circle has a **smooth boundary** with no sharp corners. When the loss contours touch the circle, the contact point is rarely exactly on an axis — both $w_1$ and $w_2$ are non-zero. This produces **shrinkage without sparsity**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing penalty formulas:** Students frequently write $\lambda \sum w_j$ instead of $\lambda \sum \vert w_j \vert$ (L1) or $\lambda \sum w_j^2$ (L2). Always include the **absolute value bars** for L1.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the sub-gradient for L1:** L1 regularization is **not differentiable at zero**. Writing $\partial \vert w_j \vert / \partial w_j = 1$ (without acknowledging the sub-gradient) loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Mixing up the update rules:** L2 has the decay factor $(1 - 2\alpha\lambda)$, but L1 does NOT. Confusing these two update rules is a frequent error.
>
> 4. **Omitting $\lambda$ in the final loss formula:** Examiners specifically look for the **regularization coefficient $\lambda$** as a separate term.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning sparsity:** When asked about L1, always explicitly state that L1 produces **sparse solutions** (feature selection). This is a guaranteed 1-mark keyword.
>
> 6. **Wrong justification for "why L2 = Weight Decay":** Explain that the multiplicative factor $(1 - 2\alpha\lambda) < 1$ **decays** the weight at every step — not just "regularization is sometimes called weight decay."

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for L1 and L2 Regularization**

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- [ ] Regularization prevents overfitting by penalizing large weights
- [ ] General formula: $J_{\text{reg}}(\mathbf{w}) = J(\mathbf{w}) + \lambda R(\mathbf{w})$
- [ ] $\lambda \geq 0$ controls the strength of regularization
- [ ] Bias term $b$ is **not** regularized
- [ ] $\lambda = 0$ means no regularization; large $\lambda$ means strong regularization

### L1 Regularization Key Points
- [ ] Penalty: $\lambda \sum \vert w_j \vert$
- [ ] Uses $L_1$ norm: $\vert\vert \mathbf{w} \vert\vert_1$
- [ ] Gradient: $\partial J/\partial w_j + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(w_j)$
- [ ] Produces **sparse models** (some weights = exactly 0)
- [ ] Useful for **feature selection**
- [ ] Non-differentiable at zero (sub-gradient)
- [ ] Robust to outliers
- [ ] Multiple optimal solutions may exist

### L2 Regularization Key Points
- [ ] Penalty: $\lambda \sum w_j^2$
- [ ] Uses squared $L_2$ norm: $\vert\vert \mathbf{w} \vert\vert_2^2$
- [ ] Gradient: $\partial J/\partial w_j + 2\lambda w_j$
- [ ] Also called **Weight Decay** or **Ridge Regression**
- [ ] Decay factor in update: $(1 - 2\alpha\lambda)$
- [ ] Produces **dense models** (all weights small but non-zero)
- [ ] Smooth and differentiable everywhere
- [ ] **Unique** optimal solution

### Geometric Intuition
- [ ] L1 constraint region = **Diamond** (sharp corners → sparsity)
- [ ] L2 constraint region = **Circle** (smooth boundary → shrinkage)

### KTU Exam Vocabulary Keywords
- **Overfitting, Generalization, Bias-Variance Tradeoff, Penalty Term, Hyperparameter, Sparsity, Weight Decay, Lasso, Ridge, Tikhonov Regularization, Sub-gradient, Constraint Region**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deep Feedforward Networks: Gradient-Based Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT745 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deep Feedforward Networks: Gradient-Based Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Deep Feedforward Network** (also called a **Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)** or **Fully-Connected Deep Network**) is a parameterized mathematical mapping $f^*(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{y}) = f(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta})$ that approximates a true underlying function $y^*(\mathbf{x})$ by composing multiple simple, differentiable functions. Information flows unidirectionally—from the input layer $\mathbf{x}$, through one or more **hidden layers**, terminating at an **output layer** $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$—with **no feedback connections**.

The **Gradient-Based Learning** paradigm refers to the iterative optimization of network parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \{\mathbf{W}^{(l)}, \mathbf{b}^{(l)}\}_{l=1}^{L}$ by minimizing a chosen **cost function** $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ using the **gradient descent** family of algorithms, where updates are driven by $\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ computed efficiently via the **backpropagation algorithm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO1** (Understand the fundamentals of deep feedforward networks and gradient-based optimization). Examiners will frequently test both the architectural intuition and the mathematical mechanics of backpropagation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef learning to bake the perfect cake** by trial and error:

1. **Forward Pass (Baking a Trial Cake):** You take inputs (flour, sugar, eggs → quantities $\mathbf{x}$), push them through a chain of mixing, folding, and baking steps (hidden layers $h^{(1)}, h^{(2)}, \dots, h^{(L)}$), and produce a cake (output $\hat{y}$).
2. **Cost Function (The Judge):** A food critic compares your cake $\hat{y}$ to the ideal recipe $y$ and gives a **loss score** $J$.
3. **Backward Pass (Critique Analysis):** The critic traces back exactly which step made the cake too sweet or too dry. They compute how much each ingredient quantity (weight $\mathbf{W}$) and each oven temperature (bias $\mathbf{b}$) contributed to the error—this is the **gradient** $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}}$.
4. **Parameter Update (Adjusting the Recipe):** You tweak each ingredient *slightly* in the direction that reduces the loss (gradient descent step). Repeat thousands of times until your cake matches the target.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A "deep" network simply means many hidden layers, each learning progressively more abstract representations of the input (edges → textures → parts → objects in image data).

---

## 1.3 Why Deep Feedforward Networks Matter in KTU Examinations

| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Historical Foundation** | The cornerstone of modern deep learning; forms the basis for CNNs, RNNs, Transformers |
| **Universal Approximation** | A feedforward network with a single hidden layer and nonlinear activation can approximate **any Borel-measurable function** on compact subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| **Exam Weightage** | Highest weightage topic in Module 1 (typically 35–40% of module marks) |
| **Optimization Engine** | Gradient-based learning is *the* training paradigm used in virtually all neural networks |

> [!TIP]
> Examiners love testing the distinction between **shallow** (1 hidden layer) and **deep** (multiple hidden layers) networks, and the role of **nonlinear activations** in breaking linear equivalence.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition — Step-by-Step

### Step 1: Define the Network Topology
A feedforward network of depth $L$ consists of:
- **Input layer** $\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_0}$
- **Hidden layers** $l = 1, 2, \dots, L-1$, each producing activations $\mathbf{a}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l}$
- **Output layer** $\mathbf{a}^{(L)} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_L}$

### Step 2: Pre-Activation (Linear Transformation)
At each layer $l$, compute the linear pre-activation:

$$\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}$$

where $\mathbf{W}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l \times n_{l-1}}$ is the **weight matrix** and $\mathbf{b}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l}$ is the **bias vector**.

### Step 3: Activation (Nonlinearity)
Apply an element-wise activation function $g^{(l)}(\cdot)$:

$$\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = g^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why nonlinearity matters:** Without $g$ being nonlinear, stacking layers collapses to a single linear transformation (since composition of linear maps is linear). Nonlinearity is what enables universal approximation.

### Step 4: Loss Computation
Compare the prediction $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{a}^{(L)}$ to the true label $\mathbf{y}$ via a loss function $\mathcal{L}(\hat{\mathbf{y}}, \mathbf{y})$; aggregate over the dataset:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}(\hat{\mathbf{y}}^{(i)}, \mathbf{y}^{(i)}) + \lambda \Omega(\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

where $\lambda \Omega(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ is the **regularization term** (often $\ell_2$-norm: $\Omega = \tfrac{1}{2} \|\mathbf{W}\|_F^2$).

### Step 5: Gradient Descent Update (Why and How)
The **"why"**: $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ measures the prediction error; we seek $\boldsymbol{\theta}^* = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$. The **"how"**: take small steps in the direction of steepest descent, $-\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J$:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t - \eta \, \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (a critical hyperparameter).

---

## 2.2 Common Cost Functions and Output Activations

| Configuration | Output Activation $g^{(L)}$ | Cost Function $\mathcal{L}$ | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gaussian / Linear Regression** | Identity (linear) | $\frac{1}{2}(\hat{y}-y)^2$ (Mean Squared Error) | Continuous real-valued targets |
| **Bernoulli / Binary Classification** | Sigmoid $\sigma(z) = \tfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Cross-Entropy $-[y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | Two-class problems |
| **Multinomial / Multi-class** | Softmax $\text{softmax}(z_i) = \tfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Categorical Cross-Entropy $-\sum_c y_c \log \hat{y}_c$ | $C$-class classification |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Combining a **sigmoid output + MSE** instead of **sigmoid + cross-entropy** leads to a vanishing gradient problem at the output layer because $\sigma'$ becomes extremely small for saturated neurons. This is a classic KTU 2-mark trap.

---

## 2.3 Common Hidden-Layer Activation Functions

| Activation | Formula $g(z)$ | Derivative $g'(z)$ | Properties |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sigmoid (Logistic)** | $\dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $g(z)\cdot(1-g(z))$ | Saturates; vanishing gradient |
| **Tanh** | $\dfrac{e^z - e^{-z}}{e^z + e^{-z}}$ | $1 - g(z)^2$ | Zero-centered; saturates |
| **ReLU** | $\max(0, z)$ | $0$ if $z<0$ else $1$ | Non-saturating; sparse; dying ReLU risk |
| **Leaky ReLU** | $\max(\alpha z, z), \alpha \approx 0.01$ | $\alpha$ if $z<0$ else $1$ | Mitigates dying ReLU |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following high-yield equations are the minimum required to score full marks in any Module 1 question on gradient-based learning.

| # | Concept | Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward propagation | $\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = g^{(l)}(\mathbf{W}^{(l)}\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)})$ | Holds for $l=1,\dots,L$ |
| 2 | Cross-entropy gradient (softmax) | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial z_i^{(L)}} = \hat{y}_i - y_i$ | Elegant linear error signal |
| 3 | MSE gradient (linear output) | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial z^{(L)}} = \hat{y} - y$ | Same structure as above |
| 4 | Backprop: layer error | $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} = ((\mathbf{W}^{(l+1)})^\top \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l+1)}) \odot g'^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$ | $\odot$ = Hadamard product |
| 5 | Weight gradient | $\nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(l)}} J = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} (\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)})^\top + \lambda \mathbf{W}^{(l)}$ | Adds $\ell_2$ regularization |
| 6 | Bias gradient | $\nabla_{\mathbf{b}^{(l)}} J = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)}$ | No regularization on bias |
| 7 | Gradient descent update | $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t - \eta \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}_t)$ | Learning rate $\eta > 0$ |
| 8 | Softmax function | $\text{softmax}(\mathbf{z})_i = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}$ | Outputs sum to **1** |
| 9 | Sigmoid function | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Range $(0, 1)$ |
| 10 | $\ell_2$ regularizer | $\Omega(\mathbf{W}) = \dfrac{1}{2}\sum_{l,i,j} (W_{ij}^{(l)})^2$ | Weight decay term |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Feedforward networks with gradient-based learning underpin:
- **Tabular data regression** (finance, manufacturing quality prediction)
- **Binary/Multi-class classification** (medical diagnosis, fault detection)
- **Function approximation** (control systems, surrogate modeling)
- **Feature extractors** feeding downstream tasks (transfer learning, embeddings)

In Kerala's industrial ecosystem, such networks are deployed in **predictive maintenance** for power systems, **crop yield forecasting** for agri-tech startups, and **anomaly detection** in IoT sensor networks — all rooted in this fundamental topic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Forward Propagation Recurrence

We begin by formalizing the layer-wise forward computation.

**Starting assumption:** Given an input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_0}$.

**Layer $l = 1$ (first hidden layer):** Compute the linear combination of inputs:

$$\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$$

**Apply activation:**

$$\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = g^{(1)}(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})$$

**Generalize for arbitrary layer $l$:**

$$\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}$$

$$\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = g^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$$

**Terminal layer $l = L$:**

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{a}^{(L)} = g^{(L)}(\mathbf{z}^{(L)})$$

> This is the **forward pass**. The output is deterministic given $\mathbf{x}$ and $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Backpropagation via the Chain Rule

**Goal:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}}$ and $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(l)}}$ efficiently for all $l$.

**Step 1 — Define the per-sample loss and total cost:**

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}^{(i)}(\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the error signal $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)}$:** The gradient of the loss w.r.t. the pre-activation of layer $l$:

$$\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} \equiv \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(l)}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(L)}$ at the output layer** (using Mean Squared Error + linear output for clarity):

$$\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(L)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(L)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(L)}} \odot \frac{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(L)}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(L)}} = (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}) \odot g'^{(L)}(\mathbf{z}^{(L)})$$

**Step 4 — Derive the recurrence for hidden layers** using the chain rule:

$$\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(l)}}$$

Expanding $\mathcal{L}$ as a function of $\mathbf{z}^{(l+1)}$ which depends on $\mathbf{a}^{(l)}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} = \left( \frac{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(l+1)}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(l)}} \right)^\top \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(l+1)}} \odot g'^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$$

Since $\mathbf{z}^{(l+1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l+1)} \mathbf{a}^{(l)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l+1)}$, we have $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(l+1)}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(l)}} = (\mathbf{W}^{(l+1)})^\top$:

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} = \left((\mathbf{W}^{(l+1)})^\top \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l+1)}\right) \odot g'^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})}$$

**Step 5 — Compute parameter gradients:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} \, (\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)})^\top$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(l)}} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)}$$

Adding $\ell_2$ regularization $\lambda \Omega(\mathbf{W})$:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(l)}} J = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} (\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)})^\top + \lambda \mathbf{W}^{(l)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Softmax + Cross-Entropy Combined Gradient

Let $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{z}^{(L)})$ and $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_i y_i \log \hat{y}_i$.

**Step 1:** Write the softmax component explicitly:

$$\hat{y}_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial z_k}$:

- If $i = k$: $\dfrac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial z_i} = \hat{y}_i (1 - \hat{y}_i)$
- If $i \neq k$: $\dfrac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial z_k} = -\hat{y}_i \hat{y}_k$

**Step 3:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k}$ using the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} = -\sum_i y_i \frac{1}{\hat{y}_i} \frac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial z_k}$$

Split into $i = k$ and $i \neq k$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} = -y_k (1 - \hat{y}_k) + \sum_{i \neq k} y_i \hat{y}_k$$

Since $\sum_i y_i = 1$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} = -y_k + \hat{y}_k \sum_i y_i = \hat{y}_k - y_k$$

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(L)} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Gold:** This elegant result — that the gradient of softmax + cross-entropy is simply $\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$ — is one of the most tested derivations in KTU Module 1. Memorize it.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why the Combination Matters

| Loss + Output Pair | $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(L)}$ | Gradient Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **MSE + Linear** | $(\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y})$ | Clean, well-conditioned |
| **MSE + Sigmoid** | $(\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}) \odot \sigma'(\mathbf{z})$ | Saturates → vanishing gradient |
| **Cross-Entropy + Sigmoid** | $\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$ | Clean — cancels saturation |
| **Cross-Entropy + Softmax** | $\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$ | Clean — used in multi-class |

> **Conclusion:** Cross-entropy with sigmoid/softmax outputs naturally cancels the activation's saturation term, yielding a strong error signal. This is why **cross-entropy is preferred over MSE for classification**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (2-Layer Network)

Consider $L = 2$, $n_0 = 2$, $n_1 = 3$, $n_2 = 1$, activation $g = \sigma$ everywhere, MSE loss.

**Setup:**
- $\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $y = 1$
- $\mathbf{W}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.3 \\ 0.2 & 0.4 \\ -0.1 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$
- $\mathbf{W}^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & -0.2 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b}^{(2)} = 0$
- Learning rate $\eta = 0.1$

**Forward Pass:**

$$\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.2 \\ -0.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6225 \\ 0.5498 \\ 0.4750 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{z}^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{a}^{(1)} = 0.7(0.6225) + (-0.2)(0.5498) + 0.3(0.4750) = 0.4357 - 0.1100 + 0.1425 = 0.4682$$

$$\hat{y} = \sigma(0.4682) = 0.6150$$

**Loss:**

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(0.6150 - 1)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.1465) = 0.0733$$

**Backward Pass:**

$$\delta^{(2)} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \sigma'(z^{(2)}) = (-0.3850)(0.6150)(1 - 0.6150) = -0.3850 \times 0.2367 = -0.0911$$

$$\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(1)} = ((\mathbf{W}^{(2)})^\top \delta^{(2)}) \odot \sigma'(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 \\ -0.2 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix}(-0.0911) \odot \begin{bmatrix} 0.6225(1-0.6225) \\ 0.5498(1-0.5498) \\ 0.4750(1-0.4750) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} -0.0638 \\ 0.0182 \\ -0.0273 \end{bmatrix} \odot \begin{bmatrix} 0.2350 \\ 0.2474 \\ 0.2494 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.01500 \\ 0.00450 \\ -0.00682 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Parameter gradients:**

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(2)}} J = \delta^{(2)} (\mathbf{a}^{(1)})^\top = -0.0911 \begin{bmatrix} 0.6225 & 0.5498 & 0.4750 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0567 & -0.0501 & -0.0433 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(1)}} J = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(1)} \mathbf{x}^\top = \begin{bmatrix} -0.01500 \\ 0.00450 \\ -0.00682 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.01500 & 0 \\ 0.00450 & 0 \\ -0.00682 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Update (with $\eta = 0.1$):**

$$\mathbf{W}^{(2)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(2)} - \eta \nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(2)}} J = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7057 & -0.1949 & 0.3043 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{W}^{(1)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(1)} - \eta \nabla_{\mathbf{W}^{(1)}} J = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5015 & -0.3 \\ 0.1996 & 0.4 \\ -0.0993 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

> This single-step illustration grounds the abstract formulas in concrete arithmetic — exactly what KTU's Part B 14-mark questions expect.
