# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Deep Feedforward Network Topology

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a feedforward neural network and a recurrent neural network. Mention any one application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Feedforward NN | Recurrent NN |
|---|---|---|
| **Information flow** | Unidirectional (input → hidden → output); no cycles | Bidirectional with feedback connections / cycles |
| **Memory** | Stateless; each input processed independently | Stateful; maintains hidden state across time steps |
| **Typical use** | Image classification, tabular regression | Time-series forecasting, NLP, speech |
| **Example application** | MNIST digit classification | Machine translation, stock price prediction |

**Scoring rubric:** 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for application each → 3 marks.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is a nonlinear activation function essential in hidden layers of a deep feedforward network? What happens if we use only linear activations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A nonlinear activation $g(\cdot)$ is essential because:

1. **(1 mark)** Without nonlinearity, the composition of layers reduces to a single linear transformation. Mathematically, if all $g^{(l)}$ are linear, say $g(z) = z$:

$$\mathbf{a}^{(L)} = \mathbf{W}^{(L)} (\mathbf{W}^{(L-1)}(\dots(\mathbf{W}^{(1)}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)})\dots) + \mathbf{b}^{(L)}) = \mathbf{W}_{\text{eff}}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_{\text{eff}}$$

2. **(1 mark)** This collapses any $L$-layer "deep" network into an equivalent single linear model — depth provides **no additional representational power**. The network loses its ability to approximate nonlinear functions and the **universal approximation theorem** fails.

3. **(1 mark)** Nonlinear activations like ReLU, sigmoid, or tanh enable the network to learn **piecewise linear / curved decision boundaries**, which is what makes deep learning powerful for tasks like image recognition, speech processing, and nonlinear regression.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the **backpropagation equations** for a deep feedforward network with arbitrary differentiable activation functions and a generic cost function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$. Clearly state the role of the **error signal $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)}$** and show how the chain rule leads to the recurrence relation across layers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 2-class classification problem with output activation $\sigma$ (sigmoid) and binary cross-entropy loss. Show that the output-layer error signal simplifies to $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(L)} = \hat{y} - y$. Hence, explain why this combination is preferred over **MSE + sigmoid** for classification tasks. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Setup [1 mark]:** Define the forward relations for $l = 1, \dots, L$:

$$\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}, \quad \mathbf{a}^{(l)} = g^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$$

with $\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}$. The cost is $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}(\hat{\mathbf{y}}^{(i)}, \mathbf{y}^{(i)}) + \lambda \Omega$.

**Step 2 — Define error signal [1 mark]:** Introduce

$$\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} \equiv \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(l)}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute output error [1 mark]:** Applying the chain rule:

$$\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(L)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(L)}} \odot g'^{(L)}(\mathbf{z}^{(L)})$$

**Step 4 — Derive the recurrence [2 marks]:** Use the chain rule across layers:

$$\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(l)}} = \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(l+1)}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(l)}}\right)^\top \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(l+1)}}$$

Since $\mathbf{z}^{(l+1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l+1)} g^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)}) + \mathbf{b}^{(l+1)}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} = \left((\mathbf{W}^{(l+1)})^\top \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l+1)}\right) \odot g'^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$$

**Step 5 — Parameter gradients [1 mark]:** Differentiate $\mathcal{L}$ w.r.t. weights and biases:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} (\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)})^\top, \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(l)}} = \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)}$$

**Step 6 — Aggregation [1 mark]:** Average over the mini-batch and add the regularization gradient $\lambda \mathbf{W}^{(l)}$ for $\ell_2$ penalty.

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Set up loss and output [1 mark]:** For sigmoid output $\hat{y} = \sigma(z)$ and binary cross-entropy:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\left[y \log \hat{y} + (1-y) \log(1-\hat{y})\right]$$

**Step 2 — Use the chain rule [1 mark]:**

$$\delta^{(L)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \hat{y}$ [1 mark]:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} = -\frac{y}{\hat{y}} + \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{y}} = \frac{\hat{y} - y}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\partial \hat{y}/\partial z$ [1 mark]:** Since $\hat{y} = \sigma(z)$, $\dfrac{d\sigma}{dz} = \hat{y}(1-\hat{y})$:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z} = \hat{y}(1-\hat{y})$$

**Step 5 — Combine [1 mark]:**

$$\delta^{(L)} = \frac{\hat{y} - y}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})} \cdot \hat{y}(1-\hat{y}) = \hat{y} - y$$

The $\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})$ terms **cancel exactly**, yielding a clean gradient.

**Step 6 — Contrast with MSE [2 marks]:** For MSE + sigmoid, $\delta^{(L)} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \hat{y}(1-\hat{y})$. When $|\hat{y} - y|$ is large (saturated sigmoid), the gradient **vanishes**, slowing learning. With cross-entropy, the saturation factor cancels, providing a **strong, stable gradient** even for confidently wrong predictions. This is why cross-entropy is the standard choice for classification.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Alternative]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a **Deep Feedforward Network (DFN)** with depth $L \geq 3$. Include definitions of input/hidden/output layers, weight matrices, bias vectors, and activation functions. State the **Universal Approximation Theorem** and its implications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a multi-class classification problem with $C$ classes, derive the gradient of the **categorical cross-entropy loss** combined with the **softmax activation**. Show that the output-layer error simplifies to $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(L)} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$, where $\mathbf{y}$ is the one-hot encoded true label vector. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Architecture overview [1 mark]:** A DFN of depth $L$ consists of $L$ layers of neurons, indexed $0$ (input) through $L$ (output). Information flows strictly forward.

**Step 2 — Input layer [1 mark]:** $\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_0}$ — just the feature vector; no computation.

**Step 3 — Hidden layers [2 marks]:** For $l = 1, \dots, L-1$:

$$\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}, \quad \mathbf{a}^{(l)} = g^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$$

- $\mathbf{W}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l \times n_{l-1}}$: trainable weights
- $\mathbf{b}^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_l}$: trainable biases
- $g^{(l)}$: nonlinear activation (ReLU, tanh, etc.)

**Step 4 — Output layer [1 mark]:** Produces $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = g^{(L)}(\mathbf{z}^{(L)})$ with output activation chosen per task (softmax for multi-class).

**Step 5 — Universal Approximation Theorem [1 mark]:** A feedforward network with a **single hidden layer** containing a **finite** number of neurons, with any "squashing" activation (e.g., sigmoid), can approximate **any continuous function on a compact subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$** to arbitrary precision — given suitable weights.

**Step 6 — Implications [1 mark]:** The theorem guarantees *existence* of weights but says nothing about *learnability*. In practice, **deeper networks** (multiple hidden layers) achieve far better **sample efficiency** and generalization than shallow ones with equivalent parameter counts.

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Setup [1 mark]:** Softmax outputs are

$$\hat{y}_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}, \quad i = 1, \dots, C$$

Categorical cross-entropy with one-hot $\mathbf{y}$:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i \log \hat{y}_i$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial z_k$ [2 marks]:** Chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i \frac{1}{\hat{y}_i} \frac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial z_k}$$

Using $\partial \hat{y}_i / \partial z_k = \hat{y}_i (\delta_{ik} - \hat{y}_k)$ (where $\delta_{ik}$ is Kronecker delta):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} = -\sum_i y_i (\delta_{ik} - \hat{y}_k) = -y_k + \hat{y}_k \sum_i y_i$$

**Step 3 — Apply one-hot constraint [1 mark]:** Since $\mathbf{y}$ is one-hot, $\sum_i y_i = 1$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} = \hat{y}_k - y_k$$

**Step 4 — Vector form [1 mark]:**

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(L)} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation [1 mark]:** This compact, linear-form error means that the gradient's **magnitude grows with the prediction error** — when $\hat{y}$ is far from $y$, the gradient is large, accelerating learning. There is no vanishing gradient from softmax saturation because the Jacobian of softmax and the loss gradient cancel.

**Step 6 — Connection to backprop [1 mark]:** This $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(L)}$ is fed into the backward recurrence $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} = ((\mathbf{W}^{(l+1)})^\top \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l+1)}) \odot g'^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$ to compute hidden-layer gradients.

---

## 5.4 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Hadamard product $\odot$** — Writing $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} = (\mathbf{W}^{(l+1)})^\top \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l+1)} \cdot g'^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$ instead of using $\odot$ (element-wise). **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Missing the regularization term** — When $\ell_2$ regularization is specified, the weight gradient must include $+\lambda \mathbf{W}^{(l)}$. Forgetting it costs a full mark.
> 3. **Confusing $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \mathbf{W}$ vs $\partial J/\partial \mathbf{W}$** — $J$ averages over the batch; $\mathcal{L}$ is per-sample. Be explicit.
> 4. **Forgetting that $\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}$** — Many students skip the boundary condition.
> 5. **Not stating the assumptions** — Always declare: differentiable activation, mini-batch gradient descent, etc.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **deep feedforward network** is a composition of affine maps + nonlinear activations; no cycles.
- The **forward pass** computes predictions; the **backward pass** propagates errors via the chain rule.
- The **error signal** $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)}$ satisfies the recurrence $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l)} = ((\mathbf{W}^{(l+1)})^\top \boldsymbol{\delta}^{(l+1)}) \odot g'^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$.
- **Cross-entropy + softmax/sigmoid** yields the clean gradient $\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$, preferred for classification.
- **MSE + linear output** is the standard for regression.
- **Gradient descent** update rule: $\boldsymbol{\theta}_{t+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_t - \eta \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J$.
- The **Universal Approximation Theorem** guarantees existence of good weights for shallow nets, but **deeper networks** generalize better in practice.
- Common hidden activations: **ReLU** (default), **tanh**, **leaky ReLU**.
- **$\ell_2$ regularization** (weight decay) adds $\lambda \mathbf{W}^{(l)}$ to the weight gradient.
- The chain rule — applied systematically layer by layer — is the **mathematical engine** of backpropagation.
- **Vanishing gradient** arises when $|g'(z)| \ll 1$; choose activations like **ReLU** to mitigate.
- Always include **boundary conditions**: $\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}$, $\boldsymbol{\delta}^{(L)}$ defined per loss/output pair.
- KTU Module 1 typically awards **2–3 marks** for definitions, **4–5 marks** for derivations, and **3–5 marks** for numerical/comparison sub-parts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hidden Units
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT745 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hidden Units

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a feed-forward **Artificial Neural Network (ANN)**, a **Hidden Unit** (also called a hidden neuron) is a computational node located in any **intermediate layer** (i.e., a layer other than the input layer or the output layer) of the network. Each hidden unit performs two sequential operations:

1. A **linear transformation** of its inputs from the previous layer, computing a weighted sum plus a bias term.
2. A **non-linear activation function** applied element-wise to that sum, producing the unit's output, which is then propagated to the next layer.

Mathematically, for the $i$-th hidden unit in layer $l$, the pre-activation (logit) and activation are:

$$z_i^{(l)} = \sum_{j=1}^{n^{(l-1)}} w_{ij}^{(l)} \, a_j^{(l-1)} + b_i^{(l)}$$

$$a_i^{(l)} = f\left( z_i^{(l)} \right)$$

where $w_{ij}^{(l)}$ are the learnable weights, $b_i^{(l)}$ is the bias, $a_j^{(l-1)}$ are the activations of the previous layer, and $f(\cdot)$ is the **activation function**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The choice of hidden unit (defined primarily by its activation function $f$) is one of the primary design decisions in a deep network. The depth and width of the hidden layer (i.e., the **number of hidden units**) directly control the **representational capacity** and **inductive bias** of the network.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **factory assembly line**:
- The **input layer** is the raw materials arriving at the loading dock (pixels of an image, words in a sentence).
- The **output layer** is the finished product leaving the warehouse (a classification label, a predicted value).
- The **hidden units** are the **specialist workers** stationed along the assembly line. Each worker looks at the partial assembly in front of them (the previous layer's output), applies a specific tool (the weighted sum + bias), and then makes a decision (the activation function) — *"Do I pass this piece forward, ignore it, or transform it?"*

A worker with a **ReLU tool** only forwards the part if it is "positive/usable," otherwise discards it. A worker with a **sigmoid tool** converts the part into a "yes/no probability." Different tools (activation functions) create different kinds of workers, and a deep network simply stacks many such workers in series to build progressively more abstract features.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Hidden units are the *learnable feature extractors* of a neural network. They do not observe the raw data or the final target directly — they only see what is passed to them from the layer before, which is why they are called **"hidden"**.

## 1.3 Role of Hidden Units in the Universal Approximation Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the **Universal Approximation Theorem**, a feed-forward network with **at least one hidden layer** containing a **finite number of hidden units** with a non-linear activation function can approximate *any* continuous function on a compact subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ to arbitrary precision. This theorem explicitly relies on the **non-linearity of the hidden units** — a network of pure linear hidden units collapses into a single linear transformation regardless of depth.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Hidden Unit (Step-by-Step)

The forward computation of a hidden unit follows a strict, deterministic pipeline:

- **Step 1 — Receive Inputs:** The unit receives a vector of activations $\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n^{(l-1)}}$ from the $(l-1)$-th layer.
- **Step 2 — Compute Pre-activation (Logit):** A linear combination is computed using a weight vector $\mathbf{w}_i^{(l)}$ and a scalar bias $b_i^{(l)}$, producing the scalar $z_i^{(l)}$.
- **Step 3 — Apply Activation Function:** The non-linear function $f(\cdot)$ is applied to $z_i^{(l)}$ to produce the unit's output activation $a_i^{(l)}$.
- **Step 4 — Propagation:** $a_i^{(l)}$ is bundled with all sibling hidden units of layer $l$ to form $\mathbf{a}^{(l)}$, which becomes the input to layer $l+1$.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Hidden Units (by Activation Function)

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises the following canonical hidden unit types:

### (a) **ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit)**
The most widely used modern default.

$$f(z) = \max(0, z)$$

- **Why it works:** It is computationally cheap, avoids saturation for $z > 0$, and induces **sparse activations** (since half the units output exactly 0 for any given input). Sparsity acts as an implicit regularizer.
- **Pitfall:** The **"dying ReLU"** problem — units can get stuck outputting 0 if their pre-activation remains negative during training.

### (b) **Leaky ReLU / Parametric ReLU (PReLU)**
A modification that allows a small, non-zero gradient when $z < 0$:

$$f(z) = \begin{cases} z, & z \geq 0 \\ \alpha z, & z < 0 \end{cases} \quad \text{with} \quad \alpha \in (0, 1)$$

- **Why it works:** Fixes the dying ReLU problem by ensuring the gradient is never exactly zero in the negative region.

### (c) **Sigmoid (Logistic) Hidden Unit**
Historically used, now mostly confined to the output layer for binary classification.

$$f(z) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

- **Properties:** Squashes input to $(0, 1)$; **not zero-centered**, which can cause zig-zagging gradient updates.
- **Pitfall:** Saturates for large $\vert z \vert$, leading to **vanishing gradients**.

### (d) **Tanh (Hyperbolic Tangent)**
Zero-centered version of the sigmoid.

$$f(z) = \tanh(z) = \frac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}$$

- **Properties:** Squashes input to $(-1, 1)$. Still suffers from saturation but is preferable to sigmoid in hidden layers.

### (e) **Maxout Unit**
A generalisation that takes the maximum over $k$ linear pieces:

$$f(z) = \max_{j \in [1, k]} \left( z_j \right)$$

where each $z_j$ is a separate linear function. Maxout is a **"learnable activation"** — it can, in principle, represent ReLU, absolute value, or any piecewise linear convex function.

### (f) **Radial Basis Function (RBF) Unit**
A historically important unit (used in **Restricted Boltzmann Machines**) that activates based on distance to a centre:

$$f(z) = e^{-\frac{\lVert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c} \rVert^2}{2\sigma^2}}$$

- **Inductive bias:** Local — unit responds most strongly when input is close to its centre $\mathbf{c}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Value / Range | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| $n^{(l)}$ | Number of hidden units in layer $l$ | $32$ to $4096+$ | Controls model capacity |
| $w_{ij}^{(l)}$ | Weight from unit $j$ in layer $l-1$ to unit $i$ in layer $l$ | Initialised small (e.g., $\mathcal{N}(0, 0.01^2)$) | Learned via back-prop |
| $b_i^{(l)}$ | Bias of unit $i$ in layer $l$ | Often initialised to 0 | Shifts activation function |
| $f(\cdot)$ | Activation function | ReLU (default) | Defines unit type |
| $\alpha$ | Leak coefficient in Leaky ReLU | $0.01$ to $0.3$ | Fixes dying ReLU |
| $\sigma^2$ | Variance in RBF unit | Hyperparameter | Controls locality |
| $\mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[f(x)]$ | Expected activation frequency | Used in **sparsity regularisation** | Penalises average activation $\approx \rho$ |
| $\lVert \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \rVert_F$ | Frobenius norm of weight matrix | Used in weight decay | L2 regularisation term |
| $\text{Parameter count}$ | Total learnable parameters | $\sum_{l} n^{(l-1)} \cdot n^{(l)} + n^{(l)}$ | Used to compute memory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sparsity Regularisation (KTU Favourite):** To encourage a hidden unit $i$ to have a low average activation $\hat{\rho}_i$ (target sparsity $\rho$), the **KL-divergence penalty** is added to the loss:
>
> $$\Omega_{\text{sparse}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n^{(l)}} \text{KL}\!\left( \rho \,\Vert\, \hat{\rho}_i \right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n^{(l)}} \left[ \rho \log \frac{\rho}{\hat{\rho}_i} + (1-\rho) \log \frac{1-\rho}{1-\hat{\rho}_i} \right]$$
>
> where $\hat{\rho}_i = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} a_i^{(l)}(x_j)$ is the average activation of unit $i$ over a mini-batch of size $m$.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Hidden units form the backbone of every modern deep learning application:

- **Computer Vision:** Early hidden layers learn **edge detectors**; middle layers learn **textures and motifs**; deep layers learn **object parts and full objects** (this hierarchical feature emergence is *only* possible because of non-linear hidden units).
- **Natural Language Processing:** Hidden units in transformers act as **context encoders**, where attention-weighted combinations feed into non-linear projections.
- **Speech Recognition:** Hidden units model spectral features and temporal patterns.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Hidden units in policy/value networks approximate complex, non-linear control surfaces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Rule (KTU Board Tip):** Always use **ReLU** (or its variants: Leaky ReLU, PReLU, GELU) in hidden layers. Use **sigmoid** only for the output layer in binary classification, and **softmax** for multi-class output. Avoid using sigmoid/tanh in **deep** hidden layers to mitigate vanishing gradients.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Pass Through a Single Hidden Unit

**Problem Setup:** Consider a fully-connected hidden layer $l$ with $n^{(l-1)} = 3$ input units and $n^{(l)} = 2$ hidden units. Input activations from the previous layer are:

$$\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} = \begin{bmatrix} a_1^{(l-1)} \\ a_2^{(l-1)} \\ a_3^{(l-1)} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ -0.2 \\ 0.9 \end{bmatrix}$$

The weight matrix connecting layer $(l-1)$ to layer $l$ is:

$$\mathbf{W}^{(l)} = \begin{bmatrix} w_{11} & w_{12} & w_{13} \\ w_{21} & w_{22} & w_{23} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & -0.4 & 0.3 \\ 0.5 & 0.2 & -0.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The bias vector is $\mathbf{b}^{(l)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ -0.2 \end{bmatrix}$. The hidden unit activation function is **ReLU**.

**Step 1 — Compute the pre-activation vector $\mathbf{z}^{(l)}$:** The pre-activation is a linear map.

$$\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = \mathbf{W}^{(l)} \, \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}$$

Substituting:

$$\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & -0.4 & 0.3 \\ 0.5 & 0.2 & -0.1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ -0.2 \\ 0.9 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ -0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Performing the matrix-vector multiplication row-by-row:

$$z_1^{(l)} = (0.1)(0.5) + (-0.4)(-0.2) + (0.3)(0.9) + 0.1$$
$$z_1^{(l)} = 0.05 + 0.08 + 0.27 + 0.1 = 0.50$$

$$z_2^{(l)} = (0.5)(0.5) + (0.2)(-0.2) + (-0.1)(0.9) + (-0.2)$$
$$z_2^{(l)} = 0.25 - 0.04 - 0.09 - 0.2 = -0.08$$

Thus, $\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.50 \\ -0.08 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the ReLU activation element-wise:** $f(z) = \max(0, z)$.

$$a_1^{(l)} = \max(0, \, 0.50) = 0.50$$
$$a_2^{(l)} = \max(0, \, -0.08) = 0.00$$

**Step 3 — Final hidden layer output:**

$$\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.50 \\ 0.00 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Unit 2 is *inactive* (outputs 0) for this particular input. This is the **sparse activation** property of ReLU hidden units — a key factor in their efficiency and implicit regularisation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Backward Pass (Gradient Flow) Through a Hidden Unit

**Why this matters for KTU:** The whole point of a non-linear hidden unit is that it allows gradients to flow during back-propagation. We derive the local gradient that each hidden unit contributes to the chain rule.

**General Setup:** Loss function $\mathcal{L}$ is at the output. We need $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i^{(l)}}$ (gradient w.r.t. pre-activation).

By the chain rule, the gradient at the **pre-activation** of a hidden unit is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a_i^{(l)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a_i^{(l)}}{\partial z_i^{(l)}}$$

Since $a_i^{(l)} = f(z_i^{(l)})$, the local derivative is $f'(z_i^{(l)})$.

The gradient w.r.t. the **weights** is then:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i^{(l)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_i^{(l)}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i^{(l)}} \cdot a_j^{(l-1)}$$

**Step-by-step local gradient for each activation function:**

### Case A: ReLU Hidden Unit
$$f(z) = \max(0, z) \quad \Rightarrow \quad f'(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & z > 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \end{cases}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i^{(l)}} = \begin{cases} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a_i^{(l)}}, & z_i^{(l)} > 0 \\ 0, & z_i^{(l)} < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Interpretation:** If a ReLU unit is inactive ($z_i^{(l)} < 0$), the gradient flowing through it is exactly **zero** — the unit neither learns nor passes information backward. This is the *origin of the dying ReLU problem*.

### Case B: Sigmoid Hidden Unit
$$f(z) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f'(z) = \sigma(z)\left[1 - \sigma(z)\right]$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a_i^{(l)}} \cdot \sigma(z_i^{(l)})\left[1 - \sigma(z_i^{(l)})\right]$$

**Interpretation:** When $\sigma(z) \approx 1$ (saturated positive) or $\sigma(z) \approx 0$ (saturated negative), the term $\sigma(z)[1-\sigma(z)] \to 0$, causing the gradient to **vanish**. This is the *vanishing gradient problem* of sigmoid hidden units in deep networks.

### Case C: Tanh Hidden Unit
$$f(z) = \tanh(z) \quad \Rightarrow \quad f'(z) = 1 - \tanh^2(z) = 1 - \left(f(z)\right)^2$$

**Interpretation:** Same vanishing problem as sigmoid at saturation ($\lvert z \rvert$ large $\Rightarrow f'(z) \to 0$), but the zero-centered nature yields faster convergence.

### Case D: Leaky ReLU Hidden Unit
For $\alpha = 0.01$:

$$f'(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & z \geq 0 \\ \alpha = 0.01, & z < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Interpretation:** Gradient is *never* exactly zero — even inactive units continue to receive a small gradient, preventing the dying-ReLU phenomenon.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Why a Purely Linear Hidden Unit Fails (Universal Approximation Caveat)

**Claim:** A network with any number of layers, each containing purely linear hidden units $f(z) = z$, is equivalent to a single linear transformation.

**Proof:**

Consider a 2-layer network where the first layer uses identity activation:

$$\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$$
$$\mathbf{a}^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{a}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}$$

Substituting $\mathbf{a}^{(1)}$ into $\mathbf{a}^{(2)}$:

$$\mathbf{a}^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \left( \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)} \right) + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}$$

$$\mathbf{a}^{(2)} = \left( \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{W}^{(1)}\right) \mathbf{x} + \left( \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{b}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)} \right)$$

Let $\mathbf{W}_{\text{eff}} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{W}^{(1)}$ and $\mathbf{b}_{\text{eff}} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{b}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}$. Then:

$$\mathbf{a}^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}_{\text{eff}} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_{\text{eff}}$$

which is exactly a single linear layer. **By induction**, stacking $L$ linear layers collapses to one linear layer. Therefore, **without a non-linear hidden unit, depth is meaningless**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the most common KTU Part B question: *"Explain why non-linearity is essential in hidden units."* Always tie the answer back to the collapse of linear compositions shown above.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Hidden Unit Types

| Property | ReLU | Leaky ReLU | Sigmoid | Tanh | Maxout |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output range | $[0, \infty)$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | $(0, 1)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ |
| Zero-centered | No | Approximately | No | Yes | No |
| Vanishing gradient risk | Low (one side) | Very low | High | Moderate | Low |
| Computational cost | Very low | Low | High (exp) | High (exp) | Moderate (k ops) |
| Sparse activation | Yes | Partial | No | No | Configurable |
| Dying unit problem | Yes | No | N/A | N/A | No |
| Default for hidden layers | **Yes (modern)** | Sometimes | No (legacy) | No (legacy) | Rare |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture Diagram: Position of Hidden Units in a Deep Network

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Input Unit 1] --> H1A[Hidden Unit H1]
    A1[Input Unit 2] --> H1A
    A2[Input Unit 3] --> H1A
    A0[Input Unit 1] --> H1B[Hidden Unit H2]
    A1[Input Unit 2] --> H1B
    A2[Input Unit 3] --> H1B
    A0[Input Unit 1] --> H1C[Hidden Unit H3]
    A1[Input Unit 2] --> H1C
    A2[Input Unit 3] --> H1C
    H1A --> H2A[Hidden Unit H4 L2]
    H1B --> H2A
    H1C --> H2A
    H1A --> H2B[Hidden Unit H5 L2]
    H1B --> H2B
    H1C --> H2B
    H2A --> O1[Output Unit 1]
    H2B --> O1
    H2A --> O2[Output Unit 2]
    H2B --> O2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram shows a 3-layer network: 1 input layer (left), 2 hidden layers (middle), 1 output layer (right). Every edge represents a learnable weight $w_{ij}$. Hidden units H1, H2, H3 are in layer 1; H4, H5 are in layer 2.

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow Diagram: Single Hidden Unit Computation

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Inputs a1 a2 a3] --> B[Weighted Sum Wx]
    B --> C[Add Bias b]
    C --> D[Pre-activation z]
    D --> E{Activation Function f}
    E --> F[Output Activation a]
    F --> G[Send to Next Layer]
    E -.->|during back-prop| H[Local Gradient f-prime of z]
    H --> I[Weight Update delta W]
    B -.->|chain rule| I
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **forward pass** flows left-to-right (blue arrows above); the **backward pass** uses the local gradient $f'(z)$ to propagate error signals back to update weights.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hidden Unit Types

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Hidden Unit Selection] --> A[ReLU Family]
    Root --> B[Sigmoid Family]
    Root --> C[Tanh Family]
    Root --> D[Maxout]
    Root --> E[RBF]
    A --> A1[Standard ReLU]
    A --> A2[Leaky ReLU alpha 0.01]
    A --> A3[PReLU learnable alpha]
    A --> A4[GELU]
    B --> B1[Logistic Sigmoid]
    B --> B2[Hard Sigmoid]
    C --> C1[Standard Tanh]
    C --> C2[Scaled Tanh]
    D --> D1[k linear pieces]
    E --> E1[Gaussian RBF]
    E --> E2[Local receptive field]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This taxonomy map is the **canonical reference** KTU examiners use when asking classification/short-note questions on hidden units.

---

## 4.4 Schematic: Why ReLU Mitigates Vanishing Gradients

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Layer 1: z large positive] -->|f prime = 1| S2[Layer 2: gradient passes unchanged]
    S3[Layer 2: z large positive] -->|f prime = 1| S4[Layer 3: gradient still 1]
    X1[Layer 1: z large positive] -->|f prime approx 0| X2[Layer 2: gradient vanishes]
    X3[Layer 2: z large positive] -->|f prime approx 0| X4[Layer 3: gradient already near 0]
    S1 -.-> X1
    S2 -.-> X3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **ReLU**, $f'(z) = 1$ on the positive side — so the gradient magnitude is *preserved* through every active hidden unit. For **sigmoid**, $f'(z) \to 0$ at saturation, so gradients *compound to near zero* through deep networks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define a hidden unit in a neural network. Why is non-linearity essential in hidden units?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **hidden unit** is a computational neuron located in any layer other than the input or output layer of a neural network. It computes a weighted sum of its inputs, adds a bias, and applies a non-linear activation function to produce its output.

Non-linearity is essential because:
- **(1 mark)** Stacking purely linear hidden units causes the entire deep network to collapse into a single linear transformation (provable by matrix composition: $\mathbf{W}^{(L)} \cdots \mathbf{W}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}_{\text{eff}}$).
- **(1 mark)** Without non-linearity, the network cannot approximate any non-linear function, violating the **Universal Approximation Theorem**.
- **(1 mark)** Non-linear hidden units enable the network to learn **hierarchical, abstract feature representations** that are essential for tasks like image recognition and language modelling.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Distinguish between ReLU, Sigmoid, and Tanh hidden units in terms of output range, zero-centeredness, and vanishing-gradient susceptibility.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | ReLU | Sigmoid | Tanh |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output range | $[0, \infty)$ | $(0, 1)$ | $(-1, 1)$ |
| Zero-centered | No | No | **Yes** |
| Vanishing gradient | Low (active side only) | High (both sides saturate) | Moderate (saturates at extremes) |

- **(1 mark)** Output ranges as above.
- **(1 mark)** Only **tanh** is zero-centered; ReLU and sigmoid produce strictly non-negative outputs, which can slow gradient descent.
- **(1 mark)** Sigmoid suffers from the most severe vanishing-gradient problem because $f'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ becomes near zero at both extremes, while ReLU maintains a constant gradient of 1 on its active side.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse **activation function** with **hidden unit** — the activation function *defines* the unit type, but the unit is the entire computational cell.
> - **Always** state the mathematical form of the activation function before describing its properties.
> - **Numerical questions:** Most KTU board marks are lost in **arithmetic errors** in the weighted-sum step. Show every multiplication explicitly.
> - **Universal Approximation Theorem** is a frequent sub-question — never claim it applies to a *single* hidden unit; it requires a *layer* of units.
> - When asked about "dying ReLU," always mention the **negative pre-activation** as the root cause and propose **Leaky ReLU** as the fix.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the role of hidden units in a multi-layer feed-forward neural network. Discuss how the number of hidden units affects the model's representational capacity and generalisation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the forward and backward pass equations for a single hidden unit using the ReLU activation. Show explicitly why ReLU hidden units mitigate the vanishing-gradient problem, while sigmoid hidden units do not. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

- **(2 marks) Diagram:** Draw a 3-layer network with input units, hidden units, and output units, marking all weight connections $w_{ij}$.

- **(2 marks) Role:** Hidden units in intermediate layers perform **non-linear feature extraction**. They transform the input representation into progressively more abstract features: early hidden units capture low-level patterns (edges, frequencies), and deep hidden units capture high-level concepts (object parts, semantic meaning). Without hidden units, a network can only learn a linear decision boundary.

- **(2 marks) Representational capacity vs. number of hidden units:** Increasing the number of hidden units increases the **capacity** (Vapnik–Chervonenkis dimension) of the network — more units means the network can represent more complex functions, and carve up the input space into finer piecewise-linear regions (in the case of ReLU). By the **Universal Approximation Theorem**, a single hidden layer with a finite number of hidden units can approximate any continuous function on a compact domain to arbitrary accuracy.

- **(1 mark) Generalisation trade-off:** Too few hidden units cause **underfitting** (high bias); too many cause **overfitting** (high variance), because the network memorises training noise. Regularisation techniques (weight decay, dropout) are used to control this.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Forward Pass (3 marks):**

For a hidden unit $i$ in layer $l$:

$$z_i^{(l)} = \sum_{j} w_{ij}^{(l)} a_j^{(l-1)} + b_i^{(l)}$$

$$a_i^{(l)} = f\left(z_i^{(l)}\right) = \max(0, z_i^{(l)})$$

**Backward Pass (3 marks):**

The local gradient is:

$$f'(z_i^{(l)}) = \begin{cases} 1, & z_i^{(l)} > 0 \\ 0, & z_i^{(l)} \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

The chain rule gives the weight gradient:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a_i^{(l)}} \cdot f'(z_i^{(l)}) \cdot a_j^{(l-1)}$$

**Why ReLU mitigates vanishing gradient (1 mark):**

On the active side ($z > 0$), $f'(z) = 1$ — a *constant*, non-vanishing derivative. Therefore, gradients flow backward through active ReLU units without exponential decay. For sigmoid, $f'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z)) \to 0$ as $\lvert z \rvert$ grows, so multiplying such factors across $L$ layers causes the gradient to shrink as $\mathcal{O}(\sigma(1-\sigma)^L) \to 0$ — the **vanishing gradient problem**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** What are the different types of hidden units used in deep learning? Compare ReLU, Leaky ReLU, Maxout, and RBF hidden units in terms of their mathematical formulation, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a fully-connected hidden layer with **3 input units** and **2 hidden units**. The input activation vector is $\mathbf{a} = [0.5, -0.2, 0.9]^T$, the weight matrix is $\mathbf{W} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & -0.4 & 0.3 \\ 0.5 & 0.2 & -0.1 \end{bmatrix}$, and the bias vector is $\mathbf{b} = [0.1, -0.2]^T$. Using the **sigmoid** activation function, compute the output of the hidden layer and the local gradient $f'(z)$ for each hidden unit. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

- **(1 mark)** Hidden unit types include ReLU, Leaky ReLU, PReLU, sigmoid, tanh, Maxout, RBF, and softmax (rarely in hidden layers).

- **(6 marks) Comparison table and explanation:**

| Unit Type | Formula | Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ReLU** | $\max(0, z)$ | Cheap, sparse, no vanishing on active side | Dying ReLU |
| **Leaky ReLU** | $\max(z, \alpha z)$, $\alpha \approx 0.01$ | No dying units | Slightly more compute |
| **Maxout** | $\max_j z_j$ over $k$ linear pieces | Learnable activation, no saturation | $k \times$ parameters per unit |
| **RBF** | $e^{-\lVert x - c \rVert^2 / 2\sigma^2}$ | Local response, used in RBMs | Expensive, hard to train in deep nets |

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Pre-activation (3 marks):**

$$\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W} \mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b}$$

$$z_1 = (0.1)(0.5) + (-0.4)(-0.2) + (0.3)(0.9) + 0.1 = 0.05 + 0.08 + 0.27 + 0.1 = 0.50$$

$$z_2 = (0.5)(0.5) + (0.2)(-0.2) + (-0.1)(0.9) + (-0.2) = 0.25 - 0.04 - 0.09 - 0.2 = -0.08$$

**Step 2 — Sigmoid activation (2 marks):**

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

$$a_1 = \sigma(0.50) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.5}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.6065} = 0.6225$$

$$a_2 = \sigma(-0.08) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{0.08}} = \frac{1}{1 + 1.0833} = 0.4800$$

**Step 3 — Local gradient $f'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ (2 marks):**

$$f'(z_1) = (0.6225)(1 - 0.6225) = (0.6225)(0.3775) = 0.2350$$

$$f'(z_2) = (0.4800)(1 - 0.4800) = (0.4800)(0.5200) = 0.2496$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final hidden layer output:** $\mathbf{a} = [0.6225, \, 0.4800]^T$. Note how the sigmoid forces both outputs into the interval $(0, 1)$ and how the local gradients are bounded away from zero only because $z_1$ and $z_2$ are near the linear region of the sigmoid. Had $z_1$ been 10, the gradient would collapse to $\approx 0$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorise for KTU 2024):**
>
> - **Definition:** A hidden unit is a neuron in any layer except the input/output layer.
> - **Two-step computation:** Pre-activation $z = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{a} + b$ → Activation $a = f(z)$.
> - **Non-linearity is mandatory:** A stack of linear units collapses to a single linear map.
> - **Default choice:** ReLU for hidden layers in modern architectures.
> - **ReLU properties:** Output range $[0, \infty)$, sparse activations, gradient = 1 when active, gradient = 0 when inactive.
> - **Dying ReLU fix:** Use Leaky ReLU (with $\alpha = 0.01$ to $0.1$) or PReLU (learnable $\alpha$).
> - **Sigmoid pitfalls:** Vanishing gradient, not zero-centered → avoid in deep hidden layers.
> - **Tanh advantage:** Zero-centered output in $(-1, 1)$ → better gradient behaviour than sigmoid.
> - **Maxout:** Learnable piecewise-linear activation, parameter-heavy.
> - **RBF:** Local activation, used in older architectures (RBMs), rare in modern CNNs/Transformers.
> - **Universal Approximation Theorem:** A single hidden layer with finitely many non-linear hidden units can approximate any continuous function on a compact domain.
> - **Sparsity regularisation:** KL-divergence penalty between average activation $\hat{\rho}_i$ and target $\rho$ — penalises hidden units that fire too often.
> - **Capacity vs. Generalisation:** More hidden units = more capacity, but more overfitting risk. Use regularisation to balance.
> - **Numerical tip:** Always compute the pre-activation **before** applying the activation function, and **show all multiplications** in KTU board answers.
> - **Examiner's favourite one-liner:** *"Hidden units are the learnable, non-linear feature extractors that give deep networks their representational power."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT745 - DEEP LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Architecture Design # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of Deep Learning, **Architecture Design** refers to the structured process of selecting, organizing, and configuring the various computational components of an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) — including the **number of layers**, **number of neurons (units) per layer**, the **type of connectivity** between layers, and the **activation/loss functions** — such that the network can effectively learn a non-linear mapping from a high-dimensional input space to a desired output space with minimal generalization error. Mathematically, a neural architecture $\mathcal{A}$ is defined as the tuple: $$\mathcal{A} = \bigl\{ L, \, W, \, \sigma, \, \mathcal{L} \bigr\}$$ where $L$ denotes the set of layers, $W$ denotes the collection of weight tensors, $\sigma$ denotes the family of activation functions, and $\mathcal{L}$ denotes the loss (objective) function. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEITT745 — Module 1):** > *Architecture Design* is the foundational pillar of any deep learning pipeline. Before training, optimization, or regularization, the student must justify *why* a particular topology, depth, and width was selected for a given problem class (classification, regression, sequence modeling, etc.). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Blueprint" View Imagine you are an architect designing a **multi-storey office building**: | Building Component | Neural Network Equivalent | |---|---| | Foundation & soil testing | **Input layer** & feature engineering | | Number of floors | **Network depth** (number of hidden layers) | | Rooms per floor | **Width** (neurons per hidden layer) | | Staircases & elevators | **Weight matrices & bias vectors** (connections) | | Electrical wiring | **Activation functions** (signal propagation rule) | | Building code / safety norms | **Loss function & regularizer** | | Final occupancy certificate | **Output layer** | Just as an architect does not randomly stack floors, a deep learning engineer does not randomly stack layers. The design must be *purpose-fit* — deeper for hierarchical features (e.g., images), wider for high-dimensional tabular features, and recurrent for temporal patterns. ## 1.3 Physical & Mathematical Constants in Architecture Design - **Bias term**: $b \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, a learnable offset vector - **Weight initialization variance** (Xavier): $\text{Var}(W) = \dfrac{2}{n_{in} + n_{out}}$ - **He initialization variance** (for ReLU): $\text{Var}(W) = \dfrac{2}{n_{in}}$ - **Learning rate** (typical): $\eta \in [10^{-4},\, 10^{-1}]$ > [!NOTE] > **Definition — "Hyperparameter" vs "Parameter":** > **Parameters** (weights $W$, biases $b$) are *learned* via backpropagation. **Hyperparameters** (depth, width, learning rate $\eta$, activation $\sigma$) are *designed* by the engineer — these are the *true levers of architecture design*. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Decomposition of Architecture Design Decisions A neural architecture is decomposed into **six orthogonal design axes**, each governed by theory, empirical heuristics, or both: ### (A) Depth of the Network ($L$) - **Why**: Deeper networks exploit the **hierarchical composition of features** (edges → textures → parts → objects). - **How**: Each additional layer $l$ transforms its input $a^{(l-1)}$ via $z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$, followed by $\sigma^{(l)}(\cdot)$. - **Pitfall**: Too deep $\Rightarrow$ vanishing/exploding gradients, degradation problem. ### (B) Width of Each Layer ($n^{(l)}$) - **Why**: Wider layers increase **representational capacity per layer**. - **Trade-off**: Width $\uparrow$ ⇒ parameters $\uparrow$ linearly, but vanishing gradient risk $\downarrow$. - **Universal Approximation Theorem**: A network with **even a single hidden layer** of *sufficient width* can approximate any continuous function on a compact domain. ### (C) Activation Function ($\sigma$) - **Why**: Introduces **non-linearity**, enabling learning of non-trivial mappings. - **How**: Applied element-wise: $a^{(l)} = \sigma(z^{(l)})$. ### (D) Connectivity Pattern - **Fully Connected (Dense)**: Every neuron in layer $l-1$ connects to every neuron in $l$. Parameters = $n^{(l-1)} \cdot n^{(l)} + n^{(l)}$. - **Convolutional**: Sparse, weight-shared connectivity. - **Residual (Skip Connections)**: $a^{(l)} = \sigma(z^{(l)}) + a^{(l-2)}$ — mitigates vanishing gradients. - **Recurrent**: Cyclic connectivity for sequences. ### (E) Loss Function ($\mathcal{L}$) - **Regression**: Mean Squared Error, $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$. - **Binary Classification**: Binary Cross-Entropy, $\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}} = -\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl[y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\bigr]$. - **Multi-class**: Categorical Cross-Entropy. ### (F) Output Layer Design - **Regression** → Linear activation (identity). - **Binary classification** → Sigmoid (1 neuron). - **Multi-class ($K$ classes)** → Softmax over $K$ neurons. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula | Units / Domain | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | Linear pre-activation | $z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$ | $z \in \mathbb{R}^{n^{(l)}}$ | Forward propagation | | Activation output | $a^{(l)} = \sigma(z^{(l)})$ | $a \in \mathbb{R}^{n^{(l)}}$ | Non-linear transformation | | Parameter count (Dense layer $l$) | $P_l = n^{(l-1)} \cdot n^{(l)} + n^{(l)}$ | Scalar (count) | Capacity estimation | | Total parameters | $P_{\text{total}} = \sum_{l=1}^{L} \bigl(n^{(l-1)} \cdot n^{(l)} + n^{(l)}\bigr)$ | Scalar | Memory budgeting | | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Output $\in (0,1)$ | Binary gates | | Tanh | $\sigma(z) = \tanh(z) = \dfrac{e^{z}-e^{-z}}{e^{z}+e^{-z}}$ | Output $\in (-1,1)$ | Zero-centered hidden | | ReLU | $\sigma(z) = \max(0, z)$ | Output $\in [0, \infty)$ | Default hidden | | Softmax | $\sigma(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j}}$ | Output $\in (0,1)$, $\sum = 1$ | Multi-class output | | MSE loss | $\mathcal{L} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_i (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | $N$ = batch size | Regression | | Cross-entropy loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_i y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | Probabilistic | Classification | | Xavier init. variance | $\text{Var}(W) = \dfrac{2}{n_{in}+n_{out}}$ | Scalar | Sigmoid / Tanh layers | | He init. variance | $\text{Var}(W) = \dfrac{2}{n_{in}}$ | Scalar | ReLU layers | | Universal Approx. (informal) | $\vert f(x) - \hat{f}(x) \vert < \epsilon$ for some $N$ hidden units | $\epsilon > 0$ | Justifies 1-hidden-layer MLP | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Note on Notation:** Throughout these notes, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes the absolute value (magnitude) operator and is rendered using the LaTeX `\vert` macro to avoid interfering with markdown table syntax. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Architecture design decisions directly impact: 1. **Embedded AI (IoT Edge Devices)** — Shallow, quantized architectures (e.g., MobileNet) are chosen for memory-bound devices. 2. **Medical Imaging (MRI / CT segmentation)** — Deep encoder-decoder (U-Net style) architectures. 3. **High-Frequency Trading** — Wide, shallow MLPs for low-latency inference. 4. **Autonomous Driving Perception** — Deep residual CNNs + recurrent layers for spatio-temporal fusion. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** When answering design questions, always justify *both* the choice of depth *and* width using the bias-variance trade-off. A common KTU pitfall is stating "more layers = better accuracy" without referencing vanishing gradients or the degradation problem. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Parameter Count of a Fully Connected MLP **Problem Statement:** Given an MLP with input dimension $n^{(0)} = d$, hidden layers of widths $n^{(1)}, n^{(2)}, \dots, n^{(L-1)}$, and output dimension $n^{(L)} = K$, derive the total number of trainable parameters. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Consider a single transition from layer $l-1$ to layer $l$. The weight matrix has dimensions: $$W^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n^{(l)} \times n^{(l-1)}}$$ **Reasoning:** Each of the $n^{(l)}$ neurons in layer $l$ must receive a weighted connection from each of the $n^{(l-1)}$ neurons in layer $l-1$. **Step 2:** The number of weight parameters in this transition is therefore: $$P_{W}^{(l)} = n^{(l)} \cdot n^{(l-1)}$$ **Step 3:** The bias vector for layer $l$ has one entry per neuron: $$b^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n^{(l)}}$$ giving $P_{b}^{(l)} = n^{(l)}$ bias parameters. **Step 4:** Total parameters for the transition $l-1 \to l$: $$P_l = P_{W}^{(l)} + P_{b}^{(l)} = n^{(l-1)} \cdot n^{(l)} + n^{(l)}$$ **Step 5:** Summing over all $L$ transitions (from input to output): $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{l=1}^{L} P_l \\[4pt] &= \sum_{l=1}^{L} \bigl( n^{(l-1)} \cdot n^{(l)} + n^{(l)} \bigr) \\[4pt] &= \sum_{l=1}^{L} n^{(l-1)} \cdot n^{(l)} \; + \; \sum_{l=1}^{L} n^{(l)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 (Worked Numerical Example):** Let $d=784$ (MNIST), $n^{(1)}=128$, $n^{(2)}=64$, $K=10$. $$ \begin{aligned} P_1 &= (784 \times 128) + 128 = 100{,}352 + 128 = 100{,}480 \\[4pt] P_2 &= (128 \times 64) + 64 = 8{,}192 + 64 = 8{,}256 \\[4pt] P_3 &= (64 \times 10) + 10 = 640 + 10 = 650 \\[4pt] P_{\text{total}} &= 100{,}480 + 8{,}256 + 650 = 109{,}386 \text{ parameters} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Forward Pass for a Single Hidden Layer **Problem Statement:** Compute the output of a 2-layer network (1 hidden, 1 output) with sigmoid activation $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Input vector:** Let $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ be the input feature vector. **Step 2 — Hidden layer linear combination:** $$z^{(1)} = W^{(1)} x + b^{(1)}, \quad W^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n^{(1)} \times d}, \; b^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n^{(1)}}$$ **Step 3 — Hidden layer activation:** $$a^{(1)} = \sigma(z^{(1)}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z^{(1)}}}$$ This is applied element-wise to each of the $n^{(1)}$ components of $z^{(1)}$. **Step 4 — Output layer linear combination:** $$z^{(2)} = W^{(2)} a^{(1)} + b^{(2)}, \quad W^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times n^{(1)}}$$ **Step 5 — Output activation (softmax for $K$-class):** $$\hat{y}_i = \frac{e^{z^{(2)}_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z^{(2)}_j}}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, K$$ **Step 6 — Composite forward mapping:** The network implements the function $f_\theta : \mathbb{R}^{d} \to \mathbb{R}^{K}$ given by: $$f_\theta(x) = \text{softmax}\bigl( W^{(2)} \cdot \sigma( W^{(1)} x + b^{(1)} ) + b^{(2)} \bigr)$$ where $\theta = \{ W^{(1)}, b^{(1)}, W^{(2)}, b^{(2)} \}$ is the parameter set. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Universal Approximation Theorem (Cybenko, 1989) — Intuitive Form **Statement:** Let $\sigma$ be a continuous, bounded, and non-constant activation function (e.g., sigmoid). Then for any continuous function $g : [0,1]^d \to \mathbb{R}$ and any $\epsilon > 0$, there exists an integer $N$, weights $\alpha_i, w_{ij}$, and biases $b_i$ such that: $$\left\vert g(x) - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \, \sigma\!\left( \sum_{j=1}^{d} w_{ij} x_j + b_i \right) \right\vert < \epsilon$$ for all $x \in [0,1]^d$. **Interpretation (line-by-line):** 1. $\sigma(\cdot)$ is the activation — sigmoid in the original theorem. 2. $\sum_{j=1}^{d} w_{ij} x_j + b_i$ is the affine projection of $x$ onto a single hidden neuron $i$. 3. $\sigma(\cdot)$ produces a *bump* or *step* in input space — a basis function. 4. $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \cdot (\cdot)$ is a *linear combination* of $N$ such basis functions. 5. As $N \to \infty$, this linear combination can approximate $g$ to arbitrary precision $\epsilon$. **Caveat (KTU-level):** The theorem guarantees *existence* of some $N$, but gives **no constructive method** to find it. In practice, deeper networks achieve the same $\epsilon$ with exponentially fewer neurons ($N$) — this is the **efficiency argument for depth**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Activation Function Selection Guide | Property | Sigmoid | Tanh | ReLU | Leaky ReLU | Softmax | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Output range | $(0,1)$ | $(-1,1)$ | $[0,\infty)$ | $(-\infty,\infty)$ | $(0,1)$, $\sum=1$ | | Zero-centered | No | Yes | No | Approximately | Yes | | Gradient at saturation | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0$ | $0$ (for $z<0$) | Small negative | Depends | | Vanishing gradient risk | **High** | **High** | Low | Low | Low | | Recommended layer | Output (binary) | Hidden (legacy) | Hidden (default) | Hidden (improved ReLU) | Output (multi-class) | | Computational cost | Expensive | Expensive | Cheap | Cheap | Expensive | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Neural Network Architecture (MLP) ```mermaid flowchart LR inputLayer["Input Layer
Dimension d equals 784 for MNIST"] hLayer1["Hidden Layer 1
n1 neurons, ReLU activation"] hLayer2["Hidden Layer 2
n2 neurons, ReLU activation"] outputLayer["Output Layer
K neurons, Softmax activation"] W1["Weight Matrix W1
Shape d by n1"] W2["Weight Matrix W2
Shape n1 by n2"] W3["Weight Matrix W3
Shape n2 by K"] lossFn["Loss Function L
Categorical Cross Entropy"] inputLayer --> W1 W1 --> hLayer1 hLayer1 --> W2 W2 --> hLayer2 hLayer2 --> W3 W3 --> outputLayer outputLayer --> lossFn ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Architecture Design Workflow (Decision Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["Start: Define Problem
Classification or Regression or Generation"] q1{"Data Modality?
Tabular, Image, Text, Audio, Time Series"} pathTab["MLP / TabNet
Shallow to Medium Depth"] pathImg["CNN or Vision Transformer
Hierarchical Feature Extraction"] pathTxt["RNN, LSTM, GRU or Transformer
Sequential Context Modeling"] pathAudio["1D CNN or RNN
Spectrogram as Input"] pathTS["Temporal CNN or LSTM
Lag Features as Input"] q2{"Output Type?
Continuous, Binary, Multi Class"} actSel["Choose Output Activation
Identity or Sigmoid or Softmax"] lossSel["Choose Loss Function
MSE or BCE or CCE"] q3{"Hidden Depth L and Width n?"} designH["Apply Heuristics
Start small, scale up
Use Cross Validation"] univApprox["Universal Approximation
Single Wide Hidden Layer Works"] trainEval["Train and Evaluate
Monitor Loss and Accuracy"] startA --> q1 q1 --> pathTab q1 --> pathImg q1 --> pathTxt q1 --> pathAudio q1 --> pathTS pathTab --> q2 pathImg --> q2 pathTxt --> q2 pathAudio --> q2 pathTS --> q2 q2 --> actSel actSel --> lossSel lossSel --> q3 q3 --> designH q3 --> univApprox designH --> trainEval univApprox --> trainEval ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Parameter Count Distribution (Worked Example) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Layer 1 Params: 100480
784 x 128 plus 128"] P2["Layer 2 Params: 8256
128 x 64 plus 64"] P3["Layer 3 Params: 650
64 x 10 plus 10"] PT["Total Params: 109386
Sum of all layers"] P1 --> PT P2 --> PT P3 --> PT ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The first (input → hidden) transition dominates the parameter budget (~92% of total parameters in this example). This is a critical insight — **wide early layers are memory-expensive**, which is why CNNs use sparse weight-sharing to drastically reduce parameter count. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Q:** Define *Architecture Design* in the context of deep learning. List any **four** orthogonal design decisions that must be made. **Model Answer:** Architecture Design is the structured selection and configuration of a neural network's components — layers, neurons, connectivity, activation functions, and loss functions — such that the resulting model can effectively learn a target function from data. The four key design decisions are: 1. **Depth** ($L$) — number of hidden layers. 2. **Width** ($n^{(l)}$) — number of neurons per layer. 3. **Activation function** ($\sigma$) — e.g., ReLU, sigmoid, tanh. 4. **Connectivity pattern** — dense, convolutional, recurrent, or residual. *(Any four of: depth, width, activation, connectivity, loss function, optimizer. 3 marks = 1 for definition + 2 for correct list.)* --- ### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q:** State the **Universal Approximation Theorem** (informally) and mention **one practical limitation** of its naive application. **Model Answer:** The Universal Approximation Theorem states that a feedforward neural network with a **single hidden layer** of *sufficient width* (i.e., finitely many neurons) can approximate **any continuous function** on a compact domain to an arbitrary precision $\epsilon > 0$. **Practical Limitation:** The theorem is *non-constructive* — it guarantees the *existence* of some width $N$ but provides **no recipe** to determine it. In practice, the required $N$ may be **exponentially large**, making deep-and-narrow architectures far more parameter-efficient. *(3 marks: 2 for statement + 1 for limitation.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question B — Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** **Q:** For an image classification task on the CIFAR-10 dataset (input dimension $d = 3072$, 10 output classes), you are required to: **(a)** Design a **fully-connected MLP** with **two hidden layers** of widths $n^{(1)} = 512$ and $n^{(2)} = 256$. Justify the choice of activation functions for the hidden and output layers. Compute the **total number of trainable parameters**. **(b)** Discuss the **bias-variance trade-off** in the context of (i) increasing depth, and (ii) increasing width. Mention how **He initialization** mitigates a specific training issue for ReLU networks. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]** **Architecture Specification:** | Layer | Dimensions | Activation | |---|---|---| | Input | $3072$ | None | | Hidden 1 | $512$ | ReLU | | Hidden 2 | $256$ | ReLU | | Output | $10$ | Softmax | **Justification of Activations:** - **Hidden layers — ReLU:** Mitigates vanishing gradient (derivative is $1$ for $z>0$), computationally cheap ($O(1)$), induces sparse activations. - **Output layer — Softmax:** Produces a valid probability distribution over 10 classes ($\sum_i \hat{y}_i = 1$, $\hat{y}_i > 0$). **Parameter Count Computation:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_1 &= (3072 \times 512) + 512 = 1{,}572{,}864 + 512 = 1{,}573{,}376 \\[4pt] P_2 &= (512 \times 256) + 256 = 131{,}072 + 256 = 131{,}328 \\[4pt] P_3 &= (256 \times 10) + 10 = 2{,}560 + 10 = 2{,}570 \\[4pt] P_{\text{total}} &= 1{,}573{,}376 + 131{,}328 + 2{,}570 = 1{,}707{,}274 \text{ parameters} \end{aligned} $$ **Answer:** $P_{\text{total}} = 1{,}707{,}274$ trainable parameters. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]** **(i) Increasing Depth — Bias-Variance View:** - **Bias $\downarrow$**: Deeper networks compose non-linear features hierarchically, reducing underfitting. - **Variance $\uparrow$**: More parameters $\Rightarrow$ risk of overfitting, especially on small datasets like CIFAR-10 (50,000 training images). - **Failure Mode**: Depth $> 20$ without skip connections $\Rightarrow$ **degradation problem** (training error increases). **(ii) Increasing Width — Bias-Variance View:** - **Bias $\downarrow$**: More neurons per layer $\Rightarrow$ richer basis functions per the Universal Approximation Theorem. - **Variance $\uparrow$**: Parameter count grows linearly with width — overfitting risk on small data. - **Empirical Rule**: It is often better to go **slightly deeper** than to make a single layer excessively wide. **He Initialization (Kaiming Initialization):** For a ReLU-activated layer, the variance of activations is preserved during forward propagation if: $$\text{Var}(W) = \frac{2}{n_{in}}$$ This **mitigates the exploding/vanishing activation problem** by ensuring the variance of pre-activations $z^{(l)}$ remains stable across layers, enabling stable training of deep ReLU networks. --- ### **Question B — Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]** **Q:** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **total number of trainable parameters** in a fully-connected feedforward network with input dimension $d$, $L-1$ hidden layers of widths $n^{(1)}, n^{(2)}, \dots, n^{(L-1)}$, and output dimension $K$. **(b)** A neural network for binary classification takes $d = 20$ input features, has **one hidden layer** with $n^{(1)} = 15$ ReLU neurons, and **one sigmoid output neuron**. Compute the total parameters. If the network replaces ReLU with **tanh**, what changes in the training dynamics must be anticipated? Justify with reference to **Xavier initialization**. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]** Let $n^{(0)} = d$, $n^{(L)} = K$. For transition $l-1 \to l$, the weight matrix $W^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n^{(l)} \times n^{(l-1)}}$ contributes $n^{(l-1)} n^{(l)}$ parameters, and the bias $b^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n^{(l)}}$ contributes $n^{(l)}$. Total parameters: $$P_{\text{total}} = \sum_{l=1}^{L} \bigl( n^{(l-1)} n^{(l)} + n^{(l)} \bigr)$$ Expanded: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{total}} &= \bigl(d \cdot n^{(1)} + n^{(1)}\bigr) + \bigl(n^{(1)} n^{(2)} + n^{(2)}\bigr) + \dots + \bigl(n^{(L-1)} K + K\bigr) \\[4pt] &= d \cdot n^{(1)} + \sum_{l=1}^{L-2} n^{(l)} n^{(l+1)} + n^{(L-1)} K \; + \; \sum_{l=1}^{L} n^{(l)} \end{aligned} $$ *(5 marks for the derivation, 2 marks for correct notation and final summation expression.)* --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]** **Parameter Computation:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_1 &= (20 \times 15) + 15 = 300 + 15 = 315 \\[4pt] P_2 &= (15 \times 1) + 1 = 15 + 1 = 16 \\[4pt] P_{\text{total}} &= 315 + 16 = 331 \text{ parameters} \end{aligned} $$ **Anticipated Training Dynamics Changes (ReLU → tanh):** 1. **Zero-centering**: tanh outputs are in $(-1, 1)$, making gradients better behaved — but **saturation at $\vert z \vert > 2$** still causes vanishing gradients. 2. **Initialization**: Switch from **He initialization** ($\text{Var}(W) = 2/n_{in}$) to **Xavier initialization**: $$\text{Var}(W) = \frac{2}{n_{in} + n_{out}}$$ Xavier is tuned for *symmetric* activations (tanh, sigmoid); using it with ReLU underestimates variance and slows convergence, while using He with tanh overestimates it and risks activation explosion. 3. **Learning Rate**: tanh networks typically require a **lower learning rate** than ReLU networks due to steeper gradient saturation regions. *(3 marks for parameter count, 2 marks for tanh dynamics, 2 marks for Xavier justification.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Areas in PEITT745 Architecture Design Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the bias term** when computing parameter count — KTU examiners allocate **1 full mark** specifically for $n^{(l)}$ bias entries. Students commonly write only $n^{(l-1)} \cdot n^{(l)}$, losing a guaranteed mark. > > 2. **Confusing "hyperparameter" with "parameter"** — Architecture choices (depth, width, activation) are *hyperparameters*, not parameters. Examiners penalize this distinction explicitly. > > 3. **Mismatched activation-loss pairs** — Using sigmoid output with MSE loss, or softmax with MSE for multi-class. KTU expects **softmax + categorical cross-entropy** for multi-class and **sigmoid + binary cross-entropy** for binary. > > 4. **Citing Universal Approximation Theorem without caveats** — It guarantees *existence* of width, not a constructive recipe. Always state the limitation. > > 5. **Wrong dimensional reasoning** — $W^{(l)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n^{(l)} \times n^{(l-1)}}$, **not** the transpose. One swap costs 2 marks in derivations. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Architecture Design (Module 1, PEITT745)** - **Definition**: Architecture $\mathcal{A} = \{ L, W, \sigma, \mathcal{L} \}$ — selection of all structural components. - **Six design axes**: depth $L$, width $n^{(l)}$, activation $\sigma$, connectivity, loss $\mathcal{L}$, output activation. - **Parameter formula**: $P_l = n^{(l-1)} \cdot n^{(l)} + n^{(l)}$; total = sum across all $L$ layers. - **Activation defaults**: ReLU (hidden), Softmax ($K$-class output), Sigmoid (binary output), Identity (regression output). - **Loss defaults**: MSE (regression), BCE (binary), CCE (multi-class). - **Universal Approximation Theorem**: 1 wide hidden layer $\Rightarrow$ any continuous function on compact domain to precision $\epsilon$. *Non-constructive.* - **He init**: $\text{Var}(W) = 2/n_{in}$ — for ReLU; prevents exploding/vanishing activations. - **Xavier init**: $\text{Var}(W) = 2/(n_{in}+n_{out})$ — for tanh/sigmoid; preserves forward + backward variance. - **Depth vs Width**: Deeper = hierarchical features, parameter-efficient; Wider = per-layer capacity, memory-hungry. - **Output layer rule**: Number of neurons = number of classes ($K$) for classification; $1$ for binary/regression. - **Common pitfall**: Confusing parameters (learned) with hyperparameters (designed).

topics.title: Backpropagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT745 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Backpropagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Backpropagation (Backprop):** A *supervised learning* algorithm used to train **Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs)** that efficiently computes the **gradient of the loss function** with respect to each weight in the network by applying the **chain rule of calculus** in a reverse pass from the output layer back to the input layer. The gradients are subsequently used by an optimization algorithm (typically **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)**) to update the network weights and minimize the loss.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, backpropagation is the **core learning engine** of deep learning. It is a two-phase iterative procedure:
- **Phase 1 — Forward Propagation:** Inputs are pushed through the network to produce a prediction $\hat{y}$, and the loss $L(\hat{y}, y)$ is computed.
- **Phase 2 — Backward Propagation:** The gradient $\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_{ij}^{(l)}}$ is propagated backwards layer-by-layer, beginning at the output layer and ending at the input layer.

Mathematically, backpropagation is essentially an **efficient application of the chain rule to a structured computational graph** — the multi-layer neural network.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 "The Blame Game" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a large corporate project with 5 departments (Network Layers). The final product (Output) is defective (High Loss). The CEO (Loss Function) holds a meeting and asks: *"Who is responsible, and by how much?"*
>
> Instead of asking every employee directly (which would be computationally expensive — equivalent to numerical gradient estimation), the CEO starts with the department head of the last department, who points to specific subordinates, who in turn identify junior employees, all the way back to the very first workers. Each person reports *how much their work contributed* to the final defect.
>
> This is **backpropagation in action** — distributing "blame" (gradients) efficiently from output to input through the structured hierarchy of the network.

## 1.3 Why Backpropagation Matters in Deep Learning

- **Computational Efficiency:** The "naive" numerical gradient computation requires $O(W)$ forward passes (where $W$ is the number of weights). Backpropagation computes **all gradients in just ONE forward + ONE backward pass**, giving an $O(W)$ speedup.
- **Scalability:** It is the foundational algorithm that makes training **deep** networks (with millions/billions of parameters) feasible.
- **Universality:** Works for **any** differentiable architecture — CNNs, RNNs, Transformers, etc.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Loss Function $L$** | Scalar measure of error between prediction and ground truth |
| **Forward Pass** | Computation flowing from input $\rightarrow$ output |
| **Backward Pass** | Gradient computation flowing from output $\rightarrow$ input |
| **Local Gradient** | Derivative of a node's output with respect to its input |
| **Global Gradient** | Derivative of final loss with respect to that node's output |
| **Epoch** | One complete pass through the entire training dataset |
| **Learning Rate $\eta$** | Step size used in weight updates (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$) |
| **Chain Rule** | Calculus rule enabling gradient composition through nested functions |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Backpropagation

The backpropagation algorithm operates on a layered feedforward neural network with the following architecture:

$$\text{Input Layer} \rightarrow \text{Hidden Layer 1} \rightarrow \text{Hidden Layer 2} \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow \text{Output Layer}$$

### Phase 1: Forward Pass Logic Steps

1. **Initialize weights** $W^{(l)}$ and biases $b^{(l)}$ for every layer $l$ (often randomly with **He/Xavier initialization**).
2. **Compute pre-activation** for each layer $l$:

$$z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$$
where $a^{(l-1)}$ is the activation from the previous layer (with $a^{(0)} = x$).

3. **Apply activation function** $\sigma$:

$$a^{(l)} = \sigma(z^{(l)})$$

4. **Compute loss** at the output layer:

$$L = \mathcal{L}(a^{(L)}, y)$$

### Phase 2: Backward Pass Logic Steps

1. **Output Layer Error Term** $\delta^{(L)}$:

$$\delta^{(L)} = \nabla_a L \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$$

2. **Recursive Backpropagation of Error** for layer $l$:

$$\delta^{(l)} = \left((W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)}\right) \odot \sigma'(z^{(l)})$$

3. **Compute Gradients w.r.t. Weights and Biases**:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$$

4. **Weight Update Rule (Gradient Descent)**:

$$W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(l)}}$$

$$b^{(l)} \leftarrow b^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial b^{(l)}}$$

## 2.2 The Chain Rule — The Mathematical Heart of Backpropagation

For a composition of functions $f(g(h(x)))$, the chain rule gives:

$$\frac{df}{dx} = \frac{df}{dg} \cdot \frac{dg}{dh} \cdot \frac{dh}{dx}$$

In neural networks, the loss $L$ is a composition of layer-wise functions. The gradient at layer $l$ is:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial a^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{(L)}}{\partial z^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(L)}}{\partial a^{(L-1)}} \cdots \frac{\partial z^{(l+1)}}{\partial a^{(l)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{(l)}}{\partial z^{(l)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(l)}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$$

This is precisely what the recursive $\delta^{(l)}$ formulation computes compactly.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **📋 High-Yield Formula Reference Table**

| Symbol | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-activation | $z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$ | Linear transformation at layer $l$ |
| Activation | $a^{(l)} = \sigma(z^{(l)})$ | Non-linear output of neuron |
| Output Error | $\delta^{(L)} = \nabla_a L \odot \sigma'(z^{(L)})$ | Error signal at output layer |
| Hidden Error | $\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \odot \sigma'(z^{(l)})$ | Recursive error propagation |
| Weight Gradient | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T$ | Gradient w.r.t. weights |
| Bias Gradient | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$ | Gradient w.r.t. biases |
| Weight Update | $W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(l)}}$ | SGD update rule |
| Mean Squared Error | $L = \frac{1}{2}(\hat{y} - y)^2$ | Common regression loss |
| Cross-Entropy Loss | $L = -[y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | Common classification loss |

## 2.4 Activation Function Derivatives (Critical for Backprop)

| Activation $\sigma(z)$ | Derivative $\sigma'(z)$ |
|---|---|
| Sigmoid: $\frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $\sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ |
| Tanh: $\tanh(z)$ | $1 - \tanh^2(z)$ |
| ReLU: $\max(0, z)$ | $1$ if $z > 0$, else $0$ |
| Leaky ReLU | $1$ if $z > 0$, else $\alpha$ |
| Softmax | $\sigma_i(\delta_{ij} - \sigma_j)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Backpropagation is the **workhorse algorithm** in every modern AI application — from **medical diagnosis systems** (CNN-based tumor detection) to **autonomous vehicles** (end-to-end steering prediction), **voice assistants** (RNN/Transformer ASR), and **recommendation engines** (deep collaborative filtering). Without backpropagation, training networks beyond 2-3 layers would be computationally infeasible.

## 2.5 Vanishing & Exploding Gradient Problem

When backpropagating through many layers, the gradient signal can either:
- **Vanish:** $\delta^{(l)} \to 0$ as $l$ decreases (especially with sigmoid/tanh activations)
- **Explode:** $\delta^{(l)} \to \infty$ as $l$ decreases

Mathematically, the gradient magnitude scales roughly as:

$$\left\|\frac{\partial L}{\partial W^{(l)}}\right\| \propto \prod_{k=l}^{L-1} \|W^{(k)}\| \cdot \|\sigma'(z^{(k)})\|$$

If $\|W^{(k)}\| \cdot \|\sigma'(z^{(k)})\| < 1$ → **vanishing**. If $> 1$ → **exploding**.

> Solutions: **ReLU activations, Batch Normalization, Residual connections (ResNets), LSTM gates, Gradient Clipping, Xavier/He initialization.**
