The model has **no visibility into future tokens**. For many Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks, this is a serious handicap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** Consider the sentence:
> *"The **bank** of the river was flooded."*
> *"I deposited money in the **bank**."*
>
> The word *"bank"* is ambiguous until you see the words to its right (*"river"* or *"money"*). A unidirectional RNN processing left-to-right would have to *guess* the meaning of *"bank"* before seeing the disambiguating context. A BiRNN solves this by reading the sentence both ways.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Reading with Two Eyes"

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: Reading a Mystery Novel**
> Imagine you are solving a mystery novel. A **unidirectional RNN** is like a reader who only reads the book *page by page from the beginning*. The reader encounters every clue as it is revealed but never knows which clue the *author will place later* that retroactively changes the meaning of an earlier page.
>
> A **Bidirectional RNN** is like a reader who has *already skimmed the entire book* before carefully reading it. At every sentence, the reader knows what came **before** (local context) AND what comes **after** (global future context). The result is a far richer, more accurate understanding of every word.

| Property | Unidirectional RNN | Bidirectional RNN |
|---|---|---|
| Context Window | Past only | Past **and** Future |
| Hidden Layers | 1 | 2 (Forward + Backward) |
| Online Prediction | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (needs full sequence) |
| Parameters | $W$ | $2W$ (approximately doubles) |
| Use Case | Streaming, real-time | Offline sequence labeling |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Exam Trap:** BiRNNs are **NOT** suitable for **real-time/online prediction tasks** (e.g., live speech recognition, time-series forecasting of stock prices) because they require the *entire future* of the sequence to be available before producing any output. KTU examiners frequently test this constraint.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition — The 5 Core Components

A BiRNN is built from the following logical components:

1. **Input Sequence Layer** $\mathcal{X} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T)$ — feeds the same input to both directional streams.
2. **Forward Hidden Layer** — Recurrent cells indexed by $\vec{h}_t$, sharing weights $\vec{W}_{xh}, \vec{W}_{hh}$ across all time steps.
3. **Backward Hidden Layer** — Recurrent cells indexed by $\overleftarrow{h}_t$, sharing weights $\overleftarrow{W}_{xh}, \overleftarrow{W}_{hh}$ across all time steps.
4. **Combination / Fusion Layer** — A merge operation (concatenation, sum, or average) at every time step.
5. **Output Layer** — Produces $\hat{y}_t$ from the merged hidden representation.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

- **Why two separate weight sets?** Because the *forward* and *backward* dynamics are temporally asymmetric. Forcing them to share weights would destroy the directionality. KTU uses the convention that forward weights carry an arrow $\rightarrow$ and backward weights carry an arrow $\leftarrow$ to keep them visually distinct.
- **How is training done?** Standard **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)** is applied. The total loss is the sum of per-time-step losses, and gradients flow *backward through both* the forward and backward time axes.
- **Why concatenate outputs?** Concatenation preserves the maximum information from both streams. Summation is cheaper but compresses the joint representation, potentially hurting accuracy. KTU standard answer: **concatenation** is the default, with sum/avg as optional.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula sheet** for BiRNN. Memorize every entry — questions are frequently asked on each equation.

| # | Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Dimensions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{h}_t = f\bigl(\vec{W}_{xh}\, x_t + \vec{W}_{hh}\, \vec{h}_{t-1} + \vec{b}_{\vec{h}}\bigr)$ | **Forward hidden state update** | $f$ = tanh / ReLU |
| 2 | $\overleftarrow{h}_t = f\bigl(\overleftarrow{W}_{xh}\, x_t + \overleftarrow{W}_{hh}\, \overleftarrow{h}_{t+1} + \overleftarrow{b}_{\overleftarrow{h}}\bigr)$ | **Backward hidden state update** | Initialized at $t = T$ |
| 3 | $h_t = \bigl[\vec{h}_t \,;\, \overleftarrow{h}_t\bigr]$ | **Concatenation of both directions** | $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{2d_h}$ |
| 4 | $\hat{y}_t = g\bigl(W_{hy}\, h_t + b_y\bigr)$ | **Output prediction** | $g$ = softmax (classification) |
| 5 | $\vec{h}_0 = \vec{0},\quad \overleftarrow{h}_{T+1} = \vec{0}$ | **Boundary conditions** | Both initialized to zero vector |
| 6 | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_t^{(k)} \log \hat{y}_t^{(k)}$ | **Cross-entropy loss** (sequence-level) | $K$ = number of classes |
| 7 | $\theta_{total} = \theta_{\text{forward}} + \theta_{\text{backward}} + \theta_{\text{output}}$ | **Total trainable parameters** | Roughly $2\times$ the unidirectional case |
| 8 | $H_{param} = d(d+d_x + d_h) + d \cdot d_y$ | **Per-direction hidden parameter count** | $d_x$ = input, $d$ = hidden, $d_y$ = output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Convention used by KTU Examiners:**
> - Vectors are written in **bold** lowercase (e.g., $\mathbf{h}_t$) or with arrows: $\vec{h}_t$ (forward), $\overleftarrow{h}_t$ (backward).
> - $\vert \cdot \vert$ is NEVER used inside tables — use $\dim(\cdot)$ for dimensions, as shown above.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Application Domain | Why BiRNN is Used |
|---|---|
| **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** | Each word's tag depends on surrounding words |
| **Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging** | Bidirectional context disambiguates word class |
| **Handwriting Recognition** | A whole word is visible before classification |
| **Protein Structure / Genomic Sequence** | Both N-terminal and C-terminal context matter |
| **Machine Translation (encoder side)** | Encoder reads the source sentence in full |
| **Speech Recognition (offline)** | Phoneme prediction uses acoustic context from both sides |

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Note:** The most famous production-grade BiRNN variant is **BiLSTM** (Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory), which combines BiRNN's two-directional structure with LSTM's gating mechanism. BiLSTM is the de-facto baseline for sequence labeling in industry as of the 2024 scheme.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Forward Pass — Step-by-Step

We will derive the BiRNN forward propagation equations from first principles.

### Step 1: Define the Input Sequence

Let the input be a sequence of $T$ vectors:

$$
\mathcal{X} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T\}, \quad x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_x}
$$

The **logic**: Each $x_t$ represents the input feature vector at time $t$, with dimensionality $d_x$ (e.g., $d_x = 300$ for a 300-dimensional word embedding).

### Step 2: Define the Forward Hidden Recurrence

For the forward direction, we move from $t=1$ to $t=T$:

$$
\vec{h}_t = f\bigl(\vec{W}_{xh}\, x_t + \vec{W}_{hh}\, \vec{h}_{t-1} + \vec{b}_{\vec{h}}\bigr)
$$

**Logic of the transition**: The new hidden state $\vec{h}_t$ is a *non-linear function* $f$ (typically $\tanh$ or ReLU) applied to an **affine combination** of:
1. The current input $x_t$ (weighted by $\vec{W}_{xh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_x}$),
2. The previous hidden state $\vec{h}_{t-1}$ (weighted by $\vec{W}_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_h}$),
3. A bias vector $\vec{b}_{\vec{h}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$.

### Step 3: Define the Backward Hidden Recurrence

For the backward direction, we move from $t=T$ to $t=1$:

$$
\overleftarrow{h}_t = f\bigl(\overleftarrow{W}_{xh}\, x_t + \overleftarrow{W}_{hh}\, \overleftarrow{h}_{t+1} + \overleftarrow{b}_{\overleftarrow{h}}\bigr)
$$

**Logic of the transition**: Note the recurrence index $t+1$ instead of $t-1$. This is the *defining feature* of the backward pass — it conditions the current state on the **future** state. The boundary condition is $\overleftarrow{h}_{T+1} = \vec{0}$.

### Step 4: Concatenation of the Two Streams

At each time step, fuse the forward and backward states:

$$
h_t = \begin{bmatrix} \vec{h}_t \\ \overleftarrow{h}_t \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 d_h}
$$

**Logic**: Concatenation preserves all $2d_h$ units of information. An alternative is $h_t = \vec{h}_t + \overleftarrow{h}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ (element-wise sum), which is parameter-efficient but less expressive.

### Step 5: Compute the Output

$$
\hat{y}_t = g\bigl(W_{hy}\, h_t + b_y\bigr)
$$

where $g$ is the **output activation**:
- **Softmax** for multi-class classification
- **Sigmoid** for binary classification
- **Linear (identity)** for regression

### Step 6: Total Loss Function

For sequence-level supervised learning:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t\bigl(\hat{y}_t, y_t\bigr)
$$

Expanding with **cross-entropy** for classification:

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_t^{(k)} \log \hat{y}_t^{(k)}
$$

**Logic**: The total loss is the *temporal sum* of per-step losses. Each $\mathcal{L}_t$ measures the discrepancy between the predicted distribution $\hat{y}_t$ and the ground-truth one-hot label $y_t$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Gradient Flow in BPTT

> [!NOTE]
> **BPTT (Backpropagation Through Time)** on a BiRNN has **two** gradient paths per time step: one flowing *backward in time* through the forward pass, and one flowing *forward in time* through the backward pass. The matrix below maps this dual flow.

| Gradient | Flows Through | Update Rule (conceptual) | Direction of Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \vec{W}_{hh}}$ | Forward cells | $\sum_t \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \vec{h}_t} \cdot \dfrac{\partial \vec{h}_t}{\partial \vec{W}_{hh}}$ | Backward in time ($T \to 1$) |
| $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \overleftarrow{W}_{hh}}$ | Backward cells | $\sum_t \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \overleftarrow{h}_t} \cdot \dfrac{\partial \overleftarrow{h}_t}{\partial \overleftarrow{W}_{hh}}$ | Forward in time ($1 \to T$) |
| $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hy}}$ | Output layer | $\sum_t \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \cdot \dfrac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial W_{hy}}$ | None (direct) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vanishing/Exploding Gradient Risk:** Standard BiRNNs suffer from the same gradient pathologies as unidirectional RNNs. In practice, **BiLSTM** and **BiGRU** are preferred because their gated cell structures mitigate this. KTU examiners often award partial credit for mentioning this mitigation in long-answer questions.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Conceptual)

Suppose $T = 4$, $d_x = 3$, $d_h = 2$, $K = 2$. The total number of trainable parameters (ignoring biases) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{total} &= \underbrace{d_h(d_x + d_h)}_{\text{forward}} + \underbrace{d_h(d_x + d_h)}_{\text{backward}} + \underbrace{2d_h \cdot K}_{\text{output}} \\[4pt]
&= 2(3+2) + 2(3+2) + 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 \\[4pt]
&= 10 + 10 + 8 = 28 \text{ parameters}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic**: Forward layer has $d_h \times d_x + d_h \times d_h = 2 \times 3 + 2 \times 2 = 10$ weights. The backward layer contributes the same 10. The output layer maps $2d_h = 4$ hidden units to $K = 2$ classes, giving $4 \times 2 = 8$ weights. **Total = 28.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Unfolded BiRNN Architecture (Time-Unrolled View)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below shows the **time-unrolled** view of a BiRNN for an input sequence of length $T = 4$. Both forward and backward hidden chains are visible, and the merge (concatenation) operation at each time step is shown explicitly. All node IDs are alphanumeric per Mermaid safety rules.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FORWARD_CHAIN
        F0["FORWARD h0 = 0"]:::init
        F1["FWD CELL t1 h_fwd1"]
        F2["FWD CELL t2 h_fwd2"]
        F3["FWD CELL t3 h_fwd3"]
        F4["FWD CELL t4 h_fwd4"]
    end

    subgraph BACKWARD_CHAIN
        B5["BACKWARD h5 = 0"]:::init
        B4["BWD CELL t4 h_bwd4"]
        B3["BWD CELL t3 h_bwd3"]
        B2["BWD CELL t2 h_bwd2"]
        B1["BWD CELL t1 h_bwd1"]
    end

    subgraph INPUT_SEQUENCE
        I1["x1"]
        I2["x2"]
        I3["x3"]
        I4["x4"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER
        O1["y_hat1"]
        O2["y_hat2"]
        O3["y_hat3"]
        O4["y_hat4"]
    end

    F0 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> F4

    B5 --> B4
    B4 --> B3
    B3 --> B2
    B2 --> B1

    I1 --> F1
    I2 --> F2
    I3 --> F3
    I4 --> F4

    I1 --> B1
    I2 --> B2
    I3 --> B3
    I4 --> B4

    F1 --> O1
    F4 --> O1
    B1 --> O1
    B4 --> O1

    F1 --> O1
    F2 --> O2
    F3 --> O3
    F4 --> O4

    B1 --> O1
    B2 --> O2
    B3 --> O3
    B4 --> O4

    classDef init fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#b7950b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fwd fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bwd fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef inp fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#e8daef,stroke:#6c3483,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class F0,B5 init
    class F1,F2,F3,F4 fwd
    class B1,B2,B3,B4 bwd
    class I1,I2,I3,I4 inp
    class O1,O2,O3,O4 out
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The blue chain flows left-to-right (forward in time). The red chain flows right-to-left (backward in time). Both chains feed the green output layer at every time step, after merging. The yellow nodes are zero-initialized boundary conditions.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Data Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[INPUT SEQUENCE X of length T]:::input
    B[FWD WEIGHT MATRIX W_fwd]:::weights
    C[BWD WEIGHT MATRIX W_bwd]:::weights
    D[FORWARD RNN STACK]:::fwdblock
    E[BACKWARD RNN STACK]:::bwdblock
    F[CONCATENATION OR SUM]:::merge
    G[OUTPUT DENSE LAYER]:::outblock
    H[SOFTMAX OR SIGMOID]:::act
    I[OUTPUT Y_HAT]:::final

    A --> D
    A --> E
    B --> D
    C --> E
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I

    classDef input fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef weights fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#b9770e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fwdblock fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bwdblock fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef merge fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outblock fill:#e8daef,stroke:#6c3483,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#5b2c6f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architecture Insight:** Notice that the input $X$ is **shared** between the forward and backward stacks — this is the key data-flow invariant of a BiRNN. Both directions see the *same* input, but process its temporal ordering differently. This sharing is also what makes BiRNN parameter-efficient relative to processing the input twice in two separate networks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Bidirectional Recurrent Neural Network (BiRNN). Mention any two advantages of using BiRNN over a standard unidirectional RNN.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Bidirectional Recurrent Neural Network (BiRNN)** is a sequence-modeling architecture that consists of two independent recurrent hidden layers: a forward layer that processes the input sequence from $t=1$ to $t=T$, and a backward layer that processes the same sequence from $t=T$ to $t=1$. At each time step, the outputs of both layers are combined (typically via concatenation) to produce the final prediction.

**Two advantages over standard RNN:**
1. **Context from both directions:** The hidden state at time $t$ depends on both *past* and *future* tokens of the sequence, leading to richer representations — especially useful for sequence-labeling tasks such as Named Entity Recognition (NER) and Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging.
2. **Higher accuracy on offline tasks:** Since the entire sequence is available, BiRNNs can exploit global context and consistently outperform unidirectional RNNs on tasks like handwriting recognition, speech recognition (offline), and machine translation encoding.

> *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two advantages.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Why is a Bidirectional RNN not suitable for real-time streaming applications? Justify your answer with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A BiRNN requires the **entire input sequence** to be observed *before* it can produce an output at any time step $t$, because the backward pass depends on future elements ($t+1, t+2, \dots, T$). In a real-time streaming application, future elements are not yet available, making the backward hidden state undefined.

**Example:** In **live speech recognition** (e.g., real-time captioning of a lecture), the audio signal arrives incrementally. At the moment a phoneme is spoken, the system has no access to the phonemes that will be spoken in the next 200 ms. A BiRNN cannot compute its backward hidden state in this setting, so a unidirectional RNN or a streaming Transformer with causal masking must be used instead.

> *(Award 2 marks for the explanation, 1 mark for the example.)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Convention:** Within a module, students must answer **either Question A OR Question B** in full. Both questions below carry equal marks (7 + 7).

---

### **❓ Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q.A(a). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Draw and explain the architecture of a Bidirectional RNN. Derive the equations for the forward hidden state, backward hidden state, and the output at any time step $t$.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Architecture (3 Marks):**

A BiRNN consists of two parallel recurrent chains operating on the same input sequence:
- The **forward chain** processes the sequence from $x_1 \to x_2 \to \dots \to x_T$.
- The **backward chain** processes the same sequence in reverse: $x_T \to x_{T-1} \to \dots \to x_1$.

Both chains have their *own* sets of weights (no weight sharing between them). At every time step $t$, the forward hidden state $\vec{h}_t$ and the backward hidden state $\overleftarrow{h}_t$ are merged and passed to an output layer.

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Forward hidden state update:**

$$
\vec{h}_t = f\bigl(\vec{W}_{xh}\, x_t + \vec{W}_{hh}\, \vec{h}_{t-1} + \vec{b}_{\vec{h}}\bigr), \quad \vec{h}_0 = \vec{0}
$$

where $f$ is a non-linear activation (typically $\tanh$), $\vec{W}_{xh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_x}$, $\vec{W}_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_h}$, and $\vec{b}_{\vec{h}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$.

**Step 2 — Backward hidden state update:**

$$
\overleftarrow{h}_t = f\bigl(\overleftarrow{W}_{xh}\, x_t + \overleftarrow{W}_{hh}\, \overleftarrow{h}_{t+1} + \overleftarrow{b}_{\overleftarrow{h}}\bigr), \quad \overleftarrow{h}_{T+1} = \vec{0}
$$

**Step 3 — Merge (concatenation):**

$$
h_t = \begin{bmatrix} \vec{h}_t \\ \overleftarrow{h}_t \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 d_h}
$$

**Step 4 — Output computation:**

$$
\hat{y}_t = g\bigl(W_{hy}\, h_t + b_y\bigr)
$$

where $g$ is the output activation (softmax for classification). The forward and backward directions are computed independently and merged at the output.

> *(Award 2 marks for the diagram/explanation, 2 marks for the forward/backward equations, 2 marks for the merge + output equations, 1 mark for boundary conditions.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q.A(b). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Discuss how Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) is applied to train a BiRNN. What are the challenges in training, and how does a BiLSTM overcome them?**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**BPTT on BiRNN (3 Marks):**

Training a BiRNN requires summing the loss over all time steps and computing gradients with respect to all weight matrices. The total loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)
$$

Gradients are computed with respect to:
- $\vec{W}_{hh}$ by backpropagating **backward in time** through the forward chain ($T \to 1$).
- $\overleftarrow{W}_{hh}$ by backpropagating **forward in time** through the backward chain ($1 \to T$).
- $W_{hy}$ directly from the output layer at each time step.

The parameter update uses gradient descent (or a variant like Adam):

$$
\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta}
$$

**Training Challenges (2 Marks):**

1. **Vanishing/Exploding Gradients:** As the sequence length $T$ grows, gradients must be multiplied across many time steps. If the spectral radius of the recurrent weight matrix is less than 1, gradients vanish; if greater than 1, they explode. This makes BiRNNs hard to train on long sequences.
2. **Double Parameter Count:** BiRNN has roughly twice the parameters of a unidirectional RNN, increasing memory and computation cost.
3. **Long-Range Dependencies:** Standard $\tanh$ activations saturate, making it difficult to capture long-range context.

**How BiLSTM Solves This (2 Marks):**

**BiLSTM** (Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory) replaces the simple $\tanh$ cell with an **LSTM cell** that contains three gates:
- **Forget gate** $f_t$ — controls what to discard from the cell state.
- **Input gate** $i_t$ — controls what new information to store.
- **Output gate** $o_t$ — controls what to output from the cell state.

The **cell state $c_t$** acts as a "highway" with additive updates, allowing gradients to flow across many time steps without vanishing. Combined with the bidirectional structure, BiLSTM can capture long-range dependencies in both temporal directions, mitigating the vanishing gradient problem.

> *(Award 1.5 marks each for BPTT explanation, challenges, and BiLSTM solution.)*

---

### **❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q.B(a). [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Explain in detail the training procedure of a Bidirectional RNN using Backpropagation Through Time. Derive the gradient of the loss with respect to the forward weight matrix $\vec{W}_{hh}$.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Training Procedure Overview (2 Marks):**

The training of a BiRNN follows these steps:
1. **Forward pass** through both the forward and backward chains to compute $\vec{h}_t$, $\overleftarrow{h}_t$, and $\hat{y}_t$ for all $t$.
2. **Loss computation** by summing the per-step losses: $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)$.
3. **Backward pass** to compute gradients with respect to all weight matrices by applying the chain rule across time.
4. **Parameter update** using an optimizer (SGD, Adam, RMSprop).

**Gradient Derivation (5 Marks):**

We want to compute $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \vec{W}_{hh}}$. By the chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \vec{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \vec{W}_{hh}}
$$

Each $\vec{h}_t$ depends on $\vec{h}_{t-1}$, which in turn depends on $\vec{h}_{t-2}$, and so on. Therefore:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \vec{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial \vec{h}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{h}_t}{\partial \vec{h}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{h}_k}{\partial \vec{W}_{hh}}
$$

The crucial term is the **temporal Jacobian**:

$$
\frac{\partial \vec{h}_t}{\partial \vec{h}_k} = \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \vec{h}_j}{\partial \vec{h}_{j-1}}
$$

Each factor in the product is:

$$
\frac{\partial \vec{h}_j}{\partial \vec{h}_{j-1}} = \text{diag}\bigl(f'(\vec{a}_j)\bigr) \cdot \vec{W}_{hh}
$$

where $\vec{a}_j = \vec{W}_{xh} x_j + \vec{W}_{hh} \vec{h}_{j-1} + \vec{b}_{\vec{h}}$. Hence:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \vec{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial \vec{h}_t} \cdot \left[\prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}\bigl(f'(\vec{a}_j)\bigr) \cdot \vec{W}_{hh}\right] \cdot \vec{h}_{k-1}^{\top}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to include the $\vec{h}_{k-1}^{\top}$ term at the end of the derivation, or write the product in the wrong direction. The product iterates from $j = k+1$ to $t$ (not $t$ to $k+1$). Losing this term costs **2 marks** in a 7-mark question.

> *(Award 2 marks for the training procedure, 3 marks for the gradient chain rule expansion, 2 marks for the final closed-form expression.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q.B(b). [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Compare Bidirectional RNN, Bidirectional LSTM, and Bidirectional GRU in terms of architecture, parameter count, training time, and typical use cases. Justify which is most suitable for a long-sequence NLP tagging task with 512-token inputs.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Feature | BiRNN | BiLSTM | BiGRU |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cell Type** | Simple $\tanh$ / ReLU | LSTM with 3 gates | GRU with 2 gates |
| **Gates** | None | Forget, Input, Output | Update, Reset |
| **Cell State** | Single hidden state | Separate $c_t$ and $h_t$ | Combined in $h_t$ |
| **Params per cell** | $d(d + d_x + d_h)$ | $4 d(d + d_x + d_h)$ | $3 d(d + d_x + d_h)$ |
| **Long-range memory** | Poor (vanishing gradient) | Excellent | Good |
| **Training time** | Fastest | Slowest | Medium |
| **Accuracy on long seq** | Lowest | Highest | Medium-High |
| **Use case** | Toy/short sequences | Long sequence tagging | Real-time + moderate length |

**Justification for 512-Token NLP Tagging Task (3 Marks):**

For a long-sequence (512-token) NLP tagging task such as **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** or **POS tagging**, the most suitable choice is **BiLSTM**. The justification is as follows:

1. **Long-range context:** 512 tokens far exceeds the effective memory of a plain BiRNN, which suffers from severe vanishing gradients. BiLSTM's gating mechanism preserves information across hundreds of time steps.
2. **Bidirectional structure:** Since the entire sentence is available (offline tagging), the bidirectional structure ensures each token's representation incorporates both preceding and succeeding context — critical for disambiguating entity boundaries.
3. **Accuracy vs. speed tradeoff:** Although BiLSTM is slower than BiGRU, for tagging tasks the *accuracy* is paramount. BiGRU can be considered as a faster alternative, but BiLSTM remains the gold standard in 2024-era research.

> *(Award 2 marks for the table, 3 marks for the justification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Drawing only one direction:** Many students draw a unidirectional RNN and label it "BiRNN" — this is the #1 reason for losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing BiRNN with deep (stacked) RNN:** A *stacked* RNN has multiple layers in the *same* direction. A BiRNN has *two* layers in *opposite* directions. Do not interchange these terms.
> 3. **Forgetting boundary conditions:** $\vec{h}_0 = \vec{0}$ and $\overleftarrow{h}_{T+1} = \vec{0}$ are frequently tested as 1-mark sub-questions. Memorize them.
> 4. **Stating BiRNN works for streaming:** This is a classic wrong answer. The correct statement is: BiRNN is *offline-only* and *cannot* be used for real-time prediction.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — BiRNN**

- ✅ **Definition:** BiRNN = Forward RNN + Backward RNN on the same input, merged at each time step.
- ✅ **Two weight sets:** $\vec{W}_{hh}$ and $\overleftarrow{W}_{hh}$ are **distinct** — no weight sharing between directions.
- ✅ **Four core equations to memorize:**
  1. $\vec{h}_t = f(\vec{W}_{xh} x_t + \vec{W}_{hh} \vec{h}_{t-1} + \vec{b}_{\vec{h}})$
  2. $\overleftarrow{h}_t = f(\overleftarrow{W}_{xh} x_t + \overleftarrow{W}_{hh} \overleftarrow{h}_{t+1} + \overleftarrow{b}_{\overleftarrow{h}})$
  3. $h_t = [\vec{h}_t; \overleftarrow{h}_t]$ (concatenation)
  4. $\hat{y}_t = g(W_{hy} h_t + b_y)$ (output)
- ✅ **Boundary conditions:** $\vec{h}_0 = \vec{0}$, $\overleftarrow{h}_{T+1} = \vec{0}$.
- ✅ **Loss function:** $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_t^{(k)} \log \hat{y}_t^{(k)}$.
- ✅ **Parameter count:** Approximately **double** that of a unidirectional RNN.
- ✅ **Training:** Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) with two gradient paths (forward-backward and backward-forward in time).
- ✅ **Constraint:** Requires the **full sequence** to be available — **NOT** suitable for real-time/streaming.
- ✅ **Best applications:** NER, POS tagging, handwriting recognition, machine translation (encoder), offline speech recognition.
- ✅ **Variants in practice:** **BiLSTM** (most popular), **BiGRU** (faster alternative), **Stacked BiRNN** (multiple BiRNN layers stacked vertically).
- ✅ **Vanishing gradient mitigation:** Use BiLSTM or BiGRU with gating instead of plain BiRNN for sequences > 50 tokens.
- ✅ **Difference from Encoder–Decoder:** BiRNN is a *sequence-to-sequence* model with the same length, while Encoder–Decoder can change sequence length.
- ✅ **Difference from Stacked RNN:** Stacked = *depth* in same direction; BiRNN = *breadth* in two directions. They are orthogonal and can be **combined** (Stacked BiLSTM).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "BFF":** **B**iRNN = **B**oth **F**orward + backward **F**usion. Remember this and you will not forget the architecture!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Encoder-Decoder Sequence-to-Sequence Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT745 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Encoder-Decoder Sequence-to-Sequence Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Encoder-Decoder Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq) Model:** A neural network architecture designed to transform an input sequence of arbitrary length into an output sequence of arbitrary length. It consists of two coupled recurrent networks: an **Encoder** that compresses the input sequence into a fixed-dimensional **context vector** (thought vector) and a **Decoder** that generates the output sequence conditioned on this context vector.

Mathematically, given an input sequence $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{T_x})$ and a target sequence $Y = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{T_y})$, the Seq2Seq model learns the conditional probability distribution:

$$p(Y \mid X) = \prod_{t=1}^{T_y} p(y_t \mid y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{t-1}, c)$$

where $c$ is the context vector produced by the encoder.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bilingual Human Translator:**
> Imagine a human translator sitting in a soundproof booth. They **listen** to an entire sentence in English (the encoder reads the whole source sequence and compresses meaning into a mental summary). Then they **start translating** word-by-word into Malayalam, constantly referring back to the mental summary and the words they have already produced (the decoder generates the target sequence step-by-step, conditioned on the context).
> 
> - The **encoder** = the human's "understanding" phase.
> - The **context vector** = the compressed mental summary of the source sentence.
> - The **decoder** = the "speaking/writing" phase producing the translation.
> - The **information bottleneck** = how the human may forget long sentences — this is the very problem that motivates **attention mechanisms** later.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Seq2Seq Applications

| Metric | Domain of Use | Brief Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **BLEU Score** | Machine Translation | n-gram overlap between predicted and reference translations (range 0 to 1) |
| **ROUGE-L** | Summarization | Longest common subsequence based recall |
| **Perplexity** | Language Modeling | Exponential of cross-entropy loss; lower is better |
| **Word Error Rate (WER)** | Speech Recognition | Edit distance normalized by reference length |
| **METEOR** | Translation | Harmonic mean of unigram precision and recall with synonyms |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU examination purposes, **BLEU score** and **Cross-Entropy Loss** are the most frequently tested metrics.

### 1.4 Applications (KTU 2024 Module 2 Coverage)

1. **Machine Translation** (English → Malayalam, English → French)
2. **Text Summarization** (long document → short abstract)
3. **Speech Recognition** (audio waveform → text transcript)
4. **Image Captioning** (CNN encoder + RNN decoder)
5. **Conversational Chatbots / Question Answering**
6. **Code Generation** (natural language description → source code)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture Breakdown — The Two Towers

#### 2.1.1 The Encoder

The encoder is typically a stack of **RNN / LSTM / GRU** cells that sequentially process the input tokens $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{T_x}$.

At each time step $t$, the hidden state is updated as:

$$h_t^{enc} = f_{enc}(h_{t-1}^{enc}, x_t)$$

where $f_{enc}$ is the recurrent cell function (e.g., LSTM or GRU). The final hidden state $h_{T_x}^{enc}$ is passed through a transformation to produce the context vector:

$$c = q(h_{T_x}^{enc}) = \tanh(W_c \cdot h_{T_x}^{enc})$$

- **Why?** The context vector $c$ is meant to summarize the *entire* input sequence into a single fixed-size representation. In the basic Seq2Seq model, all information from the source must pass through this bottleneck.

#### 2.1.2 The Decoder

The decoder is also a recurrent network, but it generates the output sequence one token at a time, conditioned on $c$ and the previously generated tokens.

$$s_t^{dec} = f_{dec}(s_{t-1}^{dec}, y_{t-1}, c)$$

The probability of emitting token $y_t$ at step $t$ is:

$$p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X) = \text{Softmax}(W_o \cdot s_t^{dec} + b_o)$$

- **How?** At inference, the token with the highest probability is selected (greedy decoding) or sampled from the distribution. At training, the ground-truth previous token is fed (teacher forcing).

### 2.2 Variants of Encoder-Decoder Models

| Variant | Encoder | Decoder | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Basic Seq2Seq (Sutskever et al., 2014)** | LSTM | LSTM | Single context vector bottleneck |
| **Bidirectional Encoder** | BiLSTM | LSTM | Captures past + future context |
| **Stacked (Deep) Seq2Seq** | Multi-layer LSTM | Multi-layer LSTM | Greater representational depth |
| **Attention-based Seq2Seq** | BiLSTM | LSTM + Attention | Dynamic context per decoder step |
| **Transformer-based Seq2Seq** | Self-Attention | Masked Self-Attention + Cross-Attention | Pure attention, no recurrence |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists **all exam-critical equations** for the Encoder-Decoder Seq2Seq topic. Memorize the symbols and boundary conditions.

| # | Equation | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h_t^{enc} = f_{enc}(h_{t-1}^{enc}, x_t)$ | Encoder hidden state update | $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ |
| 2 | $c = q(h_{T_x}^{enc}) = \tanh(W_c h_{T_x}^{enc})$ | Context vector from final state | $c \in \mathbb{R}^{d_c}$ |
| 3 | $s_t^{dec} = f_{dec}(s_{t-1}^{dec}, y_{t-1}, c)$ | Decoder hidden state update | $s_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_s}$ |
| 4 | $p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X) = \text{Softmax}(W_o s_t^{dec})$ | Output probability distribution | $\sum_y p = 1$ |
| 5 | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log p(y_t^{*} \mid y_{<t}^{*}, X)$ | Cross-entropy loss (single sample) | $\mathcal{L} \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 6 | $BLEU = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | BLEU score with brevity penalty | $BP = \min\left(1, e^{1 - \frac{r}{c}}\right)$ |
| 7 | $\text{Perplexity} = \exp\left(\frac{1}{T_y}\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} -\log p(y_t^{*} \mid \cdot)\right)$ | Language model perplexity | Lower is better |
| 8 | $h_t^{LSTM} = (o_t \odot \tanh(C_t), f_t, i_t, \tilde{C}_t)$ | LSTM gating (compact form) | Forget/Input/Output gates |
| 9 | $r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t])$ | GRU reset gate | $\sigma$ = sigmoid |
| 10 | $z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t])$ | GRU update gate | $z_t \in [0,1]$ |

> **Notation key:** $W_o, W_c$ = learnable weight matrices; $b_o$ = bias vector; $y_t^{*}$ = ground-truth token; $p_n$ = n-gram precision; $r$ = reference length; $c$ = candidate length.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Google Translate (pre-Transformer era)** used deep stacked LSTMs with attention.
- **Speech-to-text systems** in mobile assistants use a CNN/BiLSTM encoder feeding an LSTM decoder.
- **Medical report generation** from X-ray images uses a CNN encoder (image features) + LSTM decoder (text).
- **Automatic subtitle generation** for videos is a direct Seq2Seq application.

### 2.5 Why Pure Encoder-Decoder Bottlenecks Fail

> [!WARNING]
> The basic Seq2Seq model forces **all** information from a variable-length input into a **single fixed-size vector** $c$. This is the *vanishing gradient of memory* problem. Long input sequences cause early tokens to be "forgotten" by the time the encoder reaches $T_x$. This motivated:
> 1. **Bidirectional encoders** (read input forwards + backwards)
> 2. **Attention mechanisms** (decoder dynamically looks back at all encoder states)
> 3. **Transformer architectures** (eliminate recurrence entirely)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cross-Entropy Training Objective

The goal of training a Seq2Seq model is to **maximize the log-likelihood** of the target sequence $Y^*$ given the input $X$, which is equivalent to **minimizing the negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy loss)**.

**Step 1 — Joint probability of the target sequence.**
By the chain rule of probability, the probability of the entire target sequence factorizes as:

$$p(Y^{*} \mid X) = \prod_{t=1}^{T_y} p(y_t^{*} \mid y_1^{*}, y_2^{*}, \dots, y_{t-1}^{*}, X)$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm.**
Logarithms convert products into sums, which is computationally and numerically more stable:

$$\log p(Y^{*} \mid X) = \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log p(y_t^{*} \mid y_{<t}^{*}, X)$$

**Step 3 — Negate to form a loss.**
Since optimization frameworks *minimize*, we negate:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\log p(Y^{*} \mid X) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log p(y_t^{*} \mid y_{<t}^{*}, X)$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of the softmax output.**
Let $o_t = W_o s_t^{dec} + b_o$ be the pre-activation logits. Then:

$$p(y_t^{*} \mid \cdot) = \frac{\exp(o_t[y_t^{*}])}{\sum_{j=1}^{V} \exp(o_t[j])}$$

Substituting:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \left[ o_t[y_t^{*}] - \log \sum_{j=1}^{V} \exp(o_t[j]) \right]$$

where $V$ is the vocabulary size. This is the **softmax cross-entropy** form that frameworks like PyTorch and TensorFlow optimize directly.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Gradient Flow (Backpropagation Through Time)

The decoder parameters $\theta_{dec} = \{W_o, b_o, W_{dec}\}$ receive gradient from the loss at every time step. By the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_o} = \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial o_t} \cdot \frac{\partial o_t}{\partial W_o} = \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} (\hat{y}_t - y_t^{*}) \cdot (s_t^{dec})^{T}$$

where $\hat{y}_t = \text{Softmax}(o_t)$ is the predicted distribution and $y_t^{*}$ is the one-hot ground truth.

For the recurrent parameters $W_{dec}$, the gradient must be **unrolled through time**:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{dec}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial s_t^{dec}} \cdot \frac{\partial s_t^{dec}}{\partial s_k^{dec}} \cdot \frac{\partial s_k^{dec}}{\partial W_{dec}}$$

The term $\frac{\partial s_t^{dec}}{\partial s_k^{dec}}$ involves repeated multiplication of the Jacobian $J = \frac{\partial s}{\partial h}$ and is what causes **vanishing/exploding gradients** in long sequences. This is the mathematical origin of the *long-sequence forgetting problem*.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Teacher Forcing Ratio

During training, two regimes are commonly mixed:

- **Teacher forcing (probability $p_{tf}$):** feed the **ground-truth** previous token $y_{t-1}^{*}$ into the decoder.
- **Free-running (probability $1 - p_{tf}$):** feed the decoder's **own prediction** $\hat{y}_{t-1}$.

The expected input embedding is:

$$e_{t-1}^{in} = p_{tf} \cdot E[y_{t-1}^{*}] + (1 - p_{tf}) \cdot E[\hat{y}_{t-1}]$$

where $E[\cdot]$ is the embedding lookup. Typical values: $p_{tf} = 0.5$ to $1.0$ initially, then decayed (Scheduled Sampling).

---

### 3.4 Derivation of BLEU Score (for Exam Reference)

For unigram precision ($n=1$):

$$p_1 = \frac{\text{Count}_{\text{clip}}(\text{unigram}_{\text{candidate}})}{\text{Count}(\text{unigram}_{\text{candidate}})}$$

where $\text{Count}_{\text{clip}}$ caps each unigram's count at its maximum frequency in any reference translation.

For brevity penalty $BP$:

$$BP = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ \exp\left(1 - \frac{r}{c}\right) & \text{if } c \le r \end{cases}$$

Final BLEU:

$$BLEU = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right), \quad w_n = \frac{1}{N}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Summary

| Property | Basic Seq2Seq | Seq2Seq + Attention | Transformer Seq2Seq |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bottleneck | Single vector $c$ | Dynamic per-step $c_t$ | No bottleneck (full attention) |
| Long-sequence performance | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| Sequential computation | Yes (slow) | Yes (slow) | No (parallelizable) |
| Training stability | Moderate (LSTM helps) | Good | Requires warmup + LayerNorm |
| Typical use | Legacy systems | Pre-2017 SOTA | Modern SOTA |
| Number of parameters | Low | Medium | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Encoder-Decoder Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INP["Input Sequence X"]
        x1["x1"]
        x2["x2"]
        x3["x3"]
        x4["x4 EOS"]
    end

    subgraph ENC["Encoder LSTM Stack"]
        e1["h1 enc"]
        e2["h2 enc"]
        e3["h3 enc"]
        e4["h4 enc"]
    end

    subgraph CTX["Context Vector"]
        cv["c = tanh Wc h4"]
    end

    subgraph DEC["Decoder LSTM Stack"]
        d1["s1 dec"]
        d2["s2 dec"]
        d3["s3 dec"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["Output Sequence Y"]
        y1["y1 BOS"]
        y2["y2"]
        y3["y3 EOS"]
    end

    x1 --> e1
    x2 --> e2
    x3 --> e3
    x4 --> e4
    e1 --> e2
    e2 --> e3
    e3 --> e4
    e4 --> cv
    cv --> d1
    cv --> d2
    cv --> d3
    d1 --> d2
    d2 --> d3
    d1 --> y1
    d2 --> y2
    d3 --> y3
    y1 -. feed back .-> d1
    y2 -. feed back .-> d2
```

**Reading guide:** The encoder consumes the input left-to-right. Only the **final hidden state** $h_4^{enc}$ is transformed into the context vector $c$. The decoder is initialized from $c$ and generates tokens autoregressively, feeding each prediction back as the next input.

---

### 4.2 Training vs. Inference Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TR["Training Phase"]
        T1["Input X"] --> T2["Encoder forward pass"]
        T2 --> T3["Context vector c"]
        T3 --> T4["Decoder initialized with c"]
        T5["Ground truth Y star"] --> T6["Teacher forcing input"]
        T6 --> T4
        T4 --> T7["Predicted Y hat"]
        T7 --> T8["Cross entropy loss L"]
        T5 --> T8
        T8 --> T9["Backprop through time BPTT"]
        T9 --> T10["Update encoder and decoder weights"]
    end

    subgraph INF["Inference Phase"]
        I1["Input X"] --> I2["Encoder forward pass"]
        I2 --> I3["Context vector c"]
        I3 --> I4["Decoder step t equals 1 with BOS token"]
        I4 --> I5["Argmax or beam search token"]
        I5 --> I6["Append to output"]
        I6 --> I7{"End token EOS"}
        I7 -- No --> I4
        I7 -- Yes --> I8["Final output sequence Y hat"]
    end
```

**Key difference:** During training we use **teacher forcing** (parallel, stable gradients), while at inference we must **autoregressively** feed predictions back, which is slow and can compound errors (exposure bias).

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Attention-Augmented)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BE["Bidirectional Encoder"]
        BE1["Forward LSTM"]
        BE2["Backward LSTM"]
        BE1 --> BE3["Concat h forward and h backward"]
        BE2 --> BE3
    end

    subgraph ATT["Attention Module"]
        AT1["Compute alignment scores e sub t sub s"]
        AT2["Softmax over source positions"]
        AT3["Weighted sum produces c sub t"]
        AT1 --> AT2 --> AT3
    end

    subgraph DD["Decoder"]
        DD1["LSTM cell"]
        DD2["Concatenate y sub t minus 1 and c sub t"]
        DD3["Output projection and softmax"]
        DD1 --> DD2 --> DD3
    end

    BE3 --> AT1
    DD1 --> AT1
    AT3 --> DD2
```

**Block-level meaning:** This is the standard **Bahdanau (additive) attention** variant. Each decoder step now gets its **own** context vector $c_t$, dynamically computed as a weighted sum of **all** encoder states — eliminating the bottleneck.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1.** *Define the Encoder-Decoder Sequence-to-Sequence model. List any two of its real-world applications.*

**Model Answer:**
The Encoder-Decoder Seq2Seq model is a neural architecture that maps a variable-length input sequence $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{T_x})$ to a variable-length output sequence $Y = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{T_y})$ using two coupled recurrent networks. The **encoder** compresses $X$ into a fixed-dimensional context vector $c$, and the **decoder** generates $Y$ conditioned on $c$.
**Applications:** (i) Machine Translation (e.g., English → Malayalam), (ii) Text Summarization, (iii) Speech Recognition, (iv) Image Captioning. *(Any two, 1 mark each; definition 1 mark)*

---

**Q2.** *What is the role of the context vector in a Seq2Seq model? State its mathematical form.*

**Model Answer:**
The context vector $c$ is a fixed-dimensional summary of the entire input sequence produced by the encoder. It serves as the **only bridge** of information from encoder to decoder, conditioning all output token generations. Mathematically:

$$c = q(h_{T_x}^{enc}) = \tanh(W_c \, h_{T_x}^{enc} + b_c)$$

where $h_{T_x}^{enc}$ is the encoder's final hidden state and $W_c, b_c$ are learnable parameters. *(Definition 1.5 marks, equation 1.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Instructions per KTU 2024 ESE pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Encoder-Decoder architecture** for a Sequence-to-Sequence model used in Machine Translation. Clearly label the encoder, context vector, decoder, and the input/output sequences. Discuss the role of teacher forcing during training. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **cross-entropy loss** function used to train a Seq2Seq model. Show how the loss decomposes over time steps and explain why minimizing this loss is equivalent to maximizing the likelihood of the target sequence. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

1. **Architecture diagram (3 marks):** Draw the encoder LSTM stack processing $x_1, \dots, x_{T_x}$, the context vector $c$ tapped from $h_{T_x}^{enc}$, and the decoder LSTM initialized from $c$ emitting $y_1, \dots, y_{T_y}$. (Use a diagram similar to Section 4.1.)
2. **Working explanation (2.5 marks):** The encoder reads the source sentence (e.g., English) token by token, updating its hidden state. The final state is projected to $c$. The decoder, initialized with $c$ and a `<BOS>` token, generates the target sentence (e.g., Malayalam) one token at a time, with each prediction fed back as the next input.
3. **Teacher forcing (1.5 marks):** During training, instead of feeding the decoder's own prediction $\hat{y}_{t-1}$, the ground-truth token $y_{t-1}^{*}$ is supplied as input. This stabilizes training, parallelizes computation, and prevents early error propagation.

**Model Solution to (b):**

1. **Start from joint probability (2 marks):** The likelihood of the target sequence is $p(Y^{*} \mid X) = \prod_{t=1}^{T_y} p(y_t^{*} \mid y_{<t}^{*}, X)$.
2. **Apply log (1.5 marks):** $\log p(Y^{*} \mid X) = \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log p(y_t^{*} \mid y_{<t}^{*}, X)$.
3. **Negate to form loss (2 marks):** $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log p(y_t^{*} \mid y_{<t}^{*}, X)$.
4. **Substitute softmax (1.5 marks):** $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y}\left[o_t[y_t^{*}] - \log \sum_j \exp(o_t[j])\right]$.
5. **Equivalence to MLE (1 mark):** Minimizing $-\log p$ is mathematically identical to maximizing $\log p$, i.e., maximum likelihood estimation.

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Restructured]**

**(a)** Explain the **limitations of the basic Encoder-Decoder model** when handling long input sequences. How does the **attention mechanism** address these limitations? Provide the mathematical formulation of attention weights. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **LSTM-based** and **GRU-based** Seq2Seq encoders. Discuss which is more suitable for low-resource (small dataset) translation tasks and justify your answer. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

1. **Bottleneck problem (2 marks):** All source information is compressed into a single vector $c$. For long sentences, $c$ cannot retain all details, leading to degradation in translation quality and "forgetting" of early tokens.
2. **Gradient issues (1.5 marks):** Long unrolled sequences suffer from vanishing gradients during BPTT, making the encoder hard to train.
3. **Attention remedy (2 marks):** Instead of a single $c$, the decoder computes a **dynamic context** $c_t$ at every step $t$ as a weighted sum of **all** encoder hidden states $\{h_s^{enc}\}_{s=1}^{T_x}$.
4. **Mathematical formulation (1.5 marks):** Alignment score $e_{ts} = v^T \tanh(W_a s_{t-1}^{dec} + U_a h_s^{enc})$; attention weights $\alpha_{ts} = \frac{\exp(e_{ts})}{\sum_{s'} \exp(e_{ts'})}$; context $c_t = \sum_s \alpha_{ts} h_s^{enc}$.

**Model Solution to (b):**

| Criterion | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|
| Gating | 3 gates (forget, input, output) + cell state | 2 gates (reset, update) |
| Parameters | More (~4× hidden size) | Fewer (~3× hidden size) |
| Long-range memory | Excellent (cell state highway) | Good (update gate merge) |
| Training speed | Slower | Faster |
| Small-data fit | Tends to overfit | Generalizes better |

**Conclusion (for low-resource translation, 2 marks):** **GRU is preferred** because it has fewer parameters (less overfitting risk), trains faster (fewer gradient paths), and empirically performs comparably to LSTM on small corpora. LSTM is preferred when very long sequences and maximum representational power are required.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places KTU students lose marks in Seq2Seq questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `<BOS>` and `<EOS>` tokens** in the diagram — these are mandatory and worth 1 mark each in board valuation.
> 2. **Confusing the direction of teacher forcing** — students often write that the *prediction* is fed back during training, which is **wrong**. Teacher forcing feeds the **ground truth**.
> 3. **Writing cross-entropy without the softmax denominator** — the full form is $-\sum [o_{y^*} - \log \sum_j e^{o_j}]$, not just $-o_{y^*}$.
> 4. **Not drawing the recurrence arrows** in the encoder/decoder — the diagram must clearly show that each step receives the previous hidden state.
> 5. **Mixing up "BLEU" and "Perplexity"** — BLEU is a *translation* metric (higher is better, range 0–1), Perplexity is a *language model* metric (lower is better).
> 6. **Skipping the brevity penalty** in BLEU derivations — many students forget the $BP$ term entirely.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Seq2Seq = Encoder + Context Vector + Decoder**, typically LSTM/GRU based.
- The **context vector** $c$ is the *only* information pathway from encoder to decoder in the basic model.
- The **decoder is autoregressive** — each prediction becomes the next input during inference.
- **Training** uses **teacher forcing** (feed ground truth); **inference** uses **free-running** (feed predictions).
- **Loss function** = softmax cross-entropy summed over all decoder time steps.
- **Metric for translation** = **BLEU score** (with brevity penalty $BP$).
- **Metric for language modeling** = **Perplexity** = $\exp(\mathcal{L}/T_y)$.
- **Key limitation** = information bottleneck of single context vector for long sequences.
- **Solution** = **attention mechanism** (dynamic per-step context $c_t$) or **Transformer** (no recurrence).
- **LSTM** has 3 gates + cell state; **GRU** has 2 gates (reset, update) — GRU is leaner.
- **Bidirectional encoder** reads input forwards AND backwards, capturing richer context.
- **Beam search** (width $k$) is preferred over greedy decoding at inference for better translation quality.
- **Exposure bias** is the train–test mismatch caused by teacher forcing; mitigated by **scheduled sampling**.
- **Word embeddings** (Word2Vec, GloVe, or learned) are used to convert tokens to dense vectors before feeding the encoder.
- **EOS** terminates generation; **BOS** initializes the decoder; **UNK** handles out-of-vocabulary tokens.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LSTM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT745 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** LSTM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** is a specialized variant of Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) introduced by **Sepp Hochreiter and Jürgen Schmidhuber (1997)** that is designed to overcome the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** inherent in standard RNNs. It achieves this by introducing a **memory cell** regulated by three multiplicative **gating mechanisms** (forget, input, and output gates), enabling the network to selectively retain or discard information over long temporal sequences.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** LSTMs are categorized under *Advanced Recurrent Architectures* in Module 2. Students must be able to derive gate equations, explain the cell state flow, and differentiate LSTM from standard RNN and GRU.

### Formal Mathematical Form

An LSTM unit at time step $t$ maintains two state vectors:

- **Cell state** $C_t$ (long-term memory)
- **Hidden state** $h_t$ (short-term/working memory)

The network is parameterized by weight matrices $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o$ and bias vectors $b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o$ for the respective forget, input, candidate, and output gates.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The "Selective Secretary"**
>
> Imagine a busy executive's personal secretary who manages a huge filing cabinet (the **cell state $C_t$**). Every day, new documents (new input $x_t$) arrive on the desk. The secretary must decide:
> 1. **Which old files to throw away?** → This is the **Forget Gate**
> 2. **Which new information is worth filing?** → This is the **Input Gate**
> 3. **What to tell the executive right now?** → This is the **Output Gate**
>
> The executive only needs the *summary* (hidden state $h_t$), not the entire cabinet. The secretary intelligently filters what is important for long-term decisions versus what is needed *right now*. This is precisely how an LSTM cell operates — it filters, retains, and exposes information selectively.

## 1.3 Why LSTM Was Invented

| Problem in Standard RNN | LSTM Solution |
|---|---|
| Vanishing gradients over long sequences | Additive cell state update preserves gradient flow |
| Difficulty remembering long-term dependencies | Dedicated long-term memory vector $C_t$ |
| Uncontrolled information flow | Three learnable gating mechanisms |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** The cell state $C_t$ in an LSTM has an **additive** update rule (rather than purely multiplicative as in standard RNNs), which forms a **gradient highway** allowing error signals to flow backward through hundreds of time steps with minimal decay.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Gates — Operational Logic

An LSTM cell processes information in **four sequential logical steps**. Each gate is a sigmoid neural network layer outputting values in the range $[0, 1]$, where:

- **0** → "Let nothing through"
- **1** → "Let everything through"
- **Intermediate values** → "Let a fractional amount through"

### Step 1: The Forget Gate $f_t$

**Role:** Decides what information to **discard** from the previous cell state.

**Inputs considered:** Previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ and current input $x_t$.

**Logic:** The sigmoid layer looks at $h_{t-1}$ and $x_t$ and outputs a number between 0 and 1 for each number in the cell state $C_{t-1}$.

### Step 2: The Input Gate $i_t$ and Candidate Cell State $\tilde{C}_t$

**Role:** Decides what **new information** to store in the cell state.

**Two parts:**
- A sigmoid **input gate layer** $i_t$ that decides which values to update.
- A $\tanh$ layer that creates a vector of new candidate values $\tilde{C}_t$ that could be added to the state.

### Step 3: Cell State Update $C_t$

**Role:** Combines the old state (after forgetting) with the new candidate values (after input gating) to produce the new cell state.

### Step 4: The Output Gate $o_t$

**Role:** Decides what parts of the cell state to **output** as the hidden state $h_t$.

**Logic:** A sigmoid layer decides which parts of the cell state to output, then the cell state is passed through $\tanh$ (to push values to $[-1, 1]$) and multiplied by the output gate.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Component | Equation | Activation | Output Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Forget Gate** | $f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | $\sigma$ (sigmoid) | $[0, 1]$ | Discard irrelevant memory |
| **Input Gate** | $i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ | $\sigma$ (sigmoid) | $[0, 1]$ | Allow new memory |
| **Candidate State** | $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ | $\tanh$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Create new memory candidates |
| **Cell State Update** | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | None (linear) | $\mathbb{R}$ | Update long-term memory |
| **Output Gate** | $o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ | $\sigma$ (sigmoid) | $[0, 1]$ | Filter output |
| **Hidden State** | $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | $\tanh \cdot \sigma$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Working memory output |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** $\odot$ denotes the **Hadamard (element-wise) product**. $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ denotes **vector concatenation** along the feature dimension. All weight matrices $W \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (m+n)}$ where $m$ is the hidden dimension and $n$ is the input dimension.

## 2.3 Why the Cell State Is Special — The Gradient Highway

The key innovation of LSTM is the **additive update** of the cell state:

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

This is fundamentally different from standard RNNs where $h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t)$ involves a **purely multiplicative** recurrence. The additive structure creates a **gradient highway**:

$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(f_t) \approx I \text{ (when } f_t \approx 1\text{)}$$

When the forget gate is close to 1, the gradient flows **unattenuated** through the cell state — this is the mechanism that solves vanishing gradients.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | LSTM Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Natural Language Processing** | Machine translation, text generation, sentiment analysis |
| **Speech Recognition** | Acoustic modeling, voice assistants (Siri, Alexa) |
| **Time-Series Forecasting** | Stock price prediction, weather forecasting, energy load |
| **Healthcare** | ECG/EEG signal classification, patient monitoring |
| **Anomaly Detection** | Network intrusion, fraud detection in transaction sequences |
| **Music Generation** | Composing melodies, MIDI sequence modeling |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Forward Pass

We will derive the complete forward pass of an LSTM cell, step-by-step, with no skipped transitions.

### Setup: Notation and Dimensions

Let:
- $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ : input vector at time $t$, where $d$ is input dimension
- $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ : previous hidden state, where $h$ is hidden dimension
- $C_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ : previous cell state
- $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times (h+d)}$ : weight matrices
- $b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ : bias vectors
- $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ : sigmoid activation
- $\tanh(x) = \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}$ : hyperbolic tangent activation

### Step 1: Concatenation of Previous Hidden State and Current Input

The previous hidden state and current input are concatenated into a single vector:

$$
z_t = \begin{bmatrix} h_{t-1} \\ x_t \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{h+d}
$$

**Explanation:** This is a vector concatenation operation, combining the previous short-term memory $h_{t-1}$ with the new sensory input $x_t$ into a unified feature vector for the gates to process.

### Step 2: Forget Gate Computation

The forget gate determines which information from the past cell state to retain:

$$
f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot z_t + b_f)
$$

**Explanation:** A linear transformation $W_f z_t$ produces a vector in $\mathbb{R}^{h}$, then a bias $b_f$ is added, and the sigmoid squashes each element to $[0, 1]$. Values near 0 mean "forget this", values near 1 mean "keep this".

### Step 3: Input Gate Computation

The input gate decides which new values will be updated in the cell state:

$$
i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot z_t + b_i)
$$

**Explanation:** Identical functional form to the forget gate, but with different learnable parameters $W_i, b_i$. This gate effectively acts as a "filter" for the new candidate values.

### Step 4: Candidate Cell State Computation

A vector of new candidate values is created using a $\tanh$ layer:

$$
\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot z_t + b_C)
$$

**Explanation:** The $\tanh$ activation produces values in $[-1, 1]$, representing how strongly the new information should be pushed into the state. Note that this is **not gated** at this stage — the gating happens in the next step.

### Step 5: Cell State Update

The old cell state is updated by forgetting irrelevant parts and adding new candidate values:

$$
C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t
$$

**Explanation:** 
- $f_t \odot C_{t-1}$ : element-wise multiplication — selectively retains the old memory
- $i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ : element-wise multiplication — selectively admits new memory
- The two contributions are summed element-wise to produce the new cell state

### Step 6: Output Gate Computation

The output gate decides what to expose from the cell state as the hidden state:

$$
o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot z_t + b_o)
$$

**Explanation:** This gate acts as a "view filter", controlling which aspects of the long-term memory are relevant for the current time step's output.

### Step 7: Hidden State Output

The hidden state is computed as the gated, $\tanh$-squashed cell state:

$$
h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
$$

**Explanation:** The cell state is first squashed to $[-1, 1]$ by $\tanh$, then element-wise multiplied by the output gate. This produces the next hidden state, which is also the cell's output for time $t$.

## 3.2 Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) — Gradient Flow

To understand *why* LSTM solves vanishing gradients, let's derive the gradient of the loss with respect to past cell states.

### Loss Function

Let $L$ be the total loss over a sequence of length $T$:

$$
L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t
$$

### Gradient of $C_t$ w.r.t. $C_{t-1}$

Using the cell state update equation $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$:

$$
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(f_t) + \text{(other terms from } i_t, \tilde{C}_t\text{)}
$$

**Explanation:** The dominant term is $\text{diag}(f_t)$. This is a **diagonal matrix** where each diagonal entry is a value in $[0, 1]$. The gradient magnitude along the cell state chain is:

$$
\prod_{k=1}^{t} f_k
$$

If the forget gate activations $f_k$ are close to 1, this product remains near 1 — **gradient flows unimpeded**. This is the celebrated **constant error carousel** of LSTM.

### Total Gradient Through Time

The gradient of the loss w.r.t. cell state at time $k$ involves the chain:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial C_k} = \sum_{t=k}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial C_t} \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial C_j}{\partial C_{j-1}}
$$

Substituting the dominant term:

$$
\frac{\partial C_j}{\partial C_{j-1}} \approx \text{diag}(f_j)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial C_k} \approx \sum_{t=k}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial C_t} \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(f_j)
$$

**Explanation:** When $f_j \approx 1$ for all $j$, the product is $\approx I$ (identity), and the gradient signal is preserved across hundreds of time steps — directly addressing the vanishing gradient problem.

## 3.3 Variants and Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Variant | Gates | Cell State Output Coupling | Parameter Count | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Vanilla LSTM** | 3 (forget, input, output) | Separate $h_t$ and $C_t$ | $4(h^2 + hd + h)$ | General-purpose sequences |
| **Peephole LSTM** | 3 + peephole connections | Gates see $C_{t-1}$ directly | Slightly higher | Fine-grained timing tasks |
| **Coupled LSTM** | 2 (input = 1 - forget) | Single decision per unit | Reduced | Lower memory footprint |
| **GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)** | 2 (reset, update) | No separate cell state | $3(h^2 + hd + h)$ | Faster training, similar performance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LSTM Cell Architecture — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        xt["Current Input x_t"]
        ht1["Previous Hidden State h_t-1"]
    end

    subgraph CONCAT["CONCATENATION"]
        concat1["z_t = concat h_t-1, x_t"]
    end

    subgraph GATES["GATING MECHANISMS"]
        forgetGate["Forget Gate f_t = sigmoid W_f z_t + b_f"]
        inputGate["Input Gate i_t = sigmoid W_i z_t + b_i"]
        outputGate["Output Gate o_t = sigmoid W_o z_t + b_o"]
        candidate["Candidate State C_t_tilde = tanh W_C z_t + b_C"]
    end

    subgraph CELLSTATE["CELL STATE UPDATE"]
        multiply1["f_t elementwise C_t-1 : Forget Old Memory"]
        multiply2["i_t elementwise C_t_tilde : Admit New Memory"]
        sum1["C_t = f_t C_t-1 + i_t C_t_tilde : New Cell State"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        tanhOp["tanh of C_t : Squash to -1, 1"]
        multiply3["o_t elementwise tanh C_t : Final Hidden State"]
        ht["New Hidden State h_t"]
        Ct["New Cell State C_t"]
    end

    xt --> concat1
    ht1 --> concat1
    concat1 --> forgetGate
    concat1 --> inputGate
    concat1 --> candidate
    concat1 --> outputGate

    Ct1["Previous Cell State C_t-1"] --> multiply1
    forgetGate --> multiply1
    candidate --> multiply2
    inputGate --> multiply2

    multiply1 --> sum1
    multiply2 --> sum1

    sum1 --> Ct
    sum1 --> tanhOp
    outputGate --> multiply3
    tanhOp --> multiply3
    multiply3 --> ht
```

## 4.2 Information Flow Through Time — Unrolled LSTM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1["TIME STEP t-1"]
        cellA["LSTM Cell"]
        CA["C_t-1"]
        hA["h_t-1"]
        xA["x_t-1"]
    end

    subgraph T2["TIME STEP t"]
        cellB["LSTM Cell"]
        CB["C_t"]
        hB["h_t"]
        xB["x_t"]
    end

    subgraph T3["TIME STEP t+1"]
        cellC["LSTM Cell"]
        CC["C_t+1"]
        hC["h_t+1"]
        xC["x_t+1"]
    end

    xA --> cellA
    hA --> cellB
    CA --> cellB
    xB --> cellB
    hB --> cellC
    CB --> cellC
    xC --> cellC

    hB --> outB["Output y_t"]
    hC --> outC["Output y_t+1"]
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block: RNN vs LSTM Gradient Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RNN["VANILLA RNN - Multiplicative Gradient"]
        rnnFlow["h_t = tanh W_hh h_t-1 + W_xh x_t"]
        rnnGrad["Gradient: product of tanh derivatives and W_hh"]
        rnnProb["Problem: product shrinks to 0 exponentially"]
    end

    subgraph LSTM["LSTM - Additive Gradient Highway"]
        lstmFlow["C_t = f_t C_t-1 + i_t C_tilde_t"]
        lstmGrad["Gradient: dominated by diag of f_t"]
        lstmAdvantage["Advantage: if f_t approx 1, gradient preserved"]
    end

    RNN -->|Replaced by| LSTM
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In the first diagram, follow the **purple/blue** cell state path $C_{t-1} \rightarrow C_t$ — this is the "gradient highway". In the second diagram, observe how information flows **horizontally** through time via both $h_t$ and $C_t$ connections.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network. Why is it preferred over standard RNNs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

LSTM is a specialized recurrent neural network architecture introduced by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber (1997) to address the vanishing and exploding gradient problems of standard RNNs.

**Key features (1 mark each):**

1. **Memory cell with three gates:** LSTM introduces a cell state $C_t$ regulated by three gating mechanisms — forget gate $f_t$, input gate $i_t$, and output gate $o_t$ — that selectively control information flow.

2. **Additive cell state update:** The cell state is updated as $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$, which creates a gradient highway allowing error signals to propagate over long sequences without vanishing.

3. **Long-term dependency learning:** Unlike standard RNNs that struggle to remember information across hundreds of time steps, LSTMs can selectively retain relevant information for extended durations, making them ideal for tasks like language modeling, speech recognition, and time-series prediction.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List the three gates of an LSTM cell and state the activation function used by each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Gate | Purpose | Activation Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Forget Gate ($f_t$)** | Decides what to discard from previous cell state | Sigmoid $\sigma$ (outputs in $[0, 1]$) |
| **Input Gate ($i_t$)** | Decides what new information to store | Sigmoid $\sigma$ (outputs in $[0, 1]$) |
| **Output Gate ($o_t$)** | Decides what to output from cell state | Sigmoid $\sigma$ (outputs in $[0, 1]$) |

Additionally, the **candidate cell state** $\tilde{C}_t$ uses **hyperbolic tangent $\tanh$** activation (outputs in $[-1, 1]$). (1 mark for the table, 1 mark for the activation list, 1 mark for correctly identifying the candidate state activation.)

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of an LSTM cell. Label all gates and state vectors clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the forward pass equations for an LSTM cell, starting from the forget gate and ending with the hidden state output. Clearly define all symbols used. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The LSTM cell architecture consists of the following components (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

**Components and their roles (7 marks distributed as follows):**

1. **Cell state $C_t$ (1 mark):** The long-term memory vector that flows horizontally through time with minimal linear interactions. It carries information across many time steps.

2. **Hidden state $h_t$ (1 mark):** The short-term working memory output that is exposed to the next time step and used for predictions at the current step.

3. **Forget gate $f_t$ (1.5 marks):** Computed as $f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$. It controls which information from $C_{t-1}$ to discard.

4. **Input gate $i_t$ and candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ (2 marks):** Two parallel layers — a sigmoid input gate $i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ decides *what* to update, while a $\tanh$ candidate layer $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ creates the *new values* to be added.

5. **Output gate $o_t$ (1.5 marks):** Computed as $o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$. It controls which parts of the cell state to output as $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$.

**Diagram must show:** (i) three sigmoid gates, (ii) one $\tanh$ candidate layer, (iii) two $\odot$ (element-wise multiplication) operations, (iv) one $+$ (sum) operation, and (v) the final $\tanh$ on cell state for hidden state output. **Full 7 marks** requires a labeled diagram with all symbols defined.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Forget gate (1.5 marks)**

$$
f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)
$$

This sigmoid layer outputs values in $[0, 1]$ for each element of $C_{t-1}$, indicating how much of the old memory to retain.

**Step 2: Input gate (1.5 marks)**

$$
i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)
$$

A second sigmoid layer that decides which values in the candidate state to update.

**Step 3: Candidate cell state (1.5 marks)**

$$
\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)
$$

A $\tanh$ layer creating new candidate values in $[-1, 1]$.

**Step 4: Cell state update (1.5 marks)**

$$
C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t
$$

Old memory is element-wise multiplied by the forget gate, then new candidate memory is element-wise multiplied by the input gate and added.

**Step 5: Output gate and hidden state (1 mark)**

$$
o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)
$$

$$
h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
$$

**Symbol definitions (additional credit):** $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o$ are weight matrices; $b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o$ are bias vectors; $\odot$ denotes element-wise product; $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ denotes concatenation.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the vanishing gradient problem in standard RNNs. How does LSTM address it through its cell state mechanism? Derive the relevant gradient expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare LSTM with GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit) in terms of architecture, gating mechanisms, and computational complexity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Vanishing gradient problem (3 marks):**

In standard RNNs, the hidden state update is $h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t)$. During backpropagation through time (BPTT), gradients are computed as products of Jacobian matrices across time steps. The gradient of the loss $L$ with respect to an early hidden state involves a product of $\tanh$ derivatives (bounded by 1) and weight matrices. As $T$ grows, this product **exponentially shrinks** to zero, preventing the network from learning long-term dependencies.

**LSTM's solution via cell state (2 marks):**

The cell state update $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ is **additive**, not purely multiplicative. The gradient of $C_t$ w.r.t. $C_{t-1}$ is dominated by:

$$
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} \approx \text{diag}(f_t)
$$

**Derivation of gradient expression (2 marks):**

Differentiating $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ with respect to $C_{t-1}$:

$$
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(f_t) + \text{diag}(C_{t-1}) \frac{\partial f_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} + \ldots
$$

The dominant term is $\text{diag}(f_t)$. When the forget gate is close to 1, the gradient magnitude is preserved, forming the **constant error carousel** that allows long-term learning.

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Aspect | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of gates** | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (reset, update) |
| **State vectors** | Separate cell state $C_t$ and hidden state $h_t$ | Single hidden state $h_t$ |
| **Forget mechanism** | Explicit forget gate $f_t$ | Update gate $z_t$ controls retention |
| **Output control** | Explicit output gate $o_t$ | Implicit via reset gate $r_t$ |
| **Candidate state** | $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ | $\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h)$ |
| **State update** | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ |
| **Parameter count** | $4(h^2 + hd + h)$ | $3(h^2 + hd + h)$ |
| **Training speed** | Slower (more parameters) | Faster (~25% fewer parameters) |
| **Long sequences** | Generally better for very long dependencies | Competitive on shorter sequences |
| **Memory footprint** | Higher | Lower |

**Mark distribution:** 2 marks for architecture comparison, 2 marks for gating differences, 2 marks for equations, 1 mark for parameter count and conclusion.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Pitfalls in LSTM Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the candidate state:** Many students write the three gates but forget $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$. The candidate state is a **fourth essential component** — losing 1.5 to 2 marks.
> 
> 2. **Confusing activation functions:** Forgetting that gates use **sigmoid** $\sigma$ and the candidate state uses **tanh**. Mixing these up costs 1 mark.
> 
> 3. **Writing $C_t = f_t \cdot C_{t-1} + i_t \cdot \tilde{C}_t$ as scalar multiplication:** This is a **vector operation** — use $\odot$ (element-wise/Hadamard product). Examiners explicitly look for $\odot$.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the cell state in LSTM vs RNN comparison:** When asked "why LSTM over RNN", many students mention vanishing gradients but fail to derive the gradient expression. **Always show the gradient expression** $\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} \approx \text{diag}(f_t)$.
> 
> 5. **Missing the output gate in the hidden state equation:** The hidden state is $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ — students often write $h_t = \tanh(C_t)$ alone, losing 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Not labeling the diagram:** In architecture questions, every gate, arrow, and symbol must be labeled. A diagram without labels receives partial credit only.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Revision Checklist

- [ ] **LSTM stands for Long Short-Term Memory**, introduced by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber (1997)
- [ ] **Three gates:** Forget gate $f_t$, Input gate $i_t$, Output gate $o_t$ — all use **sigmoid** $\sigma$
- [ ] **Candidate state** $\tilde{C}_t$ uses **tanh** activation (this is the *fourth* component, not a gate)
- [ ] **Cell state update:** $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ — **additive**, this is the gradient highway
