regularization] DROP --> FC1[DENSE LAYER 1] FC1 --> FC2[DENSE LAYER 2] FC2 --> SOFT[SOFTMAX OUTPUT] SOFT --> LABEL[PREDICTED CLASS LABEL] style RAW fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000 style NORM fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style CONV1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000 style CONV2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000 style CONV3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000 style FLAT fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000 style DROP fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,color:#000 style FC1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style FC2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style SOFT fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000 style LABEL fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` This is the **universal block diagram** every modern CNN (LeNet, AlexNet, VGG, ResNet, EfficientNet) ultimately reduces to — the only thing that changes is the *number* of conv blocks, the *kernel sizes*, the *presence of skip connections*, and the *width* (channels per layer). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Define a Convolutional Neural Network. List its three primary building blocks with one-line descriptions.** **Model Answer:** A **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** is a deep feed-forward neural network designed to process grid-structured data (e.g., images) by employing the convolution operation in place of general matrix multiplication, exploiting **local connectivity**, **shared weights**, and **spatial pooling**. **Three primary building blocks:** 1. **Convolutional Layer** — Applies learnable $K \times K$ filters to extract local features from the input, producing a stack of feature maps. 2. **Pooling (Subsampling) Layer** — Down-samples feature maps spatially (e.g., $2 \times 2$ max-pooling) to reduce dimensionality and induce small-shift invariance. 3. **Fully Connected (Dense) Layer** — Performs high-level reasoning by connecting every neuron to the flattened feature vector, culminating in a softmax classifier for class probabilities. > *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the three blocks with one-line descriptions each.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is meant by "weight sharing" in a convolutional layer? Why is it important?** **Model Answer:** **Weight sharing** means that the *same* filter (i.e., the *same* set of $K \times K \times C_{in}$ weights and bias) is slid across all spatial positions of the input feature map to compute the output feature map. **Importance:** - **Parameter efficiency** — drastically reduces the number of trainable parameters (independent of input size). - **Translation equivariance** — a feature learned in one location is automatically detected everywhere. - **Statistical efficiency** — acts as a strong, built-in regulariser; the network must learn *position-independent* features. - **Computational speed** — fewer FLOPs and weights → faster training and inference, enabling deployment on edge devices. > *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for importance.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format) ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]** **(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **basic structure of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** using the LeNet-5 architecture. List the role of each layer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a conv layer with input size $W = 224$, kernel $K = 7$, padding $P = 3$, stride $S = 2$, compute: - (i) The output spatial size. - (ii) The number of trainable parameters if $C_{in} = 3$ and $C_{out} = 64$. - (iii) Compare the parameter count with a fully-connected layer producing the same output volume (assuming output spatial size from part (i) × 64). **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution (a):** **LeNet-5 Basic CNN Structure (refer Section 1.4 table and Section 4.1 mermaid diagram for diagram):** The LeNet-5 architecture (LeCun et al., 1998) consists of **7 trainable layers** (excluding pooling): | Layer | Role | |---|---| | $C_1$ (Conv) | Detects low-level features (edges, strokes) in $32 \times 32$ input. | | $S_2$ (Avg Pool) | Halves spatial dimensions; introduces small-shift invariance. | | $C_3$ (Conv) | Combines edges into motifs (corners, arcs) using 16 filters. | | $S_4$ (Avg Pool) | Further down-sampling to $5 \times 5$. | | $C_5$ (Conv) | Acts as a fully-connected layer producing 120 features. | | $F_6$ (FC) | Maps 120 features to 84 hidden units for high-level reasoning. | | Output (Softmax/RBF) | Classifies into 10 digit classes. | > *(7 marks: 2 for diagram + 3 for layer-wise role + 2 for design philosophy.)* #### **Model Solution (b):** **(i) Output Spatial Size:** $$O = \left\lfloor \frac{W - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{224 - 7 + 2 \cdot 3}{2} \right\rfloor + 1$$ $$O = \left\lfloor \frac{220 + 6}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 113 \right\rfloor + 1 = 114 \text{ pixels}$$ **(ii) Trainable Parameters in Conv Layer:** $$N_{conv} = (K \cdot K \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot C_{out} = (7 \cdot 7 \cdot 3 + 1) \cdot 64$$ $$N_{conv} = (147 + 1) \cdot 64 = 148 \cdot 64 = 9{,}472 \text{ parameters}$$ **(iii) Comparison with FC Layer:** A fully-connected layer mapping the input $W \times W \times C_{in} = 224 \times 224 \times 3$ directly to a flat $114 \times 114 \times 64$ output volume would require (per output neuron) all input pixels: - Input flat size: $224 \cdot 224 \cdot 3 = 150{,}528$ - FC weight count: $150{,}528 \cdot (114 \cdot 114 \cdot 64) + (114 \cdot 114 \cdot 64)$ — astronomically large ($\sim 1.0 \times 10^{11}$). **Compression Ratio:** $$\frac{N_{FC}}{N_{conv}} \approx \frac{1.0 \times 10^{11}}{9{,}472} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ (10 million times fewer parameters)}$$ This illustrates the **massive parameter efficiency** of convolutions, which is the central engineering reason for using CNNs over vanilla MLPs on images. *(7 marks: 2 for (i) + 2 for (ii) + 3 for (iii) with detailed comparison.)* --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Explain the **convolution operation** mathematically. How is the output feature map size determined from the input? Derive the formula and apply it to a $32 \times 32 \times 3$ input with a $5 \times 5$ kernel, stride 1, and padding 0. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain **max pooling** and **average pooling** with a suitable example. Why is pooling used in CNNs? Mention any **two** advantages and **one** disadvantage. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution (a):** **Mathematical Definition of Convolution (Discrete 2D):** Given an input image $X \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C_{in}}$ and a learnable kernel $W \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times K \times C_{in}}$, the output feature map entry at spatial position $(i, j)$ is: $$Y_{i, j, k} = \sum_{u=0}^{K-1} \sum_{v=0}^{K-1} \sum_{c=0}^{C_{in}-1} W_{u, v, c}^{(k)} \cdot X_{i+u, j+v, c} + b_k$$ where $k$ indexes the output channel (filter), $b_k$ is the bias, and $W^{(k)}$ are the *same* weights for all spatial positions (weight sharing). **Output Size Derivation** (full step-by-step is in Section 3.1): $$\boxed{\,O = \left\lfloor \frac{W - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1\,}$$ **Application:** $W = 32,\ K = 5,\ P = 0,\ S = 1$: $$O = \left\lfloor \frac{32 - 5 + 0}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = 28$$ Output feature map size = $28 \times 28 \times C_{out}$ (where $C_{out}$ is the number of filters). *(7 marks: 3 for math + 2 for derivation + 2 for numerical application.)* #### **Model Solution (b):** **Max Pooling** selects the **maximum** value within each $K \times K$ non-overlapping window: $$Y_{i, j} = \max_{u, v \in \text{window}} X_{i+u, j+v}$$ **Average Pooling** computes the **arithmetic mean** of the values in the window: $$Y_{i, j} = \frac{1}{K^2} \sum_{u, v \in \text{window}} X_{i+u, j+v}$$ **Example — Max Pooling on a $4 \times 4$ input with $2 \times 2$ window, stride 2:** | Input ($4 \times 4$) | Output ($2 \times 2$ Max) | |---|---| | $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 2 & 4 \\ 5 & 6 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 7 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 4 & 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} \max(1,3,5,6)=6 & \max(2,4,1,2)=4 \\ \max(0,7,2,4)=7 & \max(3,1,5,6)=6 \end{bmatrix}$ | **Why Pooling is Used:** 1. **Spatial Dimensionality Reduction** — drastically reduces the number of activations, lowering memory and compute. 2. **Translation Invariance** — small shifts in the input do not change the pooled output. 3. **Reduces Overfitting** — fewer features downstream = less prone to memorising noise. 4. **Larger Effective Receptive Field** — subsequent conv layers "see" more of the original image. **Two Advantages:** - Computational efficiency (fewer FLOPs in deeper layers). - Built-in regularisation effect. **One Disadvantage:** - Pooling is **not learnable** (in classical form) — may discard information critical to the task. Modern architectures like Capsule Networks and ResNet sometimes replace pooling with **strided convolutions** to keep the operation learnable. *(7 marks: 2 for definitions + 2 for example + 2 for "why" + 1 for advantages/disadvantage.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3 (CNN Basics):** > > 1. **Forgetting the "+1 bias" in parameter count.** Students write $(5 \cdot 5 \cdot 1) \cdot 6 = 150$ and lose 1 mark. Correct is **$156$**. Examiners specifically scan for the bias term. > > 2. **Confusing "padding" with "stride".** A common error: applying padding 0 in stride > 1 problems and producing a non-integer output. Always floor the result and verify dimensional consistency: $\text{output} = \lfloor \frac{W - K + 2P}{S} \rfloor + 1$ must be a positive integer. > > 3. **Skipping the role of the activation function.** Many students write "conv → pool → conv → pool" and forget that **ReLU sits between** conv and pool. Examiners expect: `Conv → ReLU → Pool`. Order matters for KTU boards. > > 4. **Calling pooling "a downsampling technique that learns weights."** Pooling has **zero** trainable parameters. If you claim it has weights, expect 1-mark cut. > > 5. **Writing the LeNet-5 filter sizes in reverse order** ($16$ in $C_1$, $6$ in $C_3$). The standard is $6$ first, $16$ later — LeCun's design is deliberate (sparse connectivity in $C_3$ to break symmetry with fewer parameters). > > 6. **Not distinguishing between "translation equivariance" (conv) and "translation invariance" (pooling).** Convolution is *equivariant* (output shifts when input shifts); pooling introduces *invariance* (output is robust to small shifts). This is a favourite 2-mark conceptual question. > > 7. **Mixing up "channel" terminology** — input image channels ($C_{in}$ = 3 for RGB) vs. feature-map channels (= number of filters = $C_{out}$). Label both clearly in your diagrams. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Basic CNN Structure** ### **Core Architecture Facts** - ✅ A CNN is a **deep, feed-forward** network using **convolution** as its core operation. - ✅ The **basic structure** = alternating **Conv → ReLU → Pool** blocks followed by **Fully Connected** layers and **Softmax** output. - ✅ LeNet-5 is the **canonical reference** for the "basic structure" — 2 conv layers, 2 pool layers, 2 FC layers, 1 softmax output, **~60 K parameters**. - ✅ The design philosophy: **shrink spatial size, grow channel depth** as we go deeper. ### **Layer Roles (Must Memorize)** - ✅ **Conv Layer:** Local feature extraction via learnable filters. - ✅ **ReLU:** Non-linearity injection; mitigates vanishing gradient. - ✅ **Pooling:** Spatial down-sampling; introduces invariance. - ✅ **FC Layer:** Global reasoning; classifier. - ✅ **Softmax:** Probability distribution over $C$ classes. ### **Formulas (Must Memorize — Table 2.2)** - ✅ Output size: $O = \left\lfloor \frac{W - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$ - ✅ Conv parameters: $N_{conv} = (K^2 \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot C_{out}$ - ✅ FC parameters: $N_{fc} = (N_{in} + 1) \cdot N_{out}$ - ✅ Receptive field (unit stride): $r_l = r_{l-1} + (K_l - 1)$ ### **Design Principles (Conceptual)** - ✅ **Local Receptive Fields** — biological inspiration from V1 cortex. - ✅ **Weight Sharing** — parameter efficiency, translation equivariance. - ✅ **Hierarchical Features** — edges → textures → parts → objects. - ✅ **Pooling Invariance** — robustness to small input shifts. ### **Common Examiner Triggers** - ✅ "*Why convolutions and not FC layers?*" → **Parameter efficiency, weight sharing, equivariance**. - ✅ "*Why ReLU and not sigmoid?*" → **Avoids vanishing gradient, cheaper, induces sparsity**. - ✅ "*Why does the channel depth increase as spatial size shrinks?*" → **Trade-off: spatial info vs. semantic info**. - ✅ "*Draw and explain LeNet-5.*" → Use the **7-layer table** + **layer-by-layer role description**. > **One-line takeaway:** *The basic CNN structure is a **hierarchical feature-extraction-and-classification pipeline** built on three pillars — local receptive fields, weight sharing, and spatial pooling — and its prototype LeNet-5 is the foundation on which every modern ConvNet (AlexNet, VGG, ResNet, EfficientNet) is built.*

topics.title: Training a Convolutional Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT745 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Training a Convolutional Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Training a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** is the iterative optimization procedure by which the learnable parameters of the network — namely the **convolutional kernels (filters)**, **biases**, and the **weights of the fully-connected (dense) layers** — are adjusted using a labelled dataset so as to minimize a predefined **loss function** $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$, where $\theta$ denotes the entire parameter set of the network.

The training procedure executes a repeating cycle of four canonical stages:

1. **Forward Propagation** — Input image $X$ is convolved, pooled, and passed through dense layers to produce a predicted output $\hat{Y}$.
2. **Loss Computation** — The discrepancy between the predicted output $\hat{Y}$ and the ground-truth label $Y$ is quantified by a loss function (e.g., **Categorical Cross-Entropy** for classification, **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** for regression).
3. **Backward Propagation (Backpropagation)** — The gradient of the loss with respect to every learnable parameter is computed using the **chain rule of calculus**.
4. **Parameter Update** — Parameters are updated using a gradient-based optimizer (e.g., **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)**, **Adam**, **RMSProp**) scaled by a **learning rate** $\eta$.

> [!NOTE]
> **One complete pass through the entire training dataset is called an *Epoch***. A *mini-batch* is a small subset of the dataset (typical size: **32, 64, 128, or 256 samples**) used to compute one gradient update. The number of gradient updates per epoch is $\lfloor N / B \rfloor$, where $N$ is the dataset size and $B$ is the batch size.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Apprentice Chef" Analogy

Imagine a young apprentice chef who has been given a thousand labelled photographs of pizzas and burgers and is told, "Figure out the difference."

- **The CNN is the apprentice.** Initially, his internal "rules" (weights and filters) are random — he cannot tell a pizza from a burger.
- **The Forward Pass is the apprentice's guess.** He looks at each picture and says, "I'm 60% sure this is a pizza."
- **The Loss Function is the head chef's stern rating.** The chef says, "Wrong! It was a burger. You were off by 1.4 units of confidence." (That 1.4 is the loss value.)
- **The Backward Pass is the chef's feedback.** The chef explains, "You focused too much on round shape and ignored the cheese-and-bun pattern. Next time, weight those features more heavily."
- **The Weight Update is the apprentice's self-correction.** He slightly adjusts his internal rules. After thousands of pictures and corrections, he becomes an expert pizza/burger classifier.

In mathematical terms, the chef's feedback is the **gradient** $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$, and the apprentice's self-correction is the parameter update $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$.

---

## 1.3 Key Metrics and Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\eta$ | Learning rate | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| $B$ | Mini-batch size | 32, 64, 128, 256 |
| $N$ | Total training samples | Dataset-dependent |
| $\lambda$ | L2 regularization coefficient | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| $\beta_1, \beta_2$ | Adam momentum coefficients | $0.9, 0.999$ |
| $\epsilon$ | Numerical stability constant (Adam) | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-8}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Momentum ($\mu$)** is a hyperparameter (typically **0.9**) used in SGD-with-momentum to smooth out noisy gradient updates by maintaining a running average of past gradients, analogous to the "inertia" of a heavy ball rolling down a loss landscape.

---

## 1.4 Why Training CNNs is Harder Than Training MLPs

Convolutional networks introduce three structural complexities that standard multi-layer perceptrons do not have:

1. **Weight Sharing** — The same kernel is slid across the entire input, so a single kernel parameter influences many output positions. The gradient must therefore be **summed across all spatial positions** during backpropagation.
2. **Spatial Downsampling** — Pooling layers reduce spatial dimensions but have no learnable parameters. The gradient must be **routed back** to the correct spatial locations through a *max-pooling mask* or averaged across pooling windows.
3. **Large Parameter Count with Limited Data** — A typical CNN has millions of parameters. Without **regularization** (Dropout, Batch Normalization, Data Augmentation, Weight Decay), severe **overfitting** occurs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline — The Four Stages of One Training Iteration

### Stage 1: Forward Propagation

Given an input tensor $X \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C_{in}}$ and a kernel $K \in \mathbb{R}^{k_h \times k_w \times C_{in}}$, the **discrete 2D convolution** (technically cross-correlation in deep-learning libraries) at spatial position $(i, j)$ and output channel $c$ is:

$$Z_{i,j,c} = \sum_{u=0}^{k_h - 1} \sum_{v=0}^{k_w - 1} \sum_{m=0}^{C_{in}-1} X_{i+u, j+v, m} \cdot K_{u, v, m, c} + b_c$$

This is followed by a non-linear activation $A_{i,j,c} = \sigma(Z_{i,j,c})$, where $\sigma$ is typically **ReLU**: $\sigma(z) = \max(0, z)$.

After the last pooling/convolution block, the feature map is **flattened** into a vector and passed through one or more **fully-connected (dense) layers**:

$$\mathbf{h} = \sigma(W_{fc} \cdot \text{flatten}(\mathbf{A}^{\text{last}}) + \mathbf{b}_{fc})$$

The final layer produces class scores $\mathbf{s}$, which are converted to probabilities via the **Softmax** function:

$$\hat{y}_k = \frac{e^{s_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{s_j}}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, K$$

where $K$ is the number of classes.

### Stage 2: Loss Computation

For a **multi-class classification** problem with one-hot encoded ground-truth label $\mathbf{y}$, the **Categorical Cross-Entropy Loss** is:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log(\hat{y}_k)$$

For a **batch of $B$ samples**, the average loss is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{batch}} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \mathcal{L}^{(b)}$$

Regularization is added to prevent overfitting:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{batch}} + \lambda \| \theta \|_2^2$$

### Stage 3: Backward Propagation (Backprop)

Backpropagation is simply the **chain rule of calculus** applied recursively from the loss to the earliest parameters. For a parameter $\theta^{(\ell)}$ in layer $\ell$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta^{(\ell)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(L)}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(L-1)}} \cdots \frac{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(\ell+1)}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(\ell)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(\ell)}}{\partial \theta^{(\ell)}}$$

where $\mathbf{a}^{(\ell)}$ is the activation output of layer $\ell$. The key insight: the gradient w.r.t. the **output of a layer** (the "error signal" $\delta^{(\ell)}$) can be computed in terms of the gradient w.r.t. the **output of the next layer** $\delta^{(\ell+1)}$. This is why backprop is so memory-efficient.

### Stage 4: Parameter Update

The most basic update rule is **Vanilla SGD**:

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}^{(t)}$$

**SGD with Momentum** smooths updates by maintaining a velocity vector $v$:

$$v^{(t+1)} = \mu v^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}^{(t)}$$

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} + v^{(t+1)}$$

**Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation)** maintains per-parameter adaptive learning rates:

$$m^{(t+1)} = \beta_1 m^{(t)} + (1 - \beta_1) \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}^{(t)}$$

$$v^{(t+1)} = \beta_2 v^{(t)} + (1 - \beta_2) (\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}^{(t)})^2$$

$$\hat{m} = \frac{m^{(t+1)}}{1 - \beta_1^{t+1}}, \quad \hat{v} = \frac{v^{(t+1)}}{1 - \beta_2^{t+1}}$$

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \frac{\hat{m}}{\sqrt{\hat{v}} + \epsilon}$$

---

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

Training CNNs is the engine behind nearly every modern computer-vision product: medical tumor segmentation, autonomous-vehicle perception, satellite imagery analysis, facial recognition, optical-character recognition, and industrial defect detection. The training procedure determines whether the deployed model is reliable, fair, and energy-efficient.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2D Convolution Output Size | $H_{\text{out}} = \lfloor (H - k_h + 2p) / s \rfloor + 1$ | $p$ = padding, $s$ = stride |
| 2 | Softmax | $\hat{y}_k = e^{s_k} / \sum_j e^{s_j}$ | $K$ classes |
| 3 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_k y_k \log \hat{y}_k$ | One-hot $y$ |
| 4 | Combined Softmax + CE Gradient | $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial s_k = \hat{y}_k - y_k$ | Elegant closed form |
| 5 | ReLU Derivative | $\sigma'(z) = 1$ if $z > 0$, else $0$ | Discontinuous at 0 |
| 6 | Max-Pool Gradient | Pass gradient only to argmax position | Others get 0 |
| 7 | Avg-Pool Gradient | Distribute gradient equally across window | $1 / (k_h k_w)$ per cell |
| 8 | Conv-Layer Gradient w.r.t. Kernel | $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial K_{u,v,m,c} = \sum_{i,j} \delta_{i,j,c}^{\text{conv}} \cdot X_{i+u, j+v, m}$ | Cross-correlation of input with output error |
| 9 | Conv-Layer Gradient w.r.t. Input | $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial X_{i,j,m} = \sum_{u,v,c} \delta_{i-u, j-v, c} \cdot K_{u, v, m, c}$ | Full convolution with flipped kernel |
| 10 | Bias Gradient | $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial b_c = \sum_{i,j} \delta_{i,j,c}$ | Summed over spatial positions |
| 11 | Vanilla SGD Update | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$ | Learning rate $\eta$ |
| 12 | Momentum Update | $v \leftarrow \mu v - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}; \quad \theta \leftarrow \theta + v$ | $\mu \approx 0.9$ |
| 13 | Adam Update | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \hat{m} / (\sqrt{\hat{v}} + \epsilon)$ | Bias-corrected moments |
| 14 | L2 Regularization | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}} = \mathcal{L} + \lambda \vert \theta \vert_2^2$ | Discourages large weights |
| 15 | Dropout (Inference) | Multiply activations by keep-prob $p$ | Scale-up at test time |
| 16 | Batch-Norm Normalize | $\hat{x} = (x - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}) / \sqrt{\sigma_{\mathcal{B}}^2 + \epsilon}$ | Per mini-batch |

> [!NOTE]
> **Equation 4** is one of the most exam-favoured identities in KTU papers: when Softmax is paired with Cross-Entropy, the gradient of the loss with respect to the *pre-softmax logits* is simply the **difference between predicted probability and the true one-hot label**. This avoids numerically unstable log-of-exp computations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In Equation 8, notice the **index shift** $(i+u, j+v)$: this reflects the fact that the same kernel parameter $K_{u,v,m,c}$ contributed to every output position it was "slid over." Hence the gradient must be summed over all such positions. This is the mathematical expression of **weight sharing** and is the single most counter-intuitive aspect of backprop in CNNs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Combined Softmax + Cross-Entropy Gradient

We want to compute $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial s_k$ where $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{j} y_j \log \hat{y}_j$ and $\hat{y}_j = e^{s_j} / \sum_m e^{s_m}$.

**Step 1** — Write the loss as a function of the softmax outputs:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j \log \hat{y}_j$$

**Step 2** — Differentiate $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to the logit $s_k$ using the quotient rule on the softmax. First, recall:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}_j}{\partial s_k} = \hat{y}_j (\delta_{jk} - \hat{y}_k)$$

where $\delta_{jk}$ is the Kronecker delta. This is the well-known **Jacobian of the softmax**.

**Step 3** — Apply the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial s_k} = -\sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j \cdot \frac{1}{\hat{y}_j} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_j}{\partial s_k}$$

**Step 4** — Substitute the Jacobian from Step 2:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial s_k} = -\sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j \cdot \frac{1}{\hat{y}_j} \cdot \hat{y}_j (\delta_{jk} - \hat{y}_k)$$

**Step 5** — Cancel $\hat{y}_j$ in numerator and denominator:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial s_k} = -\sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j (\delta_{jk} - \hat{y}_k)$$

**Step 6** — Split the sum into the case $j = k$ and $j \neq k$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial s_k} = -y_k (1 - \hat{y}_k) - \sum_{j \neq k} y_j (-\hat{y}_k)$$

**Step 7** — Use the fact that one-hot encoding means $y_k + \sum_{j \neq k} y_j = 1$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial s_k} = -y_k + y_k \hat{y}_k + \hat{y}_k \sum_{j \neq k} y_j = -y_k + \hat{y}_k (y_k + \sum_{j \neq k} y_j)$$

**Step 8** — Conclude:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial s_k} = \hat{y}_k - y_k$$

This beautiful closed form is what makes Softmax + Cross-Entropy the de-facto loss in classification CNNs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gradient of Loss with Respect to a Convolutional Kernel

Consider a single 2D convolution (no bias, no activation for clarity) producing feature map $Z_{i,j} = \sum_{u,v} X_{i+u, j+v} K_{u, v}$. Suppose the error signal at the output is $\delta_{i,j} = \partial \mathcal{L} / \partial Z_{i,j}$.

**Step 1** — Express the loss as a sum over all output positions:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i,j} \mathcal{L}(Z_{i,j}, \ldots) \quad \text{(locally, } \mathcal{L} \text{ depends on } Z_{i,j}\text{)}$$

**Step 2** — Differentiate w.r.t. kernel element $K_{u,v}$ using the chain rule. Each $K_{u,v}$ contributes to *all* output positions that the kernel touches:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial K_{u,v}} = \sum_{i,j} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Z_{i,j}} \cdot \frac{\partial Z_{i,j}}{\partial K_{u,v}}$$

**Step 3** — Compute $\partial Z_{i,j} / \partial K_{u,v}$ from the convolution definition:

$$\frac{\partial Z_{i,j}}{\partial K_{u,v}} = X_{i+u, j+v}$$

**Step 4** — Substitute:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial K_{u,v}} = \sum_{i,j} \delta_{i,j} \cdot X_{i+u, j+v}$$

**Step 5** — Re-index the sum with $i' = i+u, j' = j+v$ to obtain a familiar cross-correlation form:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial K_{u,v}} = \sum_{i', j'} \delta_{i' - u, j' - v} \cdot X_{i', j'}$$

This is the **cross-correlation of the input with the output error map**. This is precisely the convolution operation in deep-learning libraries (i.e., without kernel flipping).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Gradient Flow Through a Max-Pooling Layer

Max-pooling with window size $2 \times 2$ and stride 2 selects the maximum value in each window. Let $m$ be the index (within the $2 \times 2$ window) of the maximum element.

**Step 1** — Define the forward operation: out of four inputs $x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4$ in a window, the output is $y = x_m$ where $m = \arg\max(x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4)$.

**Step 2** — Differentiate: $y$ depends on only one of the four inputs, namely $x_m$. Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial x_k} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } k = m \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3** — Apply the chain rule on the upstream gradient $\delta_y = \partial \mathcal{L} / \partial y$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_k} = \delta_y \cdot \frac{\partial y}{\partial x_k} = \begin{cases} \delta_y & \text{if } k = m \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the "max-pooling mask" mechanism**: during the forward pass, we record the position $m$ of the maximum; during the backward pass, we pass the upstream gradient to *only that position*. All other positions in the pooling window receive zero gradient. This is why max-pooling is **not differentiable** in the classical sense — it has a sub-gradient — yet in practice backprop works perfectly via this mask-routing scheme.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — One Training Iteration

Consider a tiny CNN on a tiny problem to make the math concrete.

**Setup:**
- Input image $X = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$ (single channel, $1 \times 2 \times 2$)
- Kernel $K = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$ (single $2 \times 2$ filter)
- True label $y = 1$ (binary, sigmoid output $\hat{y}$)
- Learning rate $\eta = 0.1$
- Bias $b = 0$ (omitted for clarity)
- Loss: $\mathcal{L} = -[y \log \hat{y} + (1-y) \log(1 - \hat{y})]$ (binary cross-entropy)

**Step 1 — Forward pass:** Compute the convolution. Since the kernel matches the input size with no padding/stride, the output is a single scalar:

$$Z = (1)(0.5) + (2)(0.5) + (3)(0.5) + (4)(0.5) = 0.5 + 1.0 + 1.5 + 2.0 = 5.0$$

**Step 2 — Apply sigmoid:** $\hat{y} = \sigma(Z) = 1 / (1 + e^{-5}) \approx 0.9933$.

**Step 3 — Compute the loss:**

$$\mathcal{L} = -[1 \cdot \log(0.9933) + 0 \cdot \log(0.0067)] = -\log(0.9933) \approx 0.00673$$

The model is already very confident and correct, so the loss is small.

**Step 4 — Compute the gradient w.r.t. the pre-activation $Z$:** For sigmoid + binary cross-entropy, the gradient has the clean form:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Z} = \hat{y} - y = 0.9933 - 1 = -0.0067$$

**Step 5 — Compute the gradient w.r.t. the kernel element $K_{u,v}$:** Using Derivation 2 with a single output position ($i=0, j=0$):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial K_{u,v}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Z} \cdot X_{u,v} = -0.0067 \cdot X_{u,v}$$

So:
- $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial K_{0,0} = -0.0067 \cdot 1 = -0.0067$
- $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial K_{0,1} = -0.0067 \cdot 2 = -0.0134$
- $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial K_{1,0} = -0.0067 \cdot 3 = -0.0201$
- $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial K_{1,1} = -0.0067 \cdot 4 = -0.0268$

**Step 6 — Update the kernel** using gradient *descent* ($\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}$):

$$K_{0,0}^{\text{new}} = 0.5 - (0.1)(-0.0067) = 0.5 + 0.00067 = 0.50067$$

$$K_{0,1}^{\text{new}} = 0.5 + 0.00134 = 0.50134$$

$$K_{1,0}^{\text{new}} = 0.5 + 0.00201 = 0.50201$$

$$K_{1,1}^{\text{new}} = 0.5 + 0.00268 = 0.50268$$

The kernel values shift *toward* being larger in regions where the input was larger — exactly what we want, because larger inputs paired with a correct prediction should reinforce those weights.

> [!NOTE]
> The gradient is **negative** because the loss *decreases* if the weights *increase* (the model wants to be more confident in its correct prediction). The minus sign in vanilla SGD then moves the weights in the direction of *decreasing loss*, i.e., toward larger kernel values.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Gradient Flow Through Each Layer Type

| Layer Type | Forward Output | Backward Gradient w.r.t. Input | Backward Gradient w.r.t. Weights |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conv2D | $Z = X * K + b$ | $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial X = \text{full-conv}(\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial Z, \text{flip}(K))$ | $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial K = X * \partial \mathcal{L} / \partial Z$ |
| Max-Pool | $y = \max(\text{window})$ | Route $\delta$ only to argmax | None (no learnable params) |
| Avg-Pool | $y = \text{mean}(\text{window})$ | Distribute $\delta$ equally to all 4 cells | None |
| ReLU | $y = \max(0, x)$ | $\delta \cdot \mathbb{1}[x > 0]$ | None |
| Sigmoid | $y = 1/(1+e^{-x})$ | $\delta \cdot y (1 - y)$ | None |
| Fully Connected | $y = W x + b$ | $\delta \cdot W^T$ | $x \cdot \delta^T$ |
| Batch Norm | $\hat{x} = (x - \mu_B) / \sigma_B$ | Complex but well-defined | $\gamma, \beta$ updates |
| Dropout | $y = x \cdot \text{mask}$ | $\delta \cdot \text{mask}$ (rescaled at test) | None |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CNN Training Pipeline — End-to-End Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the complete training pipeline from raw data ingestion to weight updates, highlighting where forward, loss, backward, and optimizer stages interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Raw Labelled Dataset] --> splitB[Train Validation Test Split]
    splitB --> batchC[Random Mini Batch Sampling]
    batchC --> fwdD[Forward Pass Convolutional Blocks]
    fwdD --> poolE[Pooling Feature Map Reduction]
    poolE --> flatF[Flatten Vectorization]
    flatF --> fcG[Fully Connected Dense Layers]
    fcG --> softH[Softmax Class Probabilities]
    softH --> lossI[Cross Entropy Loss Computation]
    lossI --> bwdJ[Backward Pass Output to Input]
    bwdJ --> gradK[Compute Gradients per Layer]
    gradK --> optL[Optimizer SGD Adam RMSProp]
    optL --> updM[Update Weights and Biases]
    updM --> chkN[Convergence Check Validation Loss Plateau]
    chkN --> stopO[Stop Training Save Model]
    chkN --> batchC
    splitB --> valP[Validation Set Evaluation Only]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Block Diagram of a Single Training Iteration

This diagram zooms into **one mini-batch iteration** and shows the *exact direction* of data flow during the forward pass (solid arrows) and the gradient/error flow during the backward pass (dashed arrows).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1[Input Image X] --> conv2[Conv2D Kernel K1 Bias b1]
    conv2 --> relu3[ReLU Activation]
    relu3 --> pool4[Max Pooling 2x2]
    pool4 --> conv5[Conv2D Kernel K2 Bias b2]
    conv5 --> relu6[ReLU Activation]
    relu6 --> pool7[Max Pooling 2x2]
    pool7 --> flat8[Flatten Layer]
    flat8 --> fc9[Dense Layer W fc1]
    fc9 --> soft10[Softmax Output y hat]
    soft10 --> loss11[Cross Entropy Loss L]
    labelY[Ground Truth Label Y] --> loss11

    loss11 -. gradient .-> soft10
    soft10 -. delta .-> fc9
    fc9 -. delta .-> flat8
    flat8 -. mask .-> pool7
    pool7 -. mask .-> relu6
    relu6 -. mask .-> conv5
    conv5 -. kernel gradient .-> conv2
    relu3 -. mask .-> conv2
    conv2 -. input gradient .-> in1
```

## 4.3 Optimizer Comparison — Flow of State Variables

This block diagram illustrates the difference in state maintained by three popular optimizers during the parameter update stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    gradA[Gradient of Loss] --> sgdB[Vanilla SGD]
    gradA --> momC[SGD with Momentum]
    gradA --> adamD[Adam Optimizer]

    momC --> velC1[Velocity Vector v]
    velC1 --> updC2[Update theta = theta plus v]

    adamD --> momD1[First Moment m]
    adamD --> varD2[Second Moment v]
    momD1 --> biasD3[Bias Correction m hat]
    varD2 --> biasD4[Bias Correction v hat]
    biasD3 --> updD5[Update theta]
    biasD4 --> updD5

    sgdB --> updB1[Direct Update theta minus eta times grad]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the dashed arrows:** In Section 4.2, every dashed arrow is a *gradient flow* (right-to-left conceptually, but drawn left-to-right for clarity). The chain rule is applied in the order: loss → softmax → dense → flatten → pool → ReLU → conv → input. Each layer must implement a backward function that takes the upstream gradient and returns the downstream gradient plus the parameter gradient — this is the contract that all deep-learning frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow) enforce via **automatic differentiation**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the term "epoch" in the context of training a convolutional neural network. How is it different from an "iteration"?

**Model Answer:**
An **epoch** is defined as one complete pass of the entire training dataset through the forward and backward propagation cycles. If the dataset has $N$ samples and the batch size is $B$, then one epoch consists of $\lfloor N / B \rfloor$ **iterations** (also called steps or updates). Each iteration corresponds to one forward pass, one backward pass, and one weight update using one mini-batch. Thus, an *epoch* counts data passes; an *iteration* counts weight updates. For example, with $N = 60{,}000$ images and $B = 100$, one epoch contains 600 iterations. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] What is the role of the learning rate $\eta$ in CNN training? State two consequences of choosing an excessively large learning rate.

**Model Answer:**
The **learning rate** $\eta$ is a hyperparameter that controls the *step size* of the parameter update in gradient descent: $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$. A small $\eta$ leads to slow convergence; a large $\eta$ leads to unstable training. **Two consequences of an excessively large $\eta$:** (i) the loss may *diverge* or oscillate wildly instead of decreasing, and (ii) the optimizer may *overshoot* the minimum and fail to converge, with weights potentially flipping sign between updates. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Forward & Backward Pass Derivation

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the four stages involved in training a convolutional neural network. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2D convolutional layer with input $X$, kernel $K$, and output $Z_{i,j} = \sum_{u,v} X_{i+u, j+v} K_{u,v}$, derive the expressions for the gradient of the loss $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to (i) the kernel $K$ and (ii) the input $X$. State clearly where weight-sharing and the chain rule appear. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) The four stages of CNN training (7 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Forward Propagation:** The input image is convolved with the learnable kernels of layer 1, producing a feature map. A non-linear activation (typically ReLU) is applied element-wise, then a pooling operation (max or average) reduces spatial dimensions. This conv-activation-pool triplet is repeated several times. The final feature map is *flattened* into a 1D vector and fed into one or more fully-connected layers, ending with a Softmax layer that outputs class probabilities $\hat{y}_k$. **(2 marks)**

**Stage 2 — Loss Computation:** The predicted probabilities $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ are compared with the ground-truth one-hot label $\mathbf{y}$ using the **Categorical Cross-Entropy Loss**:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{y}_k$$

This quantifies how "wrong" the prediction is. **(1 mark)**

**Stage 3 — Backward Propagation:** Starting from the loss, the gradient of $\mathcal{L}$ w.r.t. each parameter is computed recursively using the chain rule, layer by layer, from the output back to the input. The error signal $\delta^{(\ell)}$ at layer $\ell$ is computed in terms of $\delta^{(\ell+1)}$ via the layer's local Jacobian. **(2 marks)**

**Stage 4 — Parameter Update:** Gradients are fed into an optimizer (SGD, Adam, RMSProp) and the parameters are updated: $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$. The cycle repeats for many epochs until validation loss plateaus. **(2 marks)**

**(A clean block diagram should be drawn — refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.)**

---

**(b) Derivation (7 marks):**

Let $Z_{i,j} = \sum_{u=0}^{k_h-1} \sum_{v=0}^{k_w-1} X_{i+u, j+v} K_{u,v}$ and let $\delta_{i,j} = \partial \mathcal{L} / \partial Z_{i,j}$ be the upstream gradient at the output.

**(i) Gradient w.r.t. kernel $K_{u,v}$ (3.5 marks):**

The loss depends on $Z_{i,j}$ for *all* $(i,j)$. By the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial K_{u,v}} = \sum_{i,j} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Z_{i,j}} \cdot \frac{\partial Z_{i,j}}{\partial K_{u,v}}$$

From the convolution equation, $\partial Z_{i,j} / \partial K_{u,v} = X_{i+u, j+v}$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial K_{u,v}} = \sum_{i,j} \delta_{i,j} \cdot X_{i+u, j+v}}$$

This is the **cross-correlation of the input $X$ with the output error map $\delta$**. The outer sum is the explicit mathematical expression of **weight sharing**: the same $K_{u,v}$ participated in producing every $Z_{i,j}$ it touched, so its gradient must aggregate over all those positions.

**(ii) Gradient w.r.t. input $X_{p,q}$ (3.5 marks):**

We need every $Z_{i,j}$ that used $X_{p,q}$, i.e., those with $i + u = p$ and $j + v = q$, equivalently $i = p - u$ and $j = q - v$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial X_{p,q}} = \sum_{u,v} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Z_{p-u, q-v}} \cdot \frac{\partial Z_{p-u, q-v}}{\partial X_{p,q}} = \sum_{u,v} \delta_{p-u, q-v} \cdot K_{u,v}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial X_{p,q}} = \sum_{u,v} K_{u,v} \cdot \delta_{p-u, q-v}}$$

This is a **full convolution** between the kernel and the (appropriately zero-padded) error map. It uses the chain rule in reverse direction: from a downstream error $\delta$, propagate upstream to the inputs so that earlier layers can compute their parameter gradients. **(Full marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Loss Functions, Optimizers & Regularization

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** Define the Categorical Cross-Entropy loss for a $K$-class classification problem. Show that when combined with a Softmax output layer, the gradient of the loss with respect to the pre-activation logits $s_k$ simplifies to $\hat{y}_k - y_k$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the parameter update rules of **Vanilla SGD**, **SGD with Momentum**, and **Adam**. For each, state one advantage and one disadvantage. Briefly explain why L2 regularization helps in reducing overfitting. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Cross-Entropy with Softmax (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** For a $K$-class problem with one-hot ground-truth vector $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, \ldots, y_K)$ and predicted probabilities $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = (\hat{y}_1, \ldots, \hat{y}_K)$, the **Categorical Cross-Entropy Loss** is:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{y}_k$$

with $\hat{y}_k = \dfrac{e^{s_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{s_j}}$, where $\mathbf{s} = (s_1, \ldots, s_K)$ are the pre-softmax logits.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

We compute $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial s_k$. The Jacobian of the softmax is:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}_j}{\partial s_k} = \hat{y}_j (\delta_{jk} - \hat{y}_k)$$

By the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial s_k} = -\sum_{j=1}^{K} \frac{y_j}{\hat{y}_j} \cdot \hat{y}_j (\delta_{jk} - \hat{y}_k) = -\sum_{j=1}^{K} y_j (\delta_{jk} - \hat{y}_k)$$

Splitting the sum:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial s_k} = -y_k (1 - \hat{y}_k) - \sum_{j \neq k} y_j (-\hat{y}_k) = -y_k + \hat{y}_k \left( y_k + \sum_{j \neq k} y_j \right) = -y_k + \hat{y}_k$$

Therefore $\boxed{\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial s_k = \hat{y}_k - y_k}$. This clean form means: for the *correct* class, the gradient is negative (push $s_k$ up); for the *incorrect* classes, the gradient is positive (push $s_k$ down). **(Full marks)**

---

**(b) Optimizer comparison + L2 regularization (7 marks):**

**Vanilla SGD (1.5 marks):**
- **Update rule:** $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$
- **Advantage:** Simple, low memory, well-understood convergence theory.
- **Disadvantage:** Same learning rate for all parameters; sensitive to saddle points and noisy gradients; can get stuck in ravines.

**SGD with Momentum (1.5 marks):**
- **Update rule:** $v \leftarrow \mu v - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}; \quad \theta \leftarrow \theta + v$
- **Advantage:** Smooths noisy gradients; accelerates convergence in ravines; escapes shallow local minima.
- **Disadvantage:** Extra hyperparameter $\mu$; can overshoot minima if $\mu$ is too large.

**Adam (1.5 marks):**
- **Update rule:** $m \leftarrow \beta_1 m + (1-\beta_1) g; \quad v \leftarrow \beta_2 v + (1-\beta_2) g^2; \quad \theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \hat{m} / (\sqrt{\hat{v}} + \epsilon)$
- **Advantage:** Per-parameter adaptive learning rates; works well out-of-the-box with default settings; fast convergence.
- **Disadvantage:** Higher memory (stores two extra state vectors per parameter); sometimes generalizes worse than SGD-momentum on certain vision tasks.

**L2 Regularization (2.5 marks):**
- L2 regularization adds a penalty term to the loss: $\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{original}} + \lambda \|\theta\|_2^2$, where $\|\theta\|_2^2 = \sum_i \theta_i^2$.
- Its gradient w.r.t. $\theta$ is $2\lambda \theta$, which is added to the parameter update. This **decay** forces weights to remain small in magnitude.
- **Why it reduces overfitting:** Large weights correspond to high-variance, high-curvature decision boundaries that fit noise. L2 regularization encourages smoother, simpler decision functions that generalize better to unseen data — a manifestation of **Occam's razor** in machine learning. **(Full marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting weight sharing in the kernel gradient derivation** costs 2–3 marks. Always explicitly state that the sum runs over all output positions $(i,j)$, not just one. Examiners specifically test this.
> 2. **Confusing "epoch" with "iteration"** is a classic 1-mark deduction in Part A. Memorize the difference: *epoch = full dataset pass; iteration = one weight update on one batch.*
> 3. **Writing Adam's update without bias correction** is marked as an incomplete answer. The $\hat{m}$ and $\hat{v}$ bias-corrected forms are mandatory for full credit.
> 4. **Failing to handle the $j \neq k$ case** in the Softmax + CE derivation will cost 1–2 marks. Always split the sum and use the one-hot property $\sum_j y_j = 1$.
> 5. **In max-pooling gradient questions**, students often say "the gradient is zero" — this is wrong. The gradient is *routed to* the argmax position, not zeroed. Precision of language matters in KTU valuations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Training = iterative optimization** of $\theta$ to minimize $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ over a labelled dataset.
- ✅ **One iteration** = one forward + one backward + one weight update on one mini-batch.
- ✅ **One epoch** = one full pass through the entire training dataset.
- ✅ **Cross-Entropy + Softmax** gradient simplifies to $\hat{y}_k - y_k$ — memorize this identity.
- ✅ **Conv kernel gradient** = cross-correlation of input with output error map; sum over all spatial positions (weight sharing).
- ✅ **Conv input gradient** = full convolution of error map with the kernel.
- ✅ **Max-pool backward** = pass gradient only to the argmax position (the "mask"); other positions get 0.
- ✅ **Avg-pool backward** = distribute gradient equally across all positions in the pooling window.
- ✅ **ReLU backward** = pass gradient unchanged if $z > 0$, else 0 (acts as a gradient gate).
- ✅ **Sigmoid backward** = $\delta \cdot \hat{y} \cdot (1 - \hat{y})$.
- ✅ **Vanilla SGD** = single learning rate, no memory; sensitive to noise.
- ✅ **Momentum** = adds velocity term $\mu v$ to smooth updates; typical $\mu = 0.9$.
- ✅ **Adam** = per-parameter adaptive learning rates using first and second moment estimates; bias correction needed.
- ✅ **L2 regularization** adds $\lambda \vert \theta \vert_2^2$ to the loss; encourages small weights → reduces overfitting.
- ✅ **Dropout** randomly zeros activations during training; must be rescaled at inference time.
- ✅ **Batch Normalization** normalizes activations per mini-batch; has its own learnable scale $\gamma$ and shift $\beta$.
- ✅ **Learning rate** $\eta$ is the single most important hyperparameter — too high causes divergence; too low causes slow convergence.
- ✅ **Backprop is just the chain rule**, applied layer-by-layer from the loss backwards.
- ✅ **Weight sharing** means the same kernel parameter affects many outputs — gradients must be *summed*, not duplicated.
- ✅ **Numerical stability**: subtract the max logit before the softmax to prevent overflow in $e^{s_k}$.
- ✅ **Common pitfall**: confusing cross-correlation (used in deep learning) with true mathematical convolution (which requires kernel flipping). For gradient purposes, the operations coincide because the gradient w.r.t. the kernel ends up being cross-correlation anyway.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GoogleNet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT745 - DEEP LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** GoogleNet # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **GoogleNet (Inception v1)** is a deep convolutional neural network architecture introduced by **Christian Szegedy et al. (Google, 2015)** in the seminal paper *"Going Deeper with Convolutions"* (CVPR 2015). It won the **ILSVRC 2014** classification challenge with a **Top-5 error of 6.67%**, surpassing both VGGNet and the human baseline performance of 5.1%. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** GoogleNet is the *first* commercially successful CNN to demonstrate that going **deeper AND wider simultaneously**, while keeping computational cost low, is achievable through the novel **Inception Module** combined with **1×1 convolution-based dimensionality reduction**. ### Architectural Specifications at a Glance | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | **Depth** | **22 learnable layers** (27 including pooling) | | **Total Parameters** | **~5 million** (12× fewer than AlexNet, 27× fewer than VGG-16) | | **ILSVRC 2014 Top-5 Error** | **6.67%** | | **Key Innovation** | **Inception Module** with parallel multi-scale feature extraction | | **Auxiliary Structures** | **2 Auxiliary Classifiers** at intermediate layers | | **Final Classifier** | **Global Average Pooling** (no fully-connected layers) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Specialist Panel" Intuition Imagine a medical diagnostic centre. Instead of relying on **one doctor** (single kernel size, e.g., 3×3) to examine a patient (an image), you assemble a **panel of specialists**: - **Specialist A** — a stethoscope doctor (small 1×1 receptive field) → looks for **fine, local textures** (edges, corners). - **Specialist B** — a radiologist (medium 3×3 receptive field) → looks for **mid-level patterns** (motifs, parts). - **Specialist C** — an MRI specialist (large 5×5 receptive field) → looks for **global, holistic structures** (objects, scenes). - **Specialist D** — a pathologist with a microscope (3×3 max-pooling) → looks for **invariant dominant features** (spatial pooling). Each specialist independently examines the **same patient (input feature map)** in parallel, then their **reports are concatenated** (channel-stacked) into a single comprehensive diagnosis. **This is precisely what an Inception Module does.** Before combining their reports, however, a **secretary (1×1 convolution)** summarizes and condenses each specialist's verbose notes, dramatically reducing paperwork (computation). > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU exams:** The "specialist panel" analogy directly maps to the four parallel branches of the Inception Module. Examiners frequently test whether students can *explain why multi-scale parallel processing helps with image classification*. ## 1.3 Historical Context & Motivation Before GoogleNet, two design philosophies dominated: 1. **AlexNet (2012)** — Used *bigger filters* (11×11, 5×5) on shallow networks. Suffered from computational explosion. 2. **VGGNet (2014)** — Used *uniform 3×3 filters* stacked deeply. Achieved strong accuracy but with **138 million parameters** (memory-heavy, slow inference). GoogleNet posed a fundamental question: > *"Is it possible to design a network that is BOTH deep AND computationally efficient?"* The answer was the **Inception Module**, which intelligently balances **depth, width, and computational budget** by extracting features at **multiple scales simultaneously** within a single layer block. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Naïve Inception Module — Baseline Design The fundamental insight behind GoogleNet is that **salient visual features exist at vastly different scales** within the same image. A single filter size cannot capture all of them efficiently. ### Construction Steps (Naïve Version) 1. Take an input feature map of dimensions $(H, W, C_{in})$. 2. Apply **four parallel convolutional branches** on the same input: - **Branch 1:** 1×1 convolution → captures point-wise features - **Branch 2:** 3×3 convolution → captures small neighborhood features - **Branch 3:** 5×5 convolution → captures larger neighborhood features - **Branch 4:** 3×3 max-pooling → captures invariant dominant features 3. **Concatenate** (channel-wise stack) the four outputs along the depth axis. 4. Output has the same spatial dimensions as input (preserved via padding) but **dramatically increased depth**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the order. The convolution is applied to the **same input** in parallel — branches do NOT operate sequentially on each other. The output is a *concatenation*, not a *sum*. ### Visual Representation of the Naïve Module ``` Input (H × W × C_in) │ ├──→ [1×1 conv] ──────────┐ ├──→ [3×3 conv] ──────────┤ ├──→ [5×5 conv] ──────────┼──→ Concatenate (depth-axis) → Output (H × W × C_out) └──→ [3×3 max-pool] ──────┘ ``` ## 2.2 The Computational Bottleneck Problem While elegant, the naïve Inception module has a **catastrophic computational cost**, especially for the 5×5 branch. ### Computational Cost Formula For a convolutional operation with: - Input feature map: $H \times W \times C_{in}$ - Filter size: $k \times k$ - Number of filters: $C_{out}$ The computational cost (in multiply-accumulate operations) is: $$ \text{Cost} = H \times W \times C_{in} \times C_{out} \times k^2 $$ ### Worked Numerical Example (The 5×5 Bottleneck) Suppose an input is $14 \times 14 \times 512$ and we apply 32 filters of size $5 \times 5$: $$ \text{Cost}_{\text{naive}} = 14 \times 14 \times 512 \times 32 \times 25 = \mathbf{80{,}236{,}800 \text{ ops}} $$ This single operation consumes approximately **120 million operations**, which is impractical when stacked across many layers. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The 5×5 convolution on a deep feature map (high $C_{in}$) is the **primary computational bottleneck**. GoogleNet's key invention solves this directly. ## 2.3 The Dimensionality Reduction Trick — 1×1 Convolutions This is the **central technical innovation** of GoogleNet. A **1×1 convolution** operates on each pixel independently across all input channels, performing a **linear combination across the depth dimension only**. It is mathematically equivalent to a **per-pixel fully-connected layer**. ### How It Saves Computation Instead of applying a 5×5 convolution directly on 512 channels, we: 1. **First** reduce depth using a 1×1 convolution (e.g., 512 → 32 channels). 2. **Then** apply the expensive 5×5 convolution on the reduced 32 channels. ### Cost Comparison **Naïve:** $14 \times 14 \times 512 \times 32 \times 25 = 80{,}236{,}800$ ops **With 1×1 bottleneck (32-dim intermediate):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1 (1×1 conv)} &: 14 \times 14 \times 512 \times 32 \times 1 = 3{,}211{,}264 \text{ ops} \\ \text{Step 2 (5×5 conv)} &: 14 \times 14 \times 32 \times 32 \times 25 = 5{,}017{,}600 \text{ ops} \\ \text{Total} &: 3{,}211{,}264 + 5{,}017{,}600 = \mathbf{8{,}228{,}864 \text{ ops}} \end{aligned} $$ ### Reduction Factor $$ \text{Reduction Factor} = \frac{80{,}236{,}800}{8{,}228{,}864} \approx \mathbf{9.75 \times} $$ The same output is achieved with **less than 1/9th** the computation. This single trick makes 22-layer training feasible on 2014-era hardware. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:** Memorize the computation reduction formula and be prepared to compute it for arbitrary layer configurations during ESE. ## 2.4 The Final Inception Module Architecture The full GoogleNet Inception module has the following structure: ``` Input (H × W × C_in) │ ├──→ [1×1 conv, C_out1] ─────────────────────────┐ │ │ ├──→ [1×1 conv, C_red2] → [3×3 conv, C_out2] ────┤ │ │ ├──→ [1×1 conv, C_red3] → [5×5 conv, C_out3] ────┼──→ Concatenate │ │ ↓ └──→ [3×3 max-pool] → [1×1 conv, C_out4] ────────┘ (H × W × ΣC_out) ``` ### Per-Branch Operation Count Formula Let the bottleneck reduction factor be $r$. The unified cost equation for any $(k \times k)$ branch becomes: $$ \text{Cost}_{k \times k} = H \times W \times \left[ C_{in} \cdot C_{red} \cdot 1 + C_{red} \cdot C_{out} \cdot k^2 \right] $$ Where $C_{red} = \lfloor C_{in} / r \rfloor$ is the bottleneck dimension. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Comprehensive Formula & Concept Table for ESE Preparation** | # | Concept / Quantity | Formula / Definition | Engineering Utility | |---|-------------------|----------------------|---------------------| | 1 | **Convolution cost (no bottleneck)** | $H \cdot W \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} \cdot k^2$ | Baseline computation estimator | | 2 | **Convolution cost (with 1×1 bottleneck)** | $H \cdot W \cdot [C_{in} \cdot C_{red} + C_{red} \cdot C_{out} \cdot k^2]$ | Real GoogleNet cost | | 3 | **Computational reduction factor** | $\dfrac{k^2 \cdot C_{in}}{C_{red} + k^2 \cdot C_{out}/C_{in} \cdot C_{in}} \approx k^2 \cdot C_{in}/C_{red}$ (when $C_{in} \gg C_{out}$) | Shows bottleneck efficiency | | 4 | **Output depth of Inception module** | $C_{out} = C_{out1} + C_{out2} + C_{out3} + C_{out4}$ | Required for next layer's $C_{in}$ | | 5 | **Total GoogleNet depth** | $22$ learnable layers ($9$ Inception modules) | Architectural identity | | 6 | **Parameter count (GoogleNet)** | $\approx \mathbf{5 \times 10^6}$ parameters | Compared to VGG ($138 \times 10^6$) | | 7 | **Auxiliary classifier loss weight** | $0.3$ (weighted contribution to total loss) | Combats vanishing gradients | | 8 | **Final classifier** | Global Average Pooling → 1 Linear → Softmax | Replaces FC layers | | 9 | **Activation function** | ReLU (after each conv) | Standard for deep nets | | 10 | **Receptive field expansion** | Pooling/strided conv reduces spatial dims while deepening channels | Standard CNN principle | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications The GoogleNet architectural philosophy has propagated far beyond image classification: - **Mobile & Edge AI** — The 1×1 bottleneck trick is now a staple in mobile-friendly networks like **MobileNet** and **EfficientNet**, where parameter efficiency is paramount. - **Medical Imaging** — Inception modules are used in tumor classification and retinal disease detection where multi-scale features (cells, tissues, organs) coexist. - **Object Detection** — Inception backbones power **YOLO-v2** and **SSD** detectors. - **Transfer Learning** — Pre-trained GoogleNet is widely used as a feature extractor for downstream tasks. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Computational Cost Comparison for a Single Inception Module > [!NOTE] > **Problem Setup:** Consider an Inception module operating on input $14 \times 14 \times 512$, with the following output channel allocations: > - Branch 1 (1×1 conv): 64 output channels > - Branch 2 (1×1 reduce to 96 → 3×3 conv to 128) > - Branch 3 (1×1 reduce to 16 → 5×5 conv to 32) > - Branch 4 (3×3 pool → 1×1 conv to 32) ### Step 1 — Branch 1: 1×1 Convolution Cost $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost}_{B1} &= H \times W \times C_{in} \times C_{out} \times k^2 \\ &= 14 \times 14 \times 512 \times 64 \times 1^2 \\ &= 196 \times 32768 \\ &= \mathbf{6{,}422{,}528 \text{ ops}} \end{aligned} $$ *Explanation:* Single-pixel spatial extent means $k^2 = 1$. Multiplications reduce to channel-mixing only. ### Step 2 — Branch 2: 1×1 Reduction + 3×3 Convolution Cost **Step 2a — 1×1 Bottleneck Reduction:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost}_{B2a} &= 14 \times 14 \times 512 \times 96 \times 1 \\ &= 196 \times 49152 \\ &= 9{,}633{,}792 \text{ ops} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2b — 3×3 Convolution on Reduced Feature Map:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost}_{B2b} &= 14 \times 14 \times 96 \times 128 \times 9 \\ &= 196 \times 110592 \\ &= 21{,}676{,}032 \text{ ops} \end{aligned} $$ **Branch 2 Total:** $$ \text{Cost}_{B2} = 9{,}633{,}792 + 21{,}676{,}032 = \mathbf{31{,}309{,}824 \text{ ops}} $$ ### Step 3 — Branch 3: 1×1 Reduction + 5×5 Convolution Cost **Step 3a — 1×1 Bottleneck Reduction (aggressive, 16 channels):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost}_{B3a} &= 14 \times 14 \times 512 \times 16 \times 1 \\ &= 196 \times 8192 \\ &= 1{,}605{,}632 \text{ ops} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3b — 5×5 Convolution on Reduced Feature Map:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost}_{B3b} &= 14 \times 14 \times 16 \times 32 \times 25 \\ &= 196 \times 12800 \\ &= 2{,}508{,}800 \text{ ops} \end{aligned} $$ **Branch 3 Total:** $$ \text{Cost}_{B3} = 1{,}605{,}632 + 2{,}508{,}800 = \mathbf{4{,}114{,}432 \text{ ops}} $$ ### Step 4 — Branch 4: 3×3 Max-Pooling + 1×1 Convolution Cost **Step 4a — Max-Pooling Cost (memory access, treated as ~1 op per pixel per channel):** $$ \text{Cost}_{B4a} \approx 14 \times 14 \times 512 \times 9 = 903{,}168 \text{ ops} $$ **Step 4b — 1×1 Convolution for Projection:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost}_{B4b} &= 14 \times 14 \times 512 \times 32 \times 1 \\ &= \mathbf{3{,}211{,}264 \text{ ops}} \end{aligned} $$ **Branch 4 Total:** $$ \text{Cost}_{B4} \approx 903{,}168 + 3{,}211{,}264 = \mathbf{4{,}114{,}432 \text{ ops}} $$ ### Step 5 — Total Module Cost (with Bottleneck) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost}_{\text{total}} &= \text{Cost}_{B1} + \text{Cost}_{B2} + \text{Cost}_{B3} + \text{Cost}_{B4} \\ &= 6{,}422{,}528 + 31{,}309{,}824 + 4{,}114{,}432 + 4{,}114{,}432 \\ &= \mathbf{45{,}961{,}216 \text{ ops}} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Hypothetical Cost WITHOUT Bottleneck (Naïve Inception) If we removed all 1×1 reductions and applied 3×3 and 5×5 directly on 512 channels: **Branch 3 (Naïve 5×5):** $$ \text{Cost}_{B3,\text{naive}} = 14 \times 14 \times 512 \times 32 \times 25 = 80{,}236{,}800 \text{ ops} $$ This single branch alone costs **~1.75× the entire optimized module's total**. ### Step 7 — Efficiency Ratio $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Efficiency Gain} &= \frac{\text{Cost}_{\text{naive 5×5 alone}}}{\text{Cost}_{\text{whole module optimized}}} \\ &= \frac{80{,}236{,}800}{45{,}961{,}216} \\ &\approx \mathbf{1.75 \times \text{ cheaper (just on this single branch)}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Takeaway:** The 1×1 bottleneck trick **transforms an intractable computation** into a tractable one without sacrificing representational power. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Why Global Average Pooling? Before GoogleNet, networks like VGG ended with massive fully-connected (FC) layers (e.g., $4096 \times 4096$ = 16.8M parameters per layer). GoogleNet replaces all FC layers with a single operation: $$ \text{GAP}: \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{C}, \quad \text{GAP}(x)_c = \frac{1}{HW}\sum_{i=1}^{H}\sum_{j=1}^{W} x_{i,j,c} $$ ### Comparison Table | Property | FC Layers (VGG-style) | Global Avg Pooling (GoogleNet) | |----------|----------------------|--------------------------------| | **Parameters** | Millions (e.g., $7 \times 7 \times 512 \times 4096$) | **Zero** | | **Overfitting risk** | High (heavy dropout required) | **Low** | | **Spatial information** | Discarded (flattened) | Aggregated (averaged) | | **Translation invariance** | Weak | **Strong** | | **Hyperparameters** | Multiple FC widths | **None** | This single design choice is responsible for a **substantial fraction of GoogleNet's parameter reduction** (from $\sim$138M in VGG to $\sim$5M in GoogleNet). ## 3.3 Auxiliary Classifier Derivation The total loss at training time is: $$ \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{main}} + 0.3 \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux1}} + 0.3 \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux2}} $$ Where each $\mathcal{L}$ is the standard softmax cross-entropy loss. During **inference**, the auxiliary heads are **discarded** entirely. ### Gradient Flow Analysis By the chain rule, the gradient of the loss with respect to an early layer weight $\mathbf{W}_l$ involves the product of Jacobians through all subsequent layers: $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_l} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{main}}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_l} + 0.3 \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux1}}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_l} + 0.3 \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux2}}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_l} $$ The auxiliary terms provide **additional gradient pathways** that mitigate vanishing gradients in very deep networks — a conceptual precursor to **ResNet's skip connections** (introduced the following year, 2015). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** Auxiliary classifiers serve the *same function* as skip connections in ResNet — both combat the vanishing gradient problem. Understanding this continuity is highly valuable for ESE answers. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level GoogleNet Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) The following diagram captures the **complete macro-architecture** of GoogleNet, including all 9 Inception modules and the two auxiliary classifiers. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Input Image 224x224x3"] --> B["Conv 7x7 stride=2 padding=3
ReLU
Output 112x112x64"] B --> C["MaxPool 3x3 stride=2
Output 56x56x64"] C --> D["Conv 1x1 stride=1 ReLU
Output 56x56x64"] D --> E["Conv 3x3 stride=1 padding=1 ReLU
Output 56x56x192"] E --> F["MaxPool 3x3 stride=2
Output 28x28x192"] F --> G["Inception Module 3a
Output 28x28x256"] G --> H["Inception Module 3b
Output 28x28x480"] H --> I["MaxPool 3x3 stride=2
Output 14x14x480"] I --> J["Inception Module 4a
Output 14x14x512"] J --> K["Auxiliary Classifier 1
Softmax over 1000 classes"] J --> L["Inception Module 4b
Output 14x14x512"] L --> M["Inception Module 4c
Output 14x14x512"] M --> N["Inception Module 4d
Output 14x14x528"] N --> O["Auxiliary Classifier 2
Softmax over 1000 classes"] O --> P["Inception Module 4e
Output 14x14x832"] P --> Q["MaxPool 3x3 stride=2
Output 7x7x832"] Q --> R["Inception Module 5a
Output 7x7x832"] R --> S["Inception Module 5b
Output 7x7x1024"] S --> T["Global Average Pooling
Output 1x1x1024"] T --> U["Dropout 0.4"] U --> V["Fully Connected 1024 to 1000"] V --> W["Softmax
Class Probabilities"] K -.aux loss 0.3.-> X["Total Loss Computation"] O -.aux loss 0.3.-> X W --> X ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Inception Module Internal Structure ```mermaid flowchart TD Input["Input Feature Map
H x W x C_in"] --> B1["1x1 Conv
ReLU"] Input --> B2["1x1 Conv (bottleneck)
ReLU"] B2 --> B2b["3x3 Conv padding=1
ReLU"] Input --> B3["1x1 Conv (bottleneck)
ReLU"] B3 --> B3b["5x5 Conv padding=2
ReLU"] Input --> B4["3x3 MaxPool stride=1
padding=1"] B4 --> B4b["1x1 Conv
ReLU"] B1 --> Concat["Concatenate along depth axis"] B2b --> Concat B3b --> Concat B4b --> Concat Concat --> Output["Output Feature Map
H x W x C_out"] ``` ## 4.3 Spatial Dimension Progression Through the Network ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["224x224x3"] -->|Conv 7x7 s=2| S2["112x112x64"] S2 -->|MaxPool s=2| S3["56x56x64"] S3 -->|Conv stack| S4["28x28x192"] S4 -->|MaxPool s=2| S5["14x14x480"] S5 -->|MaxPool s=2| S6["7x7x832"] S6 -->|Global Avg Pool| S7["1x1x1024"] S7 -->|FC + Softmax| S8["1000 class probs"] ``` ## 4.4 Auxiliary Classifier Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD AIn["Input from preceding Inception module"] --> A1["Global Average Pooling
5x5 or 14x14 to 1x1"] A1 --> A2["Conv 1x1 128 channels
ReLU"] A2 --> A3["Fully Connected 1024 units
ReLU"] A3 --> A4["Dropout 0.7"] A4 --> A5["Fully Connected 1000 units
Softmax"] A5 --> AOut["Auxiliary Prediction"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Architecture Observation:** Notice how the **spatial resolution halves** three times (via strided convolution and pooling) while the **channel depth progressively grows**. This is the classic CNN spatial-to-semantic information compression pattern. ## 4.5 Parameter Distribution Comparison Across CNN Architectures ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["LeNet-5"] -->|60K| A2["AlexNet"] A2 -->|60M| A3["VGG-16"] A3 -->|138M| A4["GoogleNet (Inception v1)"] A4 -->|5M only| A5["Inception v3"] A5 -->|24M| A6["ResNet-50"] A6 -->|25M| A7["EfficientNet-B0"] A7 -->|5.3M| A8["MobileNetV3"] A8 -->|5M| A9["Modern Mobile CNNs"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** GoogleNet achieves higher accuracy than VGG with **27× fewer parameters** — this is the *defining achievement* students must articulate in KTU ESE answers. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam - Model Question]** Explain the role of the **1×1 convolution** in GoogleNet's Inception module. **Model Answer:** A 1×1 convolution in GoogleNet's Inception module acts as a **dimensionality reduction bottleneck** applied before expensive spatial convolutions (3×3 and 5×5). It performs a per-pixel linear combination across channels, reducing the feature map's depth before the computationally heavy operations are applied. For example, applying a 5×5 convolution directly on a 512-channel feature map producing 32 output channels requires $H \cdot W \cdot 512 \cdot 32 \cdot 25$ operations. By inserting a 1×1 convolution that first reduces 512 channels to 32, the cost drops by approximately **9–10×**. This enables GoogleNet to have 22 layers with only **5 million parameters** — a 27× reduction compared to VGG-16. Additionally, 1×1 convolutions introduce **additional non-linearity** (after applying ReLU), increasing representational power without significantly affecting spatial dimensions. --- ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam - Model Question]** What are **auxiliary classifiers** in GoogleNet, and why were they introduced? **Model Answer:** Auxiliary classifiers are **side-branch classification heads** attached to intermediate layers of the GoogleNet network (specifically after Inception modules 4a and 4d). Their purpose is to **combat the vanishing gradient problem** in very deep networks by injecting additional gradient signals during backpropagation. During training, the total loss is computed as: $$ \mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{main}} + 0.3 \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux1}} + 0.3 \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{aux2}} $$ The auxiliary losses (weighted by 0.3) encourage discriminative features to emerge at intermediate depths. At **inference time**, these auxiliary heads are discarded, incurring no additional cost. Conceptually, they are a precursor to **ResNet's skip connections**, which solve the same gradient flow problem more elegantly. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of the Inception module** used in GoogleNet. Discuss how dimensionality reduction is achieved using 1×1 convolutions. **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** The Inception module is the fundamental building block of GoogleNet. It performs **multi-scale feature extraction** by applying multiple convolutional filters of different sizes in parallel on the same input, then concatenating their outputs along the depth dimension. **Naïve Inception Module (2 marks):** ``` Input ├── 1×1 conv ├── 3×3 conv ├── 5×5 conv └── 3×3 max-pool │ Concat → Output ``` The naïve module has four parallel branches producing feature maps of different receptive fields. However, this design has a **computational bottleneck**, especially in the 5×5 branch. **Improved Module with 1×1 Bottleneck (3 marks):** The 1×1 convolutions are inserted before the 3×3 and 5×5 branches (and after the pooling branch) to reduce the input channel depth before expensive operations: ``` Input ├── 1×1 conv (C_out1) ├── 1×1 conv (C_red) → 3×3 conv (C_out2) ├── 1×1 conv (C_red) → 5×5 conv (C_out3) └── 3×3 max-pool → 1×1 conv (C_out4) │ Concat → Output ``` **Computational Justification (1 mark):** Without the bottleneck, a 5×5 conv on a 512-channel input producing 32 outputs costs: $$14 \times 14 \times 512 \times 32 \times 25 = 80{,}236{,}800 \text{ ops}$$ With a 1×1 bottleneck reducing 512 → 16: $$(14 \times 14 \times 512 \times 16) + (14 \times 14 \times 16 \times 32 \times 25) = 4{,}114{,}432 \text{ ops}$$ This represents a **~19.5× reduction** in operations for that branch alone. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]** Describe the **complete GoogleNet architecture**. Discuss the role of auxiliary classifiers and global average pooling in the architecture. **Model Solution:** **Complete Architecture (4 marks):** GoogleNet is a **22-layer deep CNN** organized as follows: | Stage | Operation | Output Shape | |-------|-----------|--------------| | 1 | Conv 7×7 (stride 2) + ReLU | 112×112×64 | | 2 | MaxPool 3×3 (stride 2) | 56×56×64 | | 3 | Conv 1×1 + Conv 3×3 | 56×56×192 | | 4 | MaxPool 3×3 (stride 2) | 28×28×192 | | 5 | **Inception (3a)** | 28×28×256 | | 6 | **Inception (3b)** | 28×28×480 | | 7 | MaxPool 3×3 (stride 2) | 14×14×480 | | 8 | **Inception (4a)** + Aux-1 | 14×14×512 | | 9 | **Inception (4b–4d)** | 14×14×528 | | 10 | **Inception (4e)** + Aux-2 | 14×14×832 | | 11 | MaxPool 3×3 (stride 2) | 7×7×832 | | 12 | **Inception (5a, 5b)** | 7×7×1024 | | 13 | **Global Average Pooling** | 1×1×1024 | | 14 | Dropout (0.4) + FC + Softmax | 1000 | **Auxiliary Classifiers (2 marks):** Attached after Inception 4a and 4d, each auxiliary classifier consists of: - Global Average Pooling → 1×1 Conv (128 filters) → FC (1024 units) → Dropout (0.7) → FC (1000) → Softmax. The auxiliary losses contribute with weight **0.3** each to the total training loss, helping gradients flow through deep layers. At inference, they are discarded. **Global Average Pooling (1 mark):** Replaces the parameter-heavy FC layers used in VGG/AlexNet. It averages each feature map to a single value, producing a 1024-dimensional vector that feeds directly into the final classifier. This eliminates millions of parameters and provides built-in spatial translation invariance. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam - June 2025 Model]** Compare **GoogleNet (Inception v1)** with **VGG-16** in terms of architecture depth, parameter count, accuracy, and design philosophy. **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** Both GoogleNet and VGG-16 are landmark CNN architectures from 2014, but they represent fundamentally different design philosophies. **Comparative Analysis (5 marks):** | Feature | VGG-16 | GoogleNet (Inception v1) | |---------|--------|--------------------------| | **Depth** | 16 layers (all 3×3 conv) | 22 layers (Inception modules) | | **Total Parameters** | ~138 million | ~5 million | | **ILSVRC 2014 Top-5 Error** | 7.3% | 6.67% | | **Design Philosophy** | Uniform depth, sequential | Multi-scale parallel branches | | **Key Innovation** | Stacked 3×3 convs (receptive field decomposition) | Inception module + 1×1 bottleneck | | **Final Classifier** | 3 FC layers (4096, 4096, 1000) | Global Avg Pool + 1 FC | | **Auxiliary Structures** | None | 2 auxiliary classifiers | | **Computational Cost** | High (~15.5 GFLOPs) | Moderate (~2 GFLOPs) | | **Memory Footprint** | Very large | Compact | **Key Insights (1 mark):** 1. **Parameter efficiency:** GoogleNet achieves *better* accuracy with *27× fewer parameters* by exploiting parallel multi-scale features and 1×1 bottlenecks. 2. **VGG's strength:** VGG's uniform 3×3 design is conceptually simpler and easier to modify for transfer learning. 3. **GoogleNet's strength:** GoogleNet's architectural complexity trades off simplicity for parameter efficiency — making it more suitable for deployment on resource-constrained devices. **Conclusion:** GoogleNet established the principle that *smart architectural design* can outperform brute-force depth scaling in parameter/accuracy trade-offs. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **[KTU University Exam - June 2025 Model]** With a worked numerical example, demonstrate how the **1×1 convolution bottleneck** reduces computational cost in a GoogleNet Inception module. **Model Solution:** **Setup (1 mark):** Consider an Inception module branch processing input $H \times W \times C_{in} = 14 \times 14 \times 480$, applying a 5×5 convolution to produce 64 output channels. **Naïve Approach (No Bottleneck) — 2 marks:** $$ \text{Cost}_{\text{naive}} = 14 \times 14 \times 480 \times 64 \times 25 $$ Step-by-step computation: - $14 \times 14 = 196$ - $480 \times 64 = 30{,}720$ - $30{,}720 \times 25 = 768{,}000$ - $768{,}000 \times 196 = \mathbf{150{,}528{,}000 \text{ ops}}$ **Bottleneck Approach (1×1 Conv with 32 intermediate channels) — 3 marks:** **Step 1 — 1×1 Convolution (480 → 32 channels):** $$ \text{Cost}_1 = 14 \times 14 \times 480 \times 32 \times 1 = 196 \times 15{,}360 = 3{,}010{,}560 \text{ ops} $$ **Step 2 — 5×5 Convolution (32 → 64 channels):** $$ \text{Cost}_2 = 14 \times 14 \times 32 \times 64 \times 25 = 196 \times 51{,}200 = 10{,}035{,}200 \text{ ops} $$ **Total Bottleneck Cost:** $$ \text{Cost}_{\text{bn}} = 3{,}010{,}560 + 10{,}035{,}200 = \mathbf{13{,}045{,}760 \text{ ops}} $$ **Comparison (1 mark):** $$ \text{Speedup} = \frac{150{,}528{,}000}{13{,}045{,}760} \approx \mathbf{11.54 \times} $$ The bottleneck achieves the **same output shape** ($14 \times 14 \times 64$) with **~11.5× fewer operations**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to apply padding when stating output dimensions. The 5×5 conv uses **padding=2** to preserve spatial dimensions. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing the order of operations — the **1×1 conv comes FIRST**, then the 5×5 conv (not the reverse). > - **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to mention that the 1×1 conv also adds **non-linearity** (via ReLU), giving a small representational bonus beyond mere cost reduction. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Writing $5^2 = 10$ instead of $5^2 = 25$ — calculator/arithmetic errors are heavily penalized. > - **Marks Distribution Tip:** Always show the *cost formula* first, then plug in numbers — this earns partial credit even if the final arithmetic is wrong. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — GoogleNet (Inception v1)** ### Architecture Essentials - ☐ GoogleNet won **ILSVRC 2014** with **Top-5 error of 6.67%** (vs. VGG's 7.3%). - ☐ Total **22 learnable layers** organized into **9 Inception modules** grouped into 3 stages (3a/3b, 4a–4e, 5a/5b). - ☐ Total parameters: **~5 million** (27× fewer than VGG-16's 138M). - ☐ Final classifier uses **Global Average Pooling** + Dropout(0.4) + 1 FC layer + Softmax. ### Inception Module Core Concepts - ☐ Four parallel branches: **1×1, 3×3, 5×5 convs, and 3×3 max-pool**. - ☐ Branches operate on the **same input** in parallel;
