outputs are **concatenated** along the depth axis. - ☐ **1×1 convolutions** are inserted before 3×3 and 5×5 branches as **dimensionality reduction bottlenecks**. - ☐ A 1×1 conv on $C_{in}$ channels produces $\text{Cost} = H \cdot W \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out}$ ops. - ☐ Bottleneck reduction formula: $\text{Cost}_{\text{bn}} = H \cdot W \cdot [C_{in} \cdot C_{red} + C_{red} \cdot C_{out} \cdot k^2]$. - ☐ Typical bottleneck achieves **9–12× computation reduction** in 5×5 branches. ### Auxiliary Classifiers - ☐ Located after Inception modules **4a and 4d**. - ☐ Each consists of: GAP → 1×1 Conv (128 filters) → FC (1024) → Dropout(0.7) → FC (1000). - ☐ Weighted by **0.3** in total loss: $\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{main}} + 0.3\mathcal{L}_{\text{aux1}} + 0.3\mathcal{L}_{\text{aux2}}$. - ☐ **Discarded at inference time** — no additional cost. - ☐ Purpose: combat vanishing gradients in deep networks. ### Comparison with Other Architectures - ☐ **vs. AlexNet (2012):** GoogleNet uses smaller filters, deeper network, fewer parameters. - ☐ **vs. VGG (2014):** GoogleNet has multi-scale parallel branches; VGG has uniform sequential 3×3. - ☐ **Predecessor to ResNet (2015):** Auxiliary classifiers conceptually prefigure skip connections. ### Common Exam-Ready Phrases - ☐ *"Multi-scale feature extraction via parallel convolutional branches."* - ☐ *"1×1 convolution performs channel-wise linear recombination, acting as a learnable dimensionality reduction bottleneck."* - ☐ *"Global Average Pooling replaces FC layers, reducing parameters and overfitting."* - ☐ *"Auxiliary classifiers inject gradient signals into early layers, mitigating vanishing gradients."* > [!NOTE] > **Final Tip:** When answering any GoogleNet question in KTU ESE, always (1) mention the **Inception module**, (2) explain the **1×1 bottleneck trick** with a numerical example, and (3) discuss **auxiliary classifiers + global average pooling**. This three-pronged structure covers 90%+ of typical question patterns.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**VGG Net** (Visual Geometry Group Network) is a deep Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture proposed by **Karen Simonyan** and **Andrew Zisserman** of the Visual Geometry Group, Department of Engineering Science, University of Oxford, in their seminal 2014 paper *"Very Deep Convolutional Networks for Large-Scale Image Recognition."* The defining principle of VGG is the use of a **uniform architecture built exclusively from very small (3×3) convolutional filters** stacked to a depth of **16 to 19 weight layers**, demonstrating that depth is the critical ingredient for superior image classification accuracy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** VGG Net is studied as the canonical example of *depth-driven performance* in CNNs and the architectural precursor to modern backbones like ResNet and Inception.

The family includes several configurations standardized in the paper:

| Configuration | Weight Layers | Parameters (approx.) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **VGG-11** | 11 | 133 M |
| **VGG-13** | 13 | 133 M |
| **VGG-16** | 16 | **138 M** |
| **VGG-19** | 19 | 144 M |

The most celebrated member is **VGG-16**, which secured 1st place in the *ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC) 2014 — Localization task* and 2nd place in the Classification task.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Magnifying Glass" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are a detective examining a fingerprint left at a crime scene.
> - A **single large magnifying glass (one 7×7 filter)** gives you a wide view in one glance, but the lens is heavy, the image is blurry at the edges, and you only get **one** chance to focus.
> - Instead, VGG uses a **stack of three small high-precision magnifiers (three 3×3 filters)**, each sharper than the last. Together they cover the **same field of view** as the big lens, but you get **three chances to refine your understanding**, and the total glass is **lighter and cheaper**.
>
> **Translation:** Smaller filters stacked deep = same receptive field, fewer parameters, more non-linear decision power.

### 1.3 Standard Architectural Constants (VGG-16)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard-coded Design Rules of VGG (must memorize for KTU viva):**
> - All **convolutional layers** use **3×3 kernels**, **stride = 1**, **same padding** (pad = 1).
> - All **max-pooling layers** use **2×2 kernels**, **stride = 2**.
> - **Activation** after every conv and FC layer: **ReLU**.
> - **Channel progression** after each pooling: 64 → 128 → 256 → 512 → 512.
> - **Three Fully-Connected (FC) layers**: 4096 → 4096 → 1000 (the 1000 matches ImageNet classes).
> - **Input image**: fixed **224 × 224 × 3** RGB tensor.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Why 3×3 Filters Stacked Deep?

The genius of VGG lies in substituting a single large receptive field with multiple stacked smaller ones. Let us analyze the three advantages.

#### 2.1.1 Receptive Field Expansion
Two stacked 3×3 convolutions yield an **effective receptive field of 5×5**. Three stacked 3×3 convolutions yield **7×7**, equal to a single 7×7 layer.

**General Rule (KTU Favourite):**

$$
\text{ERF} = (k_1 + k_2 + \dots + k_n - (n-1)) \quad \text{for stride 1}
$$

where $k_i$ is each successive kernel size.

#### 2.1.2 Parameter Economy
For an input with $C$ channels, compare a single 7×7 conv vs. three stacked 3×3 convs (same input/output channels $C$):

- Single 7×7: parameters = $7 \times 7 \times C \times C = 49 C^2$
- Three 3×3: parameters = $3 \times (3 \times 3 \times C \times C) = 27 C^2$

**Savings:**

$$
\Delta P = 49C^2 - 27C^2 = 22C^2
$$

That is a **~45% reduction** in parameters while achieving the same effective receptive field.

#### 2.1.3 Increased Non-Linearity
A single 7×7 conv applies **one** ReLU non-linearity. Three stacked 3×3 convs apply **three** ReLUs, enabling the network to learn a **richer, more discriminative feature hierarchy** without suffering from vanishing gradients (mitigated by careful pre-training layer-wise).

### 2.2 Architectural Stages of VGG-16

| Stage | Layer Repetitions | Output Size (H × W × C) | Notes |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Input | — | 224 × 224 × 3 | RGB image |
| Stage 1 | 2 × Conv(3×3, 64) | 224 × 224 × 64 | Padding = 1 |
| Pool-1 | MaxPool 2×2, s=2 | 112 × 112 × 64 | Spatial halving |
| Stage 2 | 2 × Conv(3×3, 128) | 112 × 112 × 128 | Channels doubled |
| Pool-2 | MaxPool 2×2, s=2 | 56 × 56 × 128 | |
| Stage 3 | 3 × Conv(3×3, 256) | 56 × 56 × 256 | |
| Pool-3 | MaxPool 2×2, s=2 | 28 × 28 × 256 | |
| Stage 4 | 3 × Conv(3×3, 512) | 28 × 28 × 512 | |
| Pool-4 | MaxPool 2×2, s=2 | 14 × 14 × 512 | |
| Stage 5 | 3 × Conv(3×3, 512) | 14 × 14 × 512 | |
| Pool-5 | MaxPool 2×2, s=2 | 7 × 7 × 512 | |
| FC-6 | Dense | 1 × 1 × 4096 | Dropout 0.5 |
| FC-7 | Dense | 1 × 1 × 4096 | Dropout 0.5 |
| FC-8 | Dense (Softmax) | 1 × 1 × 1000 | ImageNet classes |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following six equations — these are the most repeated formulas in KTU ESE questions on VGG/CNN backbones.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| 1 | $W_{out} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{W_{in} + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$ | Conv/Pool output dimension | pixels |
| 2 | $\text{Params}_{\text{conv}} = k_h \cdot k_w \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} + C_{out}$ | Conv layer parameters (weights + bias) | scalars |
| 3 | $\text{Params}_{\text{FC}} = N_{in} \cdot N_{out} + N_{out}$ | Fully-connected layer parameters | scalars |
| 4 | $\text{ERF}_{n \text{ layers of } k \times k} = n(k-1) + 1$ | Effective Receptive Field for stride-1 stack | pixels |
| 5 | $\text{FLOPs}_{\text{conv}} \approx 2 \cdot k^2 \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} \cdot H_{out} \cdot W_{out}$ | Multiply-Accumulates per conv layer | MAC |
| 6 | $\text{Memory}_{\text{feat}} = H \cdot W \cdot C \cdot 4 \text{ bytes}$ | Feature map memory (FP32) | bytes |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Transfer Learning:** VGG-16's conv layers are the most widely used pre-trained backbone for object detection (Faster R-CNN, SSD), segmentation (FCN, SegNet), and style transfer.
- **Feature Extraction:** The FC-7 layer (4096-D) acts as a universal image descriptor for retrieval, image captioning, and visual question answering.
- **Baseline Architecture:** VGG acts as the "control experiment" against which ResNet, DenseNet, and EfficientNet measure their gains in depth-efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Spatial Dimension of a Convolutional Layer

Given the input tensor of width $W_{in}$, kernel size $k$, padding $p$, and stride $s$, derive the output width $W_{out}$.

**Step 1:** Start with the standard padding-extended input width:

$$
W_{\text{padded}} = W_{in} + 2p
$$

**Step 2:** Subtract the kernel footprint to get the number of valid starting positions:

$$
N_{\text{start}} = W_{\text{padded}} - k
$$

**Step 3:** Each subsequent window is offset by the stride $s$. Number of valid windows is therefore:

$$
n = \left\lfloor \frac{N_{\text{start}}}{s} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

**Step 4:** Combine and simplify:

$$
\boxed{\,W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{W_{in} + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1\,}
$$

**Example:** For VGG's first conv layer with $W_{in} = 224$, $k = 3$, $p = 1$, $s = 1$:

$$
W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{224 + 2(1) - 3}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 223 \right\rfloor + 1 = 224
$$

Output remains 224 — confirming the **"same" convolution** used throughout VGG.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Parameter Count of VGG-16

Compute the parameter count layer-by-layer to verify the canonical **~138 million** figure.

**Step 1 — Conv Block-1 (two 3×3, 3→64):**

$$
P_1 = 2 \times \bigl(3 \cdot 3 \cdot 3 \cdot 64 + 64\bigr) = 2 \times (1728 + 64) = 2 \times 1792 = 3584
$$

**Step 2 — Conv Block-2 (two 3×3, 64→128):**

$$
P_2 = 2 \times (3 \cdot 3 \cdot 64 \cdot 128 + 128) = 2 \times (73728 + 128) = 147712
$$

**Step 3 — Conv Block-3 (three 3×3, 128→256):**

$$
P_3 = 3 \times (3 \cdot 3 \cdot 128 \cdot 256 + 256) = 3 \times (295168) = 885504
$$

**Step 4 — Conv Block-4 (three 3×3, 256→512):**

$$
P_4 = 3 \times (3 \cdot 3 \cdot 256 \cdot 512 + 512) = 3 \times (1180160) = 3540480
$$

**Step 5 — Conv Block-5 (three 3×3, 512→512):**

$$
P_5 = 3 \times (3 \cdot 3 \cdot 512 \cdot 512 + 512) = 3 \times (2359808) = 7079424
$$

**Step 6 — FC Layers:**

$$
P_{FC} = \underbrace{(7\cdot7\cdot512 \cdot 4096 + 4096)}_{FC6} + \underbrace{(4096 \cdot 4096 + 4096)}_{FC7} + \underbrace{(4096 \cdot 1000 + 1000)}_{FC8}
$$

Evaluating each:

$$
P_{FC6} = 25088 \cdot 4096 + 4096 = 102760448 + 4096 = 102764544
$$

$$
P_{FC7} = 4096 \cdot 4096 + 4096 = 16777216 + 4096 = 16781312
$$

$$
P_{FC8} = 4096 \cdot 1000 + 1000 = 4096000 + 1000 = 4097000
$$

$$
P_{FC} = 102764544 + 16781312 + 4097000 = 123642856
$$

**Step 7 — Total Sum:**

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{total}} = 3584 + 147712 + 885504 + 3540480 + 7079424 + 123642856 \approx 138\,\text{M parameters}\,}
$$

This matches the official figure from the original paper.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Receptive Field Equivalence Proof

**Claim:** Three stacked 3×3 conv layers (stride 1, same padding) have an effective receptive field (ERF) of **7×7**.

**Step 1:** ERF after a single 3×3 conv on input pixel-grid:

$$
\text{ERF}_1 = 3
$$

**Step 2:** Each new conv adds $(k-1)$ to the receptive field (for stride 1):

$$
\text{ERF}_{n+1} = \text{ERF}_n + (k - 1)
$$

**Step 3:** After three layers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ERF}_2 &= 3 + (3 - 1) = 5 \\
\text{ERF}_3 &= 5 + (3 - 1) = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Cross-check with the general formula:

$$
\text{ERF} = n(k-1) + 1 = 3(3-1) + 1 = 3 \cdot 2 + 1 = 7 \quad \checkmark
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Parameter Savings Quantification

For input/output channel count $C$, compare:

- **Option A:** Single $7 \times 7$ conv → weights $= 7^2 \cdot C^2 = 49 C^2$
- **Option B:** Three stacked $3 \times 3$ convs → weights $= 3 \times 3^2 \cdot C^2 = 27 C^2$

The savings ratio:

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{49C^2 - 27C^2}{49C^2} = \frac{22}{49} \approx 0.449
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Three } 3 \times 3 \text{ convs save } \approx 44.9\% \text{ parameters compared to one } 7 \times 7\,}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (VGG vs. AlexNet vs. ResNet)

| Property | AlexNet (2012) | VGG-16 (2014) | ResNet-50 (2015) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Depth (weighted layers)** | 8 | 16 | 50 |
| **Conv filter sizes** | 11×11, 5×5, 3×3 | **Uniform 3×3** | Mixed 7×7 + 3×3 |
| **Top-5 ImageNet Error** | 15.3 % | **7.3 %** | 5.25 % |
| **Parameters** | ~60 M | ~138 M | ~25 M |
| **Key innovation** | ReLU + Dropout + GPU | **Depth via small filters** | Skip connections |
| **FC parameters share** | ~58 % | ~89 % | < 10 % |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — VGG-16 Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image 224x224x3] --> B[Conv3x3-64 + ReLU]
    B --> C[Conv3x3-64 + ReLU]
    C --> D[MaxPool 2x2 stride2]
    D --> E[Conv3x3-128 + ReLU]
    E --> F[Conv3x3-128 + ReLU]
    F --> G[MaxPool 2x2 stride2]
    G --> H[Conv3x3-256 + ReLU]
    H --> I[Conv3x3-256 + ReLU]
    I --> J[Conv3x3-256 + ReLU]
    J --> K[MaxPool 2x2 stride2]
    K --> L[Conv3x3-512 + ReLU]
    L --> M[Conv3x3-512 + ReLU]
    M --> N[Conv3x3-512 + ReLU]
    N --> O[MaxPool 2x2 stride2]
    O --> P[Conv3x3-512 + ReLU]
    P --> Q[Conv3x3-512 + ReLU]
    Q --> R[Conv3x3-512 + ReLU]
    R --> S[MaxPool 2x2 stride2]
    S --> T[Flatten 7x7x512]
    T --> U[FC 4096 + ReLU + Dropout]
    U --> V[FC 4096 + ReLU + Dropout]
    V --> W[FC 1000 + Softmax]
    W --> X[Class Probabilities]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Receptive Field: 7×7 vs. Three 3×3

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OptionA[OPTION A: Single 7x7 Conv]
        A1[Input Pixel Grid] --> A2[Conv 7x7 stride1]
        A2 --> A3[ReLU]
        A3 --> A4[ERF = 7x7]
        A4 --> A5[1 Non linearity]
        A5 --> A6[Params 49C squared]
    end

    subgraph OptionB[OPTION B: Three Stacked 3x3 Convs]
        B1[Input Pixel Grid] --> B2[Conv 3x3]
        B2 --> B3[ReLU 1]
        B3 --> B4[Conv 3x3]
        B4 --> B5[ReLU 2]
        B5 --> B6[Conv 3x3]
        B6 --> B7[ReLU 3]
        B7 --> B8[ERF = 7x7]
        B8 --> B9[3 Non linearities]
        B9 --> B10[Params 27C squared]
    end
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FeatureExtractor[Convolutional Feature Extractor]
        FE1[Block 1: 64 channels, 224x224] --> FE2[Block 2: 128 channels, 112x112]
        FE2 --> FE3[Block 3: 256 channels, 56x56]
        FE3 --> FE4[Block 4: 512 channels, 28x28]
        FE4 --> FE5[Block 5: 512 channels, 14x14]
    end

    FE5 --> FP[Global Feature Map 7x7x512]

    subgraph Classifier[Classification Head]
        FP --> F1[FC Layer 4096]
        F1 --> F2[FC Layer 4096]
        F2 --> F3[Softmax 1000]
    end

    F3 --> OUT[Predicted Class]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Channel-Doubling Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1: 64 ch] --> S2[Stage 2: 128 ch]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: 256 ch]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: 512 ch]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: 512 ch]
    note1[Spatial size halves after each stage via 2x2 max-pool]:::note
    S1 -.- note1
    classDef note fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List any three architectural characteristics of VGG-16 that distinguish it from AlexNet.**

**Model Answer:**
1. VGG-16 uses **uniform 3×3 convolutional filters** throughout, whereas AlexNet uses larger 11×11 and 5×5 kernels in early layers.
2. VGG-16 is significantly **deeper (16 weight layers)** than AlexNet (8 weight layers), pushing the depth to demonstrate its effect on accuracy.
3. VGG-16 consistently **doubles the number of channels (64 → 128 → 256 → 512 → 512)** after each pooling block, while AlexNet uses an irregular channel schedule.
4. VGG-16 applies **zero-padding (p = 1)** to preserve spatial resolution after each convolution, ensuring all convolutions are "same."

*(Any 3 well-explained points → full 3 marks.)*

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State the role of the 1×1 convolution in the VGG architecture. Is it used in VGG?**

**Model Answer:**
VGG-16 **does not use 1×1 convolutions** anywhere in its architecture. The network relies exclusively on **3×3 convolutions** stacked with **2×2 max-pooling** layers to progressively reduce spatial dimensions and increase feature depth. The 1×1 convolution (popularized later by GoogLeNet/Inception and ResNet) is absent from the original VGG paper, preserving the network's design simplicity and uniformity.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (KTU 2024 ESE):** *Answer any ONE full question from the choice given. Each carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of VGG-16**. Explain how the spatial resolution and channel depth change across the network.

**(b)** Prove analytically that **three stacked 3×3 convolutional layers** can replace a single **7×7 convolutional layer** with the same effective receptive field while saving parameters and adding more non-linearity.

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Overview [1 mark]:**
VGG-16 is a 16-weight-layer CNN proposed by Simonyan & Zisserman (2014). It accepts a fixed 224 × 224 × 3 RGB image and outputs 1000-way softmax probabilities.

**Step 2 — Five Convolutional Stages [3 marks]:**

| Stage | Layers | Output Resolution | Channels |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 2 × Conv(3×3) + Pool | 112 × 112 | 64 |
| 2 | 2 × Conv(3×3) + Pool | 56 × 56 | 128 |
| 3 | 3 × Conv(3×3) + Pool | 28 × 28 | 256 |
| 4 | 3 × Conv(3×3) + Pool | 14 × 14 | 512 |
| 5 | 3 × Conv(3×3) + Pool | 7 × 7 | 512 |

**Step 3 — Classification Head [1 mark]:**
Flatten → FC(4096) → FC(4096) → FC(1000) with dropout 0.5 after the first two FCs.

**Step 4 — Trend Analysis [2 marks]:**
- **Spatial resolution** halves after each pooling: 224 → 112 → 56 → 28 → 14 → 7.
- **Channel depth** doubles from 64 to 512, then plateaus.
- The design preserves the spatial-vs-channel "trade-off" — a hallmark of CNN backbones.

**Block Diagram:**
*(Use the diagram from Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Receptive Field Proof [3 marks]:**
For stride-1 convolutions, the effective receptive field grows as:

$$
\text{ERF}_{n+1} = \text{ERF}_n + (k - 1)
$$

For three 3×3 layers starting from ERF₁ = 3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ERF}_2 &= 3 + (3-1) = 5 \\
\text{ERF}_3 &= 5 + (3-1) = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, three 3×3 layers yield an ERF of **7×7**, equivalent to one 7×7 layer.

**Step 2 — Parameter Comparison [2 marks]:**

- One 7×7 layer: $P_{7} = 7 \times 7 \times C^2 = 49C^2$
- Three 3×3 layers: $P_{3 \times 3} = 3 \times (3 \times 3 \times C^2) = 27C^2$

Savings:

$$
\Delta P = 49C^2 - 27C^2 = 22C^2 \approx 44.9\%
$$

**Step 3 — Non-linearity Argument [2 marks]:**
A single 7×7 conv includes only **1 ReLU**, whereas three 3×3 convs include **3 ReLUs**. Each ReLU injects non-linearity, enabling the network to learn more discriminative decision boundaries without increasing the receptive field.

**Conclusion:** Stacked 3×3 convs are strictly superior — same ERF, fewer parameters, deeper non-linear representation.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Compute the **total number of trainable parameters** in VGG-16. Show layer-wise calculations clearly.

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of VGG-16 as a deep learning architecture. Comment on its suitability for transfer learning.

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

*(Refer to **Derivation 2** in Section 3.2 of these notes for the complete step-by-step calculation.)*

| Block | Calculation | Parameters |
| :--- | :--- | ---: |
| Conv Block-1 | 2 × (3·3·3·64 + 64) | 3,584 |
| Conv Block-2 | 2 × (3·3·64·128 + 128) | 147,712 |
| Conv Block-3 | 3 × (3·3·128·256 + 256) | 885,504 |
| Conv Block-4 | 3 × (3·3·256·512 + 512) | 3,540,480 |
| Conv Block-5 | 3 × (3·3·512·512 + 512) | 7,079,424 |
| FC-6 | 25088·4096 + 4096 | 102,764,544 |
| FC-7 | 4096·4096 + 4096 | 16,781,312 |
| FC-8 | 4096·1000 + 1000 | 4,097,000 |
| **Total** | | **≈ 138 million** |

**Observation:** FC layers contribute ~123.6 M (≈ 89 %) of the total parameters — a known inefficiency that motivated later architectures (e.g., ResNet) to use Global Average Pooling instead.

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Advantages [4 marks]:**

1. **Simplicity & Uniformity** — Every conv layer is 3×3, making the architecture easy to implement, modify, and analyze.
2. **Depth-Driven Performance** — Achieved 7.3 % top-5 error on ImageNet, a major improvement over AlexNet's 15.3 %.
3. **Excellent Transfer Learning Backbone** — Pre-trained VGG features generalize remarkably well to tasks like object detection, segmentation, and image captioning.
4. **Strong Feature Hierarchy** — Stacked 3×3 convs build rich, discriminative representations of objects and textures.

**Limitations [2 marks]:**

1. **Heavyweight** — 138 M parameters (~500 MB of storage) and ~15.5 GFLOPs make inference slow and memory-hungry.
2. **Vanishing Gradients** — Very deep plain networks without skip connections suffer from optimization difficulties, limiting further depth scaling.

**Transfer Learning Suitability [1 mark]:**
VGG-16 is **highly suitable** for transfer learning. The early layers capture universal features (edges, textures) while deeper layers specialize. Practitioners typically freeze the conv base and fine-tune the FC head on the target dataset, achieving state-of-the-art results even with limited training data.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in VGG-related Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing VGG-16 with VGG-19** — Students often interchange layer counts. Memorize: VGG-16 has **13 conv + 3 FC = 16 weight layers**; VGG-19 has **16 conv + 3 FC = 19 weight layers**.
> 2. **Forgetting to include bias terms** in parameter counts — VGG uses biases in EVERY conv and FC layer. The +C_out in the formula is **not optional**.
> 3. **Stating "VGG uses 1×1 convolutions"** — This is a hallmark of GoogLeNet/Inception, NOT VGG. Examiners deduct full marks for this error.
> 4. **Wrong padding interpretation** — VGG uses **p = 1** for 3×3 convs (same convolution). Forgetting this in dimension calculations gives a wrong feature-map size.
> 5. **Omitting FC-8 dimension** — The final FC layer is **1000**, not 4096 or 10. Examiners expect students to know the ImageNet class count.
> 6. **Confusing pooling stride with conv stride** — VGG's convolutions use **stride = 1**, but max-pooling uses **stride = 2**. Mixing them up causes cascading errors in spatial-dimension derivations.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Glance Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- ✅ VGG stands for **Visual Geometry Group**, Oxford (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2014).
- ✅ Core idea: **stack small 3×3 conv filters deep** instead of using larger kernels.
- ✅ Configurations: **VGG-11, VGG-13, VGG-16, VGG-19**.
- ✅ **All conv layers** use **3×3 filters, stride 1, padding 1 (same conv)**.
- ✅ **All pooling layers** use **2×2 max-pool with stride 2**.
- ✅ **Channel progression**: **64 → 128 → 256 → 512 → 512**.
- ✅ **Spatial progression**: **224 → 112 → 56 → 28 → 14 → 7**.
- ✅ Three stacked 3×3 convs = same **ERF of 7×7** as one 7×7 conv.
- ✅ Three 3×3 layers save **~44.9 % parameters** vs. one 7×7 layer.
- ✅ Three 3×3 layers add **3 ReLUs** vs. **1 ReLU** in a single 7×7 layer.
- ✅ VGG-16 has **~138 million parameters**; ~**89 %** reside in the FC layers.
- ✅ FC head: **4096 → 4096 → 1000** with **dropout 0.5**.
- ✅ VGG does **NOT** use 1×1 convolutions (that's GoogLeNet).
- ✅ Activation function after every layer: **ReLU**.
- ✅ VGG-16 won ILSVRC-2014 **Localization**; placed **2nd in Classification**.
- ✅ **Primary applications**: transfer learning, object detection backbones, style transfer.
- ✅ **Major limitation**: parameter bloat and slow inference — addressed by ResNet, DenseNet, and EfficientNet.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 🧠 Module 3 — Applications of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) Applications** refer to the diverse set of computer vision, pattern recognition, and signal-processing tasks where hierarchical spatial feature extraction (via convolution, pooling, and fully-connected layers) is exploited to map raw, high-dimensional inputs (images, video frames, volumetric scans, spectrograms) to meaningful outputs such as class labels, bounding boxes, pixel-wise masks, captions, or generative samples.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEITT745)**, CNN applications are studied as the **validation of the architectural choices** (convolution, pooling, receptive fields, depth) discussed earlier in Module 3. Each application essentially **tailors the backbone CNN** (LeNet, AlexNet, VGG, ResNet, etc.) with a task-specific **head** (classifier, detector, segmenter, etc.).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Doctor with a Magnifying Glass"**
>
> Imagine a doctor examining a chest X-ray. He does **not** try to understand the entire X-ray at once. Instead, he first uses a **low-power lens** to detect broad patterns (general shape, symmetry, darkness regions) → then switches to a **medium-power lens** to detect organs (heart silhouette, lungs) → and finally a **high-power lens** to spot abnormalities (nodules, fractures). Finally, he makes a **decision** ("this patient has pneumonia").
>
> A CNN does exactly the same:
> - **Early conv layers** = low-power lens (edges, color blobs)
> - **Deeper conv layers** = high-power lens (textures, object parts, full objects)
> - **Final FC / task head** = the doctor's decision
>
> Different **applications** simply correspond to different **types of decisions** the CNN is asked to make after the "magnification" pipeline.

## 1.3 Why CNNs Dominate Spatial Data

| Property of Raw Data | CNN Mechanism that Solves It |
|---|---|
| **High dimensionality** (e.g., $224 \times 224 \times 3 = 150{,}528$ values) | **Local receptive fields + weight sharing** drastically reduce parameters |
| **Spatial translation invariance** | **Pooling layers** + **convolutional weight sharing** |
| **Hierarchical feature composition** | **Stacked conv layers** (edges → textures → parts → objects) |
| **Shift, scale, and rotation tolerance** | **Data augmentation** + **deep feature hierarchies** |

## 1.4 Broad Taxonomy of CNN Applications

```
CNN Applications
├── 1. Image Classification
├── 2. Object Detection
│      ├── Two-stage: R-CNN, Fast R-CNN, Faster R-CNN
│      └── One-stage: YOLO, SSD
├── 3. Image Segmentation
│      ├── Semantic Segmentation (FCN, U-Net, SegNet, DeepLab)
│      └── Instance Segmentation (Mask R-CNN)
├── 4. Face Recognition & Verification
├── 5. Medical Image Analysis
├── 6. Video Analytics (Action Recognition, Tracking)
├── 7. Image Captioning & Visual Question Answering (VQA)
├── 8. Neural Style Transfer
├── 9. Generative Models (GAN, Diffusion)
├── 10. Pose Estimation & Keypoint Detection
├── 11. Optical Character Recognition (OCR)
└── 12. Autonomous Driving Perception
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 3 explicitly requires students to know **(a) Image Classification, (b) Object Detection (R-CNN family + YOLO), (c) Semantic & Instance Segmentation, (d) Transfer Learning, and (e) one creative application (Style Transfer / Image Captioning).** These are the **favourite ESE topics**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Application 1 — Image Classification

**Definition:** Map an input image $x \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ to a probability distribution over $K$ classes via a softmax head.

**Pipeline (KTU Board-Diagram Expectation):**
```
Input Image → [Conv → ReLU → Pool] × N → Flatten → FC → Softmax → Class Probabilities
```

**Mathematical Output:**
$$P(y=k \mid x; W) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}} \quad \text{(Softmax)}$$

$$L_{CE} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{y}_k \quad \text{(Cross-Entropy Loss)}$$

**Canonical Architectures (must know for ESE):**

| Network | Year | Depth | Key Innovation | Top-5 ImageNet Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LeNet-5** | 1998 | 7 layers | First CNN for digits | ~0.8% (MNIST) |
| **AlexNet** | 2012 | 8 layers | ReLU, Dropout, GPU training | 15.3% |
| **VGG-16** | 2014 | 16 layers | $3 \times 3$ small filters uniformly | 7.3% |
| **GoogLeNet (Inception)** | 2014 | 22 layers | Inception module, $1 \times 1$ bottleneck | 6.7% |
| **ResNet-50** | 2015 | 50 layers | Residual / skip connections | 3.57% |

## 2.2 Application 2 — Object Detection

> [!NOTE]
> **Object Detection = Classification + Localization**, generalized to multiple objects per image.

**Output must encode four things per detected object:**
1. Class label $c$
2. Bounding box $(x, y, w, h)$
3. Confidence score $s$
4. (Optional) Mask

### 2.2.1 R-CNN Family (Region-based — Two-Stage)

**R-CNN (Girshick et al., 2014):**
- Step 1: **Selective Search** generates ~**2000 region proposals** per image.
- Step 2: Each proposal is **warped** to fixed size ($227 \times 227$).
- Step 3: Each warped region is fed to **AlexNet** → 4096-d feature vector.
- Step 4: **SVM** classifies each feature vector; a **bounding-box regressor** refines $(x, y, w, h)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Drawback:** 2000 CNN forward passes per image ⇒ **inference time ≈ 47 s/image**. Almost unusable in real time.

**Fast R-CNN (2015):** Solves this by running the CNN **once on the full image**, then projecting proposals onto the shared feature map using a **RoI Pooling** layer. Speed jumps to **~0.3 s/image**.

**Faster R-CNN (2015):** Replaces Selective Search with a learned **Region Proposal Network (RPN)**. RPN slides a small network over the feature map and outputs objectness scores + box refinements at each spatial location using **anchor boxes** (multiple scales & aspect ratios).

### 2.2.2 YOLO — You Only Look Once (One-Stage)

Redmond et al., 2016. **Frames detection as a single regression problem.**

- The image is divided into an $S \times S$ grid (e.g., $7 \times 7$).
- Each grid cell predicts:
  - $B$ bounding boxes $(x, y, w, h)$ + confidence
  - Class probabilities $P(c_i)$
- The whole image is passed **once** through a CNN ⇒ real-time (~**45 FPS** on YOLOv1, **>150 FPS** on YOLOv5-nano).

**YOLO Loss (simplified):**
$$L = \lambda_{coord} \sum_{i,j}^{S^2} \sum_{b}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{obj}\left[(x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 + (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + (\sqrt{w_i} - \sqrt{\hat{w}_i})^2 + (\sqrt{h_i} - \sqrt{\hat{h}_i})^2\right]$$
$$+ \sum_{i,j}^{S^2} \sum_{b}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{obj}(C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2 + \lambda_{noobj} \sum_{i,j}^{S^2} \sum_{b}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{noobj}(C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2$$
$$+ \sum_{i,j}^{S^2} \mathbb{1}_{i}^{obj} \sum_{c \in classes}(p_i(c) - \hat{p}_i(c))^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $\sqrt{\cdot}$ on width/height is a YOLO-v1 trick to **penalize small-box errors more fairly** than large-box errors.

### 2.2.3 SSD — Single Shot MultiBox Detector

- Multi-scale feature maps (from different conv layers) are used to detect objects at **different scales**.
- Better small-object detection than YOLOv1.

### 2.2.4 Comparison Table (HIGH-YIELD for ESE)

| Method | Type | mAP (PASCAL VOC) | Speed (FPS) | Key Idea |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| R-CNN | Two-stage | 66.0 | 0.05 | 2000 region proposals per image |
| Fast R-CNN | Two-stage | 70.0 | 0.5 | RoI Pooling, shared features |
| Faster R-CNN | Two-stage | 78.8 | 7 | RPN + anchor boxes |
| YOLOv1 | One-stage | 63.7 | 45 | Grid-based regression |
| SSD | One-stage | 74.3 | 22 | Multi-scale feature maps |

## 2.3 Application 3 — Image Segmentation

**Two flavours (must distinguish in the exam):**

| Type | Question Answered | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Semantic Segmentation** | "What category does each pixel belong to?" | All "car" pixels are blue |
| **Instance Segmentation** | "Which individual object does each pixel belong to?" | Car 1, Car 2, Car 3 are red, green, yellow |

### 2.3.1 Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) — Long et al., 2015

- Replaces the FC layers of a classification CNN (e.g., VGG) with **$1 \times 1$ convolutions** that act as pixel-wise classifiers.
- Adds **skip connections** from earlier, higher-resolution layers to the upsampled output, producing **FCN-32s, FCN-16s, FCN-8s** (8s is the most accurate).

### 2.3.2 U-Net (Ronneberger, 2015) — Biomedical Standard

**Symmetric encoder-decoder with skip connections.** Encoder downsamples; decoder upsamples; skip connections preserve fine spatial detail lost in pooling.

**U-Net Equations (HIGH-YIELD):**
$$h_{\text{enc}}^{(l)} = \text{Pool}\big(\text{ReLU}(\text{Conv}_{3\times3}(h_{\text{enc}}^{(l-1)}))\big)$$
$$h_{\text{dec}}^{(l)} = \text{ReLU}\big(\text{Conv}_{3\times3}([\text{Up}(h_{\text{dec}}^{(l+1)}),\ \text{skip}^{(l)}])\big)$$
$$\hat{Y} = \text{Conv}_{1\times 1}(h_{\text{dec}}^{(0)})$$

The $[\cdot, \cdot]$ denotes **channel-wise concatenation** along the skip path.

### 2.3.3 SegNet

- Encoder–decoder but **no skip connections**.
- Transfers **pooling indices** from encoder to decoder (memory-efficient).

### 2.3.4 Mask R-CNN (Instance Segmentation)

- Extends **Faster R-CNN** with a parallel **mask branch**.
- Replaces RoI Pooling with **RoIAlign** (fixes pixel-misalignment due to quantization).
- Outputs: class + box + **binary mask per detected object**.

## 2.4 Application 4 — Face Recognition & Verification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FaceNet (Schroff et al., 2015)** introduced the **Triplet Loss**, mapping faces to a 128-D **Euclidean embedding space** where:
> $$d(f(A), f(P)) \ll d(f(A), f(N))$$
> where $A$ = anchor, $P$ = positive (same person), $N$ = negative (different person).

$$\mathcal{L}_{triplet} = \sum_{i}^{N} \max\big(0,\ d(f(A_i), f(P_i))^2 - d(f(A_i), f(N_i))^2 + \alpha\big)$$

The margin $\alpha$ enforces a gap between positive and negative distances.

## 2.5 Application 5 — Medical Image Analysis

- **Tumor segmentation** in MRI (U-Net family — BraTS challenge).
- **Diabetic retinopathy** detection (Google's algorithm using Inception-v3 on retinal fundus photos).
- **Pneumonia detection** from chest X-rays (transfer-learned ResNet).
- **Pathology slide classification** (weakly-supervised CNNs).

## 2.6 Application 6 — Image Captioning

**CNN + RNN/LSTM hybrid architecture (Show and Tell — Vinyals et al., 2015):**

$$\mathbf{v} = \text{CNN}(I) \quad \text{(image embedding)}$$
$$p(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1}, I) = \text{LSTM}(\mathbf{v},\ w_{1:t-1})$$

The CNN acts as an **image encoder**; the LSTM acts as a **sentence decoder**.

## 2.7 Application 7 — Neural Style Transfer (Gatys et al., 2016)

Combines **content** of one image with **style** of another by matching:
- **Content loss** — high-level feature activations of a pre-trained CNN (e.g., VGG).
- **Style loss** — **Gram matrices** of feature activations at multiple layers.

$$\mathcal{L}_{total} = \alpha \mathcal{L}_{content} + \beta \mathcal{L}_{style}$$

## 2.8 Application 8 — Generative Models (CNN-based)

- **DCGAN** (Radford et al., 2015) — uses **transposed convolutions** (a.k.a. deconvolutions) to upsample noise $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ into fake images.
- **StyleGAN** (Karras et al., 2019) — disentangled style control via AdaIN (Adaptive Instance Normalization).

## 2.9 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (HANDY-REFERENCE TABLE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-know for KTU ESE**. Memorize them with units / shapes.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Softmax | $\sigma(z_k) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Probabilities sum to 1 |
| 2 | Cross-Entropy | $L = -\sum_k y_k \log \hat{y}_k$ | Used in classification |
| 3 | IoU (Jaccard) | $\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Detects overlap; $>0.5$ ⇒ correct |
| 4 | mAP | $\text{mAP} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{AP}_i$ | Mean of per-class Average Precisions |
| 5 | AP (Area under PR) | $\text{AP} = \int_0^1 p(r)\,dr$ | Computed via 11-point or all-point interpolation |
| 6 | Triplet Loss | $\max(0, d^2(A,P) - d^2(A,N) + \alpha)$ | FaceNet |
| 7 | L2 Distance | $d(f_a, f_p) = \Vert f_a - f_p \Vert_2$ | Embedding similarity |
| 8 | Style Loss (Gram) | $\mathcal{L}_{style} = \sum_l w_l \Vert G^l - \hat{G}^l \Vert_F^2$ | $\Vert \cdot \Vert_F$ = Frobenius norm |
| 9 | Content Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{content} = \frac{1}{2}\Vert F^l - P^l \Vert_2^2$ | Activations of conv layer $l$ |
| 10 | Dice Coefficient | $\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Medical segmentation metric |
| 11 | Pixel Accuracy | $\frac{\text{Correct pixels}}{\text{Total pixels}}$ | Semantic segmentation metric |
| 12 | Anchor box (Faster R-CNN) | Aspect ratios $\{1:1, 1:2, 2:1\}$ at $k$ scales | Pre-defined multi-scale priors |

- **Healthcare** — automated diagnosis from radiology images.
- **Automotive** — Tesla / Waymo / Mobileye perception stacks (CNN-based detection + segmentation + depth).
- **Agriculture** — crop disease detection from drone images.
- **Manufacturing** — surface defect detection on production lines.
- **Surveillance** — anomaly detection, person re-identification.
- **Retail** — cashierless stores (Amazon Go).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Walk-Throughs & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE loves **worked numericals** on (a) IoU / mAP, (b) receptive-field / feature-map size calculation, and (c) softmax probability. We cover all three.

## 3.1 Worked Numerical #1 — Intersection over Union (IoU)

**Problem:** Ground-truth bounding box $B_{gt} = (x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2) = (50, 60, 150, 180)$. Predicted box $B_p = (40, 70, 160, 170)$. Compute **IoU**.

**Step 1 — Compute Intersection rectangle.**
$$x_{A} = \max(x_1^{gt}, x_1^{p}) = \max(50, 40) = 50$$
$$y_{A} = \max(y_1^{gt}, y_1^{p}) = \max(60, 70) = 70$$
$$x_{B} = \min(x_2^{gt}, x_2^{p}) = \min(150, 160) = 150$$
$$y_{B} = \min(y_2^{gt}, y_2^{p}) = \min(180, 170) = 170$$

**Step 2 — Verify intersection is valid.**
$$w_i = x_B - x_A = 150 - 50 = 100,\quad h_i = y_B - y_A = 170 - 70 = 100$$
$$A_{\text{inter}} = w_i \times h_i = 100 \times 100 = 10{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Compute each box's area.**
$$A_{gt} = (150 - 50) \times (180 - 60) = 100 \times 120 = 12{,}000$$
$$A_p = (160 - 40) \times (170 - 70) = 120 \times 100 = 12{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Union area.**
$$A_{\text{union}} = A_{gt} + A_p - A_{\text{inter}} = 12{,}000 + 12{,}000 - 10{,}000 = 14{,}000$$

**Step 5 — IoU.**
$$\text{IoU} = \frac{A_{\text{inter}}}{A_{\text{union}}} = \frac{10{,}000}{14{,}000} = 0.7143$$

> **Conclusion:** Since $\text{IoU} = 0.7143 > 0.5$ threshold, the prediction is a **True Positive**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical #2 — Feature Map Size Calculation (VGG-style)

**Problem:** Input $224 \times 224 \times 3$. Apply:
- Conv $3 \times 3$, 64 filters, stride $1$, **same padding**.
- Conv $3 \times 3$, 64 filters, stride $1$, **same padding**.
- MaxPool $2 \times 2$, stride $2$.
- Conv $3 \times 3$, 128 filters, stride $1$, **same padding**.
- MaxPool $2 \times 2$, stride $2$.

**Formula:**
$$\text{Output size} = \left\lfloor \frac{W - F + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$$

**Layer-wise trace:**

| Layer | Operation | $W$ in | $F$ | $P$ | $S$ | $W$ out |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conv $3\times 3$ | 224 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 224 |
| 2 | Conv $3\times 3$ | 224 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 224 |
| 3 | MaxPool $2 \times 2$ | 224 | 2 | 0 | 2 | 112 |
| 4 | Conv $3 \times 3$ | 112 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 112 |
| 5 | MaxPool $2 \times 2$ | 112 | 2 | 0 | 2 | 56 |

**Final output volume = $56 \times 56 \times 128$.**

**Parameter count check (Layer 1):**
$$\text{Params} = (F_h \times F_w \times C_{\text{in}} + 1) \times C_{\text{out}} = (3 \cdot 3 \cdot 3 + 1) \times 64 = 1{,}792$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical #3 — Softmax + Cross-Entropy

**Problem:** CNN output logits $z = [2.0,\ 1.0,\ 0.1]$ for a 3-class problem. True label = class 0 (one-hot $y = [1, 0, 0]$). Compute the **softmax probabilities** and the **cross-entropy loss**.

**Step 1 — Exponentiate.**
$$e^{2.0} = 7.389,\quad e^{1.0} = 2.718,\quad e^{0.1} = 1.105$$
$$\sum_j e^{z_j} = 7.389 + 2.718 + 1.105 = 11.212$$

**Step 2 — Softmax.**
$$\hat{y}_0 = \frac{7.389}{11.212} = 0.6590$$
$$\hat{y}_1 = \frac{2.718}{11.212} = 0.2424$$
$$\hat{y}_2 = \frac{1.105}{11.212} = 0.0986$$

**Step 3 — Cross-Entropy.**
$$L_{CE} = -\sum_k y_k \log \hat{y}_k = -(1 \cdot \log(0.6590) + 0 + 0) = -\log(0.6590) = 0.4170$$

> **Conclusion:** Loss ≈ **0.417 nats**. The model is moderately confident in the correct class.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical #4 — Receptive Field Calculation

**Problem:** Stack of 3 conv layers, each with $F = 3$, $S = 1$, no padding. Compute the **receptive field** of a single output neuron.

**Receptive field recurrence (each layer):**
$$r_{l} = r_{l-1} + (F_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l-1} s_i$$

**Computation:**

| Layer $l$ | $F_l$ | $S_l$ | $\prod_{i<l} S_i$ | $r_l$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | 1 | 1 | $1 + (3-1)(1) = 3$ |
| 2 | 3 | 1 | 1 | $3 + (3-1)(1) = 5$ |
| 3 | 3 | 1 | 1 | $5 + (3-1)(1) = 7$ |

**Receptive field = $7 \times 7$ pixels** for the final output neuron.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right CNN Application

| If your task is… | Use… | Why |
|---|---|---|
| "Is this a cat or a dog?" | **Image Classification** (VGG, ResNet) | One label per image |
| "Where are all the cars in this street?" | **Object Detection** (YOLO, Faster R-CNN) | Need boxes + class |
| "Outline the tumour pixel by pixel" | **Semantic Segmentation** (U-Net, FCN) | One label per pixel |
| "Distinguish tumour 1 from tumour 2" | **Instance Segmentation** (Mask R-CNN) | Per-object masks |
| "Whose face is this?" | **Face Recognition** (FaceNet, ArcFace) | Embedding + metric |
| "Describe this photo" | **Image Captioning** (CNN-LSTM) | Sequence output |
| "Make Van Gogh-ify my photo" | **Style Transfer** (Gatys) | Pixel generation |
| "Generate fake faces" | **DCGAN, StyleGAN** | Generative |
| "Detect actions in video" | **Two-stream / 3D-CNN / I3D** | Temporal features |
| "Recognise handwritten digits" | **LeNet / small CNN + CTC** | OCR |

---

## 3.6 Derivation — Why Gram Matrix Encodes Style (Style Transfer)

**Claim:** The correlations between feature channels of a CNN encode style information (textures, brush strokes) but not content.

**Derivation:**

Let $F^l \in \mathbb{R}^{N_l \times M_l}$ be the activations of layer $l$ flattened: $N_l$ = number of filters, $M_l$ = height × width of the feature map.

The **Gram matrix** is:
$$G_{ij}^l = \sum_{k=1}^{M_l} F_{ik}^l F_{jk}^l$$

Step 1: $G_{ij}^l$ is the **inner product** between feature maps $i$ and $j$.

Step 2: $G_{ij}^l$ is large when filters $i$ and $j$ **co-activate** at the same spatial locations.

Step 3: Co-activation patterns describe **textures and stylistic repetitions** but discard **spatial arrangement** of features.

Step 4: Therefore, matching Gram matrices $\Vert G^l - \hat{G}^l \Vert_F^2$ enforces **style consistency** while allowing the content spatial layout to remain flexible.

$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}_{style}^l = \frac{1}{4 N_l^2 M_l^2} \sum_{i,j} (G_{ij}^l - \hat{G}_{ij}^l)^2}$$

The factor $\frac{1}{4 N_l^2 M_l^2}$ normalises the loss across layers of different sizes.

---

## 3.7 Derivation — Triplet Loss Gradient Insight

Given the embedding $f(\cdot)$ parameterised by CNN weights $\theta$, the loss:
$$\mathcal{L} = \max\big(0,\ \Vert f(A) - f(P) \Vert_2^2 - \Vert f(A) - f(N) \Vert_2^2 + \alpha\big)$$

When the triplet is **active** (i.e., the margin condition is violated):
- **Pull** $f(A)$ and $f(P)$ **closer** by increasing the loss w.r.t. the positive distance.
- **Push** $f(A)$ and $f(N)$ **apart** by decreasing the loss w.r.t. the negative distance.

Gradients:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial f(A)} = 2(f(N) - f(P))$$
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial f(P)} = 2(f(P) - f(A))$$
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial f(N)} = 2(f(A) - f(N))$$

This is the same "anchor-centre repulsion" force that drives **metric learning** embeddings to be discriminative.

---

## 3.8 Comparison of Loss Functions Across Applications (Analytical Matrix)

| Application | Primary Loss | Secondary Loss | Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| Classification | Cross-Entropy | Label Smoothing | Accuracy, Top-5 |
| Detection (R-CNN) | Classification CE + L2 box | — | mAP, IoU |
| Detection (YOLO) | Sum-squared box + CE | Objectness | mAP, FPS |
| Semantic Seg. | Pixel-wise CE | Dice Loss | mIoU, Pixel Acc. |
| Instance Seg. | Mask BCE + box L1 | Classification | mAP (mask) |
| Face Recognition | Triplet Loss | Centre Loss | Accuracy, FAR/FRR |
| Captioning | Word-level CE (over time) | — | BLEU, CIDEr |
| Style Transfer | Content + Style (L2) | Total Variation | Visual |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid — KTU Board-Ready)

## 4.1 Master Architecture — The Generic CNN Application Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Image Input] --> B[Preprocessing: Resize 224x224, Normalize]
    B --> C[CNN Backbone: Conv + ReLU + Pool]
    C --> D[Feature Map H x W x D]
    D --> E{Task Head}
    E -->|Classification| F[Flatten + FC + Softmax]
    E -->|Detection| G[RPN + RoI + BBox Reg + Class]
    E -->|Segmentation| H[Upsample + 1x1 Conv + Pixel Softmax]
    E -->|Face Recognition| I[Embedding 128D + L2 Normalize]
    E -->|Captioning| J[LSTM Decoder Word by Word]
    E -->|Style Transfer| K[Gram Matrix Matching via VGG]
    F --> L[Output]
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 YOLO Detection Pipeline (Unified Single-Pass)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Image 448x448x3] --> B[CNN Backbone: 24 Conv + 2 FC]
    B --> C[Tensor 7x7x30]
    C --> D[Decode: 2 boxes per cell + 20 class probs]
    D --> E[Confidence Threshold Filter]
    E --> F[Non Maximum Suppression NMS]
    F --> G[Final Boxes with Class Labels]
```

## 4.3 Faster R-CNN Two-Stage Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Image] --> B[CNN Backbone Feature Map]
    B --> C[Region Proposal Network RPN with Anchors]
    C --> D[Object vs Background Scores]
    C --> E[BBox Refinement Offsets]
    D --> F[Top N Proposals]
    E --> F
    F --> G[RoI Pooling Fixed Size]
    G --> H[FC Layers]
    H --> I[Softmax Classifier]
    H --> J[BBox Regressor]
    I --> K[Final Detection]
    J --> K
```

## 4.4 U-Net Architecture (Segmentation Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input 572x572x1] --> B[Conv Block: 64]
    B --> B1[MaxPool 2x2]
    B1 --> C[Conv Block: 128]
    C --> C1[MaxPool 2x2]
    C1 --> D[Conv Block: 256]
    D --> D1[MaxPool 2x2]
    D1 --> E[Conv Block: 512]
    E --> E1[MaxPool 2x2]
    E1 --> F[Bottleneck 1024]
    F --> G[UpConv 512]
    G --> H[Concatenate with E]
    H --> I[Conv Block: 512]
    I --> I1[UpConv 256]
    I1 --> J[Concatenate with D]
    J --> K[Conv Block: 256]
    K --> K1[UpConv 128]
    K1 --> L[Concatenate with C]
    L --> M[Conv Block: 128]
    M --> M1[UpConv 64]
    M1 --> N[Concatenate with B]
    N --> O[Conv Block: 64]
    O --> P[Final 1x1 Conv to 2 classes]
    P --> Q[Segmentation Map]
```

## 4.5 Face Recognition — Triplet Loss Inference Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Anchor Image] --> C[Shared CNN with Synced Weights]
    B[Positive Image] --> C
    C2[Negative Image] --> C
    C --> D[Embedding fa]
    C --> E[Embedding fp]
    C --> F[Embedding fn]
    D --> G[Triplet Loss: d fa fp squared minus d fa fn squared plus alpha]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Backprop Updates CNN Weights]
```

## 4.6 Image Captioning — CNN Encoder + LSTM Decoder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Image I] --> B[CNN Encoder Pre Trained]
    B --> C[Vector v 4096D]
    C --> D[LSTM Decoder Initial State]
    D --> E[Step 1: Predict Word W1]
    E --> F[Step 2: Predict Word W2]
    F --> G[Step 3: Predict Word W3]
    G --> H[Step T: Predict End Token]
    H --> I[Generated Caption Sentence]
```

## 4.7 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right CNN Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Computer Vision Task] --> B{Need one label per image?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Image Classification CNN]
    B -->|No| D{Need bounding boxes?}
    D -->|Yes| E{Real time required?}
    E -->|Yes| F[One Stage YOLO or SSD]
    E -->|No| G[Two Stage Faster RCNN for max accuracy]
    D -->|No| H{Need per pixel labels?}
    H -->|Yes| I{Distinguish individual objects?}
    I -->|No| J[Semantic Seg: FCN or UNet]
    I -->|Yes| K[Instance Seg: Mask RCNN]
    H -->|No| L{Identify a person from face?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Face Recognition with Triplet Loss]
    L -->|No| N{Generate text from image?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Image Captioning CNN plus LSTM]
    N -->|No| P{Transfer artistic style?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Neural Style Transfer via VGG]
    P -->|No| R[Custom Hybrid Architecture]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Questions (with Model Answers)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Differentiate between **semantic segmentation** and **instance segmentation**.

**Model Answer:**
Semantic segmentation assigns a class label to every pixel in the image (e.g., all "car" pixels get the same label), but it does **not** distinguish between individual instances of the same class. Instance segmentation, on the other hand, distinguishes **each individual object** of the same class — e.g., Car 1, Car 2, and Car 3 are labelled differently. Semantic segmentation uses architectures such as FCN, U-Net, and SegNet, whereas instance segmentation typically uses Mask R-CNN. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is **IoU** and why is it important in object detection?

**Model Answer:**
Intersection over Union (IoU) is the ratio of the area of overlap between the predicted bounding box and the ground-truth bounding box to the area of their union:
$$\text{IoU} = \frac{\text{Area of Intersection}}{\text{Area of Union}} = \frac{\vert B_p \cap B_{gt} \vert}{\vert B_p \cup B_{gt} \vert}$$
It is important because it quantifies localization accuracy. A detection is considered a **True Positive** when IoU exceeds a threshold (typically **0.5**). IoU is also used in **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** to suppress duplicate detections of the same object. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q.A (a)** Explain the **architecture of YOLO** for object detection. Describe how YOLO frames detection as a single regression problem and explain its loss function. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture:**
YOLO (You Only Look Once), proposed by Redmon et al. (2016), treats object detection as a **single regression problem** from image pixels to bounding-box coordinates and class probabilities. The architecture is inspired by GoogLeNet and consists of **24 convolutional layers** followed by **2 fully-connected layers**.

**Operation:**
1. The input image is resized to **448 × 448**.
2. A single CNN pass produces an output tensor of size **$S \times S \times (5B + C)$**, where:
   - $S \times S$ = grid (e.g., $7 \times 7$),
   - $B$ = number of bounding boxes per cell (typically 2),
   - $C$ = number of classes (e.g., 20 for PASCAL VOC).
3. Each grid cell predicts:
   - $B$ boxes, each with $(x, y, w, h)$ and a confidence score,
   - $C$ conditional class probabilities.
4. Final detections are obtained by **thresholding** confidence and applying **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)**.

**Loss Function (Multi-part):**
$$L = \lambda_{coord}\sum_{i,j,b}\mathbb{1}_{ij}^{obj}\left[(x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 + (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + (\sqrt{w_i} - \sqrt{\hat{w}_i})^2 + (\sqrt{h_i} - \sqrt{\hat{h}_i})^2\right]$$
$$+ \sum_{i,j,b}\mathbb{1}_{ij}^{obj}(C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2 + \lambda_{noobj}\sum_{i,j,b}\mathbb{1}_{ij}^{noobj}(C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2$$
$$+ \sum_{i,j}\mathbb{1}_{i}^{obj}\sum_{c}(p_i(c) - \hat{p}_i(c))^2$$

- $\lambda_{coord} = 5$ and $\lambda_{noobj} = 0.5$ to **balance** the loss.
- The $\sqrt{\cdot}$ on $w$ and $h$ makes small-box errors comparable to large-box errors.

**Advantages:** YOLO is extremely fast (45 FPS real-time on YOLOv1), reasons globally about the image (low background error), and learns generalized object representations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q.A (b)** Explain the **architecture of U-Net** for semantic segmentation. Why is it well-suited for biomedical image segmentation? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture:**
U-Net (Ronneberger, 2015) is a **symmetric encoder-decoder** ("U-shaped") CNN that outputs a **per-pixel class map** of the same resolution as the input.

- **Encoder (Contracting Path):** Repeated blocks of two $3 \times 3$ convolutions (unpadded), each followed by ReLU, and a $2 \times 2$ max-pool with stride 2 for downsampling. Channel count doubles at each stage (64 → 128 → 256 → 512).
- **Bottleneck:** Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions at the lowest spatial resolution.
- **Decoder (Expanding Path):** Each stage does a $2 \times 2$ **transposed convolution** (up-conv) to halve the channels and double the spatial size.
- **Skip Connections:** At each decoder level, the corresponding encoder feature map is **cropped and concatenated** with the upsampled features. This preserves fine spatial detail lost during pooling.
- **Final Layer:** A $1 \times 1$ conv maps the 64-channel decoder output to the desired number of classes.

**Loss:** Pixel-wise **cross-entropy** or **Dice loss** for class imbalance.

**Why suited for biomedical images:**
1. **Skip connections** preserve high-resolution boundary details critical for organ/tumor segmentation.
2. It can be trained **end-to-end** from very few annotated images (data augmentation with elastic deformations is standard).
3. The **fully-convolutional** nature allows it to handle **arbitrary input sizes** (sliding-window inference).
4. It achieves **state-of-the-art** results on ISBI cell-tracking challenges, BraTS brain-tumor segmentation, and many medical tasks. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **R-CNN family** of object detectors — R-CNN, Fast R-CNN, and Faster R-CNN. Compare their speed and accuracy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**R-CNN (Girshick, 2014):**
- Uses **Selective Search** to generate ~**2000 region proposals** per image.
- Each proposal is **warped** to a fixed $227 \times 227$ size and passed through **AlexNet** to get a 4096-D feature vector.
- An **SVM** classifies each feature vector; a **bounding-box regressor** refines coordinates.
- **Drawback:** 2000 CNN forward passes ⇒ **47 s/image**. Infeasible for real-time.

**Fast R-CNN (Girshick, 2015):**
- The **whole image** is passed through a CNN **once**, producing a shared feature map.
- Region proposals are projected onto the feature map and a **RoI Pooling** layer converts each region to a fixed $7 \times 7$ feature grid.
- Two sibling heads: a **softmax classifier** and a **bounding-box regressor** are trained jointly with a multi-task loss:
$$\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{cls}(p, u) + \lambda [u \geq 1]\ \mathcal{L}_{box}(t^u, v)$$
- **Speed:** ~0.3 s/image (~150× faster than R-CNN).

**Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015):**
- Replaces Selective Search with a learned **Region Proposal Network (RPN)**.
- RPN slides over the feature map and at each location uses **anchor boxes** of multiple scales and aspect ratios to predict objectness scores and box offsets.
- RPN and Fast R-CNN detector **share features** and are trained in an alternating fashion.
- **Speed:** ~7 FPS, with **state-of-the-art accuracy (mAP ≈ 78.8 on VOC2007).**

**Comparison:**

| Method | Inference | mAP (VOC) | Bottleneck |
|---|---|---|---|
| R-CNN | ~47 s | 66.0 | Repeated CNN |
| Fast R-CNN | ~0.3 s | 70.0 | Selective Search (CPU) |
| Faster R-CNN | ~0.14 s | 78.8 | None significant |

**(7 marks)**

---

**Q.B (b)** Explain **Neural Style Transfer** using CNNs. Derive the role of the **Gram matrix** in capturing style. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Neural Style Transfer (Gatys et al., 2016) synthesizes a new image $x$ that combines the **content** of a content image $p$ with the **style** of a style image $a$. A pre-trained VGG-19 is used as a fixed feature extractor.

**Content Loss** (using activations of layer $l$):
$$\mathcal{L}_{content}(x, p, l) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}(F_{ij}^l - P_{ij}^l)^2$$
where $F^l$ and $P^l$ are feature maps of the generated and content images at layer $l$.

**Style Loss** (using Gram matrices at multiple layers):
The Gram matrix $G_{ij}^l = \sum_k F_{ik}^l F_{jk}^l$ captures the **correlations between filter responses**. Co-activation patterns correspond to **textures**; they are **location-invariant**.

The style loss at layer $l$:
$$\mathcal{L}_{style}^l(x, a) = \frac{1}{4 N_l^2 M_l^2}\sum_{i,j}(G_{ij}^l - A_{ij}^l)^2$$

Total style loss:
$$\mathcal{L}_{style}(x, a) = \sum_l w_l \mathcal{L}_{style}^l(x, a)$$

Total loss (to be minimized over generated image $x$):
$$\mathcal{L}_{total}(x, p, a) = \alpha \mathcal{L}_{content}(x, p) + \beta \mathcal{L}_{style}(x, a)$$

$\alpha / \beta$ controls the **content–style trade-off**.

**Why Gram matrix captures style (derivation):**
- $G_{ij}^l$ is the **inner product** of the $i$-th and $j$-th feature maps of layer $l$.
- A high value means the two filters **co-activate** at the same locations.
- Co-activation statistics describe **textures, brush strokes, colour palettes** — exactly the stylistic aspects.
- It **discards spatial layout** (where features occur) but **preserves feature co-occurrence** (what features occur together).
- Matching Gram matrices therefore forces the generated image to reproduce the **same texture statistics** as the style image.

**Optimization:** Backpropagate through VGG to update the **pixels of $x$** directly (the VGG weights remain frozen). Use L-BFGS optimizer. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (as flagged by KTU Board Examiners):**
> 1. **Conflating YOLO and Faster R-CNN:** Students write "YOLO is two-stage" or "Faster R-CNN uses grid cells" — both are **wrong**. YOLO = **one-stage grid-based**; Faster R-CNN = **two-stage RPN-based**.
> 2. **Confusing Semantic and Instance Segmentation:** "U-Net performs instance segmentation" is a frequent error. U-Net is **semantic**.
> 3. **Omitting the $\sqrt{\cdot}$ in YOLO loss:** Examiners specifically look for the square-root trick on width/height.
> 4. **Forgetting Skip Connections in U-Net:** When asked "why U-Net works well for medical images?", the answer **must mention skip connections preserving boundary detail**.
> 5. **No justification for IoU threshold = 0.5:** Examiners expect a one-line justification ("standard PASCAL VOC convention").
> 6. **Mixing up IoU and Dice coefficient:** Dice is a different formula:
> $$\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{IoU}}{1 + \text{IoU}}$$
> 7. **Style transfer:** Students forget that the **VGG weights are frozen** — the optimization is over the **image pixels**, not network weights.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 25 High-Yield Points — KTU ESE Rapid Revision**

**A. Foundational Concepts**
- [ ] CNN = Conv + ReLU + Pool blocks, ends in a **task-specific head**.
- [ ] Receptive field grows with depth: $r_l = r_{l-1} + (F_l - 1)\prod s_i$.
- [ ] Weight sharing ⇒ translation equivariance; pooling ⇒ translation invariance.

**B. Image Classification**
- [ ] Softmax + cross-entropy; Top-5 error on ImageNet is the standard metric.
- [ ] Landmark architectures: **LeNet → AlexNet → VGG → GoogLeNet → ResNet**.
- [ ] ResNet's residual block: $H(x) = F(x) + x$ solves vanishing gradients.

**C. Object Detection**
- [ ] **Two-stage** = region proposal + classification (R-CNN family).
- [ ] **One-stage** = dense prediction in single pass (YOLO, SSD).
- [ ] **IoU** = intersection / union; threshold = 0.5 for TP/FP.
- [ ] **mAP** = mean of per-class Average Precisions (area under Precision-Recall curve).
- [ ] **Anchor boxes** in Faster R-CNN handle multi-scale objects.
- [ ] **NMS** removes overlapping duplicate detections.

**D. YOLO Specifics**
- [ ] $S \times S$ grid; $B$ boxes + $C$ class probs per cell.
- [ ] Loss = localization + confidence + classification, with $\lambda_{coord}=5$, $\lambda_{noobj}=0.5$.
- [ ] $\sqrt{w}, \sqrt{h}$ trick for scale-invariance.

**E. Segmentation**
- [ ] **FCN** replaces FC layers with $1 \times 1$ convs; uses skip connections (FCN-32s, 16s, 8s).
- [ ] **U-Net** = symmetric encoder-decoder with skip connections.
- [ ] **SegNet** = encoder-decoder with pooling-index transfer (no skips).
- [ ] **Mask R-CNN** = Faster R-CNN + parallel mask branch + RoIAlign.

**F. Face Recognition**
- [ ] **FaceNet** uses Triplet Loss in a 128-D Euclidean embedding space.
- [ ] Triplet = (Anchor, Positive, Negative); loss enforces a margin $\alpha$.

**G. Image Captioning**
- [ ] **CNN encoder** + **LSTM decoder**.
- [ ] Image embedding $v$ initializes LSTM hidden state.

**H. Style Transfer**
- [ ] Uses **pre-trained VGG** as a feature extractor (frozen).
- [ ] **Content loss** = MSE between feature activations.
- [ ] **Style loss** = MSE between **Gram matrices** of feature activations.
- [ ] Optimization is over **image pixels**, not network weights.

**I. Generative Models (brief)**
- [ ] **DCGAN** uses transposed convolutions to upsample noise $z$ to images.
- [ ] **StyleGAN** uses AdaIN for disentangled style control.

**J. Transfer Learning**
- [ ] Pre-trained on ImageNet → fine-tune on small custom dataset.
- [ ] Freeze early conv layers (generic features) + fine-tune later layers + classifier head.

**K. Performance & Engineering**
- [ ] YOLO = real-time (45+ FPS), Faster R-CNN = max accuracy.
- [ ] U-Net = few-data friendly; works with heavy augmentation.
- [ ] mIoU is the standard segmentation metric; Dice is common in medical.

> **Final Exam Tip:** For a 14-mark question, aim for **1 neat diagram + 4–5 short paragraphs + the core equation + 1 comparison table + 1 numerical example**. KTU examiners reward **structure, diagrams, and formulae on the answer sheet**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformers and Pre-trained Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT745 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transformers and Pre-trained Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Transformer Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **Transformer** is a deep learning architecture introduced by Vaswani et al. (2017) in *"Attention Is All You Need"* that relies entirely on a **self-attention mechanism** to compute global dependencies between input and output sequences, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions. It forms the backbone of all modern **Pre-trained Networks (PTMs)** such as BERT, GPT, T5, and Vision Transformers (ViT).

A Transformer processes an entire sequence **in parallel**, learning contextual relationships between every pair of tokens via attention scores. This single design choice unlocked the era of large-scale pre-training and transfer learning in NLP, Vision, Speech, and Multimodal AI.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in KTU 2024:** Module 4 explicitly tests (a) the math of self-attention, (b) multi-head attention, (c) positional encoding, and (d) pre-trained model families (BERT, GPT, ViT).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reading Circle" Intuition

Imagine you are reading a paragraph about *"The **bank** of the river was steep"*. To understand the word *bank*, your brain doesn't read left-to-right sequentially — it **looks back and forward** at every other word simultaneously, weighing which words are most relevant to *bank*.

A Transformer does the same thing mathematically:
- Every word **asks a question** ("What am I looking for?") → **Query (Q)**
- Every word **advertises its identity** ("Here is what I contain") → **Key (K)**
- Every word **provides its content** ("Here is my information") → **Value (V)**

The match score between Q and K decides how much V each word contributes to the new representation of *bank*. This is **self-attention** — every token attends to every other token in a single parallel step.

> [!TIP]
> Think of Q, K, V as three synchronized databases that the Transformer queries in O(1) parallel steps, instead of RNN's O(n) sequential steps.

## 1.3 What are Pre-trained Networks?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** A **Pre-trained Model (PTM)** is a deep neural network trained on a massive generic dataset (e.g., Wikipedia, Common Crawl, ImageNet-21k) using self-supervised objectives, whose learned weights serve as a high-quality initialization for downstream tasks via **fine-tuning** or **prompting**.

Three families dominate the landscape:

| Family | Direction | Representative Model | Pre-training Objective |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Encoder-only** | Bidirectional | BERT, RoBERTa | Masked Language Modeling (MLM) |
| **Decoder-only** | Left-to-right | GPT-1/2/3/4, LLaMA | Next Token Prediction (Autoregressive LM) |
| **Encoder-Decoder** | Seq2Seq | T5, BART, mT5 | Span Corruption, Denoising |
| **Vision Transformer** | Patch-based | ViT, Swin, DeiT | Supervised / Masked Patch Prediction |

## 1.4 Key Vocabulary for KTU Board Exams

- **Self-Attention**: A token attends to all tokens in the same sequence.
- **Cross-Attention**: Decoder attends to encoder outputs (Q from decoder, K,V from encoder).
- **Multi-Head Attention**: Running $h$ attention operations in parallel, then concatenating.
- **Positional Encoding**: Injects sequence-order information (since attention is permutation-equivariant).
- **Scaled Dot-Product Attention**: The canonical $softmax(QK^T/\sqrt{d_k})V$ formula.
- **Layer Normalization**: Normalizes activations across the feature dimension per token.
- **Residual Connection**: $x + Sublayer(x)$ — preserves gradient flow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Transformer Block — Operational Breakdown

A Transformer is a stack of **$N$ identical encoder layers** and **$N$ identical decoder layers**. Each encoder layer has two sub-layers; each decoder layer has three.

### Encoder Layer (per block):
1. **Multi-Head Self-Attention** over the input embeddings.
2. **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)** — applied independently to each position.
3. Each sub-layer is wrapped by a **Residual Connection** and **Layer Normalization** (Post-LN or Pre-LN).

### Decoder Layer (per block):
1. **Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention** (future tokens are masked to $-infty$).
2. **Cross-Attention** (Q from decoder, K,V from encoder).
3. **Position-wise FFN**.

> [!NOTE]
> The "**masking**" in decoder self-attention prevents the model from cheating by looking at tokens it has not yet generated. This is enforced via the causal mask matrix $M$ where $M_{ij} = 0$ if $i \geq j$ else $-infty$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **EXAM GOLD:** Memorize this table verbatim. Every KTU Part B question from Module 4 will require at least two of these formulas.

| # | Component | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Projections | $Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V$ | Produce Q, K, V from input embeddings $X$ |
| 2 | Attention Scores | $S = \dfrac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$ | Compute raw similarity (pre-softmax logits) |
| 3 | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\mathrm{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$ | Weighted sum of values |
| 4 | Single Attention Head | $\mathrm{head}_i = \mathrm{Attention}(X W_i^Q, X W_i^K, X W_i^V)$ | One parallel attention subspace |
| 5 | Multi-Head Attention | $\mathrm{MultiHead}(X) = \mathrm{Concat}(\mathrm{head}_1, \ldots, \mathrm{head}_h)\,W^O$ | Combine $h$ heads |
| 6 | Positional Encoding (sin) | $PE_{(pos,\,2i)} = \sin\!\left(pos \,/\, 10000^{2i/d_{model}}\right)$ | Encode even dimensions |
| 7 | Positional Encoding (cos) | $PE_{(pos,\,2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(pos \,/\, 10000^{2i/d_{model}}\right)$ | Encode odd dimensions |
| 8 | Position-wise FFN | $\mathrm{FFN}(x) = \max(0,\, x W_1 + b_1)\, W_2 + b_2$ | Non-linear transformation per token |
| 9 | Layer Normalization | $\mathrm{LayerNorm}(x) = \gamma \cdot \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma} + \beta$ | Stabilize training |
| 10 | Residual Block | $y = \mathrm{LayerNorm}(x + \mathrm{Sublayer}(x))$ | Preserve gradient flow |
| 11 | Causal Mask (additive) | $\mathrm{masked\ scores}_{ij} = S_{ij} + M_{ij}$ where $M_{ij} \in \{0, -\infty\}$ | Prevent attending to future tokens |
| 12 | Sinusoidal Wave Length | $\lambda_i = 2\pi \cdot 10000^{2i/d_{model}}$ | Geometric progression of wavelengths |

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` (not `|`)** when writing absolute value or norm in your answer sheet to avoid markdown table breakage — this is a formatting safeguard from KTU digital templates.

## 2.3 Why Each Design Choice Matters (The "Why")

- **Why scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$?** As $d_k$ grows, dot products $QK^{\top}$ have variance $\approx d_k$, pushing softmax into regions of tiny gradients. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ keeps variance $\approx 1$.
- **Why multi-head instead of one big head?** Different heads learn **different relation types** — one head may track syntactic dependencies, another coreference, another long-range semantics.
- **Why positional encoding?** Pure self-attention is **permutation-invariant** — without positions, "dog bites man" = "man bites dog". Positional encoding breaks this symmetry.
- **Why LayerNorm and not BatchNorm?** Sequences have variable lengths; BatchNorm statistics are unstable across positions. LayerNorm normalizes per-token across features, making it length-agnostic.
- **Why residual connections?** Transformers are deep (96–128 layers in GPT-3 / GPT-4). Without residuals, gradients vanish across such depth.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Domain | Pre-trained Transformer Used | Application |
|---|---|---|
| NLP | BERT, GPT-4, LLaMA | Chatbots, search, translation, summarization |
| Computer Vision | ViT, Swin Transformer, DINOv2 | Classification, detection, segmentation |
| Speech | Whisper, Wav2Vec 2.0 | ASR, multilingual transcription |
| Biology | AlphaFold 2 (Evoformer blocks) | Protein structure prediction |
| Code | Codex, CodeLLaMA, StarCoder | Code generation, autocompletion |
| Multimodal | CLIP, Flamingo, GPT-4V | Image-text retrieval, visual QA |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Scaled Dot-Product Attention from First Principles

**Goal:** Compute a context-aware representation for every token $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model}}$ in a sequence $X = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]$.

### Step 1 — Define the three learnable projection matrices
Let $W^Q, W^K, W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$ be trainable weight matrices (with $d_k = d_v = d_{model}/h$ in multi-head setups).

$$
Q = X W^Q, \qquad K = X W^K, \qquad V = X W^V
$$

Here $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{model}}$, so $Q, K, V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$.

### Step 2 — Compute raw similarity scores
Each token $x_i$ should produce a query vector $q_i$ and every token $x_j$ produces a key $k_j$. The compatibility between token $i$ and token $j$ is the dot product:

$$
s_{ij} = q_i^{\top} k_j
$$

In matrix form (across all pairs simultaneously):

$$
S = Q K^{\top} \qquad \text{where} \qquad S \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}
$$

### Step 3 — Address the variance explosion problem
Assume $q_i$ and $k_j$ have entries drawn i.i.d. with mean 0 and variance 1. Then:

$$
\mathbb{E}[s_{ij}] = 0, \qquad \mathrm{Var}(s_{ij}) = d_k
$$

As $d_k$ grows (e.g., $d_k = 64$ in BERT-base), $\mathrm{Var}$ becomes large, pushing $\exp(s_{ij})$ to extreme values, causing softmax gradients to vanish.

**Fix:** Divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to normalize variance back to 1.

$$
\tilde{S} = \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert scores into a probability distribution
Apply row-wise softmax so each row is a valid probability distribution over the keys:

$$
A = \mathrm{softmax}(\tilde{S}), \qquad A_{ij} = \frac{\exp(\tilde{S}_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(\tilde{S}_{ik})}
$$

$A$ is the **attention weight matrix** — entry $A_{ij}$ tells "how much token $i$ attends to token $j$".

### Step 5 — Aggregate values using attention weights
The new representation of token $i$ is a weighted sum of all value vectors:

$$
z_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{ij}\, v_j
$$

In matrix form:

$$
Z = A V = \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **End Result — KTU Board Standard Form:**
>
> $$\boxed{\;\mathrm{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V\;}$$
>
> This is the canonical formula students must reproduce from memory in Part B.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Multi-Head Attention

**Motivation:** A single head may average over different relation types. Let $h$ heads each learn a different subspace.

### Step 1 — Define $h$ independent projection matrices per head
For head $i \in \{1, \ldots, h\}$:

$$
W_i^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}, \quad W_i^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}, \quad W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_v}
$$

with $d_k = d_v = d_{model}/h$.

### Step 2 — Compute each head independently

$$
\mathrm{head}_i = \mathrm{Attention}(X W_i^Q,\, X W_i^K,\, X W_i^V) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}
$$

### Step 3 — Concatenate all heads along the feature axis

$$
\mathrm{Concat}(\mathrm{head}_1, \ldots, \mathrm{head}_h) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times h d_v} = \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{model}}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply a final output projection $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_{model}}$

$$
\mathrm{MultiHead}(X) = \mathrm{Concat}(\mathrm{head}_1, \ldots, \mathrm{head}_h)\, W^O
$$

This single linear layer lets the model learn how to **mix information across heads**.

> [!TIP]
> For BERT-base: $d_{model}=768$, $h=12$, so $d_k = d_v = 64$. The total compute cost is **identical** to single-head attention with full $d_{model}$ dimensions — a beautiful design trick.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sinusoidal Positional Encoding

**Goal:** Assign each position $pos \in \{0, 1, \ldots, n-1\}$ a unique vector $PE_{pos} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model}}$ such that:
- Each dimension is bounded in $[-1, 1]$.
- Relative position $PE_{pos+k}$ is a linear function of $PE_{pos}$ (for easy relative-offset learning).

### Step 1 — Define a frequency schedule
For dimension index $i$ ranging over $\{0, 1, \ldots, d_{model}/2 - 1\}$:

$$
\omega_i = \frac{1}{10000^{2i / d_{model}}}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply $\sin$ to even indices, $\cos$ to odd indices

$$
PE_{(pos,\, 2i)} = \sin(pos \cdot \omega_i), \qquad PE_{(pos,\, 2i+1)} = \cos(pos \cdot \omega_i)
$$

### Step 3 — Verify the relative-position property (KTU favorite!)
Using the angle-addition identity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PE_{(pos+k,\, 2i)} &= \sin((pos+k)\omega_i) \\
&= \sin(pos\,\omega_i)\cos(k\,\omega_i) + \cos(pos\,\omega_i)\sin(k\,\omega_i) \\
&= \cos(k\,\omega_i)\, PE_{(pos,\,2i)} + \sin(k\,\omega_i)\, PE_{(pos,\,2i+1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $PE_{pos+k}$ is a **linear function of $PE_{pos}$** with coefficients depending only on $k$ — the model can learn relative offsets by a simple linear projection.

### Step 4 — Add to input embeddings

$$
X_{\text{input}} = \mathrm{TokenEmbedding}(x) + PE_{pos}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Complexity Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks for complexity comparison. Memorize this table for the **"Compare Transformer vs RNN vs CNN"** question.

| Layer Type | Complexity per Layer | Sequential Ops | Max Path Length |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Self-Attention** | $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Recurrent (RNN/LSTM)** | $O(n \cdot d^2)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Convolutional** | $O(k \cdot n \cdot d^2)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(\log_k n)$ |
| **Self-Attention (restricted)** | $O(r \cdot n \cdot d)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n/r)$ |

Where $n$ = sequence length, $d$ = representation dim, $k$ = kernel size, $r$ = neighborhood size.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Transformer Encoder-Decoder Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below is the **most-commonly drawn figure** in KTU Module 4 answers. Practice redrawing it freehand in the exam hall.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    srcA[Input Tokens] --> embA[Token Embedding]
    peA[Positional Encoding] --> addA((+))
    embA --> addA
    addA --> mhaA[Multi-Head Self-Attention Encoder]
    mhaA --> addA2((+))
    addA --> addA2
    addA2 --> lnA[LayerNorm]
    lnA --> ffnA[Feed Forward FFN]
    ffnA --> addA3((+))
    lnA --> addA3
    addA3 --> lnA2[LayerNorm]
    lnA2 --> encOut[Encoder Output]
    encOut --> repeat[Repeat N times]
    repeat --> encFinal[Stacked Encoder Representations]

    srcB[Target Tokens Right Shifted] --> embB[Token Embedding Decoder]
    peB[Positional Encoding Decoder] --> addB((+))
    embB --> addB
    addB --> mmha[Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention Decoder]
    mmha --> addB2((+))
    addB --> addB2
    addB2 --> lnB[LayerNorm]
    encFinal --> cross[Cross Attention Q from Decoder K V from Encoder]
    lnB --> cross
    cross --> addB3((+))
    lnB --> addB3
    addB3 --> lnB2[LayerNorm]
    lnB2 --> ffnB[Feed Forward FFN Decoder]
    ffnB --> addB4((+))
    lnB2 --> addB4
    addB4 --> lnB3[LayerNorm]
    lnB3 --> linear[Linear Projection to Vocabulary]
    linear --> softmax[Softmax Probabilities]
    softmax --> outTokens[Predicted Output Tokens]
```

## 4.2 Scaled Dot-Product Attention Block (Internal View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Query Matrix Q] --> mulMatMul[MatMul Q times K transpose]
    K[Key Matrix K] --> mulMatMul
    mulMatMul --> scale[Scale by 1 over sqrt dk]
    scale --> mask[Optional Mask Future Tokens]
    mask --> sm[Softmax over Keys]
    sm --> weights[Attention Weights A]
    weights --> mulOut[MatMul with V]
    V[Value Matrix V] --> mulOut
    mulOut --> out[Output Z n times dv]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pre-trained Model Ecosystem

```mermaid

    tokenize --> pretrain[Self Supervised Pre training]

    pretrain --> gpt[GPT Decoder Only Next Token]
    pretrain --> t5[T5 Encoder Decoder Span Corruption]
    pretrain --> vit[Vision Transformer ViT Masked Patch]

    bert --> ft1[Fine tune for Classification NER QA]
    gpt --> ft2[Fine tune or Prompt for Generation Chatbots Reasoning]
    t5 --> ft3[Fine tune for Translation Summarization]
    vit --> ft4[Fine tune for Image Classification Detection Segmentation]

```
