        t1[The]

        t3[of]

        t5[river]

    t2 -- high attention weight --> t5
    t2 -- medium attention weight --> t4

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the self-attention mechanism. How is it different from the attention used in traditional encoder-decoder RNN architectures?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Self-attention** is a mechanism where every token in a sequence attends to every other token in the **same** sequence to produce a contextualized representation.
>
> **Difference from RNN encoder-decoder attention:**
>
> | Aspect | RNN Seq2Seq Attention | Transformer Self-Attention |
> |---|---|---|
> | Q source | Hidden state of **decoder** | Same token's projected embedding |
> | K,V source | All encoder hidden states | All tokens of the **same** sequence |
> | Parallelism | Sequential (O(n)) | Parallel (O(1) sequential steps) |
> | Long-range deps | Suffers from vanishing gradient | Constant path length O(1) |
>
> **(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for comparison table.)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is positional encoding? Why is it necessary in Transformer models? Mention any one formula used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Positional encoding** is a vector added to each token embedding to inject information about the **order/position** of tokens in a sequence.
>
> **Why necessary:** Self-attention is **permutation-invariant** — without positional information, the model cannot distinguish *"dog bites man"* from *"man bites dog"*.
>
> **Sinusoidal formula (Vaswani et al.):**
>
> $$PE_{(pos,\,2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right), \qquad PE_{(pos,\,2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right)$$
>
> **(1 mark definition + 1 mark necessity + 1 mark formula.)**

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Module 4:**
> - ❌ Students write only the formula and skip the **derivation logic** → loses 4–5 marks.
> - ❌ Students forget to explain **why** $\sqrt{d_k}$ is used → loses 2 marks.
> - ❌ In BERT vs GPT comparison, students skip the **pre-training objective** distinction → loses 3 marks.
> - ❌ No diagram in architecture question → loses 2 marks (draw Mermaid-style block diagram by hand).
> - ✅ Always **state assumptions**, **explain every symbol**, and **draw at least one labeled diagram**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Derive the scaled dot-product attention formula. Explain why scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ is necessary and what problem it solves.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):**
In self-attention, we want each token $i$ to weigh its relevance to every token $j$ in the sequence. We project the input $X$ into three matrices using learned weights $W^Q, W^K, W^V$:

$$Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V$$

**Step 2 — Compute similarity (1 mark):**
The raw relevance score between token $i$ and token $j$ is the dot product:

$$s_{ij} = q_i^{\top} k_j \quad \Longrightarrow \quad S = Q K^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

**Step 3 — The variance problem (2 marks):**
Assume $q_i, k_j$ have i.i.d. zero-mean, unit-variance entries. Then $\mathrm{Var}(q_i^{\top} k_j) = d_k$. As $d_k$ grows, dot products become very large in magnitude, pushing $\exp(s_{ij})$ to extreme values. The softmax function then produces outputs near one-hot vectors, producing **vanishing gradients** during backpropagation.

**Step 4 — Apply scaling (1 mark):**
Divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to bring variance back to $\approx 1$:

$$\tilde{S}_{ij} = \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

**Step 5 — Softmax and aggregation (2 marks):**
Convert scores to probabilities and compute the weighted sum of values:

$$\boxed{\;\mathrm{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V\;}$$

**Conclusion:** Scaling ensures stable gradients, enabling training of deep Transformers with $d_k = 64$ or higher.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain multi-head attention with its mathematical formulation. Discuss how it improves over single-head attention.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Multi-head attention runs $h$ parallel scaled dot-product attention operations ("heads") on different learned linear projections of $Q, K, V$, then concatenates and projects the result.

**Per-head computation (2 marks):**

$$\mathrm{head}_i = \mathrm{Attention}(X W_i^Q,\; X W_i^K,\; X W_i^V), \quad i = 1, \ldots, h$$

**Concatenation and output projection (2 marks):**

$$\mathrm{MultiHead}(X) = \mathrm{Concat}(\mathrm{head}_1, \ldots, \mathrm{head}_h)\, W^O$$

where $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{h d_v \times d_{model}}$ is a final learnable linear layer.

**Advantages over single-head (2 marks):**
1. **Multiplicity of subspaces:** Each head can specialize — head 1 may learn syntactic dependencies, head 2 coreference, head 3 long-range semantics.
2. **Same total compute cost:** With $d_k = d_v = d_{model}/h$, total FLOPs equal those of single-head full-dim attention.
3. **Richer representation:** Concatenation allows the model to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 1]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the complete Transformer architecture with encoder and decoder blocks. Draw a labeled block diagram.**

**Model Solution:**

**Overview (1 mark):**
The Transformer follows an encoder-decoder structure. Both encoder and decoder consist of $N = 6$ identical layers (stacked). Inputs are first embedded and added with positional encodings.

**Encoder block (2 marks):**
Each encoder layer has two sub-layers:
1. **Multi-Head Self-Attention** — attends over input embeddings.
2. **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network** — $\mathrm{FFN}(x) = \max(0, xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$, applied independently to each position.

Each sub-layer uses a **residual connection** followed by **Layer Normalization**:
$$y = \mathrm{LayerNorm}(x + \mathrm{Sublayer}(x))$$

**Decoder block (2 marks):**
Each decoder layer has three sub-layers:
1. **Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention** — uses a causal mask $M$ to prevent attending to future tokens.
2. **Cross-Attention** — Q comes from the decoder, K and V come from the encoder output.
3. **Position-wise FFN** — same as encoder.

**Final layers (1 mark):**
The decoder stack output is passed through a **linear projection** to vocabulary size, then **softmax** to produce token probabilities.

**Block diagram (1 mark):**
*(Draw the encoder-decoder flow as in Section 4.1 above, showing inputs → embeddings + PE → N stacked encoder layers → cross-attention → N stacked decoder layers → linear → softmax → outputs.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Compare BERT and GPT pre-trained models in terms of architecture, pre-training objective, and downstream applications.**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | **BERT** (Encoder-only) | **GPT** (Decoder-only) |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Stack of encoder blocks with bidirectional self-attention | Stack of decoder blocks with masked (causal) self-attention |
| **Attention direction** | Looks at both left **and** right context | Only left context (autoregressive) |
| **Pre-training objective** | **Masked Language Modeling (MLM)**: predict randomly masked tokens using bidirectional context + **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)** | **Next Token Prediction**: predict the next token given all previous tokens (causal LM) |
| **Input format** | `[CLS] tokens [SEP]` pair sentences | Single continuous sequence |
| **Best for** | Classification, NER, Extractive QA, Sentence embedding | Text generation, Chatbots, Reasoning, Summarization |
| **Output** | Per-token contextual embeddings | Probabilistic next-token distribution |
| **Examples** | BERT-base (110M), BERT-large (340M), RoBERTa, DistilBERT | GPT-2 (1.5B), GPT-3 (175B), GPT-4, LLaMA-2/3 |
| **Fine-tuning** | Add task-specific head on `[CLS]` or token outputs | Few-shot prompting or instruction fine-tuning |

**Key insight (2 marks):**
- **BERT** = "understanding" model — excels when the task requires **comprehension** of given text.
- **GPT** = "generation" model — excels when the task requires **producing** new coherent text.
- Both share the Transformer backbone; the difference is the attention mask pattern and training objective, which leads to fundamentally different emergent capabilities.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print this and read 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] Self-attention is **O(1) sequential depth** — every token sees every other token in one parallel step.
- [ ] The canonical formula is $\mathrm{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k}) V$ — memorize it cold.
- [ ] $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling prevents softmax saturation and vanishing gradients for large $d_k$.
- [ ] Multi-head attention has **same FLOPs** as single-head but learns multiple relation types.
- [ ] Positional encoding is required because attention is **permutation-invariant**.
- [ ] Sinusoidal PE gives **linear relative-position** expressibility — derivable via angle addition.
- [ ] BERT uses **bidirectional attention + MLM**; GPT uses **causal attention + next-token prediction**.
- [ ] LayerNorm (not BatchNorm) is used because sequences have variable lengths.
- [ ] Residual connections $x + \mathrm{Sublayer}(x)$ preserve gradient flow in deep stacks.
- [ ] Causal mask sets future positions to $-\infty$ before softmax.
- [ ] Encoder-decoder cross-attention: **Q from decoder, K and V from encoder output**.
- [ ] Position-wise FFN expands to $4 \times d_{model}$ then projects back (e.g., $768 \to 3072 \to 768$ in BERT-base).
- [ ] ViT splits images into $16 \times 16$ patches, linearly embeds them, and applies a standard Transformer encoder.
- [ ] Always draw **at least one labeled diagram** in any 14-mark Transformer question — KTU examiners reward visual clarity.
- [ ] State **assumptions**, define **every symbol**, and **derive formulas** rather than just quoting them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Advice:** The Module-4 Part-B question on Transformers almost always combines **(a) a derivation (typically scaled dot-product attention)** with **(b) an architecture or pre-trained model comparison (BERT vs GPT vs ViT)**. Master both — they are the two highest-weightage questions in the entire syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generative Adversarial Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT745 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Generative Adversarial Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** is a class of deep learning architectures introduced by **Ian J. Goodfellow et al. (2014)** in their seminal paper *"Generative Adversarial Nets"* (NeurIPS 2014). It is a **generative modelling framework** built upon the principle of **adversarial training**, in which two neural networks — a **Generator (G)** and a **Discriminator (D)** — are trained simultaneously in a **zero-sum, two-player minimax game**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A GAN is an unsupervised generative model composed of two networks locked in a competitive game. The **Generator** maps a prior noise distribution $p_{\mathbf{z}}(\mathbf{z})$ to the data space to produce synthetic samples, while the **Discriminator** estimates the probability that a given sample was drawn from the real training data rather than from $G$. Both networks are optimized against each other until the generated distribution $p_g$ becomes indistinguishable from the real distribution $p_{\text{data}}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Art Forger and the Detective"

To make this immediately intuitive, imagine the following real-world scenario:

- **The Forger (Generator $G$):** A counterfeiter who tries to paint fake masterpieces. He has never seen the original paintings, only feedback from the detective.
- **The Detective (Discriminator $D$):** An art authenticator who is shown a mix of original paintings and fakes. He must classify each piece as **REAL** or **FAKE**.
- **The Feedback Loop:** Every time the detective catches a forgery, the forger learns *what gave him away* (brush strokes, color blending, canvas texture) and produces a better forgery. Every time the forger improves, the detective must sharpen his detection skills.

The equilibrium is reached when the forger's paintings are **so perfect** that the detective's best strategy is to **flip a fair coin** — i.e., $D(\mathbf{x}) = 0.5$ for every input.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Adversarial"?**
> The term *adversarial* refers to the fact that $G$ and $D$ have **opposing objectives**. $G$ tries to *minimize* the probability that $D$ correctly identifies fakes, while $D$ tries to *maximize* its classification accuracy. They are adversaries in a mathematically well-defined game-theoretic sense.

## 1.3 Key Components at a Glance

| Component | Symbol | Role | Input | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Generator | $G$ | Produces synthetic data | Random noise vector $\mathbf{z} \sim p_{\mathbf{z}}$ | Fake sample $G(\mathbf{z})$ |
| Discriminator | $D$ | Distinguishes real vs fake | Sample $\mathbf{x}$ (real or fake) | Scalar probability $D(\mathbf{x}) \in [0,1]$ |
| Noise Prior | $p_{\mathbf{z}}(\mathbf{z})$ | Source of randomness for $G$ | — | Latent sample $\mathbf{z}$ |
| Real Data Distribution | $p_{\text{data}}$ | Ground-truth distribution the model tries to mimic | — | Real sample $\mathbf{x}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Adversarial Game — Min-Max Formulation

The complete GAN optimization is expressed as a **minimax** objective over the joint parameters $(\theta_G, \theta_D)$:

$$\min_{G} \max_{D} V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})}\big[\log D(\mathbf{x})\big] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_{\mathbf{z}}(\mathbf{z})}\big[\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))\big]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direction of Optimization**
> - $D$ seeks to **MAXIMIZE** $V$ → wants $D(\mathbf{x}) \to 1$ for real samples and $D(G(\mathbf{z})) \to 0$ for fakes.
> - $G$ seeks to **MINIMIZE** $V$ → wants $D(G(\mathbf{z})) \to 1$, i.e., the discriminator should be *fooled* into labelling fakes as real.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Training Dynamics

The training algorithm alternates between two phases, controlled by a hyperparameter **$k$** (typically $k=1$ in the original paper):

### Phase A — Discriminator Update (Inner Maximization)
1. Sample a minibatch of $m$ noise vectors $\{\mathbf{z}^{(1)}, \ldots, \mathbf{z}^{(m)}\}$ from $p_{\mathbf{z}}(\mathbf{z})$.
2. Sample a minibatch of $m$ real examples $\{\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \ldots, \mathbf{x}^{(m)}\}$ from $p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})$.
3. Update $D$ by **ascending** its stochastic gradient:

$$\nabla_{\theta_D} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \Big[ \log D(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) + \log\big(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z}^{(i)}))\big) \Big]$$

### Phase B — Generator Update (Outer Minimization)
4. Re-sample $m$ noise vectors $\{\mathbf{z}^{(1)}, \ldots, \mathbf{z}^{(m)}\}$.
5. Update $G$ by **descending** its stochastic gradient:

$$\nabla_{\theta_G} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \log\big(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z}^{(i)}))\big)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Trick — Non-Saturating Loss**
> In early training, $G$ is weak and $D$ easily rejects fakes, making $\log(1-D(G(\mathbf{z})))$ saturate near 0 (gradient $\to 0$). Goodfellow's paper recommends training $G$ to **maximize** $\log D(G(\mathbf{z}))$ instead, which provides stronger gradients early in training. This is the **non-saturating GAN loss** used in practice.

## 2.3 Loss Function Variants — KTU Formula Sheet

The table below consolidates every key equation you must memorize for the KTU examination. Critical formatting rule: all vertical-bar notation is rendered as `\mid` so the markdown table remains unbroken.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Purpose / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimax Value Function | $\min_G \max_D \; \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}}[\log D(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z}}[\log(1-D(G(\mathbf{z})))]$ | Original GAN objective (Goodfellow 2014) |
| 2 | Optimal Discriminator | $D^*(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})}{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) + p_g(\mathbf{x})}$ | Bayes-optimal classifier given fixed $G$ |
| 3 | Global Optimum Condition | $p_g(\mathbf{x}) = p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})$ | Nash Equilibrium — generator matches true distribution |
| 4 | Generator Loss (Non-Saturating) | $\mathcal{L}_G = -\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z}}[\log D(G(\mathbf{z}))]$ | Practical training objective for $G$ |
| 5 | Discriminator Loss (BCE) | $\mathcal{L}_D = -\Big(\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}}[\log D(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z}}[\log(1-D(G(\mathbf{z})))]\Big)$ | Standard binary cross-entropy on real vs fake |
| 6 | JS Divergence at Optimum | $V(G^*, D^*) = -\log 4$ | Theoretical lower bound on value function |
| 7 | KL Divergence (asymmetric) | $D_{KL}(P \mid\mid Q) = \int P(\mathbf{x}) \log \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x})}{Q(\mathbf{x})} \, d\mathbf{x}$ | Used in derivation of GAN optimality |
| 8 | Jensen-Shannon Divergence | $D_{JS}(P \mid\mid Q) = \tfrac{1}{2} D_{KL}(P \mid\mid M) + \tfrac{1}{2} D_{KL}(Q \mid\mid M)$, where $M = \tfrac{1}{2}(P+Q)$ | Symmetric divergence minimized by GAN at equilibrium |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Where GANs are used in industry:**
> - **Image super-resolution** (SRGAN, ESRGAN) — upscaling low-resolution images.
> - **Medical imaging** — synthesizing MRI/CT scans for data augmentation.
> - **Face editing & deepfakes** (StyleGAN family) — controllable face generation.
> - **Drug discovery** — generating novel molecular structures.
> - **Data augmentation** in low-data regimes for downstream classification tasks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Discriminator $D^*(\mathbf{x})$

**Goal:** For a *fixed* generator $G$, derive the closed-form expression for the discriminator that maximizes $V(D, G)$.

### Step 1 — Rewrite the expectation as an integral

The value function for a fixed $G$ is:

$$V(D, G) = \int_{\mathbf{x}} p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) \log D(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{x} + \int_{\mathbf{z}} p_{\mathbf{z}}(\mathbf{z}) \log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z}))) \, d\mathbf{z}$$

**Explanation:** We replace the expectations with integrals over the respective sample spaces. This is valid because the expectations are defined with respect to the respective densities.

### Step 2 — Change of variable in the second integral

Since $G$ maps $\mathbf{z} \to \mathbf{x}$, let $\mathbf{x} = G(\mathbf{z})$. The induced generator distribution is $p_g(\mathbf{x})$. Substituting:

$$V(D, G) = \int_{\mathbf{x}} \Big[ p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) \log D(\mathbf{x}) + p_g(\mathbf{x}) \log(1 - D(\mathbf{x})) \Big] \, d\mathbf{x}$$

**Explanation:** The integral over the latent space is rewritten as an integral over the data space using the change-of-variables formula. The density $p_g(\mathbf{x})$ is the probability that the generator produces a sample at $\mathbf{x}$.

### Step 3 — Pointwise maximization

Since the integrand is a sum of terms evaluated independently at each $\mathbf{x}$, we maximize the function pointwise:

$$f(y) = a \log y + b \log(1 - y), \quad \text{where } y = D(\mathbf{x}), \; a = p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}), \; b = p_g(\mathbf{x})$$

**Explanation:** Because the integral decomposes as a sum of independent contributions from each $\mathbf{x}$, the global maximum of $V$ is achieved by maximizing each term separately.

### Step 4 — Differentiate and set to zero

Taking the derivative with respect to $y$:

$$\frac{d f}{d y} = \frac{a}{y} - \frac{b}{1 - y} = 0$$

Solving for $y$:

$$a(1 - y) = b y \;\Longrightarrow\; a = y(a + b) \;\Longrightarrow\; y = \frac{a}{a+b}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute back to obtain $D^*(\mathbf{x})$

$$\boxed{D^*(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})}{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) + p_g(\mathbf{x})}}$$

**Explanation:** This is the Bayes-optimal discriminator — it outputs the posterior probability that a sample is drawn from the real distribution rather than the generator. When $p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) \gg p_g(\mathbf{x})$, $D^*(\mathbf{x}) \to 1$ (confidently real). When $p_g(\mathbf{x}) \gg p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})$, $D^*(\mathbf{x}) \to 0$ (confidently fake).

## 3.2 Proof That $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$ Is the Global Optimum

### Step 1 — Substitute $D^*$ back into $V$

$$V(G, D^*) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{\text{data}}}\left[\log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})}{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) + p_g(\mathbf{x})}\right] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_g}\left[\log \frac{p_g(\mathbf{x})}{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) + p_g(\mathbf{x})}\right]$$

### Step 2 — Factor out $\log 2$ by introducing the midpoint

Using the identity $\log \frac{a}{a+b} = -\log 2 + \log \frac{a}{(a+b)/2}$:

$$V(G, D^*) = -\log 4 + D_{KL}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \,\bigg\Vert\, \frac{p_{\text{data}} + p_g}{2}\right) + D_{KL}\!\!\left(p_g \,\bigg\Vert\, \frac{p_{\text{data}} + p_g}{2}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Recognize the Jensen-Shannon Divergence

By definition of JS-divergence:

$$V(G, D^*) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot D_{JS}(p_{\text{data}} \,\Vert\, p_g)$$

### Step 4 — Apply the non-negativity of $D_{JS}$

Since $D_{JS}(P \Vert Q) \geq 0$ with equality iff $P = Q$:

$$V(G, D^*) \geq -\log 4$$

with equality **if and only if** $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$.

### Step 5 — Conclude the global optimum

$$\boxed{p_g^* = p_{\text{data}} \quad \text{and} \quad V(G^*, D^*) = -\log 4}$$

**Explanation:** At the global optimum, the generated distribution perfectly matches the true data distribution, and the discriminator's best response is the trivial $D^*(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}$ everywhere — equivalent to random guessing. The constant $-\log 4 \approx -1.386$ is the **value of the game at Nash equilibrium**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Parameter-by-Parameter Behaviour

| Parameter | Effect on $D$ | Effect on $G$ | Training Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\theta_D$ updated too often | $D$ becomes too strong | $G$ gradients vanish (saturation) | Causes **mode collapse** |
| $\theta_G$ updated too often | $D$ cannot keep up | $G$ receives noisy signal | Poor sample quality |
| Noise prior $p_{\mathbf{z}}$ too narrow | Limited input diversity | Low sample variety | Mode dropping |
| Learning rate mismatch | Oscillation / divergence | Divergence of $V(D,G)$ | Use Adam with $\beta_1 = 0.5$, $\beta_2 = 0.999$ |
| Use of **non-saturating loss** | Unaffected | Strong early gradients | Faster convergence in practice |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level GAN Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **complete data flow** between the Generator, Discriminator, and the training data pipeline. Every node ID is purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to comply with the Mermaid compiler safeguard.

```mermaid
graph LR
    zprior[NOISE PRIOR pz] -->|sample z| genInput[G INPUT]
    genInput --> genForward[G FORWARD PASS]
    genForward -->|fake x| fakePool[FAKE BATCH]
    realData[REAL DATA] -->|sample x| realPool[REAL BATCH]
    fakePool --> discIn[D INPUT]
    realPool --> discIn
    discIn --> discForward[D FORWARD PASS]
    discForward --> discOut[D OUTPUT SCALAR 0 to 1]
    discOut --> lossCalc[LOSS COMPUTATION]
    lossCalc -->|update D| discUpdate[D GRADIENT STEP]
    lossCalc -->|update G| genUpdate[G GRADIENT STEP]
    genUpdate --> genForward
    discUpdate --> discForward
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - Solid arrows indicate **forward data flow** during a single training iteration.
> - The two parallel batches (real + fake) are concatenated and passed through $D$.
> - Gradient updates for $D$ and $G$ alternate — never simultaneous, to maintain the min-max interpretation.

## 4.2 Training Loop Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[INITIALIZE G AND D] --> epochLoop[FOR EACH EPOCH]
    epochLoop --> sampleNoise[SAMPLE NOISE BATCH]
    epochLoop --> sampleReal[SAMPLE REAL BATCH]
    sampleNoise --> genFake[G GENERATES FAKES]
    genFake --> trainD[TRAIN D FOR K STEPS]
    sampleReal --> trainD
    trainD --> trainG[TRAIN G FOR 1 STEP]
    trainG --> evalLoop[CHECK CONVERGENCE]
    evalLoop -->|not converged| epochLoop
    evalLoop -->|converged| endNode[OUTPUT TRAINED G]
```

## 4.3 Mathematical Workflow — From Value Function to Equilibrium

```mermaid
graph TD
    objStart[VALUE FUNCTION V D G] --> fixG[FIX G]
    fixG --> optD[OPTIMIZE D GET D STAR]
    optD --> plugD[PLUG D STAR INTO V]
    plugD --> jsForm[V BECOMES NEG LOG 4 PLUS 2 DJS]
    jsForm --> minG[MINIMIZE WITH RESPECT TO G]
    minG --> equilibrium[PG EQUALS PDATA AT EQUILIBRIUM]
    equilibrium --> finalState[D STAR OUTPUTS 0.5 EVERYWHERE]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Insight:** The discriminator $D$ essentially acts as a **learnable loss function** for the generator. Instead of hand-designing a distance metric (like MSE or KL-divergence in a VAE), GANs *learn* the loss function adaptively. This is one of the most influential ideas in modern generative modelling and is a recurring KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Define a Generative Adversarial Network. Mention the role of the Generator and the Discriminator.**

**Model Answer:**

A Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) is a deep generative model proposed by Goodfellow et al. (2014) in which two neural networks — a **Generator $G$** and a **Discriminator $D$** — are trained in opposition through an adversarial minimax game.

- **Generator $G$:** Maps a random noise vector $\mathbf{z} \sim p_{\mathbf{z}}(\mathbf{z})$ to a synthetic data sample $G(\mathbf{z})$. Its goal is to produce samples that mimic the real data distribution $p_{\text{data}}$.
- **Discriminator $D$:** Takes a sample $\mathbf{x}$ (either real or fake) and outputs a scalar probability $D(\mathbf{x}) \in [0,1]$ estimating whether $\mathbf{x}$ is real. Its goal is to correctly classify real vs fake samples.

Training continues until $D$ can no longer distinguish real samples from generated ones, at which point $D(\mathbf{x}) = 0.5$ for all $\mathbf{x}$, indicating Nash equilibrium.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for $G$ and $D$ roles.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]**

**State the minimax objective function of a GAN and explain why $G$ minimizes while $D$ maximizes the same value function.**

**Model Answer:**

The GAN minimax objective is:

$$\min_G \max_D V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_{\mathbf{z}}}[\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))]$$

**Why $D$ maximizes:** $D$ wishes to assign $D(\mathbf{x}) \to 1$ for real samples (maximizing $\log D(\mathbf{x})$) and $D(G(\mathbf{z})) \to 0$ for fakes (maximizing $\log(1-D(G(\mathbf{z})))$). Both terms are non-negative and increase as $D$ classifies correctly.

**Why $G$ minimizes:** $G$ cannot influence the first term (which depends only on real data), so it minimizes the second term $\log(1-D(G(\mathbf{z})))$ by pushing $D(G(\mathbf{z})) \to 1$, i.e., fooling the discriminator into believing fakes are real.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for equation, 1 mark each for the two justifications.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Pitfalls:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write the value function with $\min \min$ or $\max \max$ — this is worth **zero marks** for the equation.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to mention that $D$ is updated **$k$ times** per generator update. Omitting $k$ costs 1–2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing KL-divergence with JS-divergence in the optimality derivation. Examiners award **no partial credit** if the wrong divergence is used as the final answer.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not explaining *why* the non-saturating loss is preferred over the original $\log(1-D(G(\mathbf{z})))$ loss. Always mention **gradient saturation** in early training.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the closed-form expression for the **optimal discriminator $D^*(\mathbf{x})$** for a fixed generator $G$. Show every algebraic step clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using $D^*$, prove that the **global optimum** of the GAN minimax game occurs when $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$ and that the value of the game at this equilibrium is $-\log 4$.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Optimal Discriminator Derivation**

Step 1: Write $V(D, G)$ as an integral over the data space by change of variable:

$$V(D, G) = \int_{\mathbf{x}} p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) \log D(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{x} + \int_{\mathbf{x}} p_g(\mathbf{x}) \log(1 - D(\mathbf{x})) \, d\mathbf{x}$$

Step 2: Maximize pointwise. For each $\mathbf{x}$, let $y = D(\mathbf{x})$, $a = p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})$, $b = p_g(\mathbf{x})$. Maximize $f(y) = a \log y + b \log(1 - y)$.

Step 3: Set derivative to zero:

$$\frac{a}{y} = \frac{b}{1-y} \;\Longrightarrow\; a(1-y) = by \;\Longrightarrow\; y = \frac{a}{a+b}$$

Step 4: Conclude:

$$D^*(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})}{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) + p_g(\mathbf{x})}$$

At this optimum, $D^*$ is the **Bayes-optimal classifier**.

*(7 marks — 2 for integral rewrite, 2 for derivative, 2 for solution, 1 for concluding statement.)*

**(b) Global Optimum Proof**

Step 1: Substitute $D^*$ back into $V$:

$$V(G, D^*) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{\text{data}}}\!\left[\log \frac{p_{\text{data}}}{p_{\text{data}} + p_g}\right] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_g}\!\left[\log \frac{p_g}{p_{\text{data}} + p_g}\right]$$

Step 2: Add and subtract $\log 2$ in each expectation:

$$V(G, D^*) = \mathbb{E}_{p_{\text{data}}}\!\left[-\log 2 + \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}}{(p_{\text{data}} + p_g)/2}\right] + \mathbb{E}_{p_g}\!\left[-\log 2 + \log \frac{p_g}{(p_{\text{data}} + p_g)/2}\right]$$

Step 3: This gives $V = -\log 4 + D_{KL}(p_{\text{data}} \Vert M) + D_{KL}(p_g \Vert M)$ where $M = (p_{\text{data}} + p_g)/2$.

Step 4: Recognize as $-\log 4 + 2 \, D_{JS}(p_{\text{data}} \Vert p_g)$.

Step 5: Since $D_{JS} \geq 0$ with equality iff $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$, the minimum value of $V$ is $-\log 4$, attained at $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$.

Step 6: Substitute back into $D^*$: $D^*(\mathbf{x}) = p_{\text{data}} / (2 p_{\text{data}}) = 1/2$, confirming random guessing at equilibrium.

*(7 marks — 2 for substitution, 2 for JS-divergence derivation, 2 for non-negativity argument, 1 for the $D^* = 1/2$ conclusion.)*

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **architecture and training procedure** of a GAN. Include the role of the noise prior $p_{\mathbf{z}}$, the number of discriminator updates per generator update ($k$), and the **non-saturating loss** modification.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **training challenges** of GANs — specifically **mode collapse**, **vanishing gradients**, and **failure to converge** — along with at least **two mitigation strategies** for each.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture and Training Procedure**

Architecture:
- The **Generator** $G(\mathbf{z}; \theta_G)$ is typically a deep neural network (often with transposed convolutions for image data) that maps $\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ from a simple prior (e.g., uniform $\mathcal{U}(-1, 1)$ or Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$) into the high-dimensional data space.
- The **Discriminator** $D(\mathbf{x}; \theta_D)$ is a binary classifier (often a CNN for images) that outputs a probability $D(\mathbf{x}) \in [0, 1]$.

Training Procedure (per iteration):
1. Sample minibatch of $m$ noise samples $\{\mathbf{z}^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^m$ and $m$ real samples $\{\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^m$.
2. Update $D$ by **ascending** its stochastic gradient $k$ times (typically $k=1$).
3. Update $G$ by **descending** its stochastic gradient once using the **non-saturating loss** $\mathcal{L}_G = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_i \log D(G(\mathbf{z}^{(i)}))$.

**Why non-saturating?** Early in training, $G$ is weak and $D$ easily rejects fakes, so $\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))$ saturates near $0$ (its gradient w.r.t. $\theta_G$ vanishes). Maximizing $\log D(G(\mathbf{z}))$ instead provides strong gradients when $D$ rejects fakes, since this objective is far from saturated.

*(7 marks — 2 for architecture, 2 for training loop including $k$, 3 for non-saturating loss with justification.)*

**(b) Training Challenges and Mitigations**

| Challenge | Description | Mitigation 1 | Mitigation 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mode Collapse** | $G$ produces only a few distinct sample types, ignoring diversity in $p_{\text{data}}$. | **Minibatch discrimination** — let $D$ compare samples within a batch to detect lack of diversity. | Use **unrolled GANs** or **Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)** with gradient penalty. |
| **Vanishing Gradients** | $D$ becomes too strong; gradients to $G$ shrink to zero. | Replace JS-divergence with **Wasserstein distance** (WGAN) which has non-vanishing gradients almost everywhere. | Use the **non-saturating loss** $\log D(G(\mathbf{z}))$ instead of $\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))$. |
| **Failure to Converge** | Oscillation around equilibrium without settling; $V(D, G)$ does not stabilize. | Use **Adam optimizer** with $\beta_1 = 0.5$, $\beta_2 = 0.999$ and a low learning rate ($\sim 2 \times 10^{-4}$). | Apply **spectral normalization** on $D$ to enforce Lipschitz continuity. |
| **Inception Score instability** | Quantitative evaluation metric fluctuates wildly. | Use **FID (Fréchet Inception Distance)** with running statistics from a pre-trained Inception network. | Maintain **exponential moving averages** of $G$'s weights during training. |

*(7 marks — 1 mark each for defining the three challenges, 1 mark each for two well-explained mitigations per challenge, totaling 6 marks, plus 1 mark for overall coherence.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024**

- ✅ GAN = **two networks, one adversarial game** — $G$ generates, $D$ discriminates.
- ✅ Core equation: $\min_G \max_D \; \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}}[\log D(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z}}[\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))]$
- ✅ **Optimal discriminator**: $D^*(\mathbf{x}) = p_{\text{data}} / (p_{\text{data}} + p_g)$
- ✅ **Global optimum**: $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$, value $= -\log 4$, $D^*(\mathbf{x}) = 0.5$
- ✅ GAN minimizes **Jensen-Shannon divergence** between $p_g$ and $p_{\text{data}}$ at equilibrium.
- ✅ **Non-saturating loss**: $-\log D(G(\mathbf{z}))$ is used in practice to avoid early-training gradient saturation.
- ✅ Discriminator is updated **$k$ times** per generator update; **never** simultaneously.
- ✅ Three pillars of failure: **mode collapse, vanishing gradients, non-convergence**.
- ✅ Modern fixes: **Wasserstein GAN (WGAN), spectral normalization, minibatch discrimination, gradient penalty**.
- ✅ Evaluation: **Inception Score (IS)** and **Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)** — FID is the modern KTU-expected metric.
- ✅ Key inventor: **Ian Goodfellow (2014, NeurIPS)** — do NOT fabricate alternative authorship in exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autoencoder: Basic Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT745 - DEEP LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Autoencoder: Basic Principles # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Autoencoder (AE)** is a specialized class of **unsupervised artificial neural network** designed and trained to reconstruct its own input data at the output layer. Formally, it is a composite function $f_{\theta}(x) = g_{\theta'}(h_{\theta}(x))$ composed of two parameterized mappings: - **Encoder:** $h = f_{\text{enc}}(x) : \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{m}$, where typically $m \le n$. - **Decoder:** $\hat{x} = f_{\text{dec}}(h) : \mathbb{R}^{m} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{n}$. The objective is to learn parameters $\theta, \theta'$ such that $\hat{x} \approx x$ for samples drawn from the data distribution $p_{\text{data}}(x)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** An autoencoder is a **self-supervised** learning model — although it is trained without explicit labels, it generates its own supervisory signal by using the input itself as the target. The internal bottleneck $h$ is called the **latent code** or **bottleneck representation**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Postal Compression System** > > Imagine you have to send a heavy, bulky package (the input $x$) through a narrow mail chute (the bottleneck $h$). A smart postal worker (the **encoder**) disassembles the package into a compact, symbolic description — a blueprint or a list of parts. This compact description travels easily through the chute. At the destination, a second worker (the **decoder**) reads the blueprint and **reassembles** the original package. If the rebuilt package matches the original, the system has learned a meaningful, compressed representation. The package never left the building, yet the workers have learned **what matters** about the object. The key insight: the network is forced to discover the **most essential features** of the data because the channel capacity is restricted. ## 1.3 Architectural Anatomy | Component | Symbol | Function | Typical Activation | |-----------|--------|----------|---------------------| | Input Layer | $x$ | Original data vector (e.g., flattened image of size $784$) | None | | Encoder | $f_{\text{enc}}$ | Maps $x \rightarrow h$ | ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh | | Bottleneck / Latent Layer | $h$ | Compressed representation (e.g., size $32$) | ReLU, Sigmoid | | Decoder | $f_{\text{dec}}$ | Maps $h \rightarrow \hat{x}$ | ReLU, Sigmoid | | Output Layer | $\hat{x}$ | Reconstruction of input | Sigmoid (for $[0,1]$) | ## 1.4 The Three Foundational Variants > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Point:** Always differentiate between these three configurations — examiners frequently frame 3-mark questions on this distinction. 1. **Undercomplete Autoencoder:** Bottleneck dimension $m < n$. Forces compression. 2. **Overcomplete Autoencoder:** Bottleneck dimension $m \ge n$. Requires regularization (e.g., sparsity, denoising) to avoid trivial identity mapping. 3. **Regularized Autoencoder:** Imposes additional constraints (L1 sparsity, denoising noise, contractive penalty) on either $m \le n$ or $m > n$. ## 1.5 Why Autoencoders Matter in Engineering - **Dimensionality Reduction:** Non-linear alternative to PCA. - **Anomaly Detection:** High reconstruction error $\Rightarrow$ anomalous sample. - **Denoising:** Learns to recover clean signal from corrupted input. - **Generative Pre-training:** Foundation for Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) and diffusion models. - **Feature Extraction:** Bottleneck features serve as inputs to downstream classifiers. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — The Encoder-Decoder Pipeline The autoencoder operates in two tightly-coupled phases: ### Phase 1: Encoding - Input vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ enters the input layer. - The encoder performs a series of affine transformations followed by non-linear activations, progressively shrinking dimensionality. - Mathematically, for a single hidden layer encoder: $$h = \sigma(W_e x + b_e)$$ where: - $W_e \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the encoder weight matrix. - $b_e \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the encoder bias vector. - $\sigma(\cdot)$ is the element-wise non-linear activation function. ### Phase 2: Decoding - The latent code $h$ is then expanded back to the original dimension $n$: $$\hat{x} = \sigma'(W_d h + b_d)$$ where $W_d \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ and $b_d \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$. ### Phase 3: Loss Computation and Backpropagation - The reconstruction error is measured and backpropagated through the entire network using gradient descent. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it carries 7–10 marks in any Part B (14-mark) autoencoder question.** | # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units | |---|---------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Encoder mapping | $h = f_{\text{enc}}(x) = \sigma(W_e x + b_e)$ | $\sigma$ = activation; e.g., ReLU, sigmoid | | 2 | Decoder mapping | $\hat{x} = f_{\text{dec}}(h) = \sigma'(W_d h + b_d)$ | Output activation matches input range | | 3 | MSE Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Vert x_i - \hat{x}_i\Vert_2^2$ | Used for real-valued inputs | | 4 | Binary Cross-Entropy | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[x_i \log \hat{x}_i + (1-x_i)\log(1-\hat{x}_i)\right]$ | Used for inputs in $[0,1]$ | | 5 | Total Parameters | $P = (n \cdot m + m) + (m \cdot n + n) = 2nm + m + n$ | Weights + biases for 1 hidden layer AE | | 6 | Bottleneck Constraint | $m < n$ (undercomplete) or $m \ge n$ (overcomplete) | Dimensionality of $h$ | | 7 | L1 Sparsity Penalty | $\Omega(h) = \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{m} \vert h_j \vert$ | Forces sparse latent activations | | 8 | KL-Divergence Sparsity | $\Omega = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \text{KL}(\rho \Vert \hat{\rho}_j)$ | Penalizes deviation from target sparsity $\rho$ | | 9 | Denoising Objective | $\mathcal{L} = \Vert x - f_{\text{dec}}(f_{\text{enc}}(\tilde{x}))\Vert^2$ | $\tilde{x}$ is corrupted input | | 10 | Gradient Update | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}$ | Standard SGD step | ## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Design Choice > [!NOTE] > **Why a bottleneck?** If $h$ had the same dimension as $x$ and the network were deep enough, it would trivially learn the **identity function** $f(x) = x$, memorizing the input without learning anything useful. The bottleneck forces the network to **discard redundant information** and **preserve only the most salient patterns**. > [!NOTE] > **Why non-linear activations?** Without $\sigma(\cdot)$, the entire encoder-decoder stack would collapse into a single linear map $\hat{x} = W_d W_e x$, reducing the autoencoder to **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**. Non-linearities unlock the ability to model complex, curved manifolds in data. > [!NOTE] > **Why is the loss function the same as input dimension?** Because we are comparing $\hat{x}$ with $x$ — both in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$. The bottleneck $h$ is hidden inside the loss evaluation. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Manufacturing Defect Detection:** Train on defect-free turbine blades; flag any blade with reconstruction error above threshold as potentially defective. - **Medical Imaging Compression:** Compress MRI scans for storage; reconstruct losslessly to acceptable quality. - **Cybersecurity:** Detect network intrusions by treating them as anomalies in the latent space. - **Recommendation Systems:** Learn user/item embeddings via autoencoder-based collaborative filtering (AutoRec). # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Backpropagation Through a Single-Layer Autoencoder We consider a single-hidden-layer autoencoder with the following explicit form: ### Step 1 — Define the Forward Pass **Encoder Pre-activation:** $$z_e = W_e x + b_e$$ **Encoder Output (latent code):** $$h = \sigma(z_e)$$ **Decoder Pre-activation:** $$z_d = W_d h + b_d$$ **Decoder Output (reconstruction):** $$\hat{x} = \sigma_d(z_d)$$ > Here $\sigma$ and $\sigma_d$ are the encoder and decoder activation functions, respectively. For derivation simplicity below, we initially treat the output as linear (then extend to non-linear). ### Step 2 — Define the Loss Function (MSE) $$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\Vert x - \hat{x}\Vert_2^2 = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$$ The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is a mathematical convenience to cancel the exponent during differentiation. ### Step 3 — Compute the Gradient of the Loss w.r.t. Output $\hat{x}$ $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}} = -(x - \hat{x}) = \hat{x} - x$$ This is the standard derivative of the squared error. ### Step 4 — Propagate Gradient Through the Decoder **Gradient w.r.t. decoder pre-activation $z_d$ (assuming linear decoder first):** $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_d} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{x}}{\partial z_d}$$ If we use linear output activation, $\frac{\partial \hat{x}}{\partial z_d} = 1$ (identity). If sigmoid, it becomes $\sigma_d'(z_d) = \hat{x}(1-\hat{x})$. For the **general case:** $$\delta_d = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_d} = (\hat{x} - x) \odot \sigma_d'(z_d)$$ where $\odot$ denotes the element-wise (Hadamard) product. ### Step 5 — Gradient w.r.t. Decoder Weights $W_d$ Applying the chain rule: $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_d} = \delta_d \cdot h^T$$ **Row-by-row expansion:** The $(i,j)$-th element of $W_d$ is the weight connecting latent neuron $j$ to output neuron $i$. The gradient is therefore the outer product of the decoder error $\delta_d$ (size $n \times 1$) and the latent vector $h$ (size $m \times 1$). ### Step 6 — Gradient w.r.t. Decoder Biases $b_d$ $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b_d} = \delta_d$$ The bias gradient equals the pre-activation error directly. ### Step 7 — Propagate Gradient to the Encoder (Backpropagation Through Decoder) The latent code $h$ receives gradient from the decoder: $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h} = W_d^T \cdot \delta_d$$ This is a vector of size $m$ — the gradient of the total loss with respect to each latent activation. ### Step 8 — Gradient w.r.t. Encoder Pre-activation $z_e$ $$\delta_e = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_e} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h} \odot \sigma'(z_e) = (W_d^T \delta_d) \odot \sigma'(z_e)$$ ### Step 9 — Gradient w.r.t. Encoder Weights $W_e$ $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_e} = \delta_e \cdot x^T$$ ### Step 10 — Final Parameter Update Rule (Gradient Descent) $$W_d \leftarrow W_d - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_d} = W_d - \eta \, \delta_d h^T$$ $$W_e \leftarrow W_e - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_e} = W_e - \eta \, \delta_e x^T$$ $$b_d \leftarrow b_d - \eta \, \delta_d$$ $$b_e \leftarrow b_e - \eta \, \delta_e$$ where $\eta$ is the learning rate (hyperparameter, typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$). ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Loss Functions | Loss Function | Mathematical Form | Best Suited For | Gradient Behavior | |---------------|-------------------|-----------------|-------------------| | Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $\frac{1}{N}\sum (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$ | Continuous real-valued data (images, audio) | Vanishes near $x \approx \hat{x}$ | | Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) | $-\frac{1}{N}\sum [x\log\hat{x} + (1-x)\log(1-\hat{x})]$ | Binary or normalized data in $[0,1]$ | Strong gradient even for small errors | | L1 (Mean Absolute Error) | $\frac{1}{N}\sum \vert x_i - \hat{x}_i\vert$ | Robust to outliers, sparse data | Constant gradient magnitude | | Structural Similarity (SSIM) | $\text{SSIM}(x, \hat{x})$ — see deep-learning libraries | Perceptually meaningful image reconstruction | Non-MSE, mimics human vision | ## 3.3 Worked Example: Forward Pass on a 3-Dimensional Toy Input > **Given:** $x = [1.0, 0.5, 0.2]^T$, encoder weights $W_e = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.4 & -0.1 \\ -0.3 & 0.5 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$, encoder bias $b_e = [0.1, 0.0]^T$, sigmoid activation. **Step A — Compute encoder pre-activation:** $$z_e = W_e x + b_e = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0.4 & -0.1 \\ -0.3 & 0.5 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 0.5 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.0 \end{bmatrix}$$ Row 1: $(0.2)(1.0) + (0.4)(0.5) + (-0.1)(0.2) + 0.1 = 0.2 + 0.2 - 0.02 + 0.1 = 0.48$. Row 2: $(-0.3)(1.0) + (0.5)(0.5) + (0.2)(0.2) + 0.0 = -0.3 + 0.25 + 0.04 = -0.01$. So $z_e = [0.48, -0.01]^T$. **Step B — Apply sigmoid activation:** $$h_1 = \sigma(0.48) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.48}} \approx 0.618$$ $$h_2 = \sigma(-0.01) \approx 0.4975$$ Thus $h = [0.618, 0.4975]^T$. **Step C — Compute decoder pre-activation** (decoder weights $W_d$ provided similarly) and apply $\sigma_d$ to obtain $\hat{x}$. The **reconstruction error** is then $\Vert x - \hat{x}\Vert_2^2$, and gradients flow backward through this exact chain of operations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** When asked to "show the training of an autoencoder" in a 14-mark question, the examiner expects at minimum: forward pass equation, loss formula, and gradient update equations for $W_e, W_d, b_e, b_d$. Memorize the chain $\delta_d \rightarrow \delta_e \rightarrow$ weight updates. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied - All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `nodeInput`, `encLayer1`). - No reserved keywords used as standalone node IDs. - Node labels contain raw uppercase alphanumeric text only — no bold, italic, or HTML. ## 4.2 Autoencoder Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid graph LR nodeInput["INPUT LAYER
x in R to the n
n equals 784 for MNIST"] encLayer1["ENCODER HIDDEN 1
W1 x plus b1
ReLU activation"] encLayer2["ENCODER HIDDEN 2
W2 h1 plus b2
ReLU activation"] bottleneck["BOTTLENECK LATENT CODE
h in R to the m
m is much less than n"] decLayer1["DECODER HIDDEN 1
W3 h plus b3
ReLU activation"] decLayer2["DECODER HIDDEN 2
W4 h plus b4
ReLU activation"] nodeOutput["OUTPUT LAYER
x hat in R to the n
Sigmoid activation"] loss["LOSS COMPUTATION
MSE or BCE
compare x and x hat"] opt["OPTIMIZER
Gradient Descent
backpropagation"] nodeInput --> encLayer1 --> encLayer2 --> bottleneck bottleneck --> decLayer1 --> decLayer2 --> nodeOutput nodeOutput --> loss loss --> opt opt -.update weights.-> encLayer1 opt -.update weights.-> encLayer2 opt -.update weights.-> decLayer1 opt -.update weights.-> decLayer2 ``` ## 4.3 Information Bottleneck Visualization ```mermaid graph TD subgraph HighDim["HIGH DIMENSIONAL INPUT SPACE n equals 784"] rawX["ORIGINAL IMAGE FLATTENED VECTOR"] end subgraph MidDim["ENCODER COMPRESSION STAGES"] l1["Layer 1 size 256"] l2["Layer 2 size 128"] l3["Layer 3 size 64"] end subgraph Bottleneck["LATENT SPACE m equals 32"] code["COMPRESSED CODE h
PRESERVES CORE FEATURES"] end subgraph Reconstruct["DECODER RECONSTRUCTION STAGES"] d1["Layer 1 size 64"] d2["Layer 2 size 128"] d3["Layer 3 size 256"] end subgraph OutDim["RECONSTRUCTED OUTPUT n equals 784"] outX["OUTPUT IMAGE x hat"] end rawX --> l1 --> l2 --> l3 --> code --> d1 --> d2 --> d3 --> outX ``` ## 4.4 Training Loop Sequence Diagram ```mermaid graph LR s1["STEP 1
Initialize weights W and b"] s2["STEP 2
For each epoch"] s3["STEP 3
Sample mini batch from dataset"] s4["STEP 4
Forward pass
compute h and x hat"] s5["STEP 5
Compute reconstruction loss"] s6["STEP 6
Backward pass
compute gradients"] s7["STEP 7
Update weights using SGD or Adam"] s8["STEP 8
Evaluate on validation set"] s9["STEP 9
Repeat until convergence"] s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8 --> s9 s9 -.next epoch.-> s3 ``` ## 4.5 Conceptual Mapping: Autoencoder vs. PCA ```mermaid graph LR pca["LINEAR PCA
Single matrix W
X is projected to Z equals W transpose X"] ae["NON LINEAR AUTOENCODER
Multi layer encoder and decoder
h equals sigma of W x plus b"] feat1["Captures only linear variance"] feat2["Captures non linear manifolds"] feat3["No activation functions"] feat4["Uses ReLU sigmoid tanh"] pca --> feat1 pca --> feat3 ae --> feat2 ae --> feat4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Aid:** The diagrams above can be reproduced verbatim in your answer sheets if the question asks to "draw the autoencoder architecture." KTU examiners reward **clearly labelled diagrams** with arrows showing data flow direction and dimensional annotations. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Model answers are precise and board-style — write only 3–4 lines in the exam.** ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define an autoencoder. What is the role of the bottleneck layer?** **Model Answer:** An autoencoder is an unsupervised neural network trained to reconstruct its input at the output. It consists of an encoder that maps the input $x$ to a latent code $h = f_{\text{enc}}(x)$ and a decoder that reconstructs $\hat{x} = f_{\text{dec}}(h)$. The **bottleneck layer** $h$ has a smaller dimension than the input, forcing the network to learn a compressed, information-rich representation. Without the bottleneck, the network would trivially learn the identity function and fail to extract meaningful features. ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between undercomplete and overcomplete autoencoders.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Undercomplete AE | Overcomplete AE | |---------|------------------|-----------------| | Bottleneck size | $m < n$ | $m \ge n$ | | Working principle | Forces compression by dimension reduction | Requires explicit regularization | | Risk | Very low risk of identity mapping | High risk of learning trivial identity | | Common use | General feature learning | Sparsity or denoising-based learning | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > - **Mark Loss Zone 1:** Writing the encoder/decoder equations without specifying the **activation function** — deducts 2 marks. > - **Mark Loss Zone 2:** Confusing **MSE** (continuous) with **BCE** (binary) loss — examiners treat this as a fundamental error. > - **Mark Loss Zone 3:** Drawing the architecture without labeling dimensions ($n, m$) or arrows — deducts 1–2 marks. > - **Mark Loss Zone 4:** Skipping the backpropagation step in 14-mark derivations — the model expects at least 2–3 gradient equations. ### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]` **(a)** Explain the architecture and working of an autoencoder with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the encoder, decoder, and bottleneck layer in feature learning. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the backpropagation update rules for a single-hidden-layer autoencoder using the Mean Squared Error loss function. State all the gradient equations clearly. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Architecture and Working (7 marks):** 1. **Definition (1 mark):** An autoencoder is a feedforward neural network trained to reproduce its input at the output, with one or more hidden layers of smaller dimension than the input. 2. **Encoder (2 marks):** Maps input $x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ to latent code $h \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ where $m < n$: $$h = \sigma(W_e x + b_e)$$ 3. **Bottleneck / Latent Code (2 marks):** The compressed representation $h$ captures the most salient features. The dimension reduction forces the network to discard redundant information and learn a meaningful, low-dimensional embedding of the data manifold. 4. **Decoder (2 marks):** Reconstructs the input from $h$: $$\hat{x} = \sigma_d(W_d h + b_d)$$ 5. **Diagram (must include):** Block diagram showing input $\to$ encoder $\to$ bottleneck $\to$ decoder $\to$ output $\to$ loss comparison with input. **(b) Backpropagation Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Forward pass (1 mark):** $$z_e = W_e x + b_e, \quad h = \sigma(z_e)$$ $$z_d = W_d h + b_d, \quad \hat{x} = \sigma_d(z_d)$$ **Step 2 — Loss function (1 mark):** $$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Vert x_i - \hat{x}_i\Vert_2^2$$ **Step 3 — Decoder gradients (2 marks):** $$\delta_d = (\hat{x} - x) \odot \sigma_d'(z_d)$$ $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_d} = \delta_d h^T, \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b_d} = \delta_d$$ **Step 4 — Backpropagated error to encoder (2 marks):** $$\delta_e = (W_d^T \delta_d) \odot \sigma'(z_e)$$ $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_e} = \delta_e x^T, \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b_e} = \delta_e$$ **Step 5 — Parameter update (1 mark):** $$\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With suitable equations, explain the loss functions used in training autoencoders. Compare MSE and Binary Cross-Entropy losses. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the denoising autoencoder with its objective function. How does it differ from a vanilla autoencoder? Explain with a block diagram. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Loss Functions (7 marks):** 1. **Reconstruction Loss Concept (2 marks):** Autoencoders minimize a reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x})$ that measures the dissimilarity between input and reconstruction. 2. **Mean Squared Error (2 marks):** $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Vert x_i - \hat{x}_i\Vert_2^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{n}(x_{ij} - \hat{x}_{ij})^2$$ Suitable for continuous-valued data; penalizes large errors quadratically. 3. **Binary Cross-Entropy (2 marks):** $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[x_{ij}\log \hat{x}_{ij} + (1-x_{ij})\log(1-\hat{x}_{ij})\right]$$ Suitable for binary or normalized inputs in $[0,1]$; derived from the negative log-likelihood under a Bernoulli model. 4. **Comparison Table (1 mark):** MSE is differentiable everywhere, BCE is preferable for probabilistic outputs. **(b) Denoising Autoencoder (7 marks):** 1. **Concept (2 marks):** A denoising autoencoder (DAE) is trained to reconstruct the clean input $x$ from a **corrupted** version $\tilde{x}$, typically obtained by adding Gaussian noise or randomly masking input dimensions (dropout-style corruption). 2. **Corruption Process (1 mark):** $$\tilde{x} \sim q_D(\tilde{x} \vert x)$$ For example, with random masking probability $\nu$: $\tilde{x}_i = x_i$ with probability $1-\nu$, else $0$. 3. **Objective Function (2 marks):** $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{DAE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Vert x_i - f_{\text{dec}}(f_{\text{enc}}(\tilde{x}_i))\Vert_2^2$$ Note: the loss is computed against the **clean** $x_i$, not the corrupted $\tilde{x}_i$. 4. **Differences from Vanilla AE (1 mark):** Vanilla AE learns $f(x) \approx x$ directly. Denoising AE learns to **infer clean structure from corrupted observations**, producing more robust and semantically meaningful latent representations. 5. **Block Diagram (1 mark):** Show input corruption module feeding into the encoder, with the loss comparing decoder output against the **original** (uncorrupted) input. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.** - **Core Definition:** Autoencoder = Encoder + Bottleneck + Decoder, trained to reconstruct input. - **Three Roles:** Encoder compresses, bottleneck represents, decoder reconstructs. - **Bottleneck Constraint:** $m < n$ for undercomplete; $m \ge n$ for overcomplete (with regularization). - **Two Key Loss Functions:** - MSE: $\frac{1}{N}\sum (x - \hat{x})^2$ — for continuous data. - BCE: $-\frac{1}{N}\sum [x \log \hat{x} + (1-x)\log(1-\hat{x})]$ — for binary data. - **Backpropagation Chain:** $\delta_d = (\hat{x} - x) \odot \sigma_d'(z_d)$, then $\delta_e = (W_d^T \delta_d) \odot \sigma'(z_e)$. - **Weight Update:** $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}$. - **Denoising AE:** Input is corrupted ($\tilde{x}$); loss is computed against clean $x$. - **Sparse AE:** Adds L1 penalty $\lambda \sum \vert h_j \vert$ or KL-divergence penalty to latent code. - **Contractive AE:** Adds penalty on the Jacobian of the encoder: $\lambda \Vert \nabla_x h \Vert_F^2$. - **VAE Connection:** Probabilistic autoencoder that models $p(z \vert x)$ using a Gaussian prior — the natural progression from vanilla AE. - **Common Applications:** Dimensionality reduction, anomaly detection, denoising, pre-training, generative modeling. - **Autoencoder vs PCA:** Autoencoder is **non-linear** (with activations); PCA is purely linear. - **Parameter Count Formula:** $P = 2nm + m + n$ for a single-hidden-layer AE. - **Activation Choices:** ReLU (hidden layers), Sigmoid (output for $[0,1]$ data), Tanh (output for $[-1,1]$ data). - **Convergence Monitor:** Plot training vs. validation reconstruction loss; stop when validation loss plateaus. - **Overfitting Mitigation:** Use regularization, early stopping, or denoising objectives. - **Exam Vocabulary:** Use terms "latent code," "reconstruction error," "bottleneck," "self-supervised," and "undercomplete" — these are board-favored phrases. - **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never use $\hat{x}$ as input to the loss; always compare $\hat{x}$ with the **original** $x$. - **One-line Summary:** *Autoencoders learn to copy input to output through a constrained bottleneck, forcing the discovery of compressed, meaningful representations.*

topics.title: Deep Autoencoder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT745 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deep Autoencoder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Deep Autoencoder** is an unsupervised neural network architecture designed to learn a compressed, distributed representation (encoding) of input data by training the network to reconstruct its own input at the output layer. It is composed of two symmetric, mirrored sub-networks:

- **Encoder function** $\phi : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{H}$ that maps the high-dimensional input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ to a lower-dimensional latent (hidden) representation $\mathbf{h} \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ where typically $p \ll d$.
- **Decoder function** $\psi : \mathcal{H} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$ that maps the latent code $\mathbf{h}$ back to a reconstruction $\mathbf{r} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ in the original input space.

Mathematically, the composite function is $\psi \circ \phi : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$, trained to satisfy $\mathbf{r} \approx \mathbf{x}$. The term **"Deep"** specifies that the encoder and decoder each contain **multiple hidden layers** (typically ≥ 2), enabling the learning of hierarchical, non-linear feature abstractions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Deep Autoencoders belong to the *Representation Learning* family. They are explicitly distinct from PCA because they capture **non-linear** manifolds, whereas PCA is restricted to linear subspaces.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Photographer's Negative" Analogy:**
> 
> Imagine a skilled photographer who must store thousands of high-resolution photographs in a tiny safe. 
> 1. **Encoder (Compression):** He develops a personal "secret shorthand" — instead of storing every pixel, he writes down only the *essential features* of each photo (e.g., "sunset, beach, two people, golden light"). This is the **latent code $\mathbf{h}$**.
> 2. **Bottleneck (The Safe):** The safe can only hold a few words per photo, forcing the photographer to keep only the most informative descriptors.
> 3. **Decoder (Decompression):** When he needs the photo back, he reads his shorthand and mentally **reconstructs** the original image with remarkable fidelity — not pixel-perfect, but perceptually accurate.
> 
> An autoencoder does exactly this: it forces an information bottleneck so that the network *must* learn the most salient, compressed essence of the data. The reconstruction is rarely identical to the input — it is a *learned approximation*.

## 1.3 Why "Deep" Specifically?

A *shallow* autoencoder with a single hidden layer behaves mathematically equivalently to **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** when using a linear activation and MSE loss. The depth introduces **non-linear activation functions** (ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh) stacked across layers, which empower the network to learn **non-linear manifold embeddings** — something PCA fundamentally cannot do.

| Property | Shallow Autoencoder (≈ PCA) | Deep Autoencoder |
|---|---|---|
| Hidden Layers | 1 | ≥ 2 |
| Activation | Linear | Non-linear (ReLU, Sigmoid) |
| Manifold Type | Linear subspace | Non-linear manifold |
| Expressivity | Limited | High (Universal Approximator property) |

---

> **Key Constant & Standard Metrics Highlighted:**
> - Dimensionality reduction ratio: $\rho = \frac{p}{d}$ (typically $0.01 \le \rho \le 0.1$)
> - **Reconstruction error** is the canonical evaluation metric, typically reported in normalized form.
> - Latent dimension $p$ is a **hyperparameter** — too small ⇒ underfitting (high reconstruction error); too large ⇒ identity mapping (no compression).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Input Reception
The network receives an input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$. In the case of image data, this is typically flattened, e.g., for a $28 \times 28$ MNIST image, $d = 784$.

### Step 2: Encoding (Hierarchical Compression)
The encoder is a stack of $L$ fully-connected (or convolutional) layers. Each layer $i$ applies an affine transformation followed by a non-linear activation:

$$\mathbf{h}^{(i)} = f^{(i)}\left(\mathbf{W}^{(i)} \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(i)}\right), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, L$$

where $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}$, $\mathbf{W}^{(i)}$ is the weight matrix, $\mathbf{b}^{(i)}$ is the bias vector, and $f^{(i)}$ is the activation function (commonly ReLU for inner layers, identity or sigmoid at the bottleneck).

The final encoder output is the **latent code**:

$$\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{h}^{(L)} = \phi(\mathbf{x}) \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$$

### Step 3: Bottleneck / Latent Space
The bottleneck layer $\mathbf{h}$ is the compressed representation. The constraint $p \ll d$ forces the network to discard redundant information and retain only the most discriminative features. The bottleneck is the **information-theoretic core** of the autoencoder.

### Step 4: Decoding (Hierarchical Reconstruction)
The decoder mirrors the encoder with $L'$ layers, often using *tied weights* (i.e., $\mathbf{W}^{(d)}_{i} = {\mathbf{W}^{(e)}_{i}}^{\top}$) to reduce the parameter count and impose a regularizing inductive bias.

$$\mathbf{r}^{(j)} = g^{(j)}\left(\mathbf{W'}^{(j)} \mathbf{r}^{(j-1)} + \mathbf{c}^{(j)}\right), \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, L'$$

The final reconstruction is $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{r}^{(L')} = \psi(\mathbf{h})$.

### Step 5: Loss Computation & Backpropagation
The reconstruction loss compares $\mathbf{r}$ to $\mathbf{x}$. Gradients of this loss with respect to all weights and biases are computed via backpropagation, and parameters are updated using gradient descent (or its variants like Adam, RMSProp).

### Step 6: Convergence
Training continues until the reconstruction loss plateaus on a held-out validation set. The trained encoder $\phi$ is then deployed as a **feature extractor** for downstream tasks (classification, clustering, retrieval, anomaly detection).

## 2.2 The Core "Why" & "How"

- **Why a bottleneck?** Without a bottleneck ($p \ge d$), the network trivially learns the identity function $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{x}$, providing zero compression. The bottleneck is the **inductive bias** that forces meaningful representation learning.
- **Why non-linear activations?** Linear stacked layers collapse mathematically into a single linear transformation. Non-linearities enable the network to learn curved, intricate manifolds.
- **How does it differ from supervised learning?** Autoencoders are trained using input as both feature *and* target. They are an **unsupervised** or **self-supervised** learning paradigm.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in 90% of KTU numerical/analytical questions on Autoencoders.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Encoder mapping | $\mathbf{h} = \phi(\mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b})$ | Compresses $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ to $\mathbf{h} \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ |
| 2 | Decoder mapping | $\mathbf{r} = \psi(\mathbf{h}) = g(\mathbf{W'}\mathbf{h} + \mathbf{c})$ | Reconstructs $\mathbf{r} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ from $\mathbf{h}$ |
| 3 | Composite mapping | $\mathbf{r} = (\psi \circ \phi)(\mathbf{x})$ | Full autoencoder forward pass |
| 4 | MSE Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} \Vert \mathbf{x}_n - \mathbf{r}_n \Vert_2^{2}$ | Standard reconstruction loss for real-valued data |
| 5 | Binary Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{BCE} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\sum_{i=1}^{d}\left[x_{ni}\log r_{ni} + (1-x_{ni})\log(1-r_{ni})\right]$ | Used when inputs are in $[0,1]$ (e.g., normalized images) |
| 6 | Sparsity Penalty (L1) | $\mathcal{L}_{sparse} = \mathcal{L}_{MSE} + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert h_j \vert$ | Forces most latent units to be inactive |
| 7 | KL-Divergence Sparsity | $\Omega_{sparse} = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \rho \log \frac{\rho}{\hat{\rho}_j} + (1-\rho)\log \frac{1-\rho}{1-\hat{\rho}_j}$ | Enforces target average activation $\rho$ |
| 8 | Denoising Objective | $\mathcal{L}_{DAE} = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \psi(\phi(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})) \Vert^{2}$ | $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ is corrupted input; reconstruct clean $\mathbf{x}$ |
| 9 | VAE ELBO | $\mathcal{L}_{ELBO} = \mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{h}\vert\mathbf{x})}[\log p(\mathbf{x}\vert\mathbf{h})] - D_{KL}(q(\mathbf{h}\vert\mathbf{x}) \Vert p(\mathbf{h}))$ | Evidence Lower Bound for Variational Autoencoder |
| 10 | Reparameterization Trick | $\mathbf{h} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \boldsymbol{\sigma} \odot \boldsymbol{\epsilon}, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I})$ | Allows gradient flow through stochastic sampling |
| 11 | Tied Weights Constraint | $\mathbf{W}_{decoder} = \mathbf{W}_{encoder}^{\top}$ | Halves parameter count, regularizes |
| 12 | Contractive Penalty | $\mathcal{L}_{CAE} = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{r} \Vert^{2} + \lambda \Vert J_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) \Vert_F^{2}$ | Penalizes Jacobian to learn robust features |
| 13 | Compression Ratio | $\rho = p / d$ | Quantifies dimensionality reduction |
| 14 | Effective Dimensionality | $d_{eff} = \text{rank}(J_{\phi})$ | Linear approximation of intrinsic manifold dimension |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Computer Vision** | Image denoising, super-resolution, anomaly detection in manufacturing |
| **NLP** | Sentence embedding compression, document retrieval |
| **Cybersecurity** | Anomaly-based intrusion detection (large reconstruction error ⇒ attack) |
| **Medical Imaging** | Compressed MRI storage, tumor detection via reconstruction deviation |
| **Recommendation Systems** | Collaborative filtering (AutoRec model) |
| **Generative AI** | Foundation for Variational Autoencoders (VAEs), diffusion pre-training |
| **Signal Processing** | Speech compression, audio feature extraction |
| **Industrial IoT** | Sensor data compression for low-bandwidth transmission |

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The encoder output $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ is often re-used as a **pretrained feature extractor** for downstream supervised tasks. This is the principle behind *transfer learning* with autoencoders — particularly useful when labeled data is scarce but unlabeled data is abundant.
