# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a SOM Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InputLayer["INPUT LAYER n neurons x1 x2 ... xn"]
    WeightMatrix["WEIGHT MATRIX W of size M by n each row is weight vector wj"]
    DiscBlock["DISCRIMINANT COMPUTATION d1 to dM dj squared equals sum of x minus wj squared"]
    WinnerBlock["WINNER SELECTION BMU i star equals argmin dj"]
    NeighBlock["NEIGHBOURHOOD KERNEL hji star equals exp of minus d squared over two sigma squared"]
    LRBlock["LEARNING RATE SCHEDULE alpha of t equals alpha0 exp of minus t over tau alpha"]
    UpdateBlock["WEIGHT UPDATE wj new equals wj plus alpha hji star times x minus wj"]

    InputLayer --> WeightMatrix
    WeightMatrix --> DiscBlock
    DiscBlock --> WinnerBlock
    WinnerBlock --> NeighBlock
    NeighBlock --> UpdateBlock
    LRBlock --> UpdateBlock
    UpdateBlock --> WeightMatrix

    style InputLayer fill:#FFE4B5
    style WeightMatrix fill:#B0E0E6
    style DiscBlock fill:#DDA0DD
    style WinnerBlock fill:#98FB98
    style NeighBlock fill:#F0E68C
    style LRBlock fill:#FFB6C1
    style UpdateBlock fill:#87CEEB
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The flow is cyclic — every updated weight matrix becomes the starting point for the next input. The two decay schedules ($\alpha$, $\sigma$) provide the "cooling" that converts competitive dynamics into orderly self-organisation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([START Training])

    ReadX["Read next input sample x of t"]
    FindBMU["Find BMU i star equals argmin over j of norm x minus wj"]
    ComputeH["Compute neighbourhood values hji star for all j"]
    UpdateW["Update wj using Kohonen rule"]
    Decay["Decay alpha of t plus 1 and sigma of t plus 1"]
    CheckEpochs{"t less than T"}
    Output([END Trained SOM Map W])

    Start --> InitW --> InitParams --> ReadX --> FindBMU --> ComputeH --> UpdateW --> Decay --> CheckEpochs
    CheckEpochs -- "Yes" --> ReadX
    CheckEpochs -- "No" --> Output

    style Start fill:#90EE90
    style Output fill:#FFB6C1

```

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "2D Rectangular Lattice"
        A1["N1"] --- A2["N2"] --- A3["N3"] --- A4["N4"]
        B1["N5"] --- B2["N6"] --- B3["N7"] --- B4["N8"]
        C1["N9"] --- C2["N10"] --- C3["N11"] --- C4["N12"]
        A1 --- B1
        A2 --- B2
        A3 --- B3
        A4 --- B4
        B1 --- C1
        B2 --- C2
        B3 --- C3
        B4 --- C4
    end

    style A2 fill:#FFA07A
    style A3 fill:#FFA07A
    style B2 fill:#FFA07A
    style B3 fill:#FFA07A
    style C2 fill:#FFA07A
    style C3 fill:#FFA07A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The 2-D rectangular lattice has **4 immediate neighbours** (except at edges). Hexagonal lattices have **6 immediate neighbours**, giving smoother topology preservation (Kohonen's recommendation). The orange-shaded "N6/N7/N10/N11" cluster represents the **BMU and its topological neighbourhood** during one update step.

## 4.4 Functional Mapping — From Input Space to Output Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Input Space R3"
        P1["Point A 1, 2, 3"]
        P2["Point B 2, 1, 4"]
        P3["Point C 5, 6, 1"]
    end

    subgraph "SOM 2D Grid"
        M1["Map node top left"] --- M2["Map node top right"]
        M3["Map node bottom left"] --- M4["Map node bottom right"]
        M1 --- M3
        M2 --- M4
    end

    P1 -. "BMU" .-> M1
    P2 -. "BMU" .-> M2
    P3 -. "BMU" .-> M4

    style P1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style P2 fill:#FFE4B5
    style P3 fill:#FFE4B5
    style M1 fill:#90EE90
    style M2 fill:#90EE90
    style M4 fill:#90EE90
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Three points in 3-D space (P1, P2, P3) are mapped to three neurons on the 2-D grid. Crucially, if P1 and P2 were "closer" in the input space, they would land on **adjacent** map neurons (e.g., M1 and M2), not on M4. **This is the essence of topology preservation.**

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** With a neat diagram, explain the **basic architecture of a Self-Organising Map (SOM)**. State the role of the input and output (map) layers.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A SOM has two layers — an **input layer** of $n$ neurons (one per input feature) fully connected to an **output (Kohonen) layer** arranged in a 1-D or 2-D lattice of $M$ neurons. Each output neuron $j$ stores a weight vector $\mathbf{w}_j \in \mathbb{R}^n$ of the same dimension as the input.

- **Input layer:** receives the input vector $\mathbf{x}$ and broadcasts it to all output neurons (no computation).
- **Output layer:** each neuron computes the distance $\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_j \Vert$; the neuron with the minimum distance is the **Best Matching Unit (BMU)**.

**Diagram (essential for full marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1["x1"] --> O1["w1"]
    I1 --> O2["w2"]
    I1 --> O3["w3"]
    I2["x2"] --> O1
    I2 --> O2
    I2 --> O3
    I3["xn"] --> O1
    I3 --> O2
    I3 --> O3
    O1 --- O2
    O2 --- O3
    O1 --- O3
```

(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — input layer role, 1 mark — output layer role with BMU concept.)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **competitive learning**. Why is the *neighbourhood function* essential for converting a competitive network into a SOM?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Competitive Learning** is an unsupervised learning scheme in which output neurons compete to represent an input. The neuron whose weight vector is *closest* to the current input (the **winner**) is the only one allowed to update its weights — all others are suppressed (winner-take-all).

**Why the neighbourhood function is essential:**
1. In **pure** competitive learning, only the BMU moves. Multiple neurons can collapse to the same input (dead neurons / under-utilisation).
2. The **neighbourhood function** $h_{ji^*}$ allows the BMU **and its spatial neighbours** on the lattice to update, which **enforces topological ordering** and prevents dead neurons.
3. As training progresses, the neighbourhood radius $\sigma(t)$ shrinks to zero, so the map *gradually* transitions from global ordering to fine-grained local fitting — yielding the **Self-Organising Map**.

(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — limitation of pure WTA, 1 mark — role of neighbourhood.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Module 4]** Answer the following:

**(a)** [7 marks] Describe the **Kohonen Self-Organising Map algorithm** in detail. Explain the role of the **learning rate** $\alpha(t)$ and the **neighbourhood function** $h_{ji^*}(t)$. Why are both decayed over time?

**(b)** [7 marks] For a 2-D SOM with a $3 \times 3$ rectangular lattice (9 neurons), draw the topological neighbourhood of the **corner neuron** and the **edge (non-corner) neuron** for $\sigma = 1$ and $\sigma = 2$ (using bubble neighbourhood). Justify the choice of lattice shape (rectangular vs hexagonal) in real applications.

#### Model Answer to (a)

The **Kohonen SOM algorithm** has the following steps (presented earlier; restated for completeness):

**Step 1 — Initialisation.** Initialise weight vectors $\mathbf{w}_j(0)$ randomly (or using a small random subset of the data, or principal-component samples for faster convergence). Set $\alpha(0) \in (0, 1]$ and $\sigma(0) \approx$ half the lattice dimension.

**Step 2 — Sampling.** Draw an input sample $\mathbf{x}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ from the dataset at iteration $t$.

**Step 3 — Competitive Step (BMU Search).** Compute the discriminant $d_j = \Vert \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_j(t) \Vert$ for every neuron. The winning neuron index is:

$$i^*(t) = \arg\min_{j=1,\dots,M} d_j(t)$$

**Step 4 — Cooperative Update (Kohonen Rule).** Apply the weight update gated by the neighbourhood kernel:

$$\mathbf{w}_j(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_j(t) + \alpha(t) \, h_{ji^*}(t) \big[ \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_j(t) \big]$$

**Step 5 — Cooling.** Decay both $\alpha(t)$ and $\sigma(t)$:

$$\alpha(t) = \alpha_0 \exp(-t/\tau_\alpha) \quad ; \quad \sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-t/\tau_\sigma)$$

**Step 6 — Repeat** Steps 2–5 for $T$ iterations.

**Role of $\alpha(t)$:** Controls the **magnitude of weight update** at every iteration. Large $\alpha$ → strong learning, but unstable. Small $\alpha$ → fine-tuning.

**Role of $h_{ji^*}(t)$:** Encodes **topological cooperation** — neurons spatially close to the BMU on the lattice receive a fraction of the update, ensuring smooth, ordered mapping.

**Why decay both?**
- Initially, **large $\sigma$** spreads the update over a wide region → the map acquires a **global, topology-preserving** structure.
- As $\sigma \to 0$, the update becomes **localised to the BMU alone** → the map **fine-tunes** to the local data density.
- Similarly, **large $\alpha$** allows rapid initial learning; **small $\alpha$** ensures stable convergence without oscillations.

(3 marks algorithm steps, 2 marks for $\alpha$ and $h$ roles, 2 marks for decay justification.)

#### Model Answer to (b)

**Corner neuron neighbourhood (rectangular lattice, 3×3):**

| Radius $\sigma$ | Neighbours Included (Bubble Kernel) | Count |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma = 1$ | The corner neuron itself only | 1 |
| $\sigma = 2$ | All 4 neurons within Manhattan distance $\leq 2$ — i.e., itself, 2 edge neighbours, and 1 centre | 4 |

**Edge (non-corner) neuron neighbourhood:**

| Radius $\sigma$ | Neighbours Included | Count |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma = 1$ | Itself + the 2 adjacent edge neurons + 1 centre = 4 neurons | 4 |
| $\sigma = 2$ | Itself + all 3 corners + both edges + 2 centres = 8 neurons | 8 |

**Asymmetry Note:** The corner neuron always has *fewer neighbours* than an edge or interior neuron, which is a known limitation of rectangular lattices (boundary-effect). It causes corner map distortion.

**Rectangular vs Hexagonal Lattice:**

| Feature | Rectangular | Hexagonal |
|---|---|---|
| Neighbours per interior neuron | 4 | 6 |
| Isotropy of neighbourhood | Lower (Manhattan bias) | Higher (Euclidean-like) |
| Implementation cost | Cheaper | Slightly costlier |
| Map distortion at boundaries | Higher | Lower |
| Kohonen's recommendation | Acceptable | **Preferred** |

Kohonen himself recommended **hexagonal lattices** because they provide a more isotropic (circular) neighbourhood in the 2-D plane, leading to smoother topology preservation and reduced boundary distortion.

(3 marks for neighbourhood sketches, 2 marks for lattice comparison, 2 marks for Kohonen's recommendation justification.)

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 – Module 4]** Answer the following:

**(a)** [7 marks] Distinguish between **supervised competitive learning (LVQ)** and **unsupervised competitive learning (SOM)**. Write the **weight update rule for LVQ-1** and explain the meaning of each term.

**(b)** [7 marks] Consider a **1-D SOM with 4 neurons** having initial weights $\mathbf{w}_1 = (0.1, 0.2)$, $\mathbf{w}_2 = (0.4, 0.1)$, $\mathbf{w}_3 = (0.7, 0.6)$, $\mathbf{w}_4 = (0.9, 0.8)$. An input $\mathbf{x} = (0.5, 0.3)$ is presented with $\alpha = 0.4$ and Gaussian neighbourhood with $\sigma = 1$. **Compute the updated weights** after one iteration.

#### Model Answer to (a)

**Differences between LVQ and SOM:**

| Feature | SOM (Kohonen, 1982) | LVQ (Kohonen, 1986) |
|---|---|---|
| Learning type | **Unsupervised** | **Supervised** |
| Class labels used | No | Yes (each class has a designated subset of neurons) |
| Update rule | Kohonen (neighbourhood-weighted) | Strict winner-take-all, sign depends on **classification correctness** |
| Output | Topological 2-D map preserving data structure | Codebook vectors for classification |
| Goal | Visualisation, clustering, dimensionality reduction | Classification (fine-tuning of codebook) |
| Neighbourhood function | Used (spatial cooperation) | Not used (only the single winner updates) |

**LVQ-1 Update Rule:**

For input $\mathbf{x}$ with class label $c(\mathbf{x})$, and the BMU $\mathbf{w}_i$ with class label $c_i$:

$$\mathbf{w}_i(t+1) = 
\begin{cases}
\mathbf{w}_i(t) + \alpha(t) \big[ \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_i(t) \big] & \text{if } c(\mathbf{x}) = c_i \\[4pt]
\mathbf{w}_i(t) - \alpha(t) \big[ \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_i(t) \big] & \text{if } c(\mathbf{x}) \neq c_i
\end{cases}$$

- $\alpha(t)$: monotonically decreasing learning rate.
- The first case **attracts** the BMU towards correctly classified samples (refining the class boundary).
- The second case **repels** the BMU from misclassified samples (pushing it away from the wrong class region).

(2 marks for comparison table, 3 marks for LVQ-1 rule, 2 marks for term explanation.)

#### Model Answer to (b)

**Step 1 — Compute Euclidean distances $d_j^2 = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_j \Vert^2$:**

**Neuron 1:** $d_1^2 = (0.5-0.1)^2 + (0.3-0.2)^2 = 0.16 + 0.01 = 0.17$

**Neuron 2:** $d_2^2 = (0.5-0.4)^2 + (0.3-0.1)^2 = 0.01 + 0.04 = 0.05$

**Neuron 3:** $d_3^2 = (0.5-0.7)^2 + (0.3-0.6)^2 = 0.04 + 0.09 = 0.13$

**Neuron 4:** $d_4^2 = (0.5-0.9)^2 + (0.3-0.8)^2 = 0.16 + 0.25 = 0.41$

**Step 2 — Identify the BMU:**

$$i^* = \arg\min \{0.17,\ 0.05,\ 0.13,\ 0.41\} = 2$$

The BMU is **Neuron 2**.

**Step 3 — Compute Gaussian neighbourhood values $h_{ji^*}$:**

For a 1-D lattice, $d(j, i^*) = \vert j - 2 \vert$. With $\sigma = 1$:

$$h_{ji^*} = \exp\!\left( -\frac{(j-2)^2}{2 \cdot 1^2} \right) = \exp\!\left( -\frac{(j-2)^2}{2} \right)$$

| $j$ | $(j-2)^2$ | $h_{ji^*}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | $e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$ |
| 2 | 0 | $e^{0} = 1.0000$ |
| 3 | 1 | $e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$ |
| 4 | 4 | $e^{-2} = 0.1353$ |

**Step 4 — Apply the Kohonen update $\mathbf{w}_j^{\text{new}} = \mathbf{w}_j + \alpha \cdot h_{ji^*} \cdot (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_j)$ with $\alpha = 0.4$:**

**Neuron 1:**
- $\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_1 = (0.4, 0.1)$
- $\alpha h = 0.4 \times 0.6065 = 0.2426$
- $\mathbf{w}_1^{\text{new}} = (0.1, 0.2) + 0.2426 \cdot (0.4, 0.1) = (0.1 + 0.0970,\ 0.2 + 0.0243) = (0.1970,\ 0.2243)$

**Neuron 2 (BMU):**
- $\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_2 = (0.1, 0.2)$
- $\alpha h = 0.4 \times 1.0 = 0.4$
- $\mathbf{w}_2^{\text{new}} = (0.4, 0.1) + 0.4 \cdot (0.1, 0.2) = (0.44,\ 0.18)$

**Neuron 3:**
- $\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_3 = (-0.2, -0.3)$
- $\alpha h = 0.4 \times 0.6065 = 0.2426$
- $\mathbf{w}_3^{\text{new}} = (0.7, 0.6) + 0.2426 \cdot (-0.2, -0.3) = (0.7 - 0.0485,\ 0.6 - 0.0728) = (0.6515,\ 0.5272)$

**Neuron 4:**
- $\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_4 = (-0.4, -0.5)$
- $\alpha h = 0.4 \times 0.1353 = 0.0541$
- $\mathbf{w}_4^{\text{new}} = (0.9, 0.8) + 0.0541 \cdot (-0.4, -0.5) = (0.9 - 0.0217,\ 0.8 - 0.0271) = (0.8783,\ 0.7729)$

**Step 5 — Verification:**

| Neuron $j$ | Old weight | New weight | Step magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (0.1, 0.2) | (0.197, 0.224) | 0.1 |
| 2 (BMU) | (0.4, 0.1) | (0.44, 0.18) | 0.08 (largest) |
| 3 | (0.7, 0.6) | (0.652, 0.527) | 0.088 |
| 4 | (0.9, 0.8) | (0.878, 0.773) | 0.034 (smallest) |

The BMU (Neuron 2) moved most towards $\mathbf{x}$, and the distant Neuron 4 moved least — consistent with topological cooperation.

(2 marks for BMU identification, 2 marks for neighbourhood values, 3 marks for weight updates.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing lattice distance with weight-space distance:** $d(j, i^*)$ in the Gaussian kernel refers to the **map-lattice** distance (e.g., $\vert j_1 - j_2 \vert$ in 1-D), NOT the Euclidean distance between weight vectors. Mixing these two will cost full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to decay $\alpha$ and $\sigma$:** The decay schedules are integral to the algorithm. Hand-computation problems may specify the *current* $\alpha, \sigma$ — but in viva, you must explain why they decay.
> 3. **Wrong sign in LVQ update:** LVQ-1 *repels* the BMU from misclassified samples (negative sign). Confusing this with the standard SOM update is a classic error.
> 4. **Confusing SOM with K-means:** Both are vector-quantisation schemes, but SOM has the **lattice topology** and **neighbourhood cooperation** that K-means lacks. Always mention *topology preservation*.
> 5. **Ignoring the time-step index $t$:** The Kohonen rule is a *dynamic* update — $\alpha(t)$, $\sigma(t)$, and $h_{ji^*}(t)$ are all functions of time. Writing a time-independent version will lose methodology marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Competitive Learning** is *unsupervised* and *WTA-based*; only the BMU updates in the pure form.
- **SOM** = Competitive Learning + **Neighbourhood Cooperation** + **Lattice Topology**.
- **BMU** = neuron with the **minimum Euclidean distance** between its weight vector and the input.
- **Kohonen Update:** $\Delta \mathbf{w}_j = \alpha(t) \, h_{ji^*}(t) \, [\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{w}_j]$.
- **Gaussian Neighbourhood:** $h_{ji^*} = \exp\!\big(-d^2(j, i^*) / 2\sigma^2(t)\big)$ — $d$ is the **lattice** distance.
- **Decay Schedules:** Both $\alpha(t)$ and $\sigma(t)$ decay mono-exponentially. $\sigma$ enables global ordering first, local fine-tuning later.
- **Quantization Error** $E_q$ measures average BMU–input distance; lower is better.
- **Topographic Error** $E_t$ measures topology preservation; lower is better.
- **Hexagonal lattice** is preferred over rectangular due to **isotropic neighbourhood** and **lower boundary distortion**.
- **LVQ** is the *supervised* cousin of SOM; uses class labels to attract/repel the BMU.
- **SOM preserves topology** but does **not** strictly minimise a global cost function (heuristic, not gradient-based).
- **KTU Favourite Terms:** *Kohonen 1982*, *WTA*, *BMU*, *topology preservation*, *neighbourhood function*, *vector quantisation*.
- **Common applications:** clustering, dimensionality reduction, anomaly detection, speech coding (LVQ), bioinformatics.
- **Hyperparameters to remember:** $\alpha_0 \in [0.1, 0.5]$, $\sigma_0 \approx$ half the lattice dimension, $T \geq 500 \times M$.

> **Final Word:** The KTU examiner is testing two things — your **conceptual clarity** (architecture, WTA, neighbourhood, topology) and your **computational skill** (BMU search, weight update, decay schedule). Practice 4–5 hand-traced numerical problems on 1-D and 2-D SOMs with at most 3–4 neurons. That is the sweet spot for a 14-mark question.
